topics.title: Tugger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tugger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tugger (Towing AGV):** A *Tugger* is a specialised class of **Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)** engineered primarily for the **horizontal transportation of multiple trailers, carts, or trolleys** in a serial "train" formation. Unlike unit-load AGVs which carry payloads directly on their chassis, the Tugger supplies the **drawbar pull (tractive effort)** required to tow a convoy of passive trailers from one workstation to another along a programmable guide path.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology for OEMUT611, the Tugger is classified as a **"Tow Vehicle AGV"** or **"Train AGV"** and is fundamentally characterized by:

| Property | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Load Interface | Drawbar / Pintle Hook |
| Payload Distribution | Distributed across N trailers |
| Primary Application | Line-side delivery, milk-run logistics |
| Drive Configuration | Differential, Steerable, or Quad-steered |
| Guidance | Wire, Magnetic tape, Laser, or Vision (SLAM) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a Toy Train...** Think of the Tugger exactly like a **model locomotive pulling a chain of wagons** on a circular track in a toy room. The locomotive (Tugger) has the engine, driver (or autopilot in our case), and headlights — but carries NO cargo itself. The wagons (trailers) are dumb boxes with wheels. The locomotive's only job is to *pull* the entire chain to the destination and then *push* or *shunt* the wagons so workers can unload them. In a factory, this "toy train" runs on invisible magnetic tracks painted on the floor, stopping at every workstation where an operator picks a part off a wagon and drops a finished part on. This is exactly what a milk-run Tugger does.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard industrial metrics define a Tugger's performance envelope:

- **Standard Drawbar Pull:** 500 N to 5000 N (light to heavy-duty class)
- **Towed Payload Range:** 1000 kg to 25,000 kg (aggregate convoy weight)
- **Operating Speed:** 0.5 m/s to 2.0 m/s (walking-pace for safety in shared aisles)
- **Trailer Coupling Standard:** Pintle hook with **Ø 25.4 mm (1 inch)** lunette ring
- **Battery Voltage Standard:** **24 V DC, 48 V DC, or 80 V DC**
- **Minimum Aisle Width (Turn):** Typically **Aisle Width = 2 × Trailer Length + 200 mm** clearance

> [!TIP]
> **Why a Tugger and not a forklift?** A human-driven forklift makes **~120 trips/day** of mixed loads. A Tugger running a milk-run makes **~300 deterministic trips/day** with zero idle time, no license required, and no operator fatigue. This is the core operational thesis of every Tugger deployment studied under Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Tugger System

A Tugger is never deployed as a standalone robot — it is part of a **Tugger Train System (TTS)** consisting of:

1. **The Tugger Tractor (Motive Unit):** Contains the drive motors, batteries, guidance sensors, controller, and the front drawbar.
2. **The Trailers (Passive Units):** A train of 1 to 10 passive carts equipped with steered/castor wheels, lighting, and safety bumpers.
3. **The Guide Path:** Magnetic tape, inductive wire, QR-coded floor, or LiDAR-mapped virtual path.
4. **The Fleet Management Software (FMS):** Dispatches the Tugger, schedules pick-ups/drop-offs, and monitors battery SoC.
5. **The I/O Station (Buffer):** Automatic hitching/dropping stations where trailers are parked for the next mission.

## 2.2 Types of Tugger AGVs (KTU Syllabus Sub-classification)

- **Type 1 — Pick-up & Delivery Tugger:** Travels between fixed points, drops full trailers, picks up empty ones. *Classic milk-run.*
- **Type 2 — Assembly Tugger:** Synchronised with a moving assembly line, indexes with the conveyor at a constant velocity. Used in automotive chassis lines.
- **Type 3 — Container Tugger:** Heavy-duty variant used in ports and intermodal yards to shunt ISO containers. Can tow up to 30 tonnes.
- **Type 4 — Hybrid Tugger:** Combines towing with a small onboard lift deck — can carry 1 pallet AND tow 2 trailers simultaneously.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Tugger Selection

The core engineering justification for choosing a Tugger over a fleet of unit-load AGVs is rooted in **Logistics Optimization**:

- **Consolidation of trips:** One Tugger replaces N manual trips.
- **Reduced fleet cost:** One drive system powers N payload containers.
- **Scalability:** Adding capacity = adding trailers, not vehicles.
- **Energy efficiency:** Shared drive motor amortises battery drain over the entire train's payload.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Tugger Traction Analysis** — these are board-exam favourites for Module 1.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{RR} = \mu_{r} \cdot W_{total}$ | Newtons (N) | $\mu_{r}$ for steel on concrete $\approx$ 0.015 |
| 2 | Grade Resistance | $F_{G} = W_{total} \cdot \sin\theta \approx W_{total} \cdot g\%$ | Newtons (N) | $\theta$ is slope angle; $g\%$ is grade percent |
| 3 | Acceleration Force | $F_{A} = m_{total} \cdot a$ | Newtons (N) | $m_{total} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{g}$ |
| 4 | Total Resistance | $F_{T} = F_{RR} + F_{G} + F_{A}$ | Newtons (N) | Sum of all opposing forces |
| 5 | Required Drawbar Pull | $P_{DB} = F_{T} + F_{coupling}$ | Newtons (N) | $F_{coupling} \approx 50$ N safety margin |
| 6 | Required Tractive Power | $P = F_{T} \cdot v$ | Watts (W) | $v$ is steady-state velocity |
| 7 | Available Adhesion Limit | $F_{max} = \mu_{adhesion} \cdot W_{tractor}$ | Newtons (N) | Cannot exceed this without wheel slip |
| 8 | Battery Energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Watt-hours (Wh) | Determines mission duration |
| 9 | Turning Radius (Ackermann) | $R = \dfrac{L}{\sin\delta}$ | Metres (m) | $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta$ = steer angle |
| 10 | Scaled Load Capacity | $Q = n \cdot q_{trailer}$ | kg | $n$ = number of trailers, $q$ = per-unit load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wheel Slip Warning:** The most critical design constraint on a Tugger is that **$P_{DB} \leq \mu_{adhesion} \cdot W_{tractor}$**. If the drawbar pull demand exceeds the friction limit between the tractor's drive wheels and the floor, the wheels spin, the train stalls, and the AGV faults out. This is why heavy-load Tuggers use **weighted ballast** in their chassis.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive Assembly (Tata, Toyota, Ford):** Tuggers deliver door panels, seats, and dashboards to the moving line every 90 seconds.
- **Hospital Logistics:** Tuggers tow linen carts and meal trolleys through corridors.
- **E-commerce Warehouses:** Tuggers transport empty totes from picking stations to consolidation zones.
- **Airport Baggage:** Limited deployment due to safety regulations, but used for inter-terminal cargo.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Drawbar Pull for a 3-Trailer Tugger Train

### Given Problem Statement
A Tugger AGV of mass **$M_T = 800$ kg** is required to tow **$n = 3$ trailers**, each of mass **$M_{tr} = 600$ kg** (including payload), up a **2% grade** at a constant velocity of **$v = 1.5$ m/s**, with an acceleration of **$a = 0.3$ m/s²**. The rolling resistance coefficient on the concrete floor is **$\mu_r = 0.018$**, and the adhesion coefficient for the rubber-tired tractor wheels is **$\mu_{adhesion} = 0.6$**. Calculate:

1. The total rolling resistance force.
2. The grade resistance force.
3. The acceleration force.
4. The required drawbar pull at the hitch.
5. The required motor power at the wheels.
6. Whether the wheels will slip. Justify.

### Step 1 — Total Mass of the Train

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= M_{Tractor} + n \cdot M_{trailer} \\
M_{total} &= 800 + (3 \times 600) \\
M_{total} &= 800 + 1800 \\
M_{total} &= 2600 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The tractor alone cannot define the system inertia; every trailer adds its own mass to the Newton's-Second-Law equation because the train must be accelerated as a single rigid body.

### Step 2 — Total Weight (Normal Reaction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= M_{total} \cdot g \\
W_{total} &= 2600 \times 9.81 \\
W_{total} &= 25506 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** All resistance forces scale with the normal force the floor exerts on the train. We assume a flat, level horizontal reference and isolate grade effects later.

### Step 3 — Rolling Resistance Force $F_{RR}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{RR} &= \mu_r \cdot W_{total} \\
F_{RR} &= 0.018 \times 25506 \\
F_{RR} &= 459.108 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Rolling resistance arises from hysteresis losses in the tyres and micro-deformation of the floor. Empirically tabulated as a fraction ($\mu_r$) of the normal load.

### Step 4 — Grade Resistance Force $F_G$

The grade is 2%, meaning a vertical rise of 2 metres per 100 metres of horizontal travel.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \arctan(0.02) \\
\theta &\approx 1.1458^\circ \\
F_{G} &= W_{total} \cdot \sin\theta \\
F_{G} &= 25506 \times \sin(1.1458^\circ) \\
F_{G} &= 25506 \times 0.02000 \\
F_{G} &\approx 510.12 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ in radians. We may also use the **grade-percentage shortcut**: $F_G = W_{total} \times g\%$.

### Step 5 — Acceleration Force $F_A$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{A} &= M_{total} \cdot a \\
F_{A} &= 2600 \times 0.3 \\
F_{A} &= 780 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is the inertial resistance of accelerating the entire train mass. Note that $g$ does NOT appear here — only the mass in kg and acceleration in m/s² are required.

### Step 6 — Total Tractive Effort Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{T} &= F_{RR} + F_{G} + F_{A} \\
F_{T} &= 459.108 + 510.12 + 780 \\
F_{T} &= 1749.228 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is the drawbar pull the Tugger's hitch must deliver to the first trailer.

### Step 7 — Required Motor Power at the Drive Wheels

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_{T} \cdot v \\
P &= 1749.228 \times 1.5 \\
P &= 2623.84 \text{ W} \\
P &\approx 2.624 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Power is force × velocity at steady state. The motor must continuously deliver this mechanical output to maintain 1.5 m/s motion against all resistances.

### Step 8 — Slip Verification

The drive wheels sit on the tractor (mass = 800 kg). Their vertical normal force is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{tractor} &= M_{T} \cdot g = 800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \text{ N} \\
F_{max} &= \mu_{adhesion} \cdot W_{tractor} \\
F_{max} &= 0.6 \times 7848 \\
F_{max} &= 4708.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **$F_T = 1749.228$ N $\leq$ $F_{max} = 4708.8$ N**, the wheels will NOT slip.

**Conclusion:** The Tugger design is traction-safe with a **slip margin of 2959.57 N** (≈ 63% safety).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Total Mass | $M_{total}$ | 2600 | kg |
| Total Weight | $W_{total}$ | 25506 | N |
| Rolling Resistance | $F_{RR}$ | 459.11 | N |
| Grade Resistance | $F_G$ | 510.12 | N |
| Acceleration Force | $F_A$ | 780.00 | N |
| Drawbar Pull | $F_T$ | 1749.23 | N |
| Motor Power | $P$ | 2.62 | kW |
| Adhesion Limit | $F_{max}$ | 4708.80 | N |
| Slip Margin | $\Delta F$ | 2959.57 | N |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tugger System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Operator Console] --> B1[Fleet Management Software FMS]
    B1 --> C1[Task Dispatcher]
    C1 --> D1[Wireless Link Zigbee or WiFi 5GHz]
    D1 --> E1[Tugger Tractor Onboard PLC]
    E1 --> F1[Guidance Sensor Array]
    F1 --> G1[Path Tracking Controller]
    G1 --> H1[Motor Drive Inverter]
    H1 --> I1[Drive Wheels Differential Pair]
    I1 --> J1[Drawbar Hitch Assembly]
    J1 --> K1[Trailer 1 Lead Wagon]
    K1 --> L1[Trailer 2 Middle Wagon]
    L1 --> M1[Trailer 3 End Wagon]
    E1 --> N1[Battery Pack 48V Li-ion]
    E1 --> O1[Safety Scanner SICK LiDAR]
    E1 --> P1[Obstacle Detection Logic]
    P1 --> Q1[Emergency Stop Relay]
    Q1 --> R1[Mechanical Brake Actuator]
    E1 --> S1[HMI Touchscreen Display]
```

## 4.2 Tugger Train Physical Layout (Top-Down Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Tractor Unit 800 kg] -- Drawbar --> W1[Trailer 1 600 kg]
    W1 -- Pintle Hook --> W2[Trailer 2 600 kg]
    W2 -- Pintle Hook --> W3[Trailer 3 600 kg]
    W3 --> TA[End Marker LED Beacon]
    T1 --> GP[Magnetic Tape Guide Path]
    GP --> WS1[Workstation 1 Stop]
    GP --> WS2[Workstation 2 Stop]
    GP --> WS3[Workstation 3 Stop]
```

## 4.3 Subsystem Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerTrain
        PT1[Battery 48V] --> PT2[DC-DC Converter]
        PT2 --> PT3[Inverter VFD]
        PT3 --> PT4[BLDC Drive Motor]
        PT4 --> PT5[Gear Reducer 40:1]
        PT5 --> PT6[Drive Wheels]
    end
    subgraph GuidanceStack
        GS1[Magnetic Tape Sensor] --> GS2[Encoder Odometry]
        GS2 --> GS3[Steering Angle Sensor]
        GS3 --> GS4[Sensor Fusion Filter]
        GS4 --> GS5[Path Deviation PID]
    end
    subgraph SafetyLayer
        SL1[SICK 2D LiDAR] --> SL2[Safety PLC]
        SL2 --> SL3[E-Stop Circuit]
        SL3 --> SL4[Brake Chopper]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mission Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> MissionAccepted: FMS Dispatch
    MissionAccepted --> PathAcquisition: Map Lookup
    PathAcquisition --> Hitching: Coupling Verified
    Hitching --> Navigating: Path Follower Active
    Navigating --> StopAtStation: Beacon Detected
    StopAtStation --> PayloadExchange: Doors Open 30 sec
    PayloadExchange --> Navigating: Resume Command
    Navigating --> ReturnPath: All Stations Served
    ReturnPath --> Unhitching: Drop Zone Reached
    Unhitching --> Idle: Coupling Released
    Navigating --> EmergencyStop: Obstacle or E-Stop
    EmergencyStop --> Idle: Reset by Operator
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 marks]**
> **Q:** *Define a Tugger AGV. How is it functionally different from a Unit Load AGV?*

**Model Answer:**
A Tugger AGV (Tow Vehicle AGV) is an automated vehicle designed to **tow a series of passive trailers** along a programmed path. It does not carry payload directly on its own chassis. Instead, it supplies the **drawbar pull** required to drag a train of 1 to 10 trailers.
In contrast, a **Unit Load AGV** carries the payload directly on a single deck (e.g., a single pallet or tote) mounted on top of the vehicle's chassis.
The functional distinction is the **load interface**: Tugger = indirect (drawbar + trailers); Unit Load = direct (onboard platform). *(3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 marks]**
> **Q:** *List any three navigation methods used by modern Tugger AGVs.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Inductive Wire Guidance** — a current-carrying wire buried in the floor cut is sensed by onboard coils.
2. **Magnetic Tape Guidance** — adhesive magnetic tape applied to the floor, detected by Hall-effect sensors.
3. **Laser Navigation (LiDAR)** — retroreflective targets on walls/columns used for triangulation, or natural-feature SLAM for map-based localization.
*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14 marks, Internal Choice]**

### ✅ CHOICE A — Question A (14 marks)

**Q1.** *(a)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Tugger AGV system**. Clearly identify the Power-Train, Guidance, Safety, and Payload subsystems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Tugger of mass **1500 kg** tows **4 trailers** each of mass **500 kg** along a horizontal floor at a constant velocity of **1.2 m/s**. The rolling resistance coefficient is **0.020**, and the motor-to-wheel efficiency is **85%**. Calculate:
(i) The rolling resistance force.
(ii) The drawbar pull required.
(iii) The mechanical power at the wheels.
(iv) The electrical power drawn from the battery.
*(7 marks)*

---

### ✅ CHOICE B — Question B (14 marks, Alternative)

**Q2.** *(a)* Explain the **milk-run logistics concept** and discuss how a Tugger AGV implements it in an automotive assembly plant. List the advantages over manual forklift-based material handling. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **wheel-slip condition** for a Tugger AGV moving up a slope. A Tugger of mass **1200 kg** must tow a load of **3500 kg** up a **3% grade**. The adhesion coefficient is **0.55**. Verify whether the Tugger will slip, and if it does, calculate the **additional ballast mass required** in the tractor chassis to prevent slipping. Assume rolling resistance and gradient are already balanced by the motor torque. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### 📘 Solution to Q1(a) — Functional Architecture

A Tugger AGV system comprises four tightly integrated subsystems:

**1. Power-Train Subsystem:**
- **Battery Pack (48 V Li-ion):** Source of electrical energy.
- **DC-DC Converter:** Steps down voltage for auxiliary electronics.
- **VFD Inverter:** Converts DC to 3-phase AC for BLDC drive motor.
- **Gear Reducer (40:1):** Multiplies torque at the cost of speed.
- **Drive Wheels (Differential Pair):** Provides both propulsion and steering.

**2. Guidance Subsystem:**
- **Path Sensor:** Magnetic tape / inductive wire pickup.
- **Odometry Encoder:** Measures wheel rotation for distance feedback.
- **Steering Angle Sensor:** Reports wheel orientation.
- **Sensor Fusion & PID Controller:** Minimises lateral path deviation.

**3. Safety Subsystem:**
- **SICK 2D LiDAR:** 360° horizontal scan field for obstacle detection.
- **Safety PLC:** Certified SIL-3 logic processor.
- **E-Stop Circuit:** Hardwired to brake chopper and motor contactor.

**4. Payload Interface Subsystem:**
- **Drawbar / Pintle Hook Assembly:** Connects to the lead trailer.
- **Hitch Status Sensor:** Confirms positive coupling before motion.
- **Trailer Lighting & Marker Beacons:** Visibility for safety.

The four subsystems communicate via a **central onboard PLC** over a **CAN bus** backbone.

---

### 📘 Solution to Q1(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
$M_T = 1500$ kg, $n = 4$, $M_{tr} = 500$ kg, $v = 1.2$ m/s, $\mu_r = 0.020$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$, horizontal floor, constant velocity.

**Step (i) — Total Mass & Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= 1500 + (4 \times 500) = 3500 \text{ kg} \\
W_{total} &= 3500 \times 9.81 = 34335 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Rolling Resistance:**

$$
F_{RR} = \mu_r \cdot W_{total} = 0.020 \times 34335 = 686.7 \text{ N}
$$

Since velocity is constant, acceleration force = 0 and grade force = 0 (horizontal).
Therefore **Drawbar Pull** $P_{DB} = F_{RR} = 686.7$ N.

**Step (iii) — Mechanical Power at Wheels:**

$$
P_{mech} = F_{RR} \times v = 686.7 \times 1.2 = 824.04 \text{ W} \approx 0.824 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iv) — Electrical Power from Battery:**

$$
P_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{824.04}{0.85} = 969.46 \text{ W} \approx 0.969 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| $F_{RR}$ | 686.7 N |
| $P_{DB}$ | 686.7 N |
| $P_{mech}$ | 0.824 kW |
| $P_{elec}$ | 0.969 kW |

---

### 📘 Solution to Q2(a) — Milk-Run Logistics

The **milk-run logistics** concept originates from the dairy industry, where a single delivery truck follows a fixed circular route, picking up empty cans and dropping off filled ones at multiple farms in sequence. Applied to manufacturing, it means:

- A single Tugger AGV follows a **fixed loop** through the plant.
- At each workstation, it **drops one full trailer** of parts and **picks up one empty trailer**.
- The cycle repeats until all stations are serviced, then the Tugger returns to the central supermarket.

**Advantages over forklift-based handling:**

| Aspect | Manual Forklift | Tugger Milk-Run |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trips/day | ~120 | ~300 |
| Operator cost | High (skilled labour) | Zero (autonomous) |
| Accident rate | High (human error) | Near-zero (LiDAR-guarded) |
| Route efficiency | Variable | Deterministic, optimised |
| Delivery timing | Unpredictable | Synchronised to takt time |

In an automotive assembly plant, the Tugger delivers door trims at station 1, instrument panels at station 2, and seat assemblies at station 3 — all on a 90-second takt cycle.

---

### 📘 Solution to Q2(b) — Slip Verification and Ballast Calculation

**Given:**
$M_T = 1200$ kg, $M_{load} = 3500$ kg, grade = 3%, $\mu_{adhesion} = 0.55$.

**Step 1 — Total Tractive Effort Required:**

Assuming rolling resistance and grade are balanced, the limiting case is **wheel slip at rest or low speed on slope**. The required drawbar pull is effectively the **grade force** of the load:

$$
F_{G,load} = M_{load} \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \approx 3500 \times 9.81 \times 0.03 = 1030.05 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Available Adhesion Force (Original Tractor):**

$$
F_{max,orig} = \mu_{adhesion} \cdot M_T \cdot g = 0.55 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 6474.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Slip Verification:**

Since $F_{G,load} = 1030.05$ N $\leq$ $F_{max,orig} = 6474.6$ N, the tractor **will NOT slip** under this load.

However, if the load were heavier or the grade steeper such that $F_{G,load} > F_{max}$, we would need ballast.

**Step 4 — Required Ballast for Hypothetical Critical Case:**

Suppose the load required $F_{G,load} = 7000$ N (above current adhesion). The required new tractor mass $M_T'$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_T' \cdot g \cdot \mu_{adhesion} &= F_{G,load} \\
M_T' &= \dfrac{7000}{9.81 \times 0.55} \\
M_T' &= \dfrac{7000}{5.3955} \\
M_T' &\approx 1297.4 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Additional ballast required:**

$$
M_{ballast} = M_T' - M_T = 1297.4 - 1200 = 97.4 \text{ kg}
$$

**Conclusion:** In our original scenario, **no ballast is required** — the Tugger has a healthy **5444 N slip margin**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply trailer mass by count (n).** Many students compute drawbar pull using only the tractor mass. Always compute $M_{total} = M_T + n \cdot M_{tr}$.
> 2. **Using $g$ inside the acceleration term.** The acceleration force is $F_A = m \cdot a$, NOT $F_A = (m \cdot g) \cdot a$. The $g$ conversion is unnecessary and wrong.
> 3. **Ignoring grade percentage shortcut.** Use $F_G = W \cdot g\%$ directly when grade < 5%, instead of calculating $\sin\theta$.
> 4. **Not verifying slip condition.** Even if drawbar pull is computed correctly, you MUST verify $F_{DB} \leq \mu_{adhesion} \cdot W_{tractor}$, or full marks are not awarded.
> 5. **Confusing drawbar pull with tractive effort.** Drawbar pull is what the trailer sees at the hitch; tractive effort is at the tyre contact patch. They differ by rolling resistance of the tractor.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **Tugger** is a towing AGV — it pulls trailers, never carries payload directly.
- ✅ The four core subsystems are **Power-Train, Guidance, Safety, Payload**.
- ✅ The fundamental traction equation is **$F_T = F_{RR} + F_G + F_A$**.
- ✅ **Rolling Resistance:** $F_{RR} = \mu_r \cdot W$. Typical $\mu_r$ = 0.015 to 0.025 for rubber on concrete.
- ✅ **Grade Resistance:** $F_G = W \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot g\%$ for small angles.
- ✅ **Acceleration Force:** $F_A = m \cdot a$ (no $g$!).
- ✅ **Slip Limit:** $F_{max} = \mu_{adhesion} \cdot W_{tractor}$.
- ✅ **Mechanical Power:** $P_{mech} = F_T \cdot v$.
- ✅ **Electrical Power:** $P_{elec} = P_{mech} / \eta_{motor}$.
- ✅ Milk-run = single Tugger on a fixed circular route, exchanging trailers at every station.
- ✅ The adhesion coefficient for rubber on dry concrete is **~0.6**, on wet concrete drops to **~0.4**.
- ✅ Tuggers are preferred over forklifts when trip frequency exceeds ~80/day.
- ✅ Safety subsystem must be **SIL-3 certified** per ISO 3691-4 (KTU-referenced standard).
- ✅ Battery sizing uses $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$; for an 8-hour shift, energy capacity must be at least $8 \times P_{elec}$ in Wh.
- ✅ Always compute total train mass BEFORE applying any force equation.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Tugger**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unit Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Unit Load

In the context of **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** and modern material handling engineering, a **Unit Load** is defined as a **standardized, pre-assembled collection of items, components, or raw materials that are arranged, packaged, and secured together so they can be moved, stored, and handled as a single, indivisible entity** by mechanical handling equipment.

The concept of a unit load is governed by the principle of *consolidation for mechanical advantage*. Rather than transporting individual items (which requires countless trips), a unit load aggregates items into one transportable bundle. This bundle may consist of:

- A pallet with stacked cartons
- A tote pan filled with small components
- A drum containing liquids or chemicals
- A rack containing mixed SKUs
- A roll of sheet metal or paper
- A container (ISO maritime container)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEMUT611 – Module 1):**
> Unit Loads form the foundational *cargo definition* layer in AGV design. Before specifying the vehicle, its guidance system, or its control architecture, the engineer must precisely define the **physical, dimensional, and weight characteristics** of the unit load. The AGV is then *derived* from the unit load, not the other way around.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a supermarket with a shopping list of **40 items**.

**Without Unit Load Thinking:**
You carry each item individually to the billing counter, paying and bagging them one-by-one. You make **40 separate trips**. You are exhausted. The cashier is exhausted. The store is congested.

**With Unit Load Thinking:**
You grab a **shopping cart (the unit load device)**, place all 40 items inside in a single trip, and wheel the entire *unit load* to the billing counter in **ONE trip**. You pay once. The cashier scans once. You leave efficiently.

In this analogy:
- The **shopping cart** = the **pallet, tote, or container**
- The **40 items inside** = the **individual SKUs being consolidated**
- **You wheeling the cart** = the **AGV transporting the unit load**
- The **single trip** = the **unit load principle: one transport cycle = many items moved**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Recap):**
> A *unit load* is NOT the items themselves. It is the **composite object** formed by combining the items + the carrying device (pallet/container) + the securing mechanism (stretch wrap, straps). This composite object becomes the *single handled entity*.

## 1.3 Why Unit Loads Matter in AGV Systems

In an AGV-driven warehouse or factory, **every transport mission is defined by a unit load**. The AGV's:
- **Deck size** is dictated by unit load footprint
- **Payload capacity** is dictated by unit load weight
- **Lift mechanism** is dictated by unit load transfer requirements
- **Stability calculations** are dictated by unit load center of gravity
- **Battery sizing** is dictated by cycle time which depends on load weight

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard Reference:**
> The most common unit load in AGV applications is the **GMA pallet** (also called the standard US pallet) measuring **1219 mm × 1016 mm (48 in × 40 in)** with a rated capacity of approximately **1000 kg to 1500 kg**. The European equivalent is the **EUR pallet** (Euro pallet) at **1200 mm × 800 mm**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| GMA Pallet Footprint | 1219 × 1016 | mm |
| EUR Pallet Footprint | 1200 × 800 | mm |
| ISO 6780 Standard Sizes | 6 recognized sizes | mm |
| Typical Pallet Height (loaded) | 1200 to 1800 | mm |
| Standard Fork Pocket Spacing | 660 (GMA) / 365 (EUR) | mm |
| Standard Pallet Deck Height | 120 to 150 | mm |

---

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2.1 Classification of Unit Loads

Unit loads in AGV systems can be classified along multiple axes:

### A. Based on Physical Form

1. **Palletized Loads** – Goods stacked on a flat pallet (wood, plastic, metal)
2. **Containerized Loads** – Items placed in rigid containers (totes, bins)
3. **Drum Loads** – Cylindrical vessels (steel drums, plastic drums)
4. **Rack Loads** – Items transported on wheeled racks or carts
5. **Roll Loads** – Cylindrical objects (paper rolls, cable reels, steel coils)
6. **Specialty Loads** – Custom fixtures for automotive bodies, semiconductor FOUPs, etc.

### B. Based on Handling Attachment on AGV

| Attachment Type | Suitable Unit Load | AGV Series |
|---|---|---|
| **Forklift attachment** | Palletized loads | Fork AGV |
| **Conveyor deck** | Totes, boxes | Conveyor AGV |
| **Lift deck** | Palletized loads requiring floor-level pickup | Unit Load AGV (UL-AGV) |
| **Roller bed** | Palletized loads on conveyors | Roller AGV |
| **Clamp attachment** | Drums, rolls | Special AGV |
| **Hook/tow attachment** | Trailers, carts | Tow/Tugger AGV |

> [!NOTE]
> **OEMUT611 Specific Context:**
> The course dedicates significant attention to **Unit Load AGVs (UL-AGVs)**. These are typically *counterbalanced* or *straddle* type vehicles equipped with a powered roller deck or a lifting mechanism to handle palletized unit loads between conveyors, pick-up/drop-off (P&D) stations, and storage aisles.

### C. Based on Stability

1. **Stable Loads** – Self-supporting, square footprint, low CG
2. **Unstable Loads** – Require top-load retainers, stretch wrap, or bandings
3. **Hazardous Loads** – Require special handling (chemicals, flammables)

## 2.2 Unit Load Formation

The process of creating a unit load is called **Unit Load Formation** or **Palletizing**. The objective is to maximize *cube utilization* and *load density* while maintaining stability.

**Key Formation Rules (SLAPP):**

- **S**ize: Match item dimensions to standard pallet footprint
- **L**ayer pattern: Use *interlocking* or *column stacking* patterns
- **A**lignment: Items must align with pallet edges for AGV fork access
- **P**rotection: Use slip sheets, edge protectors, stretch wrap
- **P**rofile: Keep load top flat; maximum recommended height is typically **1.8 times the pallet's shortest side**

## 2.3 Unit Load Center of Gravity (CG)

The CG of a unit load determines:
- Fork pocket positioning on the AGV deck
- Stability during transport (acceleration/braking forces)
- Torque required by AGV drive motors

For a unit load composed of *n* discrete items:

$$ X_{cg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot X_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i} $$

$$ Y_{cg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot Y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i} $$

$$ Z_{cg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot Z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i} $$

Where:
- $W_i$ = weight of item *i* (N or kg)
- $X_i, Y_i, Z_i$ = coordinates of item *i*'s CG (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> For AGV design, the *horizontal CG offset* (X_cg, Y_cg) from the geometric center of the pallet must be within **±50 mm to ±100 mm** for stable transport. Excessive offset during acceleration can cause the unit load to topple, even if the AGV remains stable.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unit Load Weight | $W_{total} = W_{pallet} + \sum W_{items}$ | kg | AGV payload spec |
| 2 | Center of Gravity X | $X_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot X_i}{\sum W_i}$ | mm | Stability check |
| 3 | Center of Gravity Y | $Y_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot Y_i}{\sum W_i}$ | mm | Stability check |
| 4 | Stability Factor | $SF = \frac{W_{base}}{W_{base} + W_{load}}$ | dimensionless | Topple resistance |
| 5 | Load Density | $\rho_{load} = \frac{W_{total}}{V_{load}}$ | kg/m³ | Warehouse space use |
| 6 | Cube Utilization | $CU = \frac{V_{items}}{V_{pallet}} \times 100\%$ | % | Palletizing efficiency |
| 7 | AGV Throughput | $T = \frac{3600}{t_{cycle}}$ | loads/hr | System design |
| 8 | Tipping Moment | $M_{tip} = W_{load} \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}$ | N·m | Dynamic stability |
| 9 | Restoring Moment | $M_{restore} = W_{total} \cdot g \cdot \frac{d_{base}}{2}$ | N·m | Stability margin |
| 10 | Pallet Footprint (GMA) | $1219 \times 1016$ | mm | North American standard |
| 11 | Pallet Footprint (EUR) | $1200 \times 800$ | mm | European standard |
| 12 | Fork Pocket Width | $w_{pocket} = 6$ to $9$ | in | AGV fork design |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Design:** Cube utilization directly determines how many SKUs fit in a facility
- **AGV Fleet Sizing:** Payload × Throughput = Required AGV count
- **Safety Engineering:** Tipping moment calculation prevents load-shift accidents
- **Cost Engineering:** Higher cube utilization = fewer transport cycles = lower $/unit cost
- **Standards Compliance:** ISO 6780, ISO 6781, GMA, EUR, ASRS pallet standards

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Derivation: Center of Gravity of a Composite Unit Load

### Problem Setup

Consider a GMA pallet (1219 mm × 1016 mm) loaded with **4 distinct item stacks**, each containing uniform cartons. We want to find the CG coordinates of the entire unit load.

**Given Data:**

| Stack | Weight $W_i$ (kg) | X position (mm) | Y position (mm) | Z position (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stack A (front-left) | 150 | 305 | 254 | 600 |
| Stack B (front-right) | 200 | 915 | 254 | 600 |
| Stack C (rear-left) | 180 | 305 | 762 | 600 |
| Stack D (rear-right) | 220 | 915 | 762 | 600 |
| Pallet itself | 25 | 610 | 508 | 75 |

The reference origin is taken at the **front-left bottom corner** of the pallet footprint.

### Step 1: Total Weight Calculation

$$ W_{total} = W_A + W_B + W_C + W_D + W_{pallet} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ W_{total} = 150 + 200 + 180 + 220 + 25 $$

$$ W_{total} = 775 \text{ kg} $$

**Explanation:** The total weight is the simple arithmetic sum because we are using the *additive property of mass*. This is the denominator for all CG calculations.

### Step 2: Moment Summation Along X-Axis

The moment of each stack about the Y-axis is $W_i \cdot X_i$. We sum all such moments:

$$ M_x = \sum_{i} W_i \cdot X_i $$

$$ M_x = (150)(305) + (200)(915) + (180)(305) + (220)(915) + (25)(610) $$

Computing each term:

$$ M_x = 45{,}750 + 183{,}000 + 54{,}900 + 201{,}300 + 15{,}250 $$

Summing:

$$ M_x = 500{,}200 \text{ kg·mm} $$

**Explanation:** The term $W_i \cdot X_i$ is the *first moment of mass* about the Y-axis, in units of kg·mm. By the principle of moments, the sum of individual moments equals the moment of the equivalent single concentrated mass located at $X_{cg}$.

### Step 3: Solve for X-coordinate of CG

By the definition of center of gravity:

$$ X_{cg} = \frac{M_x}{W_{total}} = \frac{500{,}200}{775} $$

$$ X_{cg} = 645.4 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 4: Moment Summation Along Y-Axis

$$ M_y = \sum_{i} W_i \cdot Y_i $$

$$ M_y = (150)(254) + (200)(254) + (180)(762) + (220)(762) + (25)(508) $$

Computing each term:

$$ M_y = 38{,}100 + 50{,}800 + 137{,}160 + 167{,}640 + 12{,}700 $$

Summing:

$$ M_y = 406{,}400 \text{ kg·mm} $$

### Step 5: Solve for Y-coordinate of CG

$$ Y_{cg} = \frac{M_y}{W_{total}} = \frac{406{,}400}{775} $$

$$ Y_{cg} = 524.4 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 6: Geometric Center Comparison

The geometric center of the GMA pallet footprint:

$$ X_{geo} = \frac{1219}{2} = 609.5 \text{ mm} $$

$$ Y_{geo} = \frac{1016}{2} = 508.0 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 7: CG Offset Calculation

The CG offset indicates how far the actual load CG deviates from the geometric center:

$$ \Delta X = \vert X_{cg} - X_{geo} \vert = \vert 645.4 - 609.5 \vert = 35.9 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \Delta Y = \vert Y_{cg} - Y_{geo} \vert = \vert 524.4 - 508.0 \vert = 16.4 \text{ mm} $$

**Explanation:** Both offsets are within the **±50 mm acceptable tolerance** for stable AGV transport. The heavier stack D (220 kg) and stack B (200 kg) on the right side pulls the CG slightly toward higher X. This is acceptable; the AGV can safely accelerate up to a design limit calculated in the next section.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability Factor During AGV Acceleration

### Problem Setup

The unit load from Section 3.1 (775 kg) is being transported on a Unit Load AGV. The AGV accelerates at **$a = 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$** along the X-axis. We need to verify the load will NOT tip over the front edge of the AGV deck.

**Given:**
- Load CG height above deck: $h_{cg} = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- AGV deck depth (front-to-rear): $d_{deck} = 1219 \text{ mm} = 1.219 \text{ m}$
- Load base dimension along travel direction: $L_{load} = 1016 \text{ mm} = 1.016 \text{ m}$
- Acceleration: $a = 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Step 1: Identify the Two Competing Moments

When the AGV accelerates forward, an inertial force acts on the load CG in the *opposite* direction (backward), creating a **tipping moment** about the rear edge of the load base. The weight of the load, acting downward at the CG, creates a **restoring moment** about the same pivot edge.

### Step 2: Calculate the Inertial (Tipping) Force

By Newton's Second Law:

$$ F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = 775 \cdot 0.5 = 387.5 \text{ N} $$

This force acts horizontally at the CG, height $h_{cg}$ above the deck.

### Step 3: Calculate the Tipping Moment

$$ M_{tip} = F_{inertia} \cdot h_{cg} = 387.5 \cdot 0.6 $$

$$ M_{tip} = 232.5 \text{ N·m} $$

**Explanation:** The lever arm is the vertical distance from the pivot edge (rear-bottom of load) to the CG. The inertial force tries to rotate the load about this edge.

### Step 4: Calculate the Restoring Moment

The restoring moment is provided by gravity acting at the CG, with the horizontal lever arm being the distance from the pivot to the vertical line of action of the weight:

$$ d_{lever} = \frac{L_{load}}{2} = \frac{1.016}{2} = 0.508 \text{ m} $$

$$ W_{load} = m \cdot g = 775 \cdot 9.81 = 7{,}602.75 \text{ N} $$

$$ M_{restore} = W_{load} \cdot d_{lever} = 7{,}602.75 \cdot 0.508 $$

$$ M_{restore} = 3{,}862.20 \text{ N·m} $$

### Step 5: Compute the Stability Margin

The **Anti-Tip Safety Factor (ATSF)** is:

$$ ATSF = \frac{M_{restore}}{M_{tip}} = \frac{3{,}862.20}{232.5} $$

$$ ATSF = 16.6 $$

**Explanation:** An ATSF of **16.6** is extremely high, meaning the load is *vastly* over-designed for stability against tipping. The load will remain firmly planted on the AGV deck. Industry minimum acceptable ATSF is typically **1.5 to 2.0**; values above 5.0 indicate a highly stable configuration.

### Step 6: Find the Maximum Safe Acceleration

We can solve for the maximum acceleration before the restoring moment equals the tipping moment (ATSF = 1):

$$ M_{restore} = M_{tip} $$

$$ W_{load} \cdot d_{lever} = m \cdot a_{max} \cdot h_{cg} $$

$$ a_{max} = \frac{W_{load} \cdot d_{lever}}{m \cdot h_{cg}} = \frac{g \cdot d_{lever}}{h_{cg}} $$

$$ a_{max} = \frac{9.81 \cdot 0.508}{0.6} $$

$$ a_{max} = 8.30 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

**Explanation:** The AGV could theoretically accelerate at up to **8.30 m/s²** before the load begins to tip. Actual operating accelerations (0.3–0.8 m/s²) provide a massive safety margin.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Unit Load vs Other Load Types

| Parameter | Unit Load | Bulk Load | Order Picking Load |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Aggregated standard load | Loose, continuous material | Individual SKUs |
| Examples | Pallet of cartons | Grain, coal, sand | Single item picked |
| Handling Equipment | AGV, forklift, conveyor | Conveyor, hopper, bucket | Pick cart, tote AGV |
| Throughput | High (one move = many items) | Very high (continuous) | Low (one move = one SKU) |
| Automation Level | Very high | High | Moderate to high |
| Cube Utilization | 70–90% | 100% | Variable |
| Cost per Pick | Low | Lowest | Highest |
| Typical AGV Type | Unit Load AGV (UL-AGV) | Not typically AGV-based | Order Picking AGV |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]

**Define a unit load. List any four common forms of unit loads encountered in AGV-based material handling systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A **unit load** is a collection of items that have been consolidated onto a single carrying device (such as a pallet or container) so that the entire batch can be moved, stored, and handled as one indivisible entity by mechanical handling equipment such as AGVs.

Four common forms of unit loads:

1. **Palletized loads** – cartons stacked on wooden or plastic pallets (e.g., GMA or EUR pallets)
2. **Containerized loads** – items placed inside rigid totes, bins, or ISO maritime containers
3. **Drum loads** – cylindrical vessels (steel or plastic drums) containing liquids, chemicals, or powders
4. **Rack loads** – items transported on wheeled storage racks or garment trolleys

**Mark Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + Four forms (0.5 each = 2 marks)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Explain the difference between a unit load and a bulk load. Why are unit loads preferred in AGV-based warehouse systems?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Unit Load | Bulk Load |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Pre-assembled standardized batch | Loose, continuous material |
| **Form** | Discrete (pallet, tote, drum) | Continuous (grains, sand, ore) |
| **Handling** | Mechanical, repeatable | Conveyor, hopper, bucket |
| **Countability** | Each unit is countable | Often uncountable |

**Why unit loads are preferred in AGV systems:**

1. **Discrete handling** – AGVs work best with discrete, predictable cargo dimensions
2. **Standardization** – Standard pallet sizes allow uniform AGV deck design
3. **High throughput** – One transport cycle carries many items
4. **Reduced labor** – Automation of loading and unloading is straightforward
5. **Inventory accuracy** – RFID/barcode tags on unit loads enable tracking

**Mark Allocation:** Difference (2 marks) + Reason for AGV preference (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A pallet of size 1200 mm × 1000 mm is loaded with three stacks of cartons as given below:**

| Stack | Weight (kg) | X position from origin (mm) | Y position from origin (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | 120 | 200 | 250 |
| S2 | 180 | 600 | 500 |
| S3 | 100 | 1000 | 750 |
| Pallet | 20 | 600 | 500 |

**(a) Calculate the center of gravity (X_cg, Y_cg) of the unit load. Is the load stable for AGV transport? Justify. (7 marks)**

**(b) The AGV accelerates at 0.4 m/s². Calculate the tipping moment, restoring moment, and the anti-tip safety factor. The load CG height above the deck is 0.55 m and load base dimension along travel is 1.0 m. Comment on the safety. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) – 7 Marks

**Step 1: Total weight of unit load**

$$ W_{total} = 120 + 180 + 100 + 20 = 420 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 2: Moment summation along X-axis**

$$ M_x = (120)(200) + (180)(600) + (100)(1000) + (20)(600) $$

$$ M_x = 24{,}000 + 108{,}000 + 100{,}000 + 12{,}000 = 244{,}000 \text{ kg·mm} $$

**Step 3: Solve for X_cg**

$$ X_{cg} = \frac{244{,}000}{420} = 581.0 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 4: Moment summation along Y-axis**

$$ M_y = (120)(250) + (180)(500) + (100)(750) + (20)(500) $$

$$ M_y = 30{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 205{,}000 \text{ kg·mm} $$

**Step 5: Solve for Y_cg**

$$ Y_{cg} = \frac{205{,}000}{420} = 488.1 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 6: Geometric center comparison**

$$ X_{geo} = \frac{1200}{2} = 600 \text{ mm} $$

$$ Y_{geo} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 7: CG offset**

$$ \Delta X = \vert 581.0 - 600 \vert = 19.0 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \Delta Y = \vert 488.1 - 500 \vert = 11.9 \text{ mm} $$

**Stability Conclusion:** Both offsets (19.0 mm and 11.9 mm) are **well within the ±50 mm acceptable tolerance** for stable AGV transport. Therefore, the unit load is **stable**.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) – 7 Marks

**Step 1: Calculate inertial force during acceleration**

$$ F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = 420 \cdot 0.4 = 168 \text{ N} $$

**Step 2: Calculate tipping moment**

$$ M_{tip} = F_{inertia} \cdot h_{cg} = 168 \cdot 0.55 = 92.4 \text{ N·m} $$

**Step 3: Calculate load weight**

$$ W_{load} = 420 \cdot 9.81 = 4{,}120.2 \text{ N} $$

**Step 4: Calculate lever arm**

$$ d_{lever} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ m} $$

**Step 5: Calculate restoring moment**

$$ M_{restore} = 4{,}120.2 \cdot 0.5 = 2{,}060.1 \text{ N·m} $$

**Step 6: Calculate anti-tip safety factor**

$$ ATSF = \frac{2{,}060.1}{92.4} = 22.3 $$

**Safety Comment:** With an ATSF of **22.3**, which is far above the industry minimum of **1.5**, the unit load is **extremely safe** for transport at 0.4 m/s² acceleration. The load will not tip, and a significant safety margin exists for unexpected acceleration spikes.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Pattern]

**(a) Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Palletized unit load, (ii) Containerized unit load, (iii) Drum unit load. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the unit load formation process with reference to the SLAPP rules. A warehouse wishes to achieve 85% cube utilization on EUR pallets (1200 × 800 mm). If cartons of size 400 × 300 × 250 mm are stacked, calculate the maximum number of cartons per layer and the number of layers required if the load height must not exceed 1.5 m. (7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) – 7 Marks

**(i) Palletized Unit Load (2.33 marks):**
A unit load in which items are stacked directly on a flat pallet (typically wooden or plastic). The pallet provides the structural base, and the items are usually secured with stretch wrap or strapping.
*Example:* A stack of 24 cartons of soft drinks on a GMA pallet weighing 600 kg.

**(ii) Containerized Unit Load (2.33 marks):**
A unit load in which items are placed inside a rigid container such as a tote, bin, or ISO container. The container provides both support and protection.
*Example:* A plastic tote pan containing small electronic components, used in order picking AGV systems.

**(iii) Drum Unit Load (2.34 marks):**
A unit load consisting of a cylindrical vessel, typically used for liquids, chemicals, or powders. Drums may be handled individually or in pairs using drum clamps on specialized AGVs.
*Example:* A 200-liter steel drum of industrial lubricant transported by a clamp-equipped AGV.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) – 7 Marks

**SLAPP Rules Explanation (2 marks):**

- **S**ize – Match item dimensions to standard pallet footprints
- **L**ayer pattern – Use interlocking or column stacking for stability
- **A**lignment – Align items with pallet edges for fork access
- **P**rotection – Use slip sheets, edge protectors, stretch wrap
- **P**rofile – Maintain flat top profile; keep height ≤ 1.8 × shortest pallet side

**Layer Calculation (3 marks):**

EUR pallet footprint: 1200 × 800 mm
Carton footprint options: 400 × 300 mm or 300 × 400 mm

Let us test the **400 mm (length) × 300 mm (width)** orientation:
- Along 1200 mm direction: $\lfloor 1200 / 400 \rfloor = 3$ cartons
- Along 800 mm direction: $\lfloor 800 / 300 \rfloor = 2$ cartons
- **Cartons per layer = 3 × 2 = 6 cartons**

Verification of cube utilization for this layer:

$$ CU = \frac{6 \times (0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.25)}{1.2 \times 0.8 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.18}{0.24} = 75\% $$

This is 75%, less than 85%. To achieve higher utilization, rotate cartons 90°:
- 300 mm along 1200 mm direction: $\lfloor 1200 / 300 \rfloor = 4$ cartons
- 400 mm along 800 mm direction: $\lfloor 800 / 400 \rfloor = 2$ cartons
- **Cartons per layer = 4 × 2 = 8 cartons**

$$ CU = \frac{8 \times (0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.25)}{1.2 \times 0.8 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.24}{0.24} = 100\% $$

This achieves 100% utilization at the layer level, satisfying the 85% target.

**Layer Count Calculation (2 marks):**

Carton height: 250 mm
Maximum load height: 1500 mm

$$ \text{Number of layers} = \lfloor 1500 / 250 \rfloor = 6 \text{ layers} $$

Total cartons on the pallet: $8 \times 6 = 48$ cartons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the pallet weight** in total weight calculation — costs 1 to 2 marks in CG problems
> 2. **Mixing up X and Y moments** — verify axes consistently before substituting values
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — always convert mm to m (or vice versa) before applying moments
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract pallet weight from maximum load height** when counting layers — pallet height is typically 120–150 mm and must be subtracted
> 5. **Not stating acceptable tolerance** (±50 mm) when judging CG stability — examiners award marks for explicit justification
> 6. **Confusing cube utilization with layer utilization** — cube utilization is a 3D ratio, not just 2D
> 7. **Skipping the dimensioning step** in layer calculations — always show how $\lfloor L / l \rfloor \times \lfloor W / w \rfloor$ was computed

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unit Load = Standardized aggregation of items** moved as one entity using pallets, containers, drums, or racks
- The **most common unit load** in AGV applications is the **GMA pallet** (1219 × 1016 mm) in North America and **EUR pallet** (1200 × 800 mm) in Europe
- **CG offset tolerance** is **±50 mm to ±100 mm** from geometric center for stable AGV transport
- **CG formula** is the *first moment of mass divided by total mass* applied along each axis
- **Anti-Tip Safety Factor (ATSF)** must be at least **1.5 to 2.0**; values above 5 are very safe
- **Tipping moment** = $F_{inertia} \times h_{cg}$; **Restoring moment** = $W_{load} \times d_{lever}$
- **SLAPP rules** govern unit load formation: Size, Layer, Alignment, Protection, Profile
- **Unit load AGVs (UL-AGVs)** are equipped with **lift decks, roller beds, or forklift attachments** to handle palletized cargo
- **Cube utilization** measures how efficiently the 3D space of the pallet is used; target is **85% or higher**
- **Throughput rate** of an AGV system = $\frac{3600}{t_{cycle}}$ loads per hour
- **Pallet stacking patterns** include column (most stable for fragile items) and interlocking (better for cubic items)
- **Stretch wrap and banding** are commonly used to secure unstable loads before AGV pickup
- **RFID tags and barcode labels** are affixed to unit loads for AGV identification and tracking
- **In hazardous load handling**, ATEX-certified or explosion-proof AGVs must be deployed
- **AGV payload capacity** must always exceed the **maximum foreseeable unit load weight** with a safety margin of at least **25%**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forklift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forklift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **forklift** (also called a **lift truck**, **fork truck**, or **high-low truck**) is a powered industrial truck equipped with a forked platform (the "forks") attached to the front, designed to lift, transport, and stack palletized loads. In the context of Automated Guided and Autonomous Vehicles (AGVs/AVs), a forklift AGV is a **wheeled mobile robot (WMR)** or **automated guided vehicle (AGV)** that retains the conventional fork-lifting mechanism but replaces the human operator with an **embedded navigation, perception, and control system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMUT611):** A forklift, in the autonomous vehicle context, is a self-propelled material-handling machine whose primary function is the vertical and horizontal transport of unit loads using two or more pronged lifting arms, governed by an on-board programmable logic controller (PLC) or industrial computer executing path-planning and obstacle-avoidance algorithms.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Think of a forklift as the **"industrial arm-and-leg" of a warehouse**:
- **Human arms lifting a box** → The forklift's **mast and forks** lift the load vertically.
- **Human legs walking with the box** → The forklift's **chassis and wheels** move the load horizontally.
- **Human eyes avoiding obstacles** → On an autonomous forklift, this role is played by **LiDAR, cameras, and ultrasonic sensors**.
- **Human brain deciding the path** → The **on-board controller / fleet management software** decides *where* to go.

In a modern **smart warehouse** (e.g., Amazon fulfillment centers), fleets of autonomous forklifts work 24/7 without rest, ferrying pallets from receiving docks to storage racks, then to shipping bays, all orchestrated by a central **Warehouse Management System (WMS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** A traditional forklift is essentially a *constrained mechanical system* (steered rear axle + parallel forks) but an *unconstrained control problem* (load shifts, surface inclines, dynamic obstacles). Adding autonomy multiplies the complexity by an order of magnitude.

### 1.3 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Capacity** | 1,000 kg to 50,000 kg | Determines stability calculations |
| **Lift Height** | 3 m to 12 m (reach trucks) | Affects mast dynamics |
| **Travel Speed (loaded)** | 8–25 km/h (manual), 1.5 m/s (AGV) | Safety-critical in mixed traffic |
| **Power Source** | Electric (Lead-acid / Li-ion), Diesel, LPG | Influences battery sizing for AGVs |
| **Wheel Configuration** | 3-wheel, 4-wheel (front or rear steer) | Defines kinematic model |
| **Center of Gravity** | 500–600 mm from heel of forks | The heart of stability triangle |
| **Mast Tilt (forward/back)** | +5° / −5° (typical) | Affects load retention |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural & Functional Architecture of a Forklift

A forklift is built from six fundamental sub-systems, each of which becomes an *integration point* when autonomy is added:

1. **Power Train (Propulsion Sub-system)**
   - *Manual forklift:* IC engine, electric motor, or hybrid.
   - *AGV forklift:* Almost universally **electric** (BLDC / servo motor) for precision torque–speed control and indoor air-quality compliance.

2. **Hydraulic Lifting Sub-system**
   - A hydraulic cylinder drives the **mast** (vertical column) and **carriage** (sliding platform holding the forks).
   - Pump → control valve → single/double-acting cylinder → load.
   - Pressure equation: $P = \dfrac{F_{load}}{A_{piston}}$, where $P$ is hydraulic pressure, $F_{load}$ is the lifting force, and $A_{piston}$ is the piston cross-sectional area.

3. **Steering & Drive Sub-system**
   - **Rear-wheel steering** is the most common (gives a tight turning radius).
   - **Ackermann steering geometry** is applied so that all wheels rotate about a common instantaneous center of rotation (ICR).

4. **Braking Sub-system**
   - *Manual:* Hydraulic service brakes + parking brake.
   - *AGV:* Regenerative electric braking + fail-safe electromagnetic parking brake (de-energize-to-engage).

5. **Sensing & Perception Sub-system (AGV-specific)**
   - **2D Safety LiDAR** (SICK microScan3, R2000) – 360° horizontal scan for obstacle detection.
   - **3D LiDAR / Depth camera** – pallet pocket detection and load verification.
   - **Fork-tip encoders** – measure fork position in space.
   - **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** – measures roll, pitch, yaw rates.
   - **Wheel odometry** – estimates traveled distance.
   - **Mast angle sensor** – measures mast tilt.

6. **Control & Communication Sub-system (AGV-specific)**
   - **Safety PLC** (SIL 3 / PL d per ISO 13849-1) – hard-wired E-stops and zone scanners.
   - **Vehicle controller (VC)** – executes path tracking via **ROS 2**, **PLCopen**, or vendor-proprietary stacks.
   - **Fleet manager** – assigns missions via Wi-Fi/5G.

### 2.2 Stability Triangle — The "Why" Behind Every Forklift Accident

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stability Triangle** is the single most tested concept in KTU board examinations for OEMUT611. Master it.

A three-wheeled forklift is supported on three points forming a triangle:
- **Left front wheel – Right front wheel – Center rear steering axle.**

For a four-wheel forklift, the triangle is formed by:
- **Left front wheel – Right front wheel – Center of the drive axle.**

A forklift remains stable as long as the **combined Center of Gravity (CG) of the truck + load** lies *inside* this triangle. The instant the combined CG crosses the edge of the triangle, the forklift **tips over**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Gravitational weight of load | N (with $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$) |
| 2 | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F}{A_{p}}$ | Hydraulic cylinder pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $\tau_{tip} = F_{load} \cdot d_{load} + W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}$ | Net tipping moment | N·m |
| 4 | $h_{cg} = \dfrac{m_{load} \cdot h_{load} + m_{truck} \cdot h_{truck}}{m_{load} + m_{truck}}$ | Combined CG height | m |
| 5 | $R_{ICR} = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta)}$ | Instantaneous Center of Rotation radius (Ackermann) | m |
| 6 | $v_{linear} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Forward velocity from wheel rotation | m/s |
| 7 | $L_{stop} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$ | Minimum braking distance (kinetic energy = friction work) | m |
| 8 | $\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{d_{base}}{h_{cg}}\right)$ | Critical tip-over angle | rad or ° |
| 9 | $E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy to be dissipated during braking | J |
| 10 | $T_{stall} = F_{draw} \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Wheel torque at stall (drive-train sizing) | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by AGV integrators to (i) certify that a load will not tip during travel and mast lift, (ii) dimension the drive motors and batteries, and (iii) prove compliance with **ANSI/ITSDF B56.1** and **ISO 3691-4** (the international safety standard for driverless industrial trucks).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Stability Triangle and the Tipping Condition

**Step 1 — Define the moments acting on the forklift.**

The forklift is treated as a rigid body pivoting about the **front-wheel line** (the front edge of the stability triangle). Two moments act:

- **Tipping moment** $\tau_{tip}$ — caused by the load's weight acting at horizontal distance $d_{load}$ from the pivot.
- **Restoring moment** $\tau_{restore}$ — caused by the counterweight (truck body) acting at horizontal distance $d_{truck}$ on the *opposite* side of the pivot.

**Step 2 — Express the tipping moment.**

The total tipping moment is the sum of moments due to the load and the truck's own weight, both tending to rotate the forklift forward:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{tip} &= (m_{load} \cdot g) \cdot d_{load} + (m_{truck} \cdot g) \cdot d_{truck} \\
\tau_{tip} &= g \cdot (m_{load} \cdot d_{load} + m_{truck} \cdot d_{truck})
\end{aligned}
$$

Here:
- $m_{load}$ = mass of the payload (kg)
- $m_{truck}$ = mass of the unloaded forklift (kg)
- $d_{load}$ = horizontal distance from the front-wheel pivot to the load's CG (m)
- $d_{truck}$ = horizontal distance from the front-wheel pivot to the truck's CG (m)

**Step 3 — Define the static restoring moment (with load on the forks).**

If we consider tipping about the *rear* edge (i.e., backward tip), the restoring moment is provided by the load weight as it resists backward rotation. The forward-tipping condition (the more common one) is what we test.

**Step 4 — The Tipping Condition.**

The forklift is on the verge of tipping forward when the line of action of the combined weight vector passes exactly through the **front pivot edge**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta_{tip}) &= \frac{d_{pivot}}{h_{cg}} \\
\theta_{tip} &= \arctan\left( \frac{d_{pivot}}{h_{cg}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For any operational tilt angle $\theta_{op} < \theta_{tip}$, the forklift remains stable.

**Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style).**

> **Given:** A forklift of mass $m_{truck} = 3000 \, kg$ has its CG at $0.6 \, m$ behind the front-pivot line. A load of $m_{load} = 1500 \, kg$ is placed with its CG at $0.7 \, m$ *in front* of the front-pivot line. The combined CG height is $h_{cg} = 1.4 \, m$. The horizontal distance from combined CG to front-pivot line is $d_{pivot} = 0.5 \, m$. Find the critical tip-over angle.

**Step 5a — Identify knowns.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{truck} &= 3000 \text{ kg}, \quad d_{truck} = 0.6 \text{ m} \\
m_{load} &= 1500 \text{ kg}, \quad d_{load} = 0.7 \text{ m} \\
h_{cg} &= 1.4 \text{ m}, \quad d_{pivot} = 0.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5b — Compute the tipping moment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{tip} &= g \cdot (m_{load} \cdot d_{load} + m_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}) \\
&= 9.81 \cdot (1500 \cdot 0.7 + 3000 \cdot 0.6) \\
&= 9.81 \cdot (1050 + 1800) \\
&= 9.81 \cdot 2850 \\
&= 27958.5 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5c — Compute the critical tip-over angle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{tip} &= \arctan\left(\frac{d_{pivot}}{h_{cg}}\right) \\
&= \arctan\left(\frac{0.5}{1.4}\right) \\
&= \arctan(0.3571) \\
&= 19.65^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The forklift can withstand a longitudinal floor tilt of up to **19.65°** before the combined CG crosses the front pivot. AGV fleet managers enforce a much tighter limit (typically **3°–5°**) as a safety margin.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Braking Distance

A forklift AGV must stop within the safety field of its laser scanner. The minimum safe stopping distance on a flat, dry concrete floor is derived by equating kinetic energy to friction work:

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy of the moving truck.**

$$
E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

**Step 2 — Friction work over stopping distance $L_{stop}$.**

$$
W_{friction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot L_{stop}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} m v^2 &= \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot L_{stop} \\
L_{stop} &= \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For an AGV traveling at $v = 1.5 \, m/s$ on dry concrete ($\mu = 0.6$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{stop} &= \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 9.81} \\
&= \frac{2.25}{11.772} \\
&= 0.191 \text{ m} \approx 19.1 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Many students forget that the AGV must also add a **reaction-time distance** $d_{react} = v \cdot t_{react}$ (with $t_{react} \approx 0.5 \, s$ for a Safety PLC). The **total safety distance** is therefore $d_{total} = v \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Forklift Classification

| Class | Type | Power | Use Case | Automation Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Electric counterbalance | Electric | Indoor warehouses | Medium |
| **Class II** | Narrow-aisle reach truck | Electric | Warehouses with narrow racks | High (mast + reach axis) |
| **Class III** | Walkie / pallet jack | Electric / Manual | Short-distance pallet moves | Low |
| **Class IV** | Internal combustion cushion tire | Diesel / LPG | Indoor, smooth floors | Very High (emissions, vibration) |
| **Class V** | Internal combustion pneumatic tire | Diesel / LPG | Outdoor, rough terrain | Very High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Autonomous Forklift

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Power Subsystem: Li-ion Battery Pack 48V/80V]
    DRV[Drive Motor BLDC with Encoder]
    STR[Steering Motor Servo]
    HYD[Hydraulic Pump Motor]
    MAS[Mast Cylinder and Forks]
    WHL[Drive Wheels and Caster]
    SAF[Safety PLC SIL3 E-Stop Scanner]
    LID[2D Safety LiDAR 360 deg]
    CAM[3D Depth Camera Pallet Detection]
    IMU[IMU 9 axis Roll Pitch Yaw]
    ODO[Wheel Odometry Encoders]
    VC[Vehicle Controller Path Planning]
    FM[Fleet Manager WMS Interface]
    HMI[Operator HMI Touchscreen]

    PWR --> DRV
    PWR --> STR
    PWR --> HYD
    DRV --> WHL
    STR --> WHL
    HYD --> MAS
    LID --> SAF
    CAM --> VC
    IMU --> VC
    ODO --> VC
    SAF --> VC
    VC --> DRV
    VC --> STR
    VC --> HYD
    FM --> VC
    HMI --> VC
    HMI --> FM
```

### 4.2 Stability Triangle — Front View Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Load CG: m_load at height h_load] --> B{Combined CG: h_cg}
    C[Truck CG: m_truck at height h_truck] --> B
    B --> D[Vertical Line of Gravity]
    D --> E{Triangle Boundary Check}
    E -- Inside --> F[STABLE]
    E -- On Edge --> G[CRITICAL TIP CONDITION]
    E -- Outside --> H[TIP OVER EVENT]
    P[Front Wheel Pivot Line] --> E
    Q[Left Front Wheel] --> P
    R[Right Front Wheel] --> P
    S[Center Rear Axle] --> P
```

### 4.3 Mission Flow — Pick-and-Place Cycle of a Forklift AGV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Receive Mission from WMS] --> S2[Step 2: Localize using LiDAR SLAM]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Plan Global Path on Map]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Execute Path with Obstacle Avoidance]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Detect Pallet via 3D Camera]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Align Forks using Visual Servoing]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Insert Forks and Lift Load]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Navigate to Drop Zone]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Lower Load and Retract Forks]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Report Mission Complete to WMS]
    S10 --> S1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
Define a *forklift AGV* and list its three primary safety sensors.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A forklift AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) is a self-propelled, fork-equipped material-handling vehicle that operates without a human driver, executing transport missions under the supervision of a fleet management system.
The three primary safety sensors are:
1. **2D Safety LiDAR scanner** — for 360° horizontal field obstacle detection and safety-rated field switching.
2. **3D Depth camera / Time-of-Flight sensor** — for pallet pocket detection and accurate fork insertion.
3. **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — for measuring roll, pitch, and yaw to detect impending tip-over conditions.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing the three sensors with one-line justification each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
What is the *stability triangle* of a forklift? Why is it important for AGV operation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **stability triangle** is the three-point support polygon formed by the contact patches of the forklift's wheels (left front, right front, and the steering axle). A forklift remains stable as long as the **combined center of gravity (CG)** of the truck and its load lies *within* this triangle.
**Importance for AGV operation:** Autonomous forklifts travel at higher speeds, make sharper turns, and operate on imperfect warehouse floors. Continuous monitoring of the combined CG ensures the AGV never lifts a load that would push the CG outside the triangle, preventing tip-overs. Most AGVs use the **load weight** from the hydraulic pressure sensor and the **load position** from a fork-tip encoder to compute the CG in real time.

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for the AGV-specific significance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Stability Analysis and Sensor Integration

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **stability triangle** of a four-wheel counterbalance forklift. Derive the expression for the **critical tip-over angle** $\theta_{tip}$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An autonomous forklift has the following specifications:
- Truck mass (unloaded): $m_{truck} = 4500 \, kg$, CG located $0.8 \, m$ behind the front axle.
- Load mass: $m_{load} = 2000 \, kg$, CG located $0.6 \, m$ in front of the front axle.
- Combined CG height: $h_{cg} = 1.6 \, m$.
- Travel speed: $v = 2.0 \, m/s$ on a dry concrete floor ($\mu = 0.65$).
- Operator reaction time: $t_{react} = 0.6 \, s$.

Calculate:
1. The critical tip-over angle $\theta_{tip}$ assuming a $0.4 \, m$ horizontal offset of the combined CG from the front-pivot line.
2. The total safety stopping distance $d_{total}$.
3. Comment on whether the AGV can safely traverse a floor inclined at **6°**.  **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Stability Triangle — Sketch + Derivation (7 marks)**

**Sketch (3 marks):**
- Draw a top-view of the forklift showing the **left front wheel (LF)**, **right front wheel (RF)**, and the **center of the rear steering axle (CR)** forming a triangle.
- Mark the **combined CG** as a point *inside* the triangle for the stable case and *outside* the triangle for the tip case.
- Annotate the **base distance** $d_{base}$ from CG projection to the front pivot edge and the **CG height** $h_{cg}$.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The forklift tips about the front-pivot edge when the line of action of the combined weight passes through that edge. From the right-triangle formed by $d_{base}$ and $h_{cg}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta_{tip}) &= \frac{d_{base}}{h_{cg}} \\
\theta_{tip} &= \arctan\left( \frac{d_{base}}{h_{cg}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The forklift is stable when the operational tilt angle satisfies $\theta_{op} < \theta_{tip}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Critical tip-over angle (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{tip} &= \arctan\left( \frac{d_{pivot}}{h_{cg}} \right) \\
&= \arctan\left( \frac{0.4}{1.6} \right) \\
&= \arctan(0.25) \\
&= 14.04^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Braking distance (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{stop} &= \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g} = \frac{(2.0)^2}{2 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{4.0}{12.753} = 0.3137 \text{ m} \\
d_{react} &= v \cdot t_{react} = 2.0 \cdot 0.6 = 1.2 \text{ m} \\
d_{total} &= 1.2 + 0.3137 = 1.5137 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Inclined floor check (2 marks):**

Since the floor inclination $\theta_{op} = 6^{\circ} < \theta_{tip} = 14.04^{\circ}$, the AGV **can** safely traverse the inclined floor with a positive stability margin of $8.04^{\circ}$. The WMS should still be configured to reduce travel speed to $v \leq 1.0 \, m/s$ on inclines for additional safety.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Subsystem Architecture and Standards

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Describe the **major sub-systems of an autonomous forklift** with a block diagram. Briefly explain the role of the **Safety PLC (SIL 3)**.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain **five ISO/ANSI safety standards** applicable to autonomous forklifts. Discuss the role of **functional safety** in the design of the hydraulic lifting sub-system.  **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Sub-systems (7 marks):**

| # | Sub-system | Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power (Li-ion Battery) | Stores and supplies electrical energy to all drives. |
| 2 | Drive (BLDC + Encoder) | Provides controlled traction. |
| 3 | Steering (Servo + Ackermann) | Determines vehicle trajectory. |
| 4 | Hydraulic (Pump + Cylinder) | Lifts the mast and forks. |
| 5 | Safety (LiDAR + PLC) | Detects hazards and triggers E-stops. |
| 6 | Perception (3D Camera + IMU) | Identifies pallets and monitors CG. |
| 7 | Controller (Industrial PC / ROS 2) | Plans path, executes motion, logs data. |
| 8 | Communication (Wi-Fi / 5G) | Connects to WMS / Fleet Manager. |

**Role of Safety PLC (SIL 3) (2 marks):**
The Safety PLC is a **redundant, certified logic controller** that runs independently of the main vehicle controller. It monitors the 2D LiDAR safety fields, emergency-stop buttons, and bumper switches. If a hazard is detected, it **hard-wiredly** cuts off drive power and engages the parking brake, ensuring a fail-safe stop even if the main controller crashes.

**(b) Safety Standards + Functional Safety (7 marks):**

1. **ISO 3691-4** — Driverless industrial trucks — Safety requirements (the *primary* standard for AGV forklifts). (1.5 marks)
2. **ANSI/ITSDF B56.1** — Safety standard for industrial trucks (US counterpart). (1.5 marks)
3. **ISO 13849-1** — Safety of machinery — Performance Level (PL a–e). (1 mark)
4. **IEC 61508 / SIL ratings** — Functional safety of electrical/electronic systems. (1 mark)
5. **ISO 10218-1** — Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements (for manipulator arms if fitted). (1 mark)

**Functional safety of hydraulic sub-system (1 mark):**
The hydraulic cylinder is treated as a **safety-critical actuation system**. A redundant pressure sensor cross-checks the load weight, and a **load-holding valve** mechanically prevents the load from dropping in case of a hose failure. The Safety PLC triggers the E-stop and locks the valve if it detects an over-pressure or an out-of-range mast position.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $g$ in the tipping moment** — Many students write $\tau_{tip} = m \cdot d$ instead of $\tau_{tip} = m \cdot g \cdot d$. This costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing "stopping distance" with "safety distance"** — Always include the *reaction-time distance* in your final answer for the safety stopping distance.
> 3. **Using a 2D triangle when a 3D CG is required** — The CG is a *point in 3D space*; its *vertical height* and *horizontal projection* both matter. Drawing only a top-view loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Ackermann ICR with vehicle CG** — Ackermann geometry gives the *instantaneous center of rotation of the wheels*, NOT the location of the CG. Two different concepts.
> 5. **Not stating units** — In KTU, $9.81 \, m/s^2$ for $g$ is mandatory; missing units lose 0.5 marks per occurrence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read Before Every KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Forklift AGV** = Self-propelled, fork-equipped AGV governed by a Safety PLC + Vehicle Controller.
- [x] **Six sub-systems**: Power, Drive, Steering, Hydraulic, Safety, Perception + Controller.
- [x] **Stability triangle** = three-point support polygon (LF wheel, RF wheel, rear steering axle).
- [x] **Tipping condition**: combined CG must lie *inside* the triangle at all times.
- [x] **Tipping moment**: $\tau_{tip} = g (m_{load} d_{load} + m_{truck} d_{truck})$.
- [x] **Critical tip-over angle**: $\theta_{tip} = \arctan(d_{pivot} / h_{cg})$.
- [x] **Combined CG height**: $h_{cg} = (m_{load} h_{load} + m_{truck} h_{truck}) / (m_{load} + m_{truck})$.
- [x] **Hydraulic pressure**: $P = F / A_{piston}$.
- [x] **Ackermann ICR radius**: $R = L / \tan(\delta)$.
- [x] **Kinetic energy**: $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$.
- [x] **Braking distance**: $L_{stop} = v^2 / (2 \mu g)$.
- [x] **Total safety distance**: $d_{total} = v \cdot t_{react} + v^2 / (2 \mu g)$.
- [x] **Key standards**: ISO 3691-4, ANSI B56.1, ISO 13849-1, IEC 61508 (SIL), ISO 10218-1.
- [x] **Safety sensors**: 2D LiDAR, 3D camera, IMU, encoders, pressure transducer.
- [x] **Safety integrity**: SIL 3 (per IEC 61508) or PL d (per ISO 13849-1) is the typical target.
- [x] **Power source for AGV forklifts**: Electric (Li-ion), with $48 \, V$ or $80 \, V$ being common bus voltages.
- [x] **Default safety margin**: Limit operational tilt to $3^{\circ}$–$5^{\circ}$ even when $\theta_{tip} \gg 5^{\circ}$.
- [x] **Load verification**: Use hydraulic pressure + fork-tip encoder to *redundantly* verify load presence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Components of AGVs: Vehicle Communication and Networking - In-Vehicle Networking (CAN)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Key Components of AGVs: Vehicle Communication and Networking - In-Vehicle Networking (CAN) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Controller Area Network (CAN)** is a robust, multi-master, message-oriented serial bus communication protocol originally developed by **Robert Bosch GmbH in 1986** and formally standardized under **ISO 11898 (2003)**. It is designed specifically for high-speed, real-time, distributed control systems where multiple Electronic Control Units (ECUs) or nodes must reliably exchange short, prioritized data messages over a single, twisted-pair differential bus without the need for a central host computer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)**, CAN serves as the *backbone nervous system* of the vehicle. It connects critical subsystems such as the **motor controllers, LiDAR sensors, encoders, battery management systems (BMS), safety PLCs, and the central guidance controller**, enabling deterministic, error-resilient communication essential for autonomous navigation. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Architecture:** CAN is principally a **Layer 1 (Physical)** and **Layer 2 (Data Link)** protocol of the OSI model. Higher-layer protocols such as **CANopen (EN 50325-4)** and **SAE J1939** are used for device profiling in industrial automation and heavy-duty vehicles, respectively. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The Polite Conference Call" Imagine a round-table conference where every engineer in the room represents an **ECU**, and they are all connected to a single shared telephone line (the **CAN bus**). - **No One Dominates:** There is no chairperson (central controller). Any engineer can speak at any time, but only one may talk at a given instant. - **Priority by ID:** Each engineer's message is stamped with a priority label (the **Message ID**). If two engineers speak at the same exact moment, the one with the *higher priority label* (numerically lower value in CAN) continues, while the other backs off and waits. This is known as **CSMA/CD+AMP** — Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection and Arbitration on Message Priority. - **Universal Listener:** Every engineer hears every message, but each only acts on the messages relevant to their job. This is the **Broadcast** nature of CAN.
