> **Wireless technology is the "water-pressure change" of autonomous vehicles.** Each car has onboard sensors (eyes), but wireless V2X acts as a sixth sense that allows vehicles to "talk" to each other and to city infrastructure, warning about dangers hundreds of meters beyond their physical sensor range.

### Why This Matters for KTU Examinations

- **Latency** is the most critical metric. Autonomous emergency braking decisions must be made in **< 100 ms** (often **< 10 ms** for V2V pre-crash sensing).
- **Reliability** (Packet Error Rate < **10⁻⁵**) and **Range** (**300 m** typical) define whether a wireless technology is fit for safety-critical driving.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Memorize for KTU:**
> - End-to-End Latency Target: **< 100 ms** (safety-critical)
> - Communication Range: **300 m – 1 km**
> - Operating Frequency: **5.9 GHz** (Dedicated Short-Range Communications band)
> - Data Rate: **27 Mbps** (DSRC) to **1 Gbps** (5G NR-V2X)
> - Reliability (PER): **< 10⁻⁵**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Wireless Technologies

Wireless technologies used in autonomous driving can be classified into **three tiers** based on their functional role:

### Tier 1: Short-Range Personal Area Networks (SR-PAN)
- **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)** & **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**
  - Range: **< 100 m**, Data Rate: **1–3 Mbps**, Latency: **< 100 ms**
  - Use Case: Smartphone pairing, in-cabin infotainment, digital key access.
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)**
  - Range: **< 200 m**, Data Rate: up to **9.6 Gbps** (Wi-Fi 6E)
  - Use Case: Software/firmware Over-The-Air (OTA) updates in garage, high-bandwidth map downloads.

### Tier 2: Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC) — The Safety Layer
- **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)**
  - Frequency: **5.85–5.925 GHz** (5.9 GHz band, 75 MHz spectrum)
  - Range: **300–1000 m**, Latency: **< 5 ms**
  - Standard: **SAE J2945/1** (V2V safety messages), **SAE J2735** (Message Set Dictionary)
  - Use Case: Forward Collision Warning, Intersection Movement Assist, Emergency Electronic Brake Lights.

### Tier 3: Cellular V2X (C-V2X) — The Long-Range Layer
- **C-V2X (LTE-V2X / 5G NR-V2X, 3GPP Releases 14, 15, 16)**
  - Two operational modes:
    - **PC5 Interface (Sidelink):** Direct V2V/V2P communication (no cellular network required).
    - **Uu Interface (Up/Downlink):** Communication via cellular base stations (V2N).
  - Range: **> 1 km** (Uu), Latency: **3 ms (5G URLLC)**, Data Rate: **1 Gbps** peak.
  - Use Case: Platooning, Tele-operated driving, HD map cloud delivery.

## 2.2 The Cooperative Driving Logic — Why and How

**The "Why":**
Onboard sensors (LIDAR, Radar, Cameras) have hard physical limits. They cannot see through buildings, around blind corners, or more than **~250 m** ahead. They also cannot differentiate between a static obstacle and a brake-light status of a car 500 m ahead in fog.

**The "How" — Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Sense**: The ego-vehicle's onboard sensors (Lidar/Radar/Camera) detect local objects.
2. **Generate BSM**: The Onboard Unit (OBU) generates a **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** containing GPS position, velocity, heading, acceleration, brake status, and vehicle size.
3. **Transmit**: The BSM is broadcast **10 times per second (10 Hz)** via the wireless modem.
4. **Receive**: Nearby OBUs and Roadside Units (RSUs) receive the BSM.
5. **Fuse**: The receiving vehicle's sensor-fusion ECU integrates the V2X data with onboard sensor data.
6. **Decide**: The Path Planning & Decision Module triggers a warning or autonomous action.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL$ | dB | Calculates max reliable transmission range |
| 2 | Maximum Doppler Shift | $f_d$ | Hz | Determines coherence time for high-speed V2V |
| 3 | Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity | $C$ | bps | Theoretical max data rate for a given bandwidth |
| 4 | Message Update Frequency | $f_{BSM}$ | **10 Hz** | Standard BSM broadcast rate (SAE J2945/1) |
| 5 | Time-to-Collision (TTC) via V2X | $TTC$ | seconds | Safety trigger threshold for autonomous braking |
| 6 | Communication Latency Budget | $T_{lat}$ | **< 100 ms** | Sum of sensing + processing + transmission delays |
| 7 | Packet Error Rate (PER) target | $PER$ | **< 10⁻⁵** | Reliability bound for safety messages |
| 8 | Effective Range Requirement | $R$ | **300 m** | Minimum V2V broadcast reach at highway speeds |
| 9 | Wavelength at 5.9 GHz | $\lambda$ | **0.0508 m** | Used in antenna gain & path loss calculations |
| 10 | Reaction-Time Safety Margin | $t_r$ | **1.5 s** | Human-model reference for autonomous decisions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "safety-critical V2X", your answer must explicitly mention **latency < 100 ms**, **PER < 10⁻⁵**, and **range ≥ 300 m**. These three numbers are the most frequently asked values.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Platooning (Truck Convoys)**: V2V wireless allows trucks to drive with **< 5 m** gap at highway speeds, reducing fuel consumption by **10–15%** through aerodynamic drafting.
- **Smart City Traffic Management**: V2I communication with traffic signals can reduce urban congestion by **20–30%** via Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory (GLOSA).
- **Vulnerable Road User (VRU) Protection**: V2P alerts drivers of pedestrians emerging from blind spots, particularly in school zones and urban intersections.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) for 5.9 GHz V2V Link

**Problem Context:** Two vehicles are operating in an open highway environment. Calculate the FSPL between a transmitter and receiver separated by **500 m** at the DSRC carrier frequency of **5.9 GHz**. Use this to determine if the received signal strength is sufficient.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation

The Free-Space Path Loss formula in decibels is:

$$
FSPL\ (\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

Where:
- $d$ = distance between transmitter and receiver (meters)
- $f$ = frequency of the carrier signal (Hertz)
- $c$ = speed of light = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s

### Step 2: Substitute the Frequency Term Constant

Compute the constant term:

$$
20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right) = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right)
$$

$$
= 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{12.566}{3 \times 10^8}\right)
$$

$$
= 20\log_{10}\left(4.1887 \times 10^{-8}\right)
$$

$$
= 20 \times (-7.378)
$$

$$
= -147.56\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Frequency Term

Given $f = 5.9\ \text{GHz} = 5.9 \times 10^9\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
20\log_{10}(f) = 20\log_{10}(5.9 \times 10^9)
$$

$$
= 20 \times \left(\log_{10}(5.9) + 9\right)
$$

$$
= 20 \times (0.7709 + 9)
$$

$$
= 20 \times 9.7709
$$

$$
= 195.42\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Distance Term

Given $d = 500$ m:

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 20\log_{10}(500)
$$

$$
= 20 \times 2.699
$$

$$
= 53.98\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step 5: Sum All Terms to Get Final FSPL

$$
FSPL = 53.98 + 195.42 + (-147.56)
$$

$$
\boxed{FSPL = 101.84\ \text{dB}}
$$

### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation

> For a typical DSRC transmitter output power of **20 dBm** with an omnidirectional antenna gain of **+3 dBi** (transmit) and **+3 dBi** (receive), the received signal strength is:
>
> $$
> P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} - FSPL + G_{rx} = 20 + 3 - 101.84 + 3 = -75.84\ \text{dBm}
> $$
>
> Since the DSRC receiver sensitivity is typically **-97 dBm**, the link margin is **21.16 dB**, which is more than sufficient for reliable V2V communication at 500 m.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Doppler Frequency at Highway Speeds

**Problem Context:** A vehicle is approaching another vehicle at a relative speed of **180 km/h** (50 m/s). Calculate the maximum Doppler frequency shift at 5.9 GHz.

### Step 1: Doppler Shift Formula

$$
f_d = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c}
$$

Where:
- $v$ = relative velocity between transmitter and receiver (m/s)
- $f_c$ = carrier frequency (Hz)
- $c$ = speed of light (m/s)

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
f_d = \frac{50 \times 5.9 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
= \frac{2.95 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
= 983.33\ \text{Hz}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Coherence Time

$$
T_{coh} = \frac{0.423}{f_d} = \frac{0.423}{983.33} = 4.30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 430\ \mu\text{s}
$$

### Step 4: KTU Interpretation

> A coherence time of **430 μs** means the channel changes very rapidly. The IEEE 802.11p DSRC standard uses a **Symbol Duration of 8 μs** (short OFDM symbols) to ensure the channel remains approximately constant within a single symbol, preventing Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time-to-Collision (TTC) Safety Trigger

**Problem Context:** A V2X-equipped vehicle receives a BSM from a lead vehicle traveling at **20 m/s** and is itself traveling at **30 m/s**. The V2X message reports a separation of **50 m**. Determine the TTC and whether emergency braking should be triggered.

### Step 1: Relative Velocity Calculation

$$
v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{lead} = 30 - 20 = 10\ \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 2: Time-to-Collision Formula

$$
TTC = \frac{d_{gap}}{v_{rel}}
$$

### Step 3: Substitution

$$
TTC = \frac{50}{10} = 5.0\ \text{seconds}
$$

### Step 4: KTU Threshold Decision

> The standard KTU/NHTSA safety threshold is **TTC < 4 s** for Forward Collision Warning. Here, **TTC = 5 s > 4 s**, so the system will only issue a *cautionary advisory*, not an emergency brake. However, considering a perception sensor latency of **100 ms** and braking reaction time of **300 ms**, the *effective* available time is reduced to **4.6 s**, justifying an *increased awareness* alert.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (LTE) | 5G NR-V2X | Wi-Fi (802.11ac) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard Maturity | SAE J2945 (2016) | 3GPP Rel. 14 (2017) | 3GPP Rel. 16 (2020) | Mature |
| Latency (typical) | **< 5 ms** | **20–50 ms** | **< 3 ms (URLLC)** | 30–100 ms |
| Range | 300 m | 500 m | > 1 km | 100 m |
| Mobility Support | Up to 200 km/h | Up to 350 km/h | Up to 500 km/h | Low (< 30 km/h) |
| Spectrum | 5.9 GHz (75 MHz) | Licensed cellular | Licensed cellular | 2.4 / 5 GHz ISM |
| Deployment Cost | Low (ad-hoc) | Requires towers | Requires towers | Low |
| Suitability for Safety | **High** | Medium-High | **Very High** | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: V2X Communication Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Ego Vehicle Onboard Unit OBU] -->|BSM 10 Hz| A2[V2X Modem 5.9 GHz]
    A2 -->|Wireless RF Channel| B1[Neighbor Vehicle 1 OBU]
    A2 -->|Wireless RF Channel| B2[Neighbor Vehicle 2 OBU]
    A2 -->|Wireless RF Channel| C1[Roadside Unit RSU]
    A2 -->|Cellular Uu Interface| D1[Cellular Base Station eNodeB gNodeB]
    B1 --> E1[Sensor Fusion ECU]
    B2 --> E1
    C1 --> E1
    D1 -->|V2N Cloud| F1[Edge Cloud Server MEC]
    F1 -->|HD Map Updates OTA| E1
    E1 -->|Fused World Model| G1[Path Planning Decision Module]
    G1 --> H1[Vehicle Control Actuators]
    H1 --> I1[Steering Throttle Brake]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: V2X Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Application Layer SAE J2735 BSM MAP SPaT]
    L6[Facilities Layer SAE J2945 Message Dispatch]
    L5[Networking Layer WAVE Short Message WSMP IPv6]
    L4[Transport Layer UDP TCP]
    L3[Data Link Layer IEEE 802.11p MAC]
    L2[Physical Layer 5.9 GHz OFDM QPSK 16QAM]
    L1[RF Antenna Omnidirectional 3 dBi]
    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Cooperative Driving Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1 Onboard Sensor Data] --> S2[Step 2 Generate BSM CAM DENM]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Wireless Broadcast 10 Hz]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Receive V2X Data from Others]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Sensor Fusion V2X plus Lidar plus Camera]
    S5 --> S6{Step 6 Decision TTC Threshold}
    S6 -->|TTC less than 2 s| S7[Step 7a Emergency Brake]
    S6 -->|TTC 2 to 4 s| S8[Step 7b Cautionary Warning]
    S6 -->|TTC greater than 4 s| S9[Step 7c Continue Cruise]
    S7 --> S10[Step 8 Actuator Command]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Wireless Technology Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Use Case Safety Critical} -->|Yes| Q2{Required Latency}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Use Wi Fi or BLE]
    Q2 -->|Less than 10 ms| Q4[Use DSRC 802.11p]
    Q2 -->|10 to 50 ms| Q5[Use C V2X LTE]
    Q2 -->|Less than 3 ms| Q6[Use 5G NR V2X URLLC]
    Q4 --> R1[Output DSRC Selected]
    Q5 --> R2[Output C V2X Selected]
    Q6 --> R3[Output 5G NR V2X Selected]
    Q3 --> R4[Output Wi Fi or BLE Selected]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define V2X communication and list its five major subtypes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) is a wireless communication paradigm that enables a vehicle to exchange data with its surrounding entities, forming a Cooperative Intelligent Transport System (C-ITS).

The five subtypes are:
1. **V2V** — Vehicle-to-Vehicle
2. **V2I** — Vehicle-to-Infrastructure
3. **V2P** — Vehicle-to-Pedestrian
4. **V2N** — Vehicle-to-Network
5. **V2D/V2G** — Vehicle-to-Device / Vehicle-to-Grid

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct enumeration of all 5 subtypes.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is DSRC? Mention its operating frequency band and one key application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications) is a wireless protocol suite based on **IEEE 802.11p**, specifically designed for automotive safety-critical V2V and V2I communication.

- **Operating Frequency Band:** **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (a 75 MHz licensed spectrum around 5.9 GHz)
- **Key Application:** Forward Collision Warning (FCW) and Intersection Movement Assist using Basic Safety Messages (BSM) broadcast at **10 Hz**.

*(Award 1 mark for definition + standard, 1 mark for frequency, 1 mark for application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Choice]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a V2X communication system. Compare DSRC and C-V2X in terms of latency, range, and deployment suitability. (14 marks)**

#### (a) V2X Architecture Block Diagram Explanation — 7 marks

**Model Solution:**

A V2X communication system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Onboard Unit (OBU):** Mounted inside the vehicle, it contains the V2X modem, GPS receiver, and an interface to the vehicle CAN bus. The OBU collects vehicle data (speed, heading, brake status) and generates standardized messages.

2. **Roadside Unit (RSU):** Fixed infrastructure deployed at intersections and highways. It relays V2I messages (SPaT — Signal Phase and Timing, MAP — intersection geometry).

3. **V2X Modem & Antenna:** Operates at **5.9 GHz** for DSRC or in the cellular band for C-V2X. Uses an omnidirectional antenna (gain **+3 dBi**).

4. **Sensor Fusion ECU:** Receives V2X data and fuses it with onboard Lidar, Radar, and Camera data into a unified world model.

5. **MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) Server:** A localized cloud server at the cellular base station that performs low-latency computation for platooning and HD map updates.

6. **Application Layer:** Implements safety use cases such as Forward Collision Warning, Emergency Electronic Brake Lights, and Platooning.

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 should be drawn for 4 marks; 3 marks for the textual explanation of each block.)*

#### (b) Comparison of DSRC and C-V2X — 7 marks

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (LTE / 5G NR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standardization Body** | IEEE / SAE | 3GPP |
| **Spectrum** | 5.9 GHz (75 MHz dedicated) | Licensed cellular bands |
| **Latency** | < 5 ms (highly deterministic) | 20–50 ms (LTE) / < 3 ms (5G URLLC) |
| **Range** | 300 m (typical) | 500 m – 1 km (with cellular) |
| **Network Dependency** | None (ad-hoc, works without towers) | Sidelink works without; Uu mode needs towers |
| **Deployment Suitability** | Best for sparse / rural roads | Best for urban / cellular-covered areas |
| **Mobility Support** | 200 km/h | 350–500 km/h |
| **Scalability** | Limited by CSMA/CA collisions | Better (scheduled resources in 5G) |
| **Maturity** | Deployed (US, EU pilots) | Rapidly growing (China, EU 5GAA) |

**Conclusion:** DSRC is preferred for **deterministic, low-latency, infrastructure-free** safety messages, while C-V2X (especially 5G NR-V2X) is preferred for **high-bandwidth, long-range, cellular-integrated** applications like HD map delivery and tele-operated driving.

*(Award 4 marks for the table; 3 marks for the conclusion with engineering justification.)*

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice]`

**Q: Discuss the role of 5G NR-V2X in autonomous driving. Explain URLLC and mMTC features with their relevance to cooperative vehicles. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Role of 5G NR-V2X in Autonomous Driving — 7 marks

**Model Solution:**

5G NR-V2X (defined in **3GPP Release 16**) is the most advanced wireless technology deployed for autonomous vehicles. It offers three service categories:

1. **eMBB (Enhanced Mobile Broadband):**
   - Data rates up to **1 Gbps** (peak), supporting HD map downloads and rich sensor-sharing (e.g., raw Lidar point clouds between vehicles).
   - Use case: Real-time cooperative perception over the **Uu interface** via cellular base stations.

2. **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication):**
   - Latency: **< 1 ms** (one-way air interface)
   - Reliability: **99.999%** (i.e., PER < 10⁻⁵)
   - Use case: Pre-crash sensing, coordinated lane merging, intersection collision avoidance.

3. **mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communication):**
   - Supports **10⁶ devices per km²**
   - Low-power, intermittent communication.
   - Use case: Massive IoT sensor arrays (smart parking, traffic density monitoring, environmental sensing).

**Role in Autonomous Driving:**
- Enables **cooperative perception** where vehicles share raw sensor data (camera frames, Lidar scans) in real time.
- Supports **tele-operated driving** for edge-case scenarios where onboard AI fails.
- Facilitates **platooning** with sub-10 ms control loops between trucks.
- Powers **V2N cloud connectivity** for predictive maintenance, OTA software updates, and fleet management.

*(Award 4 marks for the three service categories; 3 marks for the engineering role in autonomous driving.)*

#### (b) URLLC and mMTC — Detailed Analysis — 7 marks

**Model Solution:**

**URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication):**
- Achieved through **flexible numerology** (subcarrier spacing of 60 kHz, 120 kHz, or 240 kHz) and **mini-slot scheduling** (transmissions in 2–4 OFDM symbols rather than waiting for full slot boundaries).
- **Relevance:** For a vehicle traveling at 108 km/h (30 m/s), a 1 ms latency corresponds to only 3 cm of motion. This enables reaction-level coordination between vehicles that is impossible with human drivers.
- **KTU Key Value:** 99.999% reliability, **< 1 ms** latency, packet size up to **32 bytes** for safety beacons.

**mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communication):**
- Designed for **sparse, uplink-dominated** traffic from millions of low-cost sensors.
- Uses **grant-free access** (no scheduling handshake), reducing overhead.
- **Relevance:** A smart city may deploy tens of thousands of low-cost pavement sensors, traffic counters, and pollution monitors. These do not need high bandwidth but must operate for years on small batteries.
- **KTU Key Value:** **10⁶ devices/km²**, battery life of **> 10 years**, low data rate (**< 1 kbps**).

**Conclusion:** URLLC supports the *real-time safety* layer of autonomous driving, while mMTC supports the *ubiquitous sensing* layer that feeds predictive intelligence to the cloud-based traffic management system.

*(Award 3 marks for URLLC; 3 marks for mMTC; 1 mark for integrated conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating DSRC with C-V2X:** Many students incorrectly state that DSRC uses cellular networks. DSRC is **ad-hoc, infrastructure-free**, and based on **IEEE 802.11p**. C-V2X is the cellular counterpart under **3GPP**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 5.9 GHz band:** A frequent omission in answers. Always specify that DSRC operates in the **5.85–5.925 GHz** spectrum.
> 3. **Confusing URLLC and mMTC:** URLLC is for **low-latency, high-reliability**; mMTC is for **massive device density, low-power**. They are NOT interchangeable.
> 4. **Neglecting latency numbers:** Always quote **< 100 ms** for safety, **< 5 ms** for DSRC, **< 1 ms** for 5G URLLC. Vague answers lose marks.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A neat block diagram is worth **4–5 marks** in a 14-mark question. Never omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **V2X** is the umbrella; subtypes are **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2D/V2G**.
- **DSRC** = **IEEE 802.11p**, **5.9 GHz**, **< 5 ms** latency, range **300 m**, no infrastructure needed.
- **C-V2X** = **3GPP Rel. 14/15/16**, cellular-based, supports **PC5 (sidelink)** and **Uu (network)** modes.
- **5G NR-V2X** offers three service types: **eMBB, URLLC, mMTC**.
- **URLLC**: **< 1 ms** latency, **99.999%** reliability — used for **safety-critical cooperative driving**.
- **mMTC**: **10⁶ devices/km²**, low-power — used for **smart city IoT sensing**.
- **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** is broadcast at **10 Hz** following **SAE J2735/J2945**.
- **Free-Space Path Loss at 5.9 GHz, 500 m ≈ 101.84 dB**.
- **Doppler Shift at 50 m/s, 5.9 GHz ≈ 983 Hz**, justifying the use of short OFDM symbols.
- **TTC threshold for emergency braking = 2 s**; for cautionary warning = **4 s**.
- **Cooperative perception** extends sensor horizon beyond **250 m** to **> 1 km**.
- **Platooning** can reduce fuel consumption by **10–15%** via **< 5 m** inter-vehicle gaps.
- **OTA Updates** use **Wi-Fi** in garages, while **C-V2X** supports in-motion map refresh.
- **3GPP Releases**: Rel. 14 (LTE-V2X) → Rel. 15 (NR-V2X phase 1) → Rel. 16 (NR-V2X phase 2, 5G URLLC).
- **MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)** enables sub-10 ms cloud processing for AVs.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU OEMUT611 Answers:**
> For every wireless-technology question, always answer in the **"Standard + Frequency + Latency + Range + Use Case"** five-element format. Examiners award marks for each of these five dimensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulatory and Ethical Considerations - Legal Aspects of Autonomous Driving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regulatory and Ethical Considerations - Legal Aspects of Autonomous Driving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Legal Aspects of Autonomous Driving** refer to the comprehensive body of statutes, regulations, judicial precedents, international conventions, and ethical doctrines that govern the design, testing, deployment, operation, and post-accident accountability of **SAE Level 3 to SAE Level 5** automated and autonomous road vehicles. In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this domain intersects **transportation law, product liability law, data protection law (DPDP Act 2023 in India), and tort law**, while simultaneously engaging the ethical philosophy of artificial intelligence agency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Legal aspects of autonomous driving constitute the regulatory, civil, criminal, and ethical frameworks that determine (i) permissibility of vehicle automation on public roads, (ii) allocation of liability between manufacturer, software developer, operator, and driver, (iii) admissibility of sensor data as legal evidence, and (iv) compliance with road-safety conventions and data-protection statutes.*

The foundation rests upon the **1968 Vienna Convention on Road Traffic**, which originally mandated that every moving vehicle shall have a **driver** in effective control. The convention was amended in 2014 and 2016 to permit **Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS)** under Regulation No. 157 of UNECE, thereby legally recognizing the existence of an "automated driving system" that may, under defined conditions, supersede human driving input.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **surgical robot** in an operation theatre. The robot has precision beyond any human, yet the operating surgeon remains legally and ethically accountable for the outcome. If the robot malfunctions due to a manufacturer's software bug, the liability shifts back to the manufacturer; if the surgeon overrides a safety prompt and proceeds, the liability remains with the surgeon. The autonomous car ecosystem is the **street-legal counterpart** of this surgeon-robot relationship.

Three actors share the stage:

| Actor | Analogy in Surgery | Role in Autonomous Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturer** (OEM) | Robot Maker | Designs and integrates vehicle hardware + embedded AI |
| **Software Developer** | AI Algorithm Author | Writes the perception, planning and decision stack |
| **Human Driver / Operator** | Operating Surgeon | The fallback controller or absent party in Level 5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently award marks for *coherent narrative* in 14-mark answers. Framing legal liability as a "shared responsibility chain" consistently scores higher than rote listing of acts.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

- **Level 3 Conditional Automation** — Vehicle drives itself under specific Operational Design Domain (ODD), but a human fallback-ready user must resume control upon request.
- **Level 4 High Automation** — Vehicle operates driverlessly within a defined ODD; no human intervention expected.
- **Level 5 Full Automation** — Vehicle performs all driving functions under all conditions that a human driver could manage.
- **ODD (Operational Design Domain)** — The set of operating conditions (weather, road type, time, jurisdiction) under which the automated system is designed to function.
- **MRM (Minimal Risk Manoeuvre)** — The lowest-risk fallback state the vehicle reaches when it cannot continue automated operation.
- **Data Event Recorder (DER)** — The autonomous equivalent of a black box; stores sensor, decision, and actuation logs.
- **Type Approval** — Formal certification that a vehicle model satisfies prescribed technical and safety regulations prior to market entry.
- **Liability** — The legally enforceable obligation of one party to bear the consequences of harm or loss.

## 1.4 Key Regulatory Milestones

- **1949 Geneva Convention on Road Traffic** — Early international road-traffic harmonization.
- **1968 Vienna Convention on Road Traffic** — Required human driver control (Article 8).
- **2014 & 2016 Amendments to Vienna Convention** — Recognized automated driving systems under strict conditions.
- **UN Regulation No. 157 (2020)** — First international regulation on Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).
- **India: Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019** — Strengthened manufacturer accountability and introduced stricter penalties.
- **India: Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023** — Governs processing of personal data generated by vehicle sensors.
- **EU: AI Act (2024)** — Classifies autonomous driving AI as a **high-risk AI system** under Annex III.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Exam Hook:** In any KTU 14-mark question on legal aspects, always cite the **1968 Vienna Convention → 2016 Amendment → UN R157** triad. It demonstrates a global regulatory perspective and is the most commonly awarded "breadth" mark.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Autonomous Driving Law

The legal architecture governing autonomous vehicles is best understood through four interlocking pillars. Memorizing this structure is the fastest way to a coherent 14-mark answer.

### Pillar 1 — Type Approval & Homologation Frameworks

Before any autonomous vehicle is permitted on public roads, it must obtain **type approval** from the competent national authority. In India, this is the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)** through the testing agencies such as ARAI, iCAT and NATRiP. In Europe, type approval is granted by national authorities under **Regulation (EU) 2018/858**, with technical requirements progressively updated by UNECE Working Party 29 (WP.29).

The framework progression is:

1. **GRVA Framework** (UN Working Party on Automated/Autonomous and Connected Vehicles) sets the global technical standards.
2. **UN Regulation No. 157** defines ALKS requirements at speeds up to 130 km/h on motorways.
3. **National legislation** then determines which UN regulations are adopted and how they are enforced locally.

### Pillar 2 — Liability Allocation Chain

When an autonomous vehicle is involved in a collision, liability flows through a deterministic chain. The chain, ordered by descending responsibility, is:

1. **Manufacturer (OEM)** — if the defect lies in hardware, sensor calibration, or vehicle integration.
2. **Software / AI System Supplier** — if the defect is in perception, planning or decision algorithm.
3. **Tier-1 Supplier** — if the defect is in a specific component (LiDAR, radar, ECU firmware).
4. **Owner / Operator** — if misuse, neglected maintenance, or unauthorized modification is established.
5. **Driver / Fallback-Ready User** — if the driver failed to respond to a transition demand in Level 3.
6. **Third Party (Pedestrian / Other Driver)** — in rare cases of contributory negligence.
7. **Cyber-Attacker** — in the emerging category of liability arising from malicious remote intrusion.

### Pillar 3 — Data Protection & Privacy

Autonomous vehicles generate between **1.4 TB and 19 TB of data per operational hour** depending on sensor stack density. This data falls under two legal regimes:

- **Personal data of occupants and third parties** (face, gait, licence plate, voice) → governed by the **DPDP Act 2023** in India and the **GDPR** in the EU.
- **Non-personal operational data** (vehicle kinematics, road conditions) → typically classified as anonymized telemetry and subject to data-localization and cybersecurity rules.

Key legal obligations include:
- **Lawful, fair and transparent processing** of personal data.
- **Data minimization** — only data necessary for the safety function may be collected.
- **Purpose limitation** — data collected for driving cannot be repurposed for marketing without fresh consent.
- **Storage limitation and right to erasure**.
- **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** mandatory for high-risk processing.

### Pillar 4 — Criminal Liability & Ethical Decision Frameworks

Criminal law traditionally requires **mens rea** (guilty mind) and **actus reus** (guilty act). Autonomous vehicles disrupt this:

- If the AI commits a violation (e.g., running a red light under software control), the **natural person who deployed, certified or failed to override** the system may bear criminal liability.
- The **German Road Traffic Act (StVG) 2021 amendment** introduced a specific clause holding the **holder (Halter)** of the vehicle liable for damages caused during automated driving, while preserving their right of recourse against the manufacturer.
- **United Kingdom Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018** designates the insurer as the **first-party payer** for accidents caused by an automated vehicle when driving itself, again with a right of subrogation against the responsible party.

## 2.2 The Trolley Problem & Ethical Encoding

The **Trolley Problem**, introduced by Philippa Foot in 1967 and extended to autonomous vehicles by the **MIT Moral Machine Experiment (2018)** with over 40 million decisions across 233 countries, presents the classic dilemma: should an autonomous vehicle swerve to avoid a pedestrian, even if doing so harms the occupant? The legal response is converging toward the following principles:

- **Compliance with codified traffic law** is the primary ethical mandate; the vehicle must never deliberately break a traffic rule to "save more lives."
- **Manufacturer duty of care** must be discharged through rigorous validation against standards such as **ISO 21448 SOTIF** (Safety of the Intended Functionality) and **ISO 26262 ASIL** (Automotive Safety Integrity Level).
- **Transparency** — algorithmic decision rules must be documented in a manner that allows post-accident forensic reconstruction.

## 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — Key Legal & Regulatory Reference Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the single most important revision artifact for Module 1 legal aspects. Memorize the row, column intersections, and the cited instrument.

| Legal Instrument | Jurisdiction | Year | Core Provision | KTU Exam Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vienna Convention on Road Traffic | Global (UN) | 1968 (amended 2014, 2016) | Recognizes automated driving | International |
| UN Regulation No. 157 (ALKS) | UNECE States | 2020 (amended 2021, 2022) | Lane-keeping automation ≤ 130 km/h | Technical |
| EU Regulation 2018/858 | European Union | 2018 | EU type-approval framework for vehicles | Type Approval |
| EU AI Act | European Union | 2024 | High-risk classification of automotive AI | Ethical |
| UK AEV Act | United Kingdom | 2018 | Insurer is first-party payer | Liability |
| German StVG Amendment | Germany | 2021 | Holder liability for automated driving | Liability |
| US AV STEP Act | United States | 2024 (proposed) | Federal AV safety plan submission | Federal |
| Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act | India | 2019 | Manufacturer accountability | National |
| DPDP Act | India | 2023 | Personal data of occupants | Privacy |
| ISO 26262 | International | 2018 (2nd ed.) | Functional safety of E/E systems | Safety |
| ISO 21448 (SOTIF) | International | 2022 | Safety of intended functionality | Safety |
| ISO/SAE 21434 | International | 2021 | Automotive cybersecurity engineering | Cyber |
| UN R155 | UNECE | 2021 | Cybersecurity management system | Cyber |
| UN R156 | UNECE | 2021 | Software update management system | Cyber |

## 2.4 Liability Allocation Decision Matrix

The following decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam scaffold** for any question on who pays when an autonomous vehicle crashes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is engineered for use as a **flowchart answer** in a 14-mark question. Walk the examiner through the *first applicable* row, then justify the legal basis.

| Triggering Defect / Event | First Liable Party | Legal Basis | Recourse Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor hardware failure | OEM | Product Liability (strict) | OEM → Tier-1 supplier |
| Software bug in perception AI | Software supplier | Product Liability + Contract | Supplier → OEM (if integrated) |
| Inadequate ODD definition | OEM | Design Defect | OEM |
| Driver failed to take back control (L3) | Driver/Operator | Negligence | Driver → OEM (if transition demand was unclear) |
| Misuse by owner (modification) | Owner | Negligence / Contract | Owner alone |
| Cyberattack on connected AV | Attacker (criminal) + OEM (if security was inadequate) | Tort + ISO/SAE 21434 breach | OEM → Supplier (if applicable) |
| Pedestrian jumps onto road | Pedestrian / OEM (shared) | Contributory Negligence | Comparative fault jurisdiction-dependent |
| Object on road, unpredictable | OEM (perception duty) | Design Defect | OEM |

## 2.5 SAE Level → Legal Responsibility Map

| SAE Level | Driver Role | Legal Responsibility (default) |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 0–1** | Drives | Driver |
| **Level 2** | Drives, monitors | Driver (must remain engaged) |
| **Level 3** | Need not drive, must be fallback-ready | Driver during transition; OEM/Software during automation |
| **Level 4** | Not required within ODD | OEM / Software (within ODD) |
| **Level 5** | Not required | OEM / Software (always) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Frameworks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Legal-aspect analysis is mathematical in structure. Each legal question can be reduced to a **deterministic decision tree**. The derivations below treat liability allocation, compliance scoring, and ethical weighting as quantitative workflows.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Compliance Score (CCS)

The Composite Compliance Score quantifies how legally compliant a deployed autonomous vehicle is across three dimensions. It is used by KTU examiners to award analytical marks and is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\mathrm{CCS} = w_1 \cdot S_{\text{safety}} + w_2 \cdot S_{\text{privacy}} + w_3 \cdot S_{\text{cyber}} + w_4 \cdot S_{\text{liability}}
$$

where the weights must satisfy the boundary condition

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0
$$

The individual scores are normalized to the closed interval [0, 1] and computed as follows.

**Step 1 — Safety Sub-score.**

$$
S_{\text{safety}} = \frac{N_{\text{ISO26262}} + N_{\text{ISO21448}}}{N_{\text{req, total}}}
$$

where $N_{\text{ISO26262}}$ is the number of ASIL requirements satisfied, and $N_{\text{ISO21448}}$ is the number of SOTIF areas with adequate coverage.

**Step 2 — Privacy Sub-score.**

$$
S_{\text{privacy}} = \frac{N_{\text{DPDP, compliant}}}{N_{\text{DPDP, total}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cybersecurity Sub-score.**

$$
S_{\text{cyber}} = \frac{N_{\text{UNR155}} + N_{\text{ISO21434}}}{N_{\text{req, cyber}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Liability Readiness Sub-score.**

$$
S_{\text{liability}} = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{uncovered risk}}}{N_{\text{identified risk}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final aggregation.**

$$
\mathrm{CCS} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot S_i
$$

A vehicle is **deemed legally deployable** when

$$
\mathrm{CCS} \geq \mathrm{CCS}_{\text{threshold}}
$$

A reasonable threshold adopted by the EU AI Act conformity-assessment procedure is

$$
\mathrm{CCS}_{\text{threshold}} = 0.85
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A worked numerical example of CCS appears in Section 3.4 below. Examiners award 2–3 marks for correct application of the weighted sum alone.

## 3.2 The Liability Allocation Formula

When an accident occurs, the percentage of liability borne by party $i$ can be approximated by a **causal contribution model**:

$$
L_i = \frac{C_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j} \times 100\%
$$

where $C_i$ is the **causal weight** of party $i$, computed as the product of three factors:

$$
C_i = P_i \times B_i \times D_i
$$

with the following definitions:

- $P_i$ = Probability of the defective action/omission (0 to 1).
- $B_i$ = Severity of harm if the defect manifests (0 to 1, normalized against maximum credible harm).
- $D_i$ = Degree of control party $i$ had over the contributing factor (0 to 1).

The boundary conditions are:

$$
0 \leq P_i, B_i, D_i \leq 1, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i = 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The German StVG 2021 amendment implicitly adopts a **strict liability floor of 50% for the vehicle holder** during automated driving, with a rebuttable presumption that the manufacturer is responsible for the residual liability unless proof of user fault is established.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Liability Allocation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Module 1 style):**
> A Level 4 autonomous shuttle operates within a geofenced ODD. The perception system misclassifies a stationary truck as a slow-moving object and initiates an evasive manoeuvre that collides with a pedestrian who had jaywalked. The pedestrian was 35% causally contributory. The perception supplier's algorithm had a known edge-case defect in low-light conditions. Compute the liability distribution using the causal contribution model with $P = 0.85, B = 0.90, D = 0.95$ for the supplier and $P = 0.70, B = 0.60, D = 0.40$ for the pedestrian.

**Step 1 — Compute causal weight of supplier.**

$$
C_{\text{supplier}} = P_s \times B_s \times D_s = 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.7268
$$

**Step 2 — Compute causal weight of pedestrian.**

$$
C_{\text{ped}} = P_p \times B_p \times D_p = 0.70 \times 0.60 \times 0.40 = 0.1680
$$

**Step 3 — Total causal weight.**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 0.7268 + 0.1680 = 0.8948
$$

**Step 4 — Liability share of supplier.**

$$
L_{\text{supplier}} = \frac{0.7268}{0.8948} \times 100\% = 81.23\%
$$

**Step 5 — Liability share of pedestrian.**

$$
L_{\text{ped}} = \frac{0.1680}{0.8948} \times 100\% = 18.78\%
$$

**Step 6 — Verification.**

$$
L_{\text{supplier}} + L_{\text{ped}} = 81.23\% + 18.78\% = 100.01\% \approx 100\%
$$

(The 0.01% residual is absorbed by rounding in a real legal pleading.)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Reasoning Anchor:** Always conclude with the verification step. Showing $\sum L_i \approx 100\%$ demonstrates methodological discipline and routinely secures the **last method-mark**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Composite Compliance Score

> **Problem Statement:** An OEM submits its Level 3 ADAS for type approval. The four sub-scores are $S_{\text{safety}} = 0.92, S_{\text{privacy}} = 0.78, S_{\text{cyber}} = 0.88, S_{\text{liability}} = 0.95$. The KTU evaluator applies weights $w_1 = 0.35, w_2 = 0.20, w_3 = 0.25, w_4 = 0.20$. Compute CCS and decide if the vehicle passes the EU AI Act threshold of 0.85.

**Step 1 — Verify weight constraint.**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Apply weighted sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CCS} &= (0.35 \times 0.92) + (0.20 \times 0.78) + (0.25 \times 0.88) + (0.20 \times 0.95) \\
&= 0.3220 + 0.1560 + 0.2200 + 0.1900 \\
&= 0.8880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Threshold check.**

$$
\mathrm{CCS} = 0.8880 \geq 0.85 = \mathrm{CCS}_{\text{threshold}} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The vehicle passes conformity assessment. However, the privacy sub-score of 0.78 is the weakest dimension; the manufacturer must close this gap before deployment in India (DPDP Act 2023 consent architecture) and the EU (GDPR Article 9 sensitive-data controls).

## 3.5 Derivation of the Transition-Demand Compliance Time (Legal Relevance)

Under UN Regulation No. 157, when the automated driving system issues a **transition demand** to the human fallback-ready user, the system must allow a minimum compliance window before initiating a Minimal Risk Manoeuvre. Let the system state be modelled as:

$$
T_{\text{reaction}} = T_{\text{visual}} + T_{\text{cognitive}} + T_{\text{motor}}
$$

A legal presumption of *unreasonable risk* arises if

$$
T_{\text{reaction, design}} < T_{\text{reaction, human, 95th percentile}} = 9.75 \text{ s}
$$

Empirically validated values are:

$$
T_{\text{visual}} \approx 0.5 \text{ s}, \quad T_{\text{cognitive}} \approx 7.0 \text{ s}, \quad T_{\text{motor}} \approx 0.75 \text{ s}
$$

Summing:

$$
T_{\text{reaction, 95th}} \approx 8.25 \text{ to } 9.75 \text{ s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The legal significance is profound. If a Level 3 system issues a transition demand with a window shorter than this 95th-percentile reaction time, and an accident occurs, the **manufacturer bears strict liability** for designing an unsafe handover interface. This is a KTU-favourite 7-mark sub-question.

## 3.6 Ethical Decision Weighting — A Reproducible Method

To convert the MIT Moral Machine dilemma into a legally defensible algorithm, the principle of **expected utility maximization under constraint** is applied:

$$
U_{\text{decision}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \cdot O_i
$$

subject to the **hard constraint**:

$$
\text{decision} \not\rightarrow \text{intentional violation of codified traffic law}
$$

where $O_i$ are the outcomes (life saved, life lost, property damage) and $\alpha_i$ are the legally permissible weights. The hard constraint is non-negotiable; the EU AI Act and German Ethics Commission on Automated and Connected Driving both reject the maximization of *any* utilitarian function that produces a deliberate traffic-law violation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** A frequent KTU 14-mark mistake is to argue that "an autonomous car should sacrifice its occupant to save a pedestrian." This is **legally untenable** in most jurisdictions. Always pair such a statement with the explicit qualification that the action remains within codified traffic law and the manufacturer's discharge of duty of care.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams below use **Mermaid** syntax with strict adherence to the alpha-prefix node identifier rule and clean, unformatted labels. They are designed for direct copy-paste into a KTU answer script as visual reinforcement.

## 4.1 Regulatory Framework — Global to National Cascading Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[UN Vienna Convention 1968] --> nodeB[Amendments 2014 and 2016]
    nodeB --> nodeC[UNECE GRVA Standards]
    nodeC --> nodeD[UN Regulation 157 ALKS]
    nodeC --> nodeE[UN Regulation 155 Cybersecurity]
    nodeC --> nodeF[UN Regulation 156 Software Updates]
    nodeD --> nodeG[National Type Approval]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[India MoRTH and ARAI]
    nodeG --> nodeI[EU Type Approval Authority]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[US DOT NHTSA]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Public Road Deployment]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Continuous Post-Market Surveillance]
```

## 4.2 Liability Allocation Flowchart — Who Pays When an AV Crashes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[AV Collision Event Detected] --> nodeB[Retrieve Data Event Recorder]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Active Driving Mode]
    nodeC --> nodeD{L3 Transition Demand Active?}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Driver Failure to Respond]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeF{Within ODD?}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[System Driving L4 L5]
    nodeF -->|No| nodeH[Driver in Control]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Driver Primarily Liable]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[OEM and Software Supplier Liable]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Driver Liable]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Right of Recourse to OEM]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Subrogation Against Defect Source]
    nodeK --> nodeN[OEM Liable only if Defect Proven]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Final Settlement]
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

## 4.3 SAE Level vs Legal Responsibility Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[SAE Level 0 No Automation] --> nodeB[Driver 100 percent Liable]
    nodeC[SAE Level 1 Driver Assist] --> nodeB
    nodeD[SAE Level 2 Partial Automation] --> nodeE[Driver 100 percent Liable but must monitor]
    nodeF[SAE Level 3 Conditional] --> nodeG[Driver during handover and OEM during automation]
    nodeH[SAE Level 4 High Automation] --> nodeI[OEM and Software 100 percent within ODD]
    nodeJ[SAE Level 5 Full Automation] --> nodeK[OEM and Software 100 percent always]
```

## 4.4 Data Protection Compliance Architecture under DPDP Act 2023

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[AV Sensor Data Acquisition] --> nodeB[Personal Data Identified]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Non-Personal Telemetry]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Consent or Legitimate Use Verification]
    nodeD -->|Approved| nodeE[Data Minimization Filter]
    nodeD -->|Rejected| nodeF[Discard and Log]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Purpose Limitation Check]
    nodeG -->|Within Purpose| nodeH[Encrypted Storage with Retention Timer]
    nodeG -->|Outside Purpose| nodeI[Fresh Consent Required]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Right to Erasure Endpoint]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Anonymized Telemetry Storage]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Data Protection Impact Assessment Annual]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Audit and Compliance Report to Data Protection Board]
```

## 4.5 Ethical Decision-Making Architecture (Hard-Constraint Approach)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Perception Event with Collision Risk] --> nodeB{Is Action Within Codified Traffic Law?}
    nodeB -->|No| nodeC[Reject Action and Select Minimum Harm Within Law]
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeD[Compute Expected Utility Score]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Score Above Threshold and Justifiable?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[Execute Planned Manoeuvre]
    nodeE -->|No| nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeG[Log Decision to DER with Reasoning Trace]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Post-Event Forensic Reconstruction]
```

## 4.6 Type-Approval and Conformity Assessment Workflow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[OEM Design Documentation] --> nodeB[ISO 26262 Functional Safety Evidence]
    nodeA --> nodeC[ISO 21448 SOTIF Evidence]
    nodeA --> nodeD[ISO 21434 Cybersecurity Evidence]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Conformity Assessment by Technical Service]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Type Approval Certificate Issued by MoRTH or EU Authority]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Market Surveillance and Field Monitoring]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Software Update Management per UN R156]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Cybersecurity Monitoring per UN R155]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Re-Assessment if Major Change]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```
