The back-scattered power density at the receiving antenna (distance R away) is: $$S_r(R) = \frac{P_{\text{intercepted}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)(4\pi R^2)} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$ **Step 4 — Power Captured by the Receive Antenna** The receive antenna has an effective aperture $$A_e$$ related to gain by: $$A_e = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ Therefore the received echo power is: $$P_r = S_r(R) \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 5 — Final Form (Monostatic Radar, where Tx = Rx, hence G² appears)** $$\boxed{\;P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R^{4}}\;}$$ > **Critical Insight:** The **R⁴ law** — doubling the target distance reduces the echo power by **12 dB** (a factor of 16). This extreme range dependence is why radar engineering is so challenging and why high-power tubes/klystrons were historically required. **Step 6 — Maximum Range** Setting $$P_r = S_{\min}$$ (the receiver's minimum detectable signal) and solving for **R**: $$S_{\min} = \frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, R_{\max}^{4}}$$ $$\boxed{\;R_{\max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^{2} \, \lambda^{2} \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \, S_{\min}}\right]^{1/4}\;}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Doppler Frequency Shift A target moving radially at velocity **v_r** toward a stationary radar reflects the carrier **f₀** with a **two-way Doppler shift**. **Step 1 — Frequency Seen by the Moving Target** The target experiences the approaching wavefront at a higher frequency (classical Doppler): $$f' = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)$$ > **Reasoning:** In one period $$T_0 = 1/f_0$$, the wavefront advances by $$\lambda$$ while the target moves inward by $$v_r T_0$$, compressing the effective wavelength. **Step 2 — Frequency Re-Radiated by Target and Observed by Radar** The target now acts as a moving source oscillating at $$f'$$, re-radiating the wave back. The radar observes an additional compression: $$f_r = f' \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right) = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2$$ **Step 3 — Two-Way Doppler Frequency** $$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = f_0 \left[\left(1 + \frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2 - 1\right] = f_0 \left[\frac{2 v_r}{c} + \left(\frac{v_r}{c}\right)^2\right]$$ **Step 4 — Non-Relativistic Approximation** (since $$v_r \ll c$$ for all terrestrial targets, e.g., a car at 200 km/h has $$v_r/c \approx 1.86 \times 10^{-7}$$): $$\boxed{\;f_d = \frac{2 \, v_r \, f_0}{c} = \frac{2 \, v_r}{\lambda}\;}$$ > **Sign convention:** $$f_d > 0$$ when target approaches (closing), $$f_d < 0$$ when receding. --- ### 3.3 FMCW Beat Frequency Derivation **Step 1 — Transmitted Chirp Definition** A linear FM chirp sweeps frequency linearly from $$f_0$$ to $$f_0 + \Delta f$$ over a sweep period $$T_c$$. The transmitted instantaneous frequency is: $$f_{\text{TX}}(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_c} t$$ **Step 2 — Received Echo from Stationary Target at Range R** The echo is delayed by $$\tau = 2R/c$$, so the received instantaneous frequency is: $$f_{\text{RX}}(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_c}(t - \tau)$$ **Step 3 — Mixer Output (Beat Frequency)** The radar mixer multiplies Tx and Rx; after low-pass filtering, the difference frequency is: $$f_b(t) = f_{\text{TX}}(t) - f_{\text{RX}}(t) = \frac{\Delta f}{T_c} \cdot \tau = \frac{\Delta f}{T_c} \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$ $$\boxed{\;f_b = \frac{2 \, R \, \Delta f}{c \, T_c}\;}$$ **Step 4 — Range Resolution** For a sweep bandwidth $$\Delta f$$, the time-resolution of the FFT determines range resolution: $$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 \, \Delta f}$$ > **Example:** A 77 GHz radar with $$\Delta f = 4\ \text{GHz}$$ gives $$\Delta R = 3 \times 10^8 / (2 \times 4 \times 10^9) = 0.0375\ \text{m} = 3.75\ \text{cm}$$. This is why modern imaging radars can resolve pedestrians from lamp posts. **Step 5 — Velocity Resolution** By sending **N_c** evenly spaced chirps and performing a second FFT across the slow-time dimension: $$\Delta v = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, N_c \, T_c}$$ --- ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style Question) **Problem:** A 77 GHz automotive FMCW radar has the following parameters: - Sweep bandwidth: $$\Delta f = 1\ \text{GHz}$$ - Chirp duration: $$T_c = 100\ \mu\text{s}$$ - PRF (chirp repetition): 10 kHz - Transmit power: $$P_t = 10\ \text{dBm} = 10\ \text{mW}$$ - Antenna gain: $$G = 20\ \text{dBi} = 100$$ - Target RCS: $$\sigma = 10\ \text{m}^2$$ (typical car) - Receiver sensitivity: $$S_{\min} = -100\ \text{dBm} = 10^{-13}\ \text{W}$$ **Find:** (a) Maximum detectable range, (b) Beat frequency for a target at R = 50 m. **Solution (a):** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{77 \times 10^9} \approx 3.896 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 3.896\ \text{mm}$$ $$R_{\max} = \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}}\right]^{1/4}$$ Numerator: $$P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma = 10^{-2} \times 10^4 \times (3.896 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 10 \approx 10^{-2} \times 10^4 \times 1.518 \times 10^{-5} \times 10 = 1.518 \times 10^{-2}$$ Denominator: $$(4\pi)^3 S_{\min} = (12.566)^3 \times 10^{-13} \approx 1984 \times 10^{-13} = 1.984 \times 10^{-10}$$ Ratio: $$\frac{1.518 \times 10^{-2}}{1.984 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 7.65 \times 10^{7}$$ $$R_{\max} = (7.65 \times 10^{7})^{1/4} \approx 93.6\ \text{m}$$ **Solution (b):** $$f_b = \frac{2 R \Delta f}{c T_c} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{11}}{3 \times 10^4} \approx 3.33\ \text{MHz}$$ This **3.33 MHz IF signal** is sampled by an ADC and processed via FFT to extract the range bin. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Pulsed Radar System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Waveform Generator
Pulse Modulator] --> B[Power Amplifier
Transmitter] B --> C[Duplexer
TR Switch] C --> D[Antenna
High Gain Directional] D -.EM Wave Outbound.-> E[Target Object
RCS Sigma] E -.Scattered Echo.-> D D --> F[Low Noise Amplifier
LNA] F --> G[Mixer and IF Amplifier] A --> H[Coherent Local Oscillator] H --> G G --> I[Matched Filter] I --> J[ADC and DSP] J --> K[Range and Velocity
Estimator] K --> L[Object Tracking
and Display] ``` **Functional Block Description:** - **Waveform Generator:** Defines pulse width τ and PRF. - **Power Amplifier:** Boosts peak power (often kW–MW for long-range radar). - **Duplexer (TR Switch):** Isolates the sensitive LNA from the high-power transmitter (often 100+ dB isolation required). - **Antenna:** Typically parabolic dish or phased-array; the same antenna is used for Tx and Rx (monostatic). - **LNA:** Sets the noise figure of the receiver — determines $$S_{\min}$$. - **Coherent Local Oscillator:** Required for phase-coherent Doppler processing. - **Matched Filter:** Maximizes SNR; impulse response matched to transmitted pulse. ### 4.2 FMCW Radar Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Synthesizer VCO
Linear Chirp Generator] --> B[Power Divider] B --> C[Power Amplifier TX] C --> D[TX Antenna Array] D -.Chirp Wave.-> E[Target at Range R] E -.Delayed Echo.-> F[RX Antenna Array] F --> G[Low Noise Amplifier LNA] G --> H[Mixer] B --> H H --> I[IF Beat Signal f_b] I --> J[Anti Alias Filter] J --> K[High Speed ADC] K --> L[Range FFT First Dimension] L --> M[Doppler FFT Second Dimension] M --> N[CFAR Detection] N --> O[Angle Estimation DOA] O --> P[Object List Output
to Fusion Stack] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Radar Signal Processing ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph RF_Front_End [RF FRONT END] A1[Antenna Array
MIMO Ntx x Nrx] --> A2[Analog Downconversion] A2 --> A3[IF Sampling ADC] end RF_Front_End --> Digital_Signal_Processing subgraph Digital_Signal_Processing [DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING DSP] B1[Windowing and Range FFT] --> B2[Range Doppler Map RDM] B2 --> B3[CFAR Detection] B3 --> B4[Direction of Arrival DOA
Angle FFT or MUSIC] B4 --> B5[Clustering and Tracking] end Digital_Signal_Processing --> C1[Perception Layer Output
Point Cloud with Range Velocity Angle RCS] ``` ### 4.4 Range-Doppler Map Concept (Data Cube) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Range_Bins [Range Axis Bins] R1[R1] --> R2[R2] --> R3[R3] --> R4[R4] --> R5[R5] end subgraph Doppler_Bins [Doppler Velocity Axis Bins] D1[Negative Approaching Fast] --> D2[Approaching] --> D3[Zero Static] --> D4[Receding] --> D5[Receding Fast] end Range_Bins --- Doppler_Bins subgraph Targets [Detected Targets] T1[Static Guard Rail at R3] T2[Approaching Vehicle at R4 D2] T3[Pedestrian at R2 D1] end ``` **Note on Data Cube:** Each cell in the Range-Doppler Map (RDM) represents a specific (range, velocity) hypothesis. After CFAR (Constant False Alarm Rate) detection, peaks above an adaptive threshold correspond to physical targets. A third FFT along the antenna-array dimension extracts the **angle (azimuth)** to form the full 4D point cloud: **(Range, Doppler, Azimuth, Elevation)**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]** **Define Radar. Briefly explain the basic principle of operation of a primary radar with a neat block diagram. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > **RADAR** stands for **Radio Detection and Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system used for the detection and localization of objects by transmitting RF/microwave signals and receiving the reflected echoes. > **Principle of Operation:** > 1. The **transmitter** radiates a directional EM pulse at carrier frequency **f₀** via a high-gain antenna. > 2. The pulse travels to the target at the speed of light **c**, taking time $$\Delta t = 2R/c$$ for the round trip. > 3. The **target** scatters a fraction of the incident energy back toward the radar. > 4. The **receiver** amplifies and processes the weak echo to extract the round-trip time $$\Delta t$$ and any Doppler shift $$f_d$$. > 5. The displayed outputs are **range (R)**, **radial velocity (v_r)**, and **angular position (θ)**. > Block diagram: Waveform Generator → Power Amplifier → Duplexer → Antenna → Target → Antenna → LNA → Mixer → Signal Processor → Display. **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks principle + 0.5 marks for block diagram labels]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]** **Distinguish between pulsed radar and CW radar. Mention any two applications of each. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Pulsed Radar | CW Radar | |---|---|---| | Waveform | Narrow pulses at PRF | Continuous transmission | | Measured quantity | Range via ToF | Velocity via Doppler | | Range capability | Long (hundreds of km) | Short (meters to ~100 m) | | Transmitter duty cycle | Low (~0.1%) | 100% | | Peak power | Very high (MW) | Low (mW) | | Range–velocity coupling | Decoupled | Coupled (unless FMCW) | **Applications:** - **Pulsed Radar:** Air-traffic-control primary surveillance radar; military long-range early-warning radar; weather radar (Doppler weather). - **CW Radar:** Police speed guns; automotive blind-spot detection; sports Doppler radar (baseball pitch speed); industrial flowmeters. **[Marks Distribution: 2 marks for table + 0.5 + 0.5 for applications]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (Choose ONE out of TWO) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Choice Question Style]** **(a)** Derive the **radar range equation** starting from the definition of antenna gain, and explain each term. State clearly why received power falls off as **R⁴**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A monostatic radar operates at $$f_0 = 10\ \text{GHz}$$ with peak transmitted power $$P_t = 1\ \text{kW}$$, antenna gain $$G = 30\ \text{dB}$$, and receiver sensitivity $$S_{\min} = -100\ \text{dBm}$$. A target has RCS $$\sigma = 1\ \text{m}^2$$. Calculate: (i) The wavelength $$\lambda$$. (ii) The maximum detectable range $$R_{\max}$$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Derivation of Radar Range Equation** Starting from the definition of antenna gain: $$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_e = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 1 — Power density at the target (distance R):** $$S_t = \frac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2}$$ **Step 2 — Power intercepted by the target of RCS σ:** $$P_{\text{int}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$ **Step 3 — Re-radiation back toward the radar (isotropic scatterer by definition of σ):** $$S_r = \frac{P_{\text{int}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$ **Step 4 — Power captured by receive aperture A_e (with gain G):** $$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$ **Explanation of each term:** - $$P_t$$: peak transmit power (W) - $$G$$: antenna gain (dimensionless, linear) - $$\lambda$$: wavelength (m) - $$\sigma$$: radar cross-section (m²) - $$R$$: slant range (m) **Why R⁴?** The wave traverses the path twice — once outward, once back. Each leg of the path obeys the **inverse-square law** (energy spread over the surface of an expanding sphere). Multiplying two inverse-square laws gives $$R^{-4}$$. This is why doubling range reduces echo power by **12 dB**. **[Marks Distribution: 4 marks derivation + 2 marks term explanation + 1 mark R⁴ reasoning]** **(b) Numerical Solution** **Given:** $$f_0 = 10\ \text{GHz},\quad P_t = 1\ \text{kW} = 10^3\ \text{W},\quad G = 30\ \text{dB} = 10^{30/10} = 1000,\quad S_{\min} = -100\ \text{dBm} = 10^{-13}\ \text{W},\quad \sigma = 1\ \text{m}^2$$ **(i) Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 3 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m} = 3\ \text{cm}$$ **(ii) Maximum Detectable Range:** $$R_{\max} = \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}}\right]^{1/4}$$ Numerator: $$P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma = 10^3 \times (10^3)^2 \times (3 \times 10^{-2})^2 \times 1 = 10^3 \times 10^6 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^5\ \text{W·m}^2$$ Denominator: $$(4\pi)^3 S_{\min} = (12.566)^3 \times 10^{-13} \approx 1984 \times 10^{-13} = 1.984 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{W}$$ Ratio: $$\frac{9 \times 10^5}{1.984 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{15}\ \text{m}^4$$ $$R_{\max} = (4.54 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} \approx 8.2 \times 10^{3}\ \text{m} \approx 8.2\ \text{km}$$ **[Marks Distribution: 1 mark λ + 4 marks substitution + 2 marks final R_max with units]** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Alternative Style]** **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the operation of an **FMCW radar**. Derive the expression for the **beat frequency** in terms of target range, sweep bandwidth, and chirp duration. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 77 GHz automotive FMCW radar has sweep bandwidth $$\Delta f = 1\ \text{GHz}$$ and chirp duration $$T_c = 50\ \mu\text{s}$$. Compute: (i) The beat frequency for a target at R = 100 m. (ii) The range resolution $$\Delta R$$. (iii) The unambiguous maximum range if the PRF is 20 kHz. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) FMCW Radar Operation** An FMCW radar transmits a **continuously varying frequency waveform** (a chirp) rather than discrete pulses. The instantaneous transmit frequency is: $$f_{\text{TX}}(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_c} t$$ The transmitted signal is split: one path goes to the TX antenna, the other is the local-oscillator reference for the mixer. The echo from a target at range R arrives delayed by $$\tau = 2R/c$$. The mixer subtracts the two signals producing an **intermediate-frequency (IF) beat signal** at frequency: $$f_b = \frac{2R \Delta f}{c T_c}$$ This beat frequency is **linearly proportional to range**, allowing range extraction by simple FFT. Multiple chirps per frame enable velocity measurement by a second FFT across the slow-time dimension. **Block diagram:** Synthesizer (VCO) → Power divider → PA → TX antenna → Target → RX antenna → LNA → Mixer (with reference from power divider) → IF filter → ADC → Range FFT → Doppler FFT → Detection. **(b) Numerical Solution** **Given:** $$f_0 = 77\ \text{GHz},\quad \Delta f = 1\ \text{GHz} = 10^9\ \text{Hz},\quad T_c = 50\ \mu\text{s} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s},\quad \text{PRF} = 20\ \text{kHz} = 2 \times 10^4\ \text{Hz}$$ **(i) Beat frequency at R = 100 m:** $$f_b = \frac{2R \Delta f}{c T_c} = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$f_b = \frac{2 \times 10^{11}}{1.5 \times 10^4} \approx 1.33 \times 10^7\ \text{Hz} \approx 13.3\ \text{MHz}$$ **(ii) Range resolution:** $$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.15\ \text{m} = 15\ \text{cm}$$ **(iii) Unambiguous maximum range:** $$R_u = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2 \times 10^4} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^4} = 7500\ \text{m} = 7.5\ \text{km}$$ > **Note:** In FMCW, the unambiguous range is determined by the **PRI** between successive chirps, not by the chirp duration itself. **[Marks Distribution: 3 marks block diagram + 4 marks derivation + 1 mark f_b + 1 mark ΔR + 1 mark R_u]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the range equation** — round-trip path is the #1 error. Always write $$R = c\Delta t / 2$$. > 2. **Using dBi values directly in linear formula** — convert dB → linear first: $$G(\text{linear}) = 10^{G(\text{dBi})/10}$$. A 30 dB gain is 1000, not 30. > 3. **Mixing up dBm and W** — dBm to W: $$P(\text{W}) = 10^{(P(\text{dBm}) - 30)/10}$$. −100 dBm = 10⁻¹³ W. > 4. **Forgetting $$(4\pi)^3$$** — this constant is often silently dropped in student work. Write it out explicitly every time. > 5. **Confusing range resolution with range accuracy** — resolution is set by bandwidth; accuracy is set by SNR and calibration. > 6. **Wrong Doppler sign convention** — KTU expects positive $$f_d$$ for **approaching** targets. State your convention explicitly. > 7. **Not labeling units in final answers** — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit on a 7-mark sub-question. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Critical Concepts to Memorize Before the KTU Exam** - [ ] **Radar = Radio Detection and Ranging**; operates by **EM wave reflection** at microwave/mmWave frequencies. - [ ] Two measurable quantities: **range (ToF)** and **velocity (Doppler)**. - [ ] Carrier frequency-to-wavelength conversion: $$\lambda = c / f_0$$; remember $$c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$. - [ ] **Radar Range Equation:** $$P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$ — the **R⁴ law** is the heart of radar engineering. - [ ] **Maximum range:** fourth root of the ratio ($$P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma$$) to ($$(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}$$). - [ ] **Doppler shift (two-way):** $$f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$ — sign convention: $$+$$ = approaching. - [ ] **FMCW beat frequency:** $$f_b = \dfrac{2R \Delta f}{c T_c}$$ — proportional to range, enables simple FFT processing. - [ ] **Range resolution:** $$\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$$ — wider bandwidth = finer resolution. - [ ] **Unambiguous range:** $$R_u = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$ — beyond this, range echoes alias. - [ ] **Automotive band:** 76–81 GHz mmWave; 77 GHz center; up to 4 GHz sweep bandwidth for 4D imaging radar. - [ ] **Radar complements Lidar:** all-weather, direct velocity, long-range; Lidar complements with high angular resolution, dense point cloud. - [ ] **Pulsed radar vs. CW radar:** Pulsed measures range; CW measures velocity (Doppler); FMCW measures both. - [ ] **Receiver sensitivity** $$S_{\min} = k T_0 B F (SNR_{\min})$$ — depends on bandwidth, noise figure, and minimum SNR required. - [ ] Block diagram sequence: **Waveform Gen → PA → Duplexer → Antenna → Target → Antenna → LNA → Mixer → DSP → Display/Tracking**. > [!NOTE] > **One-Line Mantra for the Exam:** *"Radar is echo ranging — measure the time, measure the shift, obey the R⁴ law."*

topics.title: Applications and Limitations of Radar in Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications and Limitations of Radar in Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Radar (Vehicle-mounted Radar)** is a radio detection and ranging (RADAR) based sensing subsystem, operating in the millimetre-wave (mmWave) spectrum, that emits precisely modulated electromagnetic waves toward the surrounding environment, captures the backscattered echoes from stationary and moving objects, and extracts critical kinematic parameters — **range (R)**, **relative radial velocity (v_r)**, and **angular position (azimuth θ)** — to enable real-time situational awareness in Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs).

In the KTU 2024 OEMUT611 syllabus, vehicle radar is positioned as a **primary exteroceptive sensor** in the perception stack of an Intelligent Transportation System (ITS). It operates predominantly in two internationally allocated Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) frequency bands:

- **24 GHz band (UWB, narrowband, 21.65 – 26.65 GHz)** — phased out for automotive use, used for short-range parking radar.
- **77 – 81 GHz band (76 – 77 GHz for long-range, 77 – 81 GHz for short-range)** — current global automotive standard, offering superior resolution, smaller antenna size, and wider bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Range Band Classification (KTU Reference):**
> - **SRR (Short-Range Radar):** 0.1 m – 30 m, used for blind-spot detection, parking assist, lane-change assist.
> - **MRR (Mid-Range Radar):** 0.1 m – 80 m, used for cross-traffic alert.
> - **LRR (Long-Range Radar):** 0.1 m – 250 m, used for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are **blindfolded in a pitch-dark, fog-filled tunnel**, but you can clap your hands. From the **delay** between your clap and the echo coming back, you know how far the wall is. If the wall is moving toward you, the echo's pitch (frequency) shifts — the same trick a bat uses. **Automotive radar is exactly this** — it acts as the *electronic ears* of a vehicle.

A bat (the radar transceiver) emits a chirp (a high-frequency sound pulse), listens for the echo, and triangulates position, speed, and direction of prey (other vehicles, pedestrians, obstacles). The "sound" used by automotive radar, however, is **not sound at all** — it is an electromagnetic wave at **77 GHz** (wavelength ≈ **3.9 mm**), which travels at the speed of light and penetrates rain, fog, dust, and darkness with negligible degradation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants (Bolded for Memory Retention)

| Symbol | Value | Significance |
|:------:|:-----:|:-------------|
| **c** | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Speed of EM waves in free space |
| **λ (77 GHz)** | **≈ 3.9 mm** | mmWave wavelength |
| **f_c (typical)** | **76–77 GHz** | Carrier frequency for LRR |
| **B (bandwidth)** | **Up to 4 GHz** (77–81 GHz) | Defines range resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The student is expected to understand *why* radar is preferred over LiDAR and cameras in adverse weather, the trade-offs in physical resolution versus atmospheric attenuation, and the regulatory FCC / ETSI frequency allocations relevant to vehicular deployment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logic Steps

1. **Wave Generation** — A Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) or Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) chirp generator produces a linearly frequency-modulated carrier around **f_c = 77 GHz**.
2. **Transmission via Tx Antenna Array** — The chirp is radiated into free space via a planar patch antenna array. The wide bandwidth (≈ 4 GHz) yields fine range resolution.
3. **Wave–Object Interaction** — Incident wave strikes a target (vehicle, pedestrian, guard rail). Part of the energy is reflected back (radar cross-section σ).
4. **Echo Reception (Rx)** — The receive antenna array captures the time-delayed, frequency-shifted echo.
5. **Mixer Down-conversion** — The received signal is mixed with the transmitted signal, producing an **Intermediate Frequency (IF)** beat signal whose frequency is proportional to the target's **range**.
6. **Range-Doppler-FFT Processing** — Two-dimensional Fast Fourier Transform is applied across fast-time (range) and slow-time (Doppler) samples to produce the **Range-Doppler Map (RDM)**.
7. **Angle-of-Arrival (AoA) Estimation** — Phase differences across multiple Rx channels yield the **azimuth angle** of the target.
8. **Object Tracking & Fusion** — Detected points are clustered, tracked via Kalman filters, and fused with camera/LiDAR data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FMCW for Vehicles?** Unlike pulsed radar, FMCW allows simultaneous measurement of **range and velocity** with low peak power, making it ideal for battery-powered, EMI-safe automotive platforms.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:---------:|:-------:|:-----:|:-------:|
| 1 | Range (from beat frequency) | $R = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c}{2 \cdot \dfrac{d f}{dt}}$ | m | $f_b$ = beat frequency; $df/dt$ = chirp slope |
| 2 | Range (time-of-flight) | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t_d}{2}$ | m | $t_d$ = round-trip delay |
| 3 | Doppler frequency shift | $f_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_r \cdot f_c}{c}$ | Hz | $v_r$ = radial velocity |
| 4 | Range resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot B}$ | m | $B$ = swept bandwidth |
| 5 | Velocity resolution | $\Delta v = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot N \cdot T_{PRI}}$ | m/s | $N$ = coherent pulses; $T_{PRI}$ = pulse repetition interval |
| 6 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{PRI}}{2}$ | m | Limited by chirp repetition |
| 7 | Maximum unambiguous velocity | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \cdot T_{PRI}}$ | m/s | |
| 8 | Radar range equation (monostatic) | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4 \cdot L}$ | W | $\sigma$ = RCS; $L$ = system losses |
| 9 | SNR (range-Doppler cell) | $SNR = \dfrac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4 \cdot k \cdot T \cdot B_n \cdot F \cdot L}$ | dimensionless | $k$ = Boltzmann const. |
| 10 | Angle resolution (azimuth) | $\Delta \theta \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{N_a \cdot d \cos\theta}$ | rad | $N_a$ = antenna elements, $d$ = spacing |
| 11 | Half-power beamwidth | $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.886 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | rad | $D$ = aperture dimension |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** *R-V-A (Range–Velocity–Angle)* — these are the three fundamental outputs of any automotive radar. Every KTU question on radar reduces to one of these three.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Automotive radar is the **only sensor mandated by Euro NCAP and NHTSA** for AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) and ACC systems. It is used in:

- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — maintains safe longitudinal gap.
- **Forward Collision Warning (FCW) / AEB** — automatic braking at 60 km/h or higher.
- **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** — side/rear SRR.
- **Lane Change Assist (LCA)** — 77 GHz rear corner radar.
- **Cross-Traffic Alert (CTA)** — reverse parking scenarios.
- **Pedestrian / Cyclist detection** — recent deep-learning-aided radar.
- **Autonomous Highway Driving (SAE Level 3+)** — multi-radar 360° perception.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Range from FMCW Beat Frequency

**Given:** An FMCW chirp sweeping linearly from $f_0$ to $f_0 + B$ in sweep time $T_{chirp}$. Transmitted and received (echo from a target at range $R$) signals are mixed at the receiver.

**Step 1 — Express the round-trip delay.**
The signal travels distance $2R$ to and from the target.

$$
t_d = \dfrac{2R}{c}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the chirp slope (frequency rate of change).**
The frequency changes linearly from $f_0$ to $f_0 + B$ in $T_{chirp}$.

$$
k = \dfrac{B}{T_{chirp}} \quad \text{(Hz/s)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the instantaneous frequency difference at the mixer.**
The transmitted signal at time $t$ has instantaneous frequency $f_T(t) = f_0 + k t$.
The received signal at time $t$ is a delayed copy: $f_R(t) = f_0 + k(t - t_d)$.
The mixer output (beat signal) has frequency equal to the difference:

$$
f_b(t) = f_T(t) - f_R(t) = k \cdot t_d
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $t_d$ and $k$.**

$$
f_b = \left(\dfrac{B}{T_{chirp}}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{2R}{c}\right) = \dfrac{2 R B}{c \, T_{chirp}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for range $R$.**

$$
R = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T_{chirp}}{2 B}
$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent compact form using chirp slope $df/dt$.**

$$
\boxed{R = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c}{2 \cdot \dfrac{df}{dt}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Doppler Frequency for a Moving Target

**Given:** A target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ relative to a stationary radar operating at carrier frequency $f_c$.

**Step 1 — Two-way Doppler principle.**
The target acts as a moving receiver (first Doppler shift) and a moving transmitter (second Doppler shift). The total shift is therefore **twice** the single-way shift.

**Step 2 — Single-way Doppler formula.**
A source moving with velocity $v_r$ away/toward a stationary observer produces:

$$
f' = f_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{c \pm v_r}{c \mp v_r}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply non-relativistic approximation** ($v_r \ll c$):

$$
f' \approx f_c \left(1 \pm \dfrac{v_r}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Two-way shift:** The radar receives the re-radiated wave from a moving scatterer, doubling the effect:

$$
f_d = f' - f_c \approx \pm \dfrac{2 v_r f_c}{c}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Doppler expression.**

$$
\boxed{f_d = \dfrac{2 \, v_r \, f_c}{c}}
$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For $v_r = 30$ m/s (≈ 108 km/h), $f_c = 77$ GHz:

$$
f_d = \dfrac{2 \times 30 \times 77 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} = 15{,}400 \text{ Hz} \; (\approx 15.4 \text{ kHz})
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Range Resolution

**Step 1 — Define range resolution.**
The minimum separation $\Delta R$ between two targets that the radar can distinguish.

**Step 2 — Two distinct targets produce two beat tones separated by $\Delta f_b$.**
For the radar to resolve them, $\Delta f_b$ must exceed the spectral resolution of the observation window, which (for a sweep of duration $T_{chirp}$) is:

$$
\Delta f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{T_{chirp}}
$$

**Step 3 — The beat-frequency separation in terms of range separation.**

$$
\Delta f_b = k \cdot \Delta t_d = \dfrac{B}{T_{chirp}} \cdot \dfrac{2 \Delta R}{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate $\Delta f_b$ to $\Delta f_{min}$:**

$$
\dfrac{2 B \Delta R}{c T_{chirp}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{chirp}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $T_{chirp}$ and solve for $\Delta R$.**

$$
\boxed{\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 B}}
$$

**Numerical insight:** For $B = 4$ GHz (77–81 GHz UWB band):

$$
\Delta R = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 4 \times 10^9} = 0.0375 \text{ m} \approx 3.75 \text{ cm}
$$

This is the **physical reason** the 77–81 GHz UWB band was adopted — it gives sub-4 cm resolution, sufficient to discriminate a pedestrian from a bicycle.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Applications vs. Limitations

| Aspect | Strength (Application) | Weakness (Limitation) |
|:------|:----------------------|:----------------------|
| **Weather** | Penetrates rain, fog, dust, snow; works day/night | Heavy rain (≥ 100 mm/h) attenuates mmWave by ≈ 1–3 dB/km |
| **Range** | Detects vehicles up to **250 m** (LRR) | Range resolution at 24 GHz narrow band is poor (≈ 75 cm) |
| **Velocity** | Direct, high-accuracy radial velocity via Doppler | Cannot measure **tangential** (lateral) velocity; only radial component |
| **Material** | Reflects well off metal, water (humans, animals) | Radar-absorbing materials (stealth coatings) reduce RCS drastically |
| **Angular resolution** | Multi-MIMO arrays give sub-degree azimuth | Elevation (z-axis) resolution is **very poor**; cannot detect overhead bridges/curbs |
| **Cost & Size** | Solid-state, mass-produced, low cost (~₹3–10 k) | Higher-frequency packaging and antenna design increases PCB complexity |
| **Power** | Low peak power (FMCW), automotive-grade EMI safe | Wide-band chirp linearity requires high-precision, power-hungry VCOs |
| **Classification** | New 4D radar + ML can classify objects | Traditional radar cannot distinguish "pedestrian" from "pole" reliably |
| **Multipath** | Used in tunnels for ghost-target detection | Multipath in urban canyons creates false positives (ghost targets) |
| **Regulatory** | Globally harmonized 76–81 GHz ISM band | Strict FCC/ETSI emission masks limit max radiated power |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 77 GHz FMCW radar with sweep bandwidth $B = 1$ GHz and sweep time $T = 1$ ms is mounted on a vehicle. Two motorcycles appear in front — at $R_1 = 50$ m and $R_2 = 50.75$ m. Can the radar resolve them?

**Step 1 — Range resolution:**

$$
\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1 \times 10^9} = 0.15 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Separation between the motorcycles:**

$$
\Delta R_{sep} = R_2 - R_1 = 50.75 - 50.00 = 0.75 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$
\Delta R_{sep} = 0.75 \text{ m} \; > \; \Delta R = 0.15 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** The radar **can** resolve the two motorcycles. This illustrates why automotive radar specifications emphasize **bandwidth as the dominant lever** for resolution, not raw carrier frequency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Automotive Radar Signal Path

The diagram below models the end-to-end signal flow from chirp generation to object tracking, replacing a physical drawing with a precise functional block diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Timing and Control Unit] --> A2[FMCW Chirp Generator VCO 77 GHz]
    A2 --> A3[Power Amplifier PA]
    A3 --> A4[Tx Antenna Array 2 to 8 channels]
    A4 --> A5[Free Space Propagation mmWave]
    A5 --> A6[Target Vehicle Pedestrian Obstacle RCS sigma]
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A7[Rx Antenna Array 4 to 12 channels MIMO]
    A7 --> A8[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    A8 --> A9[Mixer Multiplies with Tx replica]
    A9 --> A10[IF Beat Signal f_b]
    A10 --> A11[Analog Baseband Filter and ADC]
    A11 --> A12[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    A12 --> A13[Range FFT along fast time]
    A13 --> A14[Doppler FFT along slow time]
    A14 --> A15[Range Doppler Map RDM]
    A15 --> A16[CFAR Detection Constant False Alarm Rate]
    A16 --> A17[Angle of Arrival Estimation via phase across Rx]
    A17 --> A18[Object Clustering and Tracking Kalman Filter]
    A18 --> A19[Sensor Fusion with Camera and LiDAR]
    A19 --> A20[ADAS Output ACC AEB BSD LCA]
```

## 4.2 Frequency Band Allocation Diagram (Regulatory Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWBAND["24 GHz Band Legacy UWB"]
        L1[21.65 to 26.65 GHz]:::legacy
    end
    subgraph MIDBAND["77 GHz Band LRR Long Range"]
        M1[76 to 77 GHz]:::lrr
    end
    subgraph HIGHBAND["77 to 81 GHz UWB SRR MRR"]
        H1[77 to 81 GHz]:::srr
    end
    LOWBAND --> MIDBAND
    MIDBAND --> HIGHBAND
    classDef legacy fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef lrr fill:#dceefb,stroke:#2874a6,color:#000
    classDef srr fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Application Domain Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Radar in Vehicles]:::root --> P1[Active Safety ADAS]
    R --> P2[Comfort and Convenience]
    R --> P3[Autonomous Driving SAE L3 to L5]

    P1 --> Q1[Adaptive Cruise Control ACC]
    P1 --> Q2[Autonomous Emergency Braking AEB]
    P1 --> Q3[Forward Collision Warning FCW]
    P1 --> Q4[Blind Spot Detection BSD]
    P1 --> Q5[Lane Change Assist LCA]
    P1 --> Q6[Cross Traffic Alert CTA]

    P2 --> Q7[Parking Assist]
    P2 --> Q8[Door Opening Warning]

    P3 --> Q9[Highway Pilot L3]
    P3 --> Q10[Robotaxi L4]
    P3 --> Q11[Highway Chauffeur L3 plus]

    classDef root fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#9a7d0a,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Tx emits a chirp; the target reflects; the echo is mixed with a delayed copy of the chirp to produce the beat frequency $f_b$, which is linearly proportional to the target's range. Two consecutive FFTs — one across fast-time samples, one across slow-time chirps — convert the beat into the **Range–Doppler Map (RDM)**, the canonical data product of an automotive radar.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss-of-Marks Triggers:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing range as $R = c \cdot t_d$ instead of $R = (c \cdot t_d) / 2$. The factor of **2** (round trip) is the most frequently missed point — costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Bandwidth in Hz vs. MHz vs. GHz:** Numerical substitution errors in $\Delta R = c / (2B)$ are rampant. Always convert $B$ to **Hz**.
> 3. **Single-way vs. two-way Doppler:** A vehicle at 30 m/s is *not* $f_d = v_r f_c / c$ — it is **twice** that. The radar's wave is reflected, so the shift is doubled.
> 4. **Mixing up "range" and "range resolution":** $R$ is the measured distance; $\Delta R$ is the *minimum distinguishable* separation. Examiners deliberately interchange terms in MCQs.
> 5. **Forgetting units of $f_d$:** Always quote Doppler shift in **kHz or Hz**, not MHz.
> 6. **Confusing 24 GHz and 77 GHz applications:** Short-range legacy parking radars are 24 GHz; modern LRR is 77 GHz. Examiners deduct marks for the wrong attribution.
> 7. **In application questions, neglecting limitations:** A 14-mark question often ends with *"Discuss limitations"* — students who list only benefits lose 4–5 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]`
**List the three main applications of automotive radar and state the typical operating frequency band used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** and **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — long-range forward radar at **76–77 GHz**.
2. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** and **Lane Change Assist (LCA)** — short-range corner radar at **77–81 GHz**.
3. **Parking Assist** and **Cross-Traffic Alert** — ultra-short-range radar at **77–81 GHz** UWB.

Operating band: predominantly the **76–81 GHz mmWave band**, allocated by ITU for vehicular radar globally. *(1 mark for each correct application with band, plus concluding sentence.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]`
**State any three limitations of radar when used as a perception sensor in autonomous vehicles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Poor elevation (z-axis) resolution** — radar cannot reliably distinguish overhead objects (bridges, traffic signs) from ground-level obstacles. *(1 mark)*
2. **Inability to classify objects** — traditional radar cannot differentiate a pedestrian from a lamp post without ML-based 4D radar. *(1 mark)*
3. **Multipath and ghost targets** in urban canyons produce false positives, degrading detection reliability. *(1 mark)*

*(Alternative: vulnerability to radar-absorbing materials, narrow angular resolution in 2D arrays, regulatory power limits.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Q3. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q(ii)]`

**Question A (14 marks):**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **FMCW automotive radar** for vehicle applications. (7 marks)
**(b)** Derive the **radar range equation** and compute the maximum detectable range for the following specifications: $P_t = 10$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 20$ dBi, $\lambda = 3.9$ mm, $\sigma = 10$ m² (typical car RCS), $L = 6$ dB, minimum received power $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q3-A

**(a) Working Principle of FMCW Radar (7 marks):**
- **Block diagram:** (1 mark) — Use Fig. 4.1 above; reference the Tx chirp generator, PA, Tx/Rx antennas, mixer, IF amplifier, ADC, DSP, and Range-Doppler-FFT processor.
- **Chirp generation:** A VCO generates a linear FM chirp centered at **77 GHz**, sweeping across bandwidth $B$ in time $T_{chirp}$. (1 mark)
- **Transmission and echo:** Tx antenna radiates the chirp; targets reflect a fraction determined by the **radar cross-section $\sigma$**; Rx antenna captures the delayed echo. (1 mark)
- **Mixing and beat frequency:** The mixer multiplies Rx with a delayed copy of Tx, producing an **IF beat signal** of frequency $f_b = (2 R B)/(c T_{chirp})$. (2 marks)
- **FFT processing:** Fast-time FFT extracts range; slow-time FFT across consecutive chirps extracts **Doppler velocity**. AoA is obtained from phase differences across MIMO Rx channels. (1 mark)
- **Output:** Range–Doppler–angle tuple per detected object, fed to ADAS/ECU. (1 mark)

**(b) Range Equation Derivation and Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the monostatic radar range equation.**

$$
P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4 \, L}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_{max}$ by setting $P_r = P_{r,min}$.**

$$
R_{max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,min} \, L} \right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert all quantities to linear (watts and meters).**
- $P_t = 10$ dBm $= 10^{-2}$ W
- $G_t = G_r = 20$ dBi $= 100$ (linear)
- $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm $= 10^{-12}$ W
- $L = 6$ dB $\approx 3.98$ (linear)
- $\lambda = 3.9 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\sigma = 10$ m²

**Step 4 — Substitute into the numerator.**

$$
P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma = (10^{-2}) \cdot (100) \cdot (100) \cdot (3.9 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 10
$$

$$
= 10^{-2} \cdot 10^4 \cdot (15.21 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 10 = 1.521 \text{ W·m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,min} \, L = (4 \times 3.1416)^3 \cdot 10^{-12} \cdot 3.98
$$

$$
(4\pi)^3 = (12.566)^3 \approx 1984 \; ; \quad 1984 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.98 \approx 7.897 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Step 6 — Form the ratio and take the fourth root.**

$$
R_{max} = \left[ \dfrac{1.521}{7.897 \times 10^{-9}} \right]^{1/4} = \left[1.926 \times 10^{8}\right]^{1/4}
$$

$$
R_{max} \approx 117.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The radar can detect a typical passenger car up to **≈ 118 m**, consistent with mid-range automotive radar specs.

---

**Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** Explain the **Doppler principle** in an FMCW radar. A 77 GHz radar mounted on a vehicle moving at 20 m/s detects an oncoming car. The relative radial velocity is 35 m/s. Compute the **Doppler frequency shift** and the **beat frequency** if the target is at 60 m, sweep bandwidth $B = 600$ MHz, and sweep time $T = 1$ ms. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss in detail the **applications and limitations of radar in autonomous vehicles**, with reference to ADAS levels. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q3-B

**(a) Doppler Principle and Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Theory (3 marks):**
When a target has a radial velocity $v_r$ relative to the radar, the received echo experiences a frequency shift of:

$$
f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_c}{c}
$$

In an FMCW radar, the **total IF frequency** is the superposition of range and Doppler components:

$$
f_{IF} = f_b \pm f_d
$$

where the sign depends on whether the target is approaching (+) or receding (−). The positive sign corresponds to a target moving **toward** the radar in the standard convention.

**Computation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Doppler shift:**

$$
f_d = \dfrac{2 \times 35 \times 77 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{5.39 \times 10^{12}}{3 \times 10^8} = 17{,}966.7 \text{ Hz} \approx 17.97 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Beat frequency from range:**

$$
f_b = \dfrac{2 R B}{c T_{chirp}} = \dfrac{2 \times 60 \times 600 \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^8 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
f_b = \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^5} = 2.4 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 240 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Resulting IF frequencies (approaching target):**

$$
f_{IF,1} = f_b + f_d = 240{,}000 + 17{,}966.7 \approx 257.97 \text{ kHz}
$$

$$
f_{IF,2} = f_b - f_d = 240{,}000 - 17{,}966.7 \approx 222.03 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The oncoming vehicle produces two distinct IF tones 35.94 kHz apart, both derivable from the single received echo, confirming simultaneous range-Doppler measurement.

---

**(b) Applications and Limitations (7 marks):**

**Applications (4 marks):**

| ADAS Level | Application | Radar Role |
|:----------:|:-----------:|:----------:|
| L1 | AEB, FCW, ACC | Forward LRR provides longitudinal control |
| L2 | BSD, LCA, CTA | Corner SRR covers blind spots and rear cross-traffic |
| L3 | Highway Pilot, Traffic Jam Pilot | 6–8 radar 360° surround sensing |
| L4 | Robotaxi (highway, geo-fenced urban) | Multi-radar fusion with LiDAR/cameras |
| L5 | Full automation | Radar remains a *redundant safety* sensor |

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Poor elevation resolution** — cannot differentiate overhead vs. ground-level obstacles.
2. **Classification weakness** — needs 4D radar + ML for object categorization.
3. **Multipath ghosting** in urban canyons and tunnels leads to false positives.
4. **Radial-only velocity** — tangential motion is invisible.
5. **Radar-absorbing materials** can make certain targets (e.g., composite-bodied EVs) harder to detect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three primary outputs of automotive radar:** Range $R$, radial velocity $v_r$, azimuth angle $\theta$ — collectively the *R–V–A tuple*.
- **Operating bands:** 24 GHz (legacy, parking); 76–77 GHz (LRR); 77–81 GHz UWB (SRR/MRR).
- **Range equation:** $R = (c t_d)/2$ — the factor of **2** is non-negotiable.
- **Doppler shift:** $f_d = 2 v_r f_c / c$ — two-way, hence the factor 2.
- **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = c / (2B)$ — wider bandwidth gives finer resolution; **bandwidth, not frequency**, is the lever.
- **Velocity resolution:** $\Delta v = \lambda / (2 N T_{PRI})$ — more coherent chirps and faster PRF improve it.
- **Maximum unambiguous range:** $R_{max} = c T_{PRI} / 2$.
- **Maximum unambiguous velocity:** $v_{max} = \lambda / (4 T_{PRI})$ — the classic range–velocity ambiguity trade-off.
- **Monostatic radar equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4 L]$ — fourth-power falloff with range.
- **FMCW advantages:** simultaneous range + velocity, low peak power, mature silicon (SiGe BiCMOS, RFCMOS).
- **Key applications:** ACC, AEB, BSD, LCA, CTA, parking assist, L3+ highway pilot, robotaxi 360° perception.
- **Key limitations:** poor elevation resolution, weak object classification, tangential-velocity blindness, multipath ghosts, RCS variability, regulatory power caps.
- **Physical constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $\lambda_{77\,GHz} \approx 3.9$ mm; $B_{max} = 4$ GHz → $\Delta R \approx 3.75$ cm.
- **Examiner trigger words:** *bandwidth*, *chirp slope*, *range–Doppler ambiguity*, *RCS*, *4D radar*, *MIMO*, *CFAR* — at least one appears in every Module 2 long-answer question.
- **Evolution note:** Conventional 3D radar (R, V, $\theta$) is being superseded by **4D radar** adding **elevation** and **point-cloud density** (e.g., Arbe, Continental ARS 540, Mobileye imaging radar).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Lidar Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Lidar Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Fundamentals of Lidar Technology — Module 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** is a remote-sensing active optical measurement technique that determines the distance (range) to a target by illuminating it with pulsed laser light and measuring the time delay, phase shift, or returned photon count of the reflected signal. In the context of Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs), LiDAR serves as a high-resolution **3D environmental perception sensor** that generates a dense *point cloud* representing the geometry of the surrounding world.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** LiDAR is classified as an *active sensor* because it emits its own illumination source (laser) rather than depending on ambient light (as cameras do). The 2024 scheme explicitly identifies it as a primary sensor for SLAM, obstacle detection, and dynamic mapping in AGV/AV stacks.

### 1.2 Operational Wavelengths
LiDAR systems for autonomous vehicles primarily operate in the **Near-Infrared (NIR)** band:
- **905 nm** — Silicon-based detectors, cheaper, eye-safety limit ~Class 1 at low power.
- **1550 nm** — InGaAs detectors, higher eye-safety threshold, longer range, preferred for highway AVs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eye Safety Standard:** IEC 60825-1 Class 1 — LiDAR must be "eye-safe under all reasonably foreseeable conditions." The 1550 nm wavelength allows up to **10× higher peak power** than 905 nm because the human cornea and aqueous humor do not transmit light beyond ~1400 nm to the retina.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **blindfolded person standing in a dark room holding a laser pointer and a stopwatch**. Every second, they flash the laser in one direction. When the beam hits a wall and bounces back into their hand, the stopwatch has recorded a travel time of, say, 10 nanoseconds. Since light travels at roughly **0.3 m/ns**, the wall is **1.5 m away**. Now imagine doing this **hundreds of thousands of times per second**, sweeping the laser in all directions, and recording each echo. That dense map of "distance points" is exactly what a LiDAR produces — a 3D point cloud of the environment.

This is the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle, analogous to a bat's echolocation but using photons (light) instead of sound waves.

### 1.4 Primary Performance Metrics
| Metric | Typical Value (Automotive) | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 100 m – 250 m | Highway braking distance |
| Angular Resolution | 0.1° – 0.4° | Object separation at distance |
| Field of View (FoV) | 120° (H) × 25° (V) | Coverage per scan |
| Point Rate | 1–10 million points/sec | Density of point cloud |
| Frame Rate | 10–20 Hz | Dynamic object tracking |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles of LiDAR

LiDAR systems measure distance using one of three physical principles. Each has distinct engineering trade-offs.

### 2.1.1 Pulsed Time-of-Flight (Pulsed ToF) — *Most common in AVs*
The laser emits a narrow pulse of duration **τ (typically 1–10 ns)**. The receiver detects the returning echo. The range is calculated as:

**Step 1:** Photon leaves the laser at $t = 0$.

**Step 2:** Photon travels distance $R$ to target and back, distance $2R$.

**Step 3:** Photon returns at $t = t_r$ where $t_r = \frac{2R}{c}$.

**Step 4:** Range is recovered as:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t_r}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Range resolution is limited by pulse width:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}
$$

For a 1 ns pulse: $\Delta R = 0.15$ m. For a 100 ps pulse: $\Delta R = 0.015$ m.

### 2.1.2 Continuous Wave / Frequency Modulated (FMCW LiDAR)
The laser emits a chirped sinusoid whose frequency linearly ramps. The returning echo has a frequency offset $\Delta f$ proportional to range. Range is recovered by mixing the return with a reference copy of the transmitted signal and measuring the beat frequency:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 \cdot \frac{df}{dt}}
$$

where $\frac{df}{dt}$ is the chirp rate (Hz/s). Range resolution:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 \cdot B}
$$

where $B$ is the total chirp bandwidth. FMCW also directly yields **instantaneous velocity** via the Doppler shift, making it highly attractive for autonomous vehicles that need both distance and relative speed.

### 2.1.3 Phase-Shift Detection (CW Phase Comparison)
The laser emits a continuous wave of fixed frequency. The phase difference $\Delta\phi$ between transmitted and received signals is measured. Range:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}
$$

Limited to unambiguous range of $\frac{c}{2f_{mod}}$, suitable for short-range industrial AGVs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Why' and 'How' explanation:** Pulsed ToF is preferred for long range (>100 m) because each pulse is short and energy-packed, so it survives the round-trip attenuation. FMCW is gaining traction because it can co-exist with other LiDARs (coherent detection) and offers velocity vector in a single measurement.

## 2.2 The LiDAR Range Equation (Power Budget)

The **received optical power** $P_r$ from a target at range $R$ with reflectivity $\rho$ and cross-sectional area $A_t$ is given by the standard LiDAR radar-style equation:

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} \cdot \frac{A_r}{R^2} \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_r \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot A_t}{\pi \cdot R^2} \cdot T_{atm}^2
$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = transmitted peak power (W)
- $\tau$ = pulse width (s)
- $A_r$ = receiver aperture area (m²)
- $\eta_t, \eta_r$ = transmitter/receiver optical efficiency
- $T_{atm}$ = one-way atmospheric transmittance
- $\rho$ = target reflectivity (Lambertian approximation)
- $A_t$ = target cross-section (m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $R^4$ matters:** Notice the $R^2$ in the beam spreading term and the $R^2$ in the return spreading term — the **inverse fourth power law** is fundamental to LiDAR. Doubling the range reduces the received signal power by a factor of 16. This is why long-range automotive LiDARs require either 1550 nm wavelength (lower atmospheric attenuation, higher eye-safety allowance) or large receiver apertures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \, t_r}{2}$ | Pulsed ToF range from round-trip time | m, ns, m/s |
| 2 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | Range resolution from pulse width | m, ns |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 \cdot \frac{df}{dt}}$ | FMCW range from beat frequency | m, Hz, Hz/s |
| 4 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | FMCW range resolution | m, Hz |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{c \, \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ | Phase-shift CW range | m, Hz, rad |
| 6 | $P_r \propto \dfrac{P_t \tau A_r \rho A_t}{R^4}$ | Received power (R⁴ law) | W |
| 7 | $v_r = \dfrac{f_D \, \lambda}{2}$ | Radial velocity from Doppler | m/s, Hz, m |
| 8 | $d_{min} = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | Minimum separable target distance | m |
| 9 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_r}{\sqrt{2 e P_b B}}$ | Photon-limited SNR (Poisson stats) | dB |
| 10 | $\theta_{div} = \dfrac{4\lambda}{\pi D}$ | Beam divergence (diffraction) | rad, m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Critical Note:** All absolute values and brackets in equations above use `\vert` and `\mid` LaTeX syntax (or the formulas as written) to preserve the markdown table integrity. Do not embed raw `|` characters inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **automotive and AGV domain**, the LiDAR range equation directly drives design choices:

- **Highway AV (120 km/h operation)** → requires $R > 200$ m → mandates 1550 nm FMCW or 905 nm with high pulse energy.
- **Warehouse AGV (low speed)** → $R > 30$ m suffices → 2D scanning LiDAR (e.g., SICK NAV series) is cost-effective.
- **Last-mile delivery robot** → $R > 15$ m → solid-state flash LiDAR preferred for compactness.
- **Mining/Off-road AV** → ruggedized 1550 nm with dust/fog penetration algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pulsed Time-of-Flight Range Equation

**Given:** A laser pulse leaves the LiDAR at $t_0 = 0$ s, travels to a target at distance $R$ (m), reflects, and returns to the detector at $t = t_r$ (s). The medium is air, with speed of light $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**To Derive:** An expression for $R$ in terms of $t_r$.

**Step 1 — Total optical path length:**
The photon traverses the distance from source to target (call it $R$) and then from target back to source (again $R$). Therefore, the total distance traveled is:

$$
L_{total} = R + R = 2R
$$

**Step 2 — Kinematic relationship:**
For light traveling at constant speed $c$:

$$
c = \frac{L_{total}}{t_r}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$
c = \frac{2R}{t_r}
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic isolation of $R$:**

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t_r}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the value of $c$:**

$$
R = \frac{2.998 \times 10^8 \cdot t_r}{2} = 1.499 \times 10^8 \cdot t_r
$$

**Step 6 — Practical example:** If $t_r = 667$ ns, then:

$$
R = 1.499 \times 10^8 \times 667 \times 10^{-9} \approx 100 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The factor of **2** in the denominator is the single most-forgotten element in KTU board exams. Always remember: light makes a *round trip*; the measured time is *twice* the one-way travel.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — FMCW Beat Frequency Relation

**Given:** A linear chirp sweeps frequency from $f_0$ to $f_0 + B$ over a sweep duration $T_s$. The chirp rate is $\alpha = \frac{B}{T_s} = \frac{df}{dt}$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous frequency of transmitted chirp:**

$$
f_t(t) = f_0 + \alpha \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency of returned echo delayed by $t_r$:**

$$
f_r(t) = f_0 + \alpha \cdot (t - t_r)
$$

**Step 3 — Beat frequency** is the difference (mixer output):

$$
f_b = f_t(t) - f_r(t) = \alpha \cdot t_r = \frac{B}{T_s} \cdot t_r
$$

**Step 4 — Replace $t_r = \frac{2R}{c}$:**

$$
f_b = \frac{B}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2R}{c}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R$:**

$$
R = \frac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T_s}{2B}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\alpha = \frac{B}{T_s}$ to match the standard textbook form:**

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot f_b}{2 \alpha} = \frac{c \cdot f_b}{2 \cdot \frac{df}{dt}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because the return also carries a Doppler shift $f_D = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$, an FMCW LiDAR can simultaneously determine **range and radial velocity** in a single measurement — a capability pulsed ToF LiDARs lack without multiple pulses.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Range Resolution of FMCW

**Step 1 — Range resolution requires distinguishing two targets at ranges $R_1$ and $R_2 = R_1 + \Delta R$.**

**Step 2 — Their beat frequencies are $f_{b1}$ and $f_{b2}$. The minimum distinguishable beat frequency equals the inverse of the sweep duration:**

$$
\Delta f_b = \frac{1}{T_s}
$$

(This is the Fourier transform limit for a chirp of length $T_s$.)

**Step 3 — From the range-beat relation:**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f_b}{2 \alpha} = \frac{c}{2 \alpha T_s} = \frac{c}{2B}
$$

since $B = \alpha T_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result:** FMCW range resolution depends only on **chirp bandwidth $B$**, not on pulse width. A 1 GHz bandwidth yields $\Delta R = 0.15$ m, equal to a 1 ns pulsed system but with the added benefit of velocity measurement.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of LiDAR Principles

| Parameter | Pulsed ToF | FMCW | Phase-Shift CW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | Up to 250 m | Up to 300 m | < 50 m |
| Velocity measurement | No (multi-pulse) | Yes (Doppler) | No |
| Range resolution | Limited by $\tau$ | Limited by $B$ | Limited by SNR |
| Eye safety (1550 nm) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Complexity | Medium | High | Low |
| Co-channel interference | High | Low (coherent) | High |
| Preferred AV use | Mass-market L2/L3 | Premium L4 | Indoor AGV |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a LiDAR System

The diagram below maps the **signal flow** from laser emission to point cloud output. It is the canonical architecture examiners expect for a Module 2 short-note question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Timing and Control Unit FPGA or MCU] --> B1[Laser Driver]
    A1 --> C1[Beam Steering Module]
    B1 --> D1[Laser Diode Source 905nm or 1550nm]
    D1 --> E1[Transmit Optics Collimator]
    E1 --> F1[Beam Steering MEMS Galvo or OPA]
    F1 --> G1[Scanning Field of View]
    G1 --> H1[Target Object Reflects Beam]
    H1 --> I1[Receive Optics Aperture]
    I1 --> J1[Narrowband Optical Filter]
    J1 --> K1[Photodetector APD or SPAD]
    K1 --> L1[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    L1 --> M1[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    A1 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Signal Processor DSP or SoC]
    N1 --> O1[Point Cloud Generation XYZ intensity timestamp]
    A1 --> P1[Vehicle Interface CAN or Ethernet]
    O1 --> P1
    A1 --> Q1[INS and GNSS Sync]
    Q1 --> O1
    A1 --> R1[Eye Safety Monitor IEC 60825]
    R1 --> B1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **FPGA/MCU** acts as the central nervous system, synchronizing the laser pulse, beam steering, and TDC.
- The **TDC** (Time-to-Digital Converter) is the heart of pulsed ToF — it timestamps the return with picosecond resolution.
- The **INS/GNSS** block is mandatory for automotive LiDAR because each point cloud frame must be georeferenced to the vehicle's pose.
- The **Eye Safety Monitor** is a regulatory hard-interlock: it must shut down the laser if the scan fails or an object enters the *NOHD* (Nominal Ocular Hazard Distance).

## 4.2 Operating Principle Flow (Pulsed Time of Flight)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Emit Laser Pulse t=0] --> S2[Step 2 Pulse Travels R to Target]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Photons Scatter off Target]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Return Pulse Travels R back]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Detector Catches Echo at t=tr]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 TDC Measures tr]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Compute Range R equals c times tr divided by 2]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Append Azimuth Elevation from Scanner]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Emit Point XYZI to Point Cloud Buffer]
```

## 4.3 Scanning Mechanism Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SCAN[LiDAR Scanning Mechanisms] --> M1[Mechanical Spinning 360 deg]
    SCAN --> M2[MEMS Mirror 2D small aperture]
    SCAN --> M3[Optical Phased Array OPA solid state]
    SCAN --> M4[Flash LiDAR No Scanner Full FoV flood]
    SCAN --> M5[Risley Prisms Pair Rotating Wedges]
    M1 --> M1A[Example Velodyne HDL 64E]
    M2 --> M2A[Example Innoviz One]
    M3 --> M3A[Example Quanergy S3]
    M4 --> M4A[Example Ouster ES2]
    M5 --> M5A[Example Neptec Design]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note (Module 2):** When asked to "explain scanning mechanisms" or "classify LiDAR," present this taxonomy. The 2024 scheme emphasizes the shift from **mechanical** to **solid-state** architectures due to automotive-grade reliability (MTBF) and cost-down requirements for mass deployment.

## 4.4 Sensor Fusion Context (LiDAR in AV Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CAM[Camera RGB] --> FUS[Central Fusion ECU]
    RAD[Millimeter Wave Radar 77 GHz] --> FUS
    LID[LiDAR 3D Point Cloud] --> FUS
    ULTR[Ultrasonic Short Range] --> FUS
    IMU[IMU 6DoF] --> FUS
    GPS[GNSS Receiver] --> FUS
    FUS --> PER[Perception Layer Object Detection Tracking Classification]
    PER --> LOC[Localization Layer HD Map Matching]
    LOC --> PLAN[Planning Layer Motion Prediction Trajectory]
    PLAN --> CTRL[Control Layer Steering Throttle Brake]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The 2024 Module 2 explicitly positions LiDAR within the **multi-sensor fusion** paradigm. A pure-LiDAR autonomous stack is infeasible — camera provides color/semantic data, radar provides velocity in adverse weather, and LiDAR provides geometric precision. KTU questions frequently test this complementarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define LiDAR. List any four key specifications of an automotive-grade LiDAR sensor.**

**Model Answer:**
LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is an active remote sensing technique that measures distance to a target by emitting laser pulses and analysing the returned signal (time-of-flight, phase shift, or frequency shift). It generates a 3D point cloud of the environment for AV perception.

Four key specifications:
1. **Maximum range** — typically 100–250 m for highway use.
2. **Angular resolution** — 0.1° to 0.4° horizontal.
3. **Field of View (FoV)** — 120° H × 25° V typical.
4. **Point cloud density** — 1 to 10 million points per second.
5. **Eye safety class** — IEC 60825-1 Class 1.

*(Any four correctly explained specifications fetch full marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between ToF and FMCW LiDAR in terms of operating principle and velocity measurement capability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pulsed ToF | FMCW |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Measures round-trip time of a short laser pulse | Measures beat frequency of linearly chirped CW laser |
| Range equation | $R = \frac{c \cdot t_r}{2}$ | $R = \frac{c \cdot f_b}{2\alpha}$ |
| Velocity measurement | Requires multiple pulses (no direct Doppler) | Direct via Doppler shift $f_D = \frac{2v_r}{\lambda}$ |
| Range resolution | $\Delta R = \frac{c\tau}{2}$ | $\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B}$ |
| Interference rejection | Low (incoherent) | High (coherent) |

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the LiDAR range equation for a pulsed time-of-flight system and explain the inverse-fourth-power law for received power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the functional architecture of an automotive LiDAR sensor. Explain the role of TDC and INS/GNSS synchronisation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Range from time of flight:** As derived in Section 3.1, the total path traversed by the photon is $2R$ at speed $c$ in time $t_r$, giving:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t_r}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Pulse energy transmitted:** A pulse of duration $\tau$ and peak power $P_t$ carries energy $E_t = P_t \cdot \tau$.

**Step 3 — Beam spreading at the target:** The transmitted beam diverges with solid angle $\Omega_t$. At range $R$, the illuminated area is $\Omega_t \cdot R^2$. The fraction captured by a target of area $A_t$ is $\frac{A_t}{\Omega_t R^2}$.

**Step 4 — Reflection:** With target reflectivity $\rho$, the reflected power is $P_t \tau \cdot \frac{\rho A_t}{\Omega_t R^2}$ (radiated isotropically as Lambertian approximation).

**Step 5 — Return spreading:** The receiver of aperture $A_r$ at distance $R$ intercepts a fraction $\frac{A_r}{4\pi R^2}$ of the reflected radiation (Lambertian). Combined with the one-way atmospheric transmittance $T_{atm}$ for both directions:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot \tau \cdot \rho \cdot A_t \cdot A_r \cdot T_{atm}^2 \cdot \eta_t \eta_r}{\Omega_t \cdot 4\pi R^4}
$$

**Step 6 — Inverse fourth power law:** The $R^4$ dependence shows that doubling the range reduces the received power by $2^4 = 16$ times. This is the **fundamental physical limit** that drives all LiDAR engineering choices (wavelength, aperture size, pulse energy).

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

The block diagram is as given in **Section 4.1**. Key explanations:

**Role of TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter):**
- The TDC is a high-precision timer that records the time interval between the outgoing laser pulse trigger and the incoming detector signal.
- Modern TDCs achieve **picosecond resolution** (e.g., 10 ps → 1.5 mm range bin).
- It works in tandem with the FPGA control unit to ensure deterministic timing.
- Multiple-stop TDCs allow simultaneous recording of multiple echoes (useful for seeing through semi-transparent obstacles like rain or foliage).

**Role of INS/GNSS Synchronisation:**
- Each LiDAR point is collected at a specific timestamp and a specific vehicle pose.
- The INS (Inertial Navigation System, comprising IMU gyroscopes and accelerometers) provides **roll, pitch, yaw** at high frequency (200 Hz).
- The GNSS provides **global position and heading** at lower frequency (10 Hz).
- Together, they allow **motion compensation**: correcting the point cloud for vehicle movement during one scan frame, otherwise fast-moving AVs produce smeared point clouds.
- Georeferencing also enables **HD map matching** for localization.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the FMCW LiDAR principle. Derive the expression for range resolution in terms of chirp bandwidth $B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare mechanical, MEMS, and flash LiDAR scanning mechanisms. State two applications of solid-state LiDAR in autonomous vehicles. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**FMCW Principle (explanation):**
A frequency-modulated continuous wave (FMCW) LiDAR emits a sinusoidal laser whose frequency is linearly swept (chirped) over a band of width $B$. The transmitted and received signals are mixed in a photodetector, producing a beat frequency proportional to the target's range. Because the return is **coherent** (preserves phase), the system can also extract Doppler shift for velocity.

**Derivation of Range Resolution:** As shown in **Section 3.3**, the range resolution of an FMCW LiDAR is:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B}
$$

The minimum resolvable beat frequency equals $\frac{1}{T_s}$ (Fourier limit), and substituting $B = \alpha T_s$ yields the result.

**Example:** For $B = 1$ GHz, $\Delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.15$ m. For $B = 5$ GHz, $\Delta R = 0.03$ m (3 cm resolution).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Mechanical Spinning | MEMS Mirror | Flash LiDAR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scanning element | Rotating motor with multiple lasers | Tiny silicon mirror oscillating on 2 axes | No scanner — flood illumination |
| FoV | 360° H × 26° V | ~120° H × 25° V | ~90° × 30° |
| Reliability | Lower (moving parts) | Medium | High (solid state) |
| Cost | High | Medium | Falling |
| Point density | High (multi-beam) | Medium | Low at far range |
| Vehicle use | Robo-taxi prototypes | Production L2/L3 AVs | Short-range / corner LiDAR |

**Two Applications of Solid-State LiDAR in AVs:**
1. **Forward long-range sensing** (e.g., Innoviz One, Luminar Iris) — MEMS-based, mounted behind windshield for highway driving.
2. **Corner / short-range LiDAR** (e.g., Continental, Valeo SCALA 2) — flash LiDAR, used for blind-spot monitoring, lane-change assist, and parking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ToF range equation — happens in ~40% of scripts. Always write the full derivation: $c = \frac{2R}{t_r} \Rightarrow R = \frac{c \cdot t_r}{2}$.
> 2. **Mixing FMCW and ToF formulas** — some students write $R = \frac{c \cdot f_b}{2\alpha}$ for a pulsed system. Distinguish them clearly.
> 3. **Confusing angular resolution with range resolution** — angular resolution is in degrees/radians and comes from the beam divergence; range resolution is in metres and comes from pulse width or chirp bandwidth.
> 4. **Omitting INS/GNSS in the block diagram** — examiners specifically look for the georeferencing block in automotive LiDAR diagrams.
> 5. **Writing 'LiDAR uses radar waves'** — LiDAR uses **light** (laser), not radio waves. Radar uses radio waves. This is a guaranteed zero if confused.
> 6. **Not mentioning eye safety** — for any LiDAR question worth ≥ 7 marks, the IEC 60825-1 Class 1 mention is compulsory in the KTU 2024 valuation key.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Fundamentals of Lidar Technology**

- **LiDAR = Light Detection and Ranging** — an *active* optical sensor emitting its own laser illumination.
- Three core principles: **Pulsed ToF, FMCW, Phase-Shift CW**.
- **Master Formula (Pulsed):** $R = \dfrac{c \cdot t_r}{2}$ — remember the *factor of 2* for round trip.
- **Range Resolution (Pulsed):** $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ — smaller pulse = finer resolution.
- **FMCW Range:** $R = \dfrac{c \cdot f_b}{2\alpha}$ where $\alpha = df/dt$ is the chirp rate.
- **FMCW Resolution:** $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ — depends only on chirp bandwidth.
- **FMCW advantage:** simultaneous range + velocity (Doppler $f_D = \dfrac{2v_r}{\lambda}$).
- **Inverse-fourth-power law** — $P_r \propto \dfrac{1}{R^4}$ — fundamental physical limit of LiDAR.
- **Wavelengths:** 905 nm (silicon, cheaper) vs 1550 nm (InGaAs, longer range, higher eye-safety).
- **Eye safety:** IEC 60825-1 **Class 1** mandatory for AVs; 1550 nm allows higher peak power.
- **Key components:** Laser source → Beam steering → Receive optics → Photodetector (APD/SPAD) → TDC → DSP → Point cloud.
- **Scanning taxonomy:** Mechanical 360° | MEMS mirror | OPA | Flash (solid-state) | Risley prisms.
- **Automotive trend:** shift from mechanical to **solid-state** for reliability and cost.
- **Block diagram must include:** Laser, Beam steering, TDC, INS/GNSS synchronisation, Eye-safety monitor, Vehicle interface (CAN/Ethernet).
- **LiDAR is part of sensor fusion** — camera (semantic), radar (velocity in fog), LiDAR (geometry), ultrasonic (parking).
- **NoHD** — Nominal Ocular Hazard Distance must be respected for Class 1 certification.
- **Point cloud** = $(x, y, z, I, t)$ tuple per laser shot; $I$ is intensity, $t$ is timestamp.
- **Common pitfall:** LiDAR ≠ Radar. Light vs Radio waves. Different physics, different equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lidar Applications in 3D Mapping and Obstacle Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR)** is a remote-sensing, active optical measurement technique that determines the distance to a target (or the spatial distribution of a scene) by illuminating it with a pulsed or modulated laser beam and analysing the characteristics of the back-scattered radiation. In the context of **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** and **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**, LiDAR serves as a primary 3D environmental perception sensor, producing a dense georeferenced **point cloud** that is consumed by perception, mapping, localisation, and obstacle-avoidance stacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"LiDAR is an active time-of-flight sensor that emits eye-safe laser pulses (typically at **905 nm** or **1550 nm** wavelengths) and converts the round-trip time of each photon echo into a high-resolution 3D point cloud, enabling real-time mapping, localisation, and obstacle detection in autonomous ground vehicles."*

A LiDAR system simultaneously measures, for every emitted laser pulse, the following four quantities:
1. **Range** $R$ — distance from sensor to reflecting surface.
2. **Intensity** $I$ — strength of the returned optical power (depends on reflectance $\rho$ and incidence angle).
3. **Azimuth angle** $\theta$ — horizontal scan angle.
4. **Elevation angle** $\phi$ — vertical scan angle.

These four values, when stacked across time, form the raw **point cloud** $\mathcal{P} = \{(x_i, y_i, z_i, I_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Bat with a Stopwatch"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are standing in a pitch-dark room, blindfolded, holding a laser pointer and a very precise stopwatch. You flash the laser at a wall, and the moment you see the dot, you stop the clock. If you know the speed of light (**$c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s**), the half of the recorded time directly tells you how far the wall is. A LiDAR does exactly this — but it does it **millions of times per second**, sweeping the laser across a full 360° field of view. The result is a "3D photograph" of the world made of millions of laser dots.

## 1.3 Why LiDAR is Indispensable for AGVs / AVs

| Property | Value / Characteristic | Engineering Significance |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Range accuracy** | $\pm$ **2 to 5 cm** | Sub-decimetre localisation for lane-level path tracking |
| **Angular resolution** | **0.05° to 0.4°** horizontal | Detects thin objects (poles, sign posts, pedestrians' limbs) |
| **Frame rate** | **5 to 25 Hz** typical, **100+ Hz** research grade | Real-time obstacle tracking at vehicle speeds up to 120 km/h |
| **Wavelengths** | **905 nm** (Si detectors, cheaper) / **1550 nm** (eye-safe at high power) | Trade-off between cost and permissible transmitted power |
| **Eye-safety class** | **Class 1** (IEC 60825-1) | Safe for deployment in populated environments |
| **Operating range** | **0.1 m to 250 m** | Covers both indoor AGV (warehouses) and outdoor AV (highways) |
| **Independence from ambient light** | Active illumination | Works in tunnels, night, glare — unlike cameras |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook — The 3-LiDAR-Rule:** A LiDAR provides **Range**, **Reflectance** (intensity), and **Reflectivity geometry** (3D shape). These three together are what enable both mapping and obstacle detection in a single sensor modality.
