## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define sensor fusion. List any four benefits of sensor fusion in autonomous vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Sensor fusion is the intelligent combination of data from multiple heterogeneous sensors to produce a unified, more accurate, and more reliable estimate of the vehicle's state and surroundings than is possible with any single sensor.

**Four Benefits (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Enhanced Accuracy** — reduces measurement error via inverse-variance weighting.
2. **Improved Reliability** — provides redundancy against sensor failure.
3. **Extended Coverage** — overcomes individual field-of-view and range limitations.
4. **Robustness to Environment** — operates in fog, night, tunnels, and rain.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners allocate 0.5 marks per clearly-stated benefit. Vague answers like "it is better" score **zero**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between data-level, feature-level, and decision-level sensor fusion. Give one example application of each in AGVs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Level | Definition (0.5 marks) | Example (0.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data-Level** | Combines raw sensor signals before any feature extraction | Raw LiDAR + Camera fusion for depth estimation |
| **Feature-Level** | Extracts features (edges, corners, blobs) from each sensor, then fuses | Fusing SIFT visual features with LiDAR geometric features for object detection |
| **Decision-Level** | Each sensor makes an independent decision; the decisions are then combined | Voting between three classifiers (camera, radar, LiDAR) for pedestrian detection |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. The question typically splits as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six benefits of sensor fusion** in enhancing the accuracy and reliability of an autonomous vehicle. For each benefit, identify the underlying sensor limitation that fusion overcomes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two independent position sensors measure the location of an AGV. Sensor A reports $z_A = 10.2$ m with variance $\sigma_A^2 = 0.04$ m², and Sensor B reports $z_B = 9.9$ m with variance $\sigma_B^2 = 0.01$ m². Using **inverse-variance weighted fusion**, compute:
(i) the optimal weights $w_A$ and $w_B$,
(ii) the fused position estimate $\hat{x}_{fused}$,
(iii) the fused variance $\sigma_{fused}^2$,
(iv) verify that the fused variance is smaller than both $\sigma_A^2$ and $\sigma_B^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Six Benefits (7 marks — 1.0 mark for each + 1.0 mark for overview):**

1. **Enhanced Accuracy** — Solves the problem of single-sensor noise floors by combining independent measurements using inverse-variance weighting.
2. **Improved Reliability via Redundancy** — Solves single-point-of-failure risk by providing overlapping measurements.
3. **Extended Spatial–Temporal Coverage** — Solves limited FOV and range by combining wide-FOV cameras with long-range radar.
4. **Increased Confidence** — Solves the ambiguity of single-sensor classification by Bayesian multiplication of likelihoods.
5. **Environmental Robustness** — Solves weather/lighting failure by combining modalities with non-overlapping failure modes.
6. **Fault Detection and Isolation (FDI)** — Solves silent sensor faults by chi-square monitoring of innovation sequences.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Optimal Weights (2 marks):**

$$w_A = \frac{\sigma_B^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2} = \frac{0.01}{0.04 + 0.01} = \frac{0.01}{0.05} = 0.2$$

$$w_B = \frac{\sigma_A^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2} = \frac{0.04}{0.05} = 0.8$$

**(ii) Fused Estimate (2 marks):**

$$\hat{x}_{fused} = w_A z_A + w_B z_B = (0.2)(10.2) + (0.8)(9.9) = 2.04 + 7.92 = 9.96 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Fused Variance (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = \frac{\sigma_A^2 \sigma_B^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2} = \frac{(0.04)(0.01)}{0.05} = \frac{0.0004}{0.05} = 0.008 \text{ m}^2$$

**(iv) Verification (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = 0.008 \text{ m}^2 < \sigma_A^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2 \text{ AND } < \sigma_B^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2 \;\; \checkmark$$

Note: The fused estimate is **closer to Sensor B (9.9 m)** because Sensor B has a smaller variance (more accurate) and hence receives the larger weight (0.8).

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three hierarchical levels of sensor fusion** (data, feature, decision) in an AGV perception stack. Compare them in terms of information content, computational cost, and fault tolerance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Kalman filter** is used to fuse GPS and IMU measurements for an AGV. The state vector is position and velocity along a 1-D track. The state transition model is $x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k-1} + w_k$ with

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \Delta t^2 \\ \Delta t \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Delta t = 0.1 \text{ s}$$

The process noise covariance is $Q = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.1)$ and measurement noise covariance is $R = 0.5$. The prior state estimate is $\hat{x}_{0 \vert 0} = [0, 0]^T$ with covariance $P_{0 \vert 0} = I_2$. The IMU input is $u_0 = 1$ m/s². A GPS measurement $z_1 = 0.5$ m arrives with $H = [1, 0]$.
Compute the **(i) predicted state**, **(ii) innovation**, **(iii) Kalman gain**, and **(iv) updated state** at $k = 1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Levels of Sensor Fusion (7 marks — 2.0 marks per level + 1.0 mark for comparison):**

**Data-Level Fusion:** Combines raw signals at the lowest level. Highest information preservation, but requires precisely co-registered sensors. Example: pixel-level fusion of stereo camera + LiDAR intensity for dense depth maps.

**Feature-Level Fusion:** Each sensor first extracts features (edges from cameras, planes from LiDAR), then features are combined. Balanced information and computation. Example: BEV (Bird's-Eye-View) fusion of camera semantic features with LiDAR geometric features.

**Decision-Level Fusion:** Each sensor independently classifies or estimates; outputs are merged by voting or Bayesian methods. Most modular and fault-tolerant, but loses raw information. Example: three independent object detectors producing a consensus bounding box.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Criterion | Data | Feature | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| Info content | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Compute cost | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Fault tolerance | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

**(b) Kalman Filter Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Predicted State (2 marks):**

$$\hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} = A \hat{x}_{0 \vert 0} + B u_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Predicted covariance:

$$P_{1 \vert 0} = A P_{0 \vert 0} A^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} I \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 1.01 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(ii) Innovation (1 mark):**

$$\tilde{y}_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} = 0.5 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = 0.5 - 0.005 = 0.495 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Kalman Gain (2 marks):**

$$S_1 = H P_{1 \vert 0} H^T + R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.01 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 = 1.01 + 0.5 = 1.51$$

$$K_1 = P_{1 \vert 0} H^T S_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.01 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{1.51} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.669 \\ 0.0662 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(iv) Updated State (2 marks):**

$$\hat{x}_{1 \vert 1} = \hat{x}_{1 \vert 0} + K_1 \tilde{y}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.669 \\ 0.0662 \end{bmatrix} (0.495)$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.005 + 0.331 \\ 0.1 + 0.0328 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.336 \\ 0.133 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Fused Estimate:** Position = **0.336 m**, Velocity = **0.133 m/s**.

The fused position (0.336) lies **between the IMU-only prediction (0.005)** and the **GPS-only measurement (0.5)**, weighted by their respective covariances. This is the essence of Kalman fusion.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 1. **Averaging instead of inverse-variance weighting** — costs **3 marks** instantly. Always show the formula $\frac{1}{\sigma^2_{fused}} = \sum \frac{1}{\sigma^2_i}$.
> 2. **Confusing sensor fusion levels** — many students write "high-level fusion uses raw data." This is **the opposite**; high-level = decision-level = after each sensor has already decided.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if the final answer does not state units (e.g., "0.336" without "m" loses a mark).
> 4. **Forgetting the unbiasedness constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$** in derivations — this step is mandatory; omitting it loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing Kalman filter $Q$ (process noise) with $R$ (measurement noise)** — these are **different**; $Q$ models uncertainty in the motion model, $R$ models uncertainty in the sensor.
> 6. **Writing "sensor fusion improves accuracy" without proof** — examiners expect the formula derivation in Section 3.1. A qualitative answer alone scores at most **2 of 7 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this section the night before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Sensor fusion = combining multiple sensors for a better estimate.
- **Three Levels:** Data (raw), Feature (extracted), Decision (classified). Hybrid is most common in practice.
- **Benefit 1 — Accuracy:** Mathematically proven via $\frac{1}{\sigma_{fused}^2} = \sum \frac{1}{\sigma_i^2}$. Fused variance is always **strictly less** than any individual variance.
- **Benefit 2 — Reliability:** Achieved through N-modular redundancy (TMR = Triple Modular Redundancy).
- **Benefit 3 — Coverage:** Complementary FOV from cameras + LiDAR + radar gives 360° awareness.
- **Benefit 4 — Confidence:** Bayesian fusion of independent classifiers multiplies likelihoods, sharpening the posterior.
- **Benefit 5 — Environmental Robustness:** Camera + Radar + LiDAR covers fog, night, rain, tunnels, and sun glare.
- **Benefit 6 — Fault Detection:** Innovation sequence monitored via $\chi^2$ test; divergence flags faulty sensor.
- **Kalman Filter Equations to Memorize:** $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$, $\tilde{y}_k$, $K_k$, $P_{k \vert k}$. All five formulas are essential.
- **Complementary Filter Formula:** $\hat{x} = \alpha \cdot x_{low} + (1-\alpha) \cdot x_{high}$ — used for IMU + gyro fusion.
- **Bayesian Fusion Formula:** $P(C \vert z_1, z_2) = \frac{P(C \vert z_1) P(C \vert z_2)}{P(C \vert z_1) P(C \vert z_2) + (1 - P(C \vert z_1))(1 - P(C \vert z_2))}$.
- **Industry Examples:** Waymo (LiDAR+Camera+Radar), Tesla (Camera-only with BEV Transformer), Amazon Kiva (2D LiDAR + IMU + Wheel Odom).
- **MTBF Target:** Six Sigma = 99.99966% uptime for safety-critical AGV/AV systems.
- **Common Pitfall:** Do not confuse "fusion improves accuracy" with "fusion averages values" — the correct mechanism is **inverse-variance weighting**.
- **Exam Tip:** Always carry units in numerical answers; show all intermediate steps in derivations; memorize the boxed formulas in Section 2.2.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ROS (Robot Operating System)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ROS (Robot Operating System)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Robot Operating System (ROS)** is an open-source, meta-operating system (or middleware framework) designed specifically for heterogeneous robotic systems. It is not a true operating system in the classical sense (it runs *on top of* Linux/Ubuntu), but rather a collection of tools, libraries, drivers, conventions, and communication infrastructure that abstracts hardware and provides a unified software development platform for robot applications.

In the context of **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** and **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**, ROS serves as the *central nervous system* — coordinating perception, localization, path planning, and control subsystems through standardized message-passing interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ROS is covered in **Module 3** under the broad umbrella of software architectures for autonomous vehicles. The emphasis is on its role as a *distributed communication backbone* rather than as a generic robotics framework.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — ROS as a "Post Office for Robots"

Imagine an autonomous warehouse AGV as a **team of specialized workers** (a camera operator, a GPS reader, a steering controller, a map builder, a safety monitor) all stationed in different rooms. They cannot shout across rooms, but they can send and receive **letters** (messages) through a **central post office** (the ROS Master).

- Each worker is a **Node** (an independent executable program).
- The post office keeps a **registry** of every node's address.
- Workers send letters into labeled **mailboxes (Topics)** or request specific **courier deliveries (Services)**.
- A worker who cares about camera data simply **subscribes** to the `/camera/image` mailbox; the camera operator **publishes** to it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions (KTU Board-Examiner Favorites):**
> - **Node:** A process performing computation (e.g., one wheel encoder reader, one lidar filter).
> - **Master (roscore):** The name service and parameter server that enables nodes to find each other.
> - **Topic:** A named bus over which nodes exchange *streaming* messages (asynchronous, many-to-many).
> - **Service:** A synchronous request/reply interaction (one-to-one, blocking).
> - **Message:** Strictly typed data structure (defined in `.msg` files) flowing over topics.
> - **Package:** Atomic unit of ROS software — code, messages, configs bundled together.
> - **Workspace:** Directory (typically `catkin_ws/`) where you build and manage packages.

---

## 1.3 Why ROS for AGVs/AVs?

| AGV/AV Subsystem | ROS Component |
|---|---|
| LiDAR sensing | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` messages on `/scan` topic |
| Wheel odometry | `nav_msgs/Odometry` published at 50 Hz |
| Map building | `gmapping` or `cartographer` SLAM packages |
| Path planning | `move_base` with global & local planners |
| Visualization | `rviz` 3D viewer |
| Simulation | `gazebo` physics engine integration |
| Hardware abstraction | URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Memorize:** ROS typically operates with topic publish rates between **10 Hz** (slow sensors like GPS) to **100 Hz** (IMU) to **30 Hz** (cameras at standard frame rates).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ROS Computational Graph — The Heart of the System

The **ROS Computational Graph** is the peer-to-peer network of ROS processes that are processing data together. It comprises:

### 2.1.1 Nodes
A node is a single executable file within a ROS package. ROS uses a client-library-based design — nodes connect to other nodes through APIs in:
- **roscpp** (C++)
- **rospy** (Python)

Each node is registered with the Master under a unique name and advertises the topics/services it provides.

### 2.1.2 Master (`roscore`)
The Master is launched via the `roscore` command. It provides:
1. **Name registration** — lookup of named nodes.
2. **Parameter server** — central key-value store for configuration.
3. **XMLRPC server** — the wire protocol for inter-node communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without `roscore`, no ROS node can find another.** This is the #1 troubleshooting scenario in KTU lab viva questions.

### 2.1.3 Parameter Server
A shared, multi-variate dictionary accessible by all nodes. Typical parameters:
```yaml
wheel_diameter: 0.30   # meters
encoder_resolution: 4096  # ticks/revolution
max_velocity: 1.5      # m/s
```

### 2.1.4 Topics, Messages, and the Publisher–Subscriber Model
Communication is **anonymous, asynchronous, and many-to-many**. A publisher does not know which nodes (if any) subscribe; a subscriber does not know who publishes. Decoupling is the central design principle.

The underlying transport uses **TCPROS** (TCP) for reliability and **UDPROS** (UDP) for low-latency streaming (e.g., camera feeds).

### 2.1.5 Services
Services implement **synchronous remote procedure calls**. They use a `.srv` file with two parts separated by `---`:
```
int64 target_pose
---
bool success
string message
```

### 2.1.6 Bags (`rosbag`)
`.bag` files record and play back message streams — the **black box** of an autonomous vehicle for post-incident analysis.

---

## 2.2 The Filesystem Level

The on-disk organization layered above the computational graph:

| Directory | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Workspace** (`catkin_ws/`) | Top-level build folder |
| `src/` | Source space — holds packages |
| `build/` | Build space — intermediate CMake artifacts |
| `devel/` | Development space — generated setup scripts |
| `install/` | (Catkin-only) install space |
| **Package** | Atomic unit; contains `package.xml`, `CMakeLists.txt`, `src/`, `launch/` |
| **Metapackage** | A package that groups other packages (no code, only dependencies) |

---

## 2.3 ROS 1 vs ROS 2 — Critical Distinction for KTU Viva

| Feature | ROS 1 (Noetic) | ROS 2 (Foxy/Humble) |
|---|---|---|
| Middleware | Custom XMLRPC + TCPROS | DDS (Data Distribution Service) |
| Real-time support | None | Yes (with RT-PREEMPT kernel) |
| Master required | **Yes** | **No** (discovery via DDS) |
| Multi-robot | Possible but fragile | Native |
| Embedded support | Poor | Excellent (micro-ROS for MCUs) |
| Default OS | Ubuntu 20.04 | Ubuntu 22.04 / Windows / RTOS |

> [!NOTE]
> For **production autonomous vehicles**, ROS 2 is the de facto choice due to DDS-based Quality of Service (QoS) guarantees.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Standard Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Topic publish rate (typical) | $f_{pub} = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz; $T_s$ = sample period (s) |
| 2 | LiDAR angular resolution | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{beams}}$ | radians; $N_{beams}$ = ray count |
| 3 | Scan frequency | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{v_{rot}}{360^\circ}$ | Hz; $v_{rot}$ = rotation rate |
| 4 | Effective data rate (LiDAR) | $R_{data} = 2 \cdot f_{scan} \cdot N_{beams} \cdot n_{bytes}$ | bytes/s; $\times 2$ for range+intensity |
| 5 | Odometry update from encoder ticks | $d = \dfrac{2\pi r \cdot N_{ticks}}{N_{resolution}}$ | meters; $r$ = wheel radius |
| 6 | Message latency (TCP, ideal) | $\tau = \dfrac{L_{msg}}{C} + t_{queue}$ | s; $L$ = length, $C$ = capacity |
| 7 | Topic Bandwidth (avg) | $B = \bar{m}_s \cdot f_{pub}$ | bits/s; $\bar{m}_s$ = mean message size |
| 8 | Doppler-limit safe publish | $f_{pub} \le \dfrac{v_{max}}{2 \cdot \Delta s_{min}}$ | Hz; Nyquist criterion for control loop |

> Use `\vert` instead of `|` when absolute values appear in formulas — for example, a control error becomes $e(t) = \vert x_{ref}(t) - x_{act}(t) \vert$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) use ROS as the on-robot software stack.
- **Autonomous cars** (Apollo, Autoware) are built on ROS 2 with DDS.
- **Research platforms** (TurtleBot, Husky, Jackal) ship with pre-configured ROS packages.
- **Drone swarms** rely on ROS 2 for decentralized multi-agent coordination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Topic Publish Rate for Vehicle Control

A common KTU numerical problem asks: *Given a maximum AGV speed and a minimum safe sampling distance, find the minimum publish rate for the wheel odometry node.*

### Problem Setup
- Maximum AGV velocity: $v_{max} = 1.5$ m/s
- Minimum spatial resolution required by downstream planner: $\Delta s_{min} = 0.05$ m (5 cm)
- We must sample at least once per $\Delta s_{min}$ to satisfy the Nyquist–Shannon-like criterion.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Time taken to traverse $\Delta s_{min}$ at top speed.

$$
T_s = \frac{\Delta s_{min}}{v_{max}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numbers.

$$
T_s = \frac{0.05 \text{ m}}{1.5 \text{ m/s}} = 0.0333\text{ s} = 33.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** The minimum publish rate is the reciprocal.

$$
f_{pub,min} = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{v_{max}}{\Delta s_{min}} = \frac{1.5}{0.05} = 30 \text{ Hz}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Selecting 50 Hz (twice the minimum) gives a safety margin against packet drops and jitter — the standard rate chosen in production TurtleBot stacks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LiDAR Data Throughput to ROS Topic

### Problem Setup
- LiDAR with $N_{beams} = 1080$ rays/scan
- Rotation rate: $v_{rot} = 10$ Hz (600 RPM)
- Each ray reports range (4 bytes float) + intensity (1 byte) = **5 bytes**

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Scan frequency.

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{v_{rot} \text{ (rev/s)}}{1 \text{ rev/scan}} = 10 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Bytes per scan.

$$
L_{scan} = 2 \cdot N_{beams} \cdot n_{bytes} = 2 \cdot 1080 \cdot 5 = 10{,}800 \text{ bytes/scan}
$$

**Step 3:** Average data rate on `/scan` topic.

$$
R_{data} = f_{scan} \cdot L_{scan} = 10 \cdot 10{,}800 = 108{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 105.5 \text{ KB/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to bits per second.

$$
R_{data} = 108{,}000 \cdot 8 = 864{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \approx 864 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Practical Impact:** This fits comfortably on a 1 Gbps Ethernet bus — but **10 such LiDARs** would saturate a 100 Mbps link. ROS 2 DDS allows per-topic QoS to throttle bulk sensors without affecting safety-critical topics.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ROS Communication Primitives Compared

| Criterion | Topic (Pub/Sub) | Service (Req/Rep) | Action (Goal/Result/Feedback) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronous? | **No** | **Yes** | No (asynchronous but preemptable) |
| Many-to-many? | **Yes** | No (1 client ↔ 1 server) | No (1 client ↔ 1 server) |
| Suitable for | Streaming sensor data | Quick queries (e.g., "where am I?") | Long tasks (e.g., "navigate to goal") |
| Preemption | N/A | N/A | **Yes** |
| History | Last N messages (latched) | One-shot | Persistent until goal achieved |
| KTU Example | `/scan`, `/odom`, `/cmd_vel` | `/get_plan`, `/set_pose` | `/move_base` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule for KTU Essays:** "Use a **topic** for continuous data, a **service** for instantaneous queries, and an **action** for goal-oriented behaviors that may take time."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing ROS 1 vs ROS 2 for an AGV Project

| Project Requirement | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Single indoor AGV, R&D lab | **ROS 1 (Noetic)** | Mature packages, abundant tutorials |
| Multi-robot warehouse fleet | **ROS 2 (Humble)** | Native DDS discovery, QoS |
| Safety-critical (ISO 26262) | **ROS 2 + RT-PREEMPT** | Real-time guarantees |
| Edge MCU (STM32, ESP32) | **micro-ROS** | Embedded footprint |
| Legacy code compatibility | **ros1_bridge** | Interoperability layer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROS Computational Graph for a Typical AGV

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **publish/subscribe topology** of an autonomous guided vehicle using ROS — showing how perception, localization, planning, and control nodes interact through topics and services.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    masterNode[ROS MASTER roscore]:::core
    paramServer[(PARAMETER SERVER)]:::core
    lidarPub[LIDAR DRIVER NODE]:::sensor
    odomPub[WHEEL ODOMETRY NODE]:::sensor
    imuPub[IMU NODE]:::sensor
    camPub[CAMERA NODE]:::sensor
    scanTopic[/scan topic/]:::topic
    odomTopic[/odom topic/]:::topic
    imuTopic[/imu topic/]:::topic
    imgTopic[/camera image topic/]:::topic
    slamNode[SLAM NODE gmapping]:::perception
    localNode[AMCL LOCALIZATION]:::perception
    mapTopic[/map topic/]:::topic
    tfServer[TF BROADCASTER]:::core
    moveBase[move_base NAV STACK]:::planner
    globalPlan[/global plan topic/]:::topic
    localPlan[/local plan topic/]:::topic
    ctrlNode[CONTROLLER NODE]:::control
    cmdTopic[/cmd_vel topic/]:::topic
    motorNode[MOTOR DRIVER NODE]:::actuator
    rvizNode[RVIS VISUALIZER]:::viz
    safetyNode[SAFETY MONITOR]:::safety

    masterNode --- paramServer
    masterNode --- tfServer

    lidarPub --> scanTopic
    odomPub --> odomTopic
    imuPub --> imuTopic
    camPub --> imgTopic

    scanTopic --> slamNode
    odomTopic --> slamNode
    imuTopic --> slamNode
    scanTopic --> localNode
    odomTopic --> localNode

    slamNode --> mapTopic
    mapTopic --> moveBase
    scanTopic --> moveBase
    odomTopic --> moveBase

    moveBase --> globalPlan
    moveBase --> localPlan
    globalPlan --> ctrlNode
    localPlan --> ctrlNode
    ctrlNode --> cmdTopic
    cmdTopic --> motorNode

    mapTopic --> rvizNode
    scanTopic --> rvizNode
    odomTopic --> rvizNode
    cmdTopic --> rvizNode

    safetyNode -. monitors .-> cmdTopic
    safetyNode -. emergency stop .-> motorNode

    classDef core fill:#1f2937,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sensor fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff
    classDef topic fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    classDef perception fill:#10b981,color:#fff
    classDef planner fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff
    classDef control fill:#f59e0b,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    classDef viz fill:#64748b,color:#fff
    classDef safety fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
```

### 4.1.1 Reading the Diagram

- **Blue nodes** are sensors producing raw data.
- **Yellow rounded boxes** are topics — the message highways.
- **Green nodes** perform perception (SLAM, localization).
- **Purple node** is the central planner (`move_base`).
- **Orange node** is the controller that outputs `cmd_vel`.
- **Red node** is the actuator (motor driver).
- **Grey node** is `rviz` for visualization.
- **Crimson node** is the safety monitor — a cross-cutting concern that supervises command outputs.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AGV Software Stack on ROS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L4 [LEVEL 4 APPLICATION LAYER]
        app1[Warehouse Order Dispatcher]
        app2[Fleet Manager GUI]
    end
    subgraph L3 [LEVEL 3 DECISION LAYER]
        planner[Global Path Planner]
        localplanner[Local Trajectory Planner]
        behaviour[Behaviour State Machine]
    end
    subgraph L2 [LEVEL 2 MIDDLEWARE LAYER ROS CORE]
        roscomm[ROS Communication Graph Topics Services Actions]
        tfmod[TF Transform System]
        parammod[Parameter Server]
    end
    subgraph L1 [LEVEL 1 PERCEPTION ABSTRACTION]
        lidarab[LiDAR Abstraction]
        camab[Camera Abstraction]
        imuab[IMU Abstraction]
        odomab[Odometry Abstraction]
    end
    subgraph L0 [LEVEL 0 HARDWARE LAYER]
        hw1[Velodyne VLP16]
        hw2[Stereo Camera ZED2]
        hw3[BNO055 IMU]
        hw4[Wheel Encoders]
        hw5[Differential Drive Motors]
    end

    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0
    L0 -. raw signals .-> L1
    L1 -. abstracted msgs .-> L2
    L2 -. trajectory goals .-> L3
    L3 -. task status .-> L4
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the canonical 5-level AGV software stack used in the Autoware/Apollo reference architectures. **ROS occupies the middleware tier**, providing the inter-layer abstraction that lets the same planner code run on a real AGV or in a `gazebo` simulator.

---

## 4.3 ROS Message-Flow Sequence for a Navigation Goal

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant GUI as rviz / GUI
    participant MB as move_base
    participant GP as Global Planner
    participant LP as Local Planner
    participant Ctrl as Controller
    participant Motor as Motor Driver
    participant Lidar as /scan Source

    Op->>GUI: Click goal pose on map
    GUI->>MB: Action Goal (geometry_msgs/PoseStamped)
    MB->>GP: make_plan service call
    GP-->>MB: global path (nav_msgs/Path)
    MB->>LP: feed local segment + costmap
    loop at control rate 20 Hz
        Lidar->>MB: LaserScan
        MB->>LP: update local plan
        LP->>Ctrl: cmd_vel (Twist)
        Ctrl->>Motor: PWM signals
        Motor-->>Lidar: wheel ticks (feedback)
    end
    MB-->>GUI: Action Result success
    GUI-->>Op: Path completed notification
```

> **Reading aid:** This sequence diagram is the **single most frequently drawn schematic** in KTU Module-3 viva questions. Memorize the order: *Goal → Global Plan → Local Plan → cmd_vel → Motor → Sensor Feedback*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a ROS node and a ROS topic. How do they differ from a ROS service?**

**Model Answer:**
A **ROS node** is an independent executable process within the ROS computational graph that performs a specific computation (e.g., reading a sensor, running a planner). A **ROS topic** is a named, asynchronous message bus that supports many-to-many publish–subscribe communication — publishers push typed messages without knowing subscribers, and subscribers receive without knowing publishers. A **ROS service**, in contrast, implements synchronous request/reply communication: a client sends a request, blocks until the server returns a response, and the relationship is strictly one-to-one. Topics suit streaming data (sensor readings), while services suit instantaneous queries (`get_plan`, `set_pose`).

> **Examiner Tip:** Award 1 mark each for node, topic, and a clear contrast with service.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of `roscore` in a ROS system? What happens if it crashes during operation?**

**Model Answer:**
`roscore` launches the **ROS Master**, which (i) maintains the name registry that allows nodes to locate each other, (ii) hosts the **parameter server** for global configuration storage, and (iii) provides the XMLRPC server for inter-node handshakes. If `roscore` crashes, **all topic and service connections drop** because existing nodes rely on cached connections that may time out; new connections cannot be established. Any node still publishing will produce warnings like *"Couldn't find an AF_INET address for the master"*. In safety-critical AGV deployments, this is unacceptable — leading to adoption of ROS 2 (which is *master-less* via DDS peer discovery).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the ROS computational graph in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe how a typical autonomous guided vehicle uses ROS nodes and topics for perception, localization, planning, and control.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the differences between ROS 1 and ROS 2. Why is ROS 2 preferred for production-grade autonomous vehicles?

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):** The ROS computational graph is the peer-to-peer network of nodes exchanging data through topics, services, and the parameter server — coordinated by `roscore`.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Use the diagram from SECTION 4.1 above, annotated with key topics (`/scan`, `/odom`, `/cmd_vel`).

**Layered Explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Perception Layer:** LiDAR and camera driver nodes publish `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` and `sensor_msgs/Image` on `/scan` and `/camera/image` topics respectively. An IMU node publishes on `/imu` at ~100 Hz.
2. **Localization Layer:** The `amcl` (Adaptive Monte-Carlo Localization) node fuses `/scan` and `/odom` to estimate pose on the `/map`, broadcasting transforms via the **TF system**.
3. **Planning Layer:** `move_base` consumes `/map`, `/scan`, `/odom`, and a goal (via action) to produce a `nav_msgs/Path` global plan, then a local trajectory at 20 Hz.
4. **Control Layer:** A controller node subscribes to the local plan and publishes `geometry_msgs/Twist` on `/cmd_vel` for the motor driver.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The decoupled, topic-based architecture allows modules to be developed, tested, and replaced independently — accelerating AGV development.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

| Aspect | ROS 1 (1 mark) | ROS 2 (1 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| Middleware | XMLRPC + TCPROS/UDPROS | DDS (RTPS) standard |
| Master | Required (`roscore`) | Master-less (peer discovery) |
| Real-time | No native support | Yes (RT-PREEMPT, deadline QoS) |
| Multi-robot | Difficult | Native |
| Security | None | SROS2 (DDS-Security) |
| Embedded | Limited | micro-ROS |
| Platforms | Linux only | Linux, Windows, RTOS |

**Why ROS 2 for AVs (4 marks):**

1. **Safety & Real-time:** DDS offers *deadline* and *lifespan* QoS — guarantees on message delivery time crucial for ISO 26262 ASIL-rated functions.
2. **No Single Point of Failure:** Master-less architecture suits distributed fleets.
3. **Deterministic Communication:** Critical for sensor-fusion loops that fail at random latencies.
4. **Ecosystem Maturity:** Autoware.Auto and Apex.AI (the only certified AV stacks) are built on ROS 2.
5. **Embedded Footprint:** micro-ROS lets ECUs running on RTOS participate in the same data graph.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Confusing **ROS Master** with a robot's onboard computer. (The Master is a *software process*, not hardware.)
> - Writing "ROS is an operating system" without the qualifier *meta-operating system*.
> - Forgetting that **topics are anonymous** — the publisher has no idea who subscribes.
> - In ROS 1 vs ROS 2 comparisons, omitting the **DDS middleware** as the key differentiator.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable examples, explain the concepts of ROS **messages**, **topics**, **services**, and **actions**. State one real-world use case for each in an AGV.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2D LiDAR on an AGV has 720 beams/scan, rotates at 15 Hz, and each beam reports range (4-byte float) and intensity (1 byte). Compute:
- (i) the angular resolution in degrees,
- (ii) the average data rate on the `/scan` topic in KB/s,
- (iii) whether a 1 Mbps bandwidth link is sufficient. Justify.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Primitive | Definition (1.5 marks each) | AGV Use Case (0.25 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Message** | Strictly typed data structure (`.msg` file) — fields with primitive types. | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` carries range/intensity arrays. |
| **Topic** | Named, asynchronous, many-to-many channel for streaming messages. | `/scan` — multiple subscribers (SLAM, AMCL, safety monitor). |
| **Service** | Synchronous, one-to-one request/reply RPC, defined in `.srv`. | `/get_plan` — ask global planner for a path between two poses. |
| **Action** | Long-running, preemptable goal with feedback and result, defined in `.action`. | `/move_base` action — goal: navigate to dock; feedback: distance remaining. |

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $N_{beams} = 720$, $f_{scan} = 15$ Hz, payload per beam $= 4 + 1 = 5$ bytes.

**(i) Angular Resolution:**

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{360^\circ}{N_{beams}} = \frac{360}{720} = 0.5^\circ
$$

**(ii) Bytes per scan:**

$$
L_{scan} = 2 \cdot N_{beams} \cdot n_{bytes} = 2 \cdot 720 \cdot 5 = 7{,}200 \text{ bytes/scan}
$$

**Average data rate:**

$$
R_{data} = f_{scan} \cdot L_{scan} = 15 \cdot 7{,}200 = 108{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

Converting to KB/s:

$$
R_{data} = \frac{108{,}000}{1024} \approx 105.47 \text{ KB/s}
$$

Converting to bits/s:

$$
R_{data} = 108{,}000 \cdot 8 = 864{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 864 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(iii) Bandwidth sufficiency (1.5 marks):**

Required rate $\approx 0.864$ Mbps. A 1 Mbps link provides only $\approx 1$ Mbps of useful capacity, of which overheads (TCP/IP headers, ROS message framing $\approx$ 20–40 bytes per packet, retransmissions) consume **15–25%**. Net usable capacity $\approx$ 750–850 kbps — **barely sufficient**. In a multi-LiDAR AGV, this link would be a bottleneck; a **10 Mbps** link or Ethernet with QoS is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the `×2` factor (range AND intensity) in step (ii) and lose 1–2 marks. Also, converting KB/s using $1024$ (binary) vs $1000$ (decimal) can shift answers — examiners accept both if the unit is labelled explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ROS = Meta-OS**, not a true OS — it sits on top of Linux.
- **Nodes** are independent executables; **Master (`roscore`)** is the rendezvous point.
- **Topics** = anonymous, async, many-to-many streams (use for sensors).
- **Services** = sync, blocking, one-to-one RPCs (use for quick queries).
- **Actions** = preemptable, long-running goals with feedback (use for navigation).
- **Messages** are typed by `.msg` files; **bags** record streams for offline analysis.
- **TF (Transform)** system maintains time-stamped coordinate frames — essential for sensor fusion.
- **Package** = atomic software unit; **Workspace** (`catkin_ws/`) = top-level build folder.
- **ROS 2 (DDS-based)** is the modern choice — master-less, real-time capable, embedded-friendly.
- **Typical rates to memorize:** LiDAR 10–20 Hz, Odometry 50 Hz, IMU 100 Hz, Cameras 30 Hz.
- **Publish-rate formula:** $f_{pub} \geq \dfrac{v_{max}}{\Delta s_{min}}$ (Nyquist-like criterion).
- **Data rate formula:** $R_{data} = 2 \cdot f_{scan} \cdot N_{beams} \cdot n_{bytes}$.
- **Decision rule for communication primitive:** streaming → topic, instant query → service, goal-driven task → action.
- **`roscore` failure kills ROS 1** but not ROS 2 — a key reason for AV adoption of ROS 2.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks for a diagram, *always include at least one topic name and one message type* in your drawing — it earns the "applied understanding" marks that distinguish a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autoware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Autoware

**Autoware** is the world's first "all-in-one" open-source software stack for autonomous driving, built primarily on the **Robot Operating System (ROS/ROS 2)**. It is engineered to enable autonomous vehicle research, development, and deployment by providing a unified, modular, and hardware-agnostic framework. The software stack encompasses capabilities spanning sensing, perception, localization, mapping, path planning, vehicle control, and human-machine interfacing.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

> **Autoware** is an integrated autonomous driving middleware and application framework that fuses heterogeneous sensor data (LiDAR, camera, radar, GNSS/IMU) into a high-resolution environmental model and executes a real-time decision-making pipeline — perception → localization → prediction → planning → control — to drive a vehicle without human intervention.

The current active development line, **Autoware Universe** (succeeding Autoware.AI and Autoware.Auto), is maintained by the **Autoware Foundation** and is aligned with the **Autonomous Driving Stack Reference Architecture** used in the *Autoware Course / Pilot Course* standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> Autoware is studied under Module 3 of OEMUT611 because it is the **de-facto industry-academia bridge software** for Autonomous Guided Vehicles (AGVs) and SAE Level **3, 4, and 5** autonomous cars. Expect direct questions on its **architecture, modules, and key algorithms** in university exams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cockpit of the Self-Driving Car"

Imagine you are sitting inside a futuristic cockpit of a self-driving car. You have:

- **Eyes** → LiDAR (3D laser scanner) and cameras that "see" the road in real time.
- **Ears & Inner Ear (Balance)** → GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) + IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) that tell the car *where* it is.
- **A mental Map** → a pre-built **3D point-cloud map** stored in memory.
- **A Brain** → a high-performance computing unit running **Autoware**, which acts as the central "driver AI".
- **Hands & Feet** → drive-by-wire actuators that steer, accelerate, and brake.

**Autoware is essentially this "Brain"** — a piece of software that ingests all sensory input, thinks about it, remembers where it is on the map, decides where to go next, and commands the car to act. In short, **Autoware is to a self-driving car what an Operating System is to a personal computer.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> | Car Component | Autoware Counterpart |
> |---|---|
> | Eyes | LiDAR/Camera Drivers (Sensing Layer) |
> | Inner Ear | EKF/UKF Localization Module |
> | Memory | Point Cloud Map (PCD / voxel grid) |
> | Prefrontal Cortex | Behavior & Mission Planner |
> | Motor Cortex | Vehicle Control Module (Pure Pursuit / MPC) |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

Key standardized terms a KTU student must remember:

- **SAE Levels of Automation:** Level 0 → Level 5 (Level 3 = Conditional Automation, Level 4 = High Automation, Level 5 = Full Automation).
- **NDT (Normal Distributions Transform):** a LiDAR-based scan-matching algorithm with typical cell resolution of **1.0 m to 2.0 m**.
- **HD Map:** High-Definition Map with centimeter-level accuracy (≤ 10 cm positional error).
- **Lookahead Distance (ld):** typically **4 m to 12 m** for highway driving.
- **Control Frequency:** main control loop runs at **50 Hz to 100 Hz**.
- **Frame Rate (Sensors):** LiDAR typically 10 Hz – 20 Hz, Cameras 30 Hz, IMU 100 Hz – 200 Hz, GNSS 1 Hz – 10 Hz.

## 1.4 Evolution Timeline (Syllabus Context)

1. **Autoware.AI (2015 – 2020):** First generation, ROS 1-based, widely adopted in academic research.
2. **Autoware.Auto (2019 – 2021):** ROS 2-based, more rigorous safety/quality standards (AUTOSAR-inspired).
3. **Autoware Universe (2021 – Present):** Modular, ROS 2 Humble/Iron/... based, industry-grade, community-driven.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact for KTU Viva:** The original Autoware project was launched in 2015 by **Tier IV, Japan** and later transferred to the **Autoware Foundation** in 2018 as an open governance project.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Autoware Functional Architecture

Autoware is logically organized as a **layered, modular stack**. Each layer can be developed, tested, and deployed independently — a critical design choice for safety-critical autonomous systems.

### Layer 1: Hardware & Sensor Abstraction Layer
This layer abstracts the physical sensors and vehicle actuators. It includes:
- **Drivers** for Velodyne, Hesai, RoboSense LiDARs
- **Drivers** for USB/Ethernet cameras (e.g., Allied Vision, FLIR)
- **Drivers** for NovAtel, u-blox GNSS receivers
- **Vehicle Interface** packages (e.g., `vehicle_interface`, `pacmod_interface`, `lexus_*`)
- **CAN bus** protocol abstraction for drive-by-wire

### Layer 2: Computing Platform
- Runs on **x86_64** or **ARM64** platforms
- GPU acceleration via **CUDA** for deep-learning perception
- Real-time guarantees often rely on **PREEMPT_RT** Linux kernel patch
- Containerized via **Docker** for reproducibility

### Layer 3: ROS 2 Middleware
- Uses **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** for real-time pub/sub
- QoS (Quality of Service) policies ensure deterministic data flow
- Standard message types: `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`, `nav_msgs/Odometry`, `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`

### Layer 4: Core Functional Modules

#### A. Localization Module
- **3D Map-Based Localization** using **NDT (Normal Distributions Transform)** or **ICP (Iterative Closest Point)** scan matching
- **Sensor Fusion** via **EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** or **UKF (Unscented Kalman Filter)** fusing IMU + GNSS + LiDAR odometry
- Output: 6-DoF pose `(x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw)` published at ≥ 50 Hz

#### B. Perception Module
- **Point Cloud Preprocessing**: voxel grid downsampling, ground segmentation (RANSAC-based), clustering (Euclidean / DBSCAN)
- **Object Detection**: 3D bounding boxes via **PointPillars**, **CenterPoint**, or **YOLOv3/v5 + LiDAR fusion**
- **Object Tracking**: Multi-Object Tracking using **IMM (Interacting Multiple Model)** or **SORT/DeepSORT**
- **Traffic Light Recognition**: CNN-based classification (ResNet, MobileNet)

#### C. Planning Module (Hierarchical)

| Sub-Stage | Algorithm | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Route Planning** (Lane-level) | OpenDRIVE / Lanelet2 map parsing | Once per mission |
| **Behavior Planning** | State machine + traffic rule engine | 10 Hz |
| **Motion Planning** | Lattice Planner / OpenPlanner | 10 Hz – 20 Hz |
| **Trajectory Optimization** | Quadratic programming (QP) | 10 Hz – 50 Hz |

#### D. Control Module
- **Lateral Control**: Pure Pursuit, Stanley Controller, or **MPC (Model Predictive Control)**
- **Longitudinal Control**: PID, adaptive cruise control law
- **Output**: Steering angle (rad), throttle (%), brake (%)

### Layer 5: Application / HMI Layer
- **RViz2** for 3D visualization
- **Web-based fleet manager**
- **Run-time monitoring and logging** (ROS 2 bag → MCAP format)

## 2.2 Why Each Module Matters — The Engineering Rationale

- **Localization is the foundation** — without knowing *where the car is within 10 cm*, no decision is valid.
- **Perception gives meaning to raw data** — turning point clouds into "there is a pedestrian 15 m ahead".
- **Planning converts intent into geometry** — a smooth, kinematically-feasible trajectory from A to B.
- **Control ensures physical execution** — translates the desired trajectory into actuator commands within milliseconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Rule (Memorize for KTU):**
> `Sensors → Localization → Perception → Prediction → Planning → Control → Vehicle Actuators`
> This is the **canonical "Sense-Plan-Act" loop of Autoware**. Examiners love to test this exact ordering.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Pipe Escape Rule:** All vertical bars (`|`) inside table cells are written as `\vert` so that Markdown table syntax is never broken.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | SI Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NDT Cell Probability Density | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \sqrt{\det \boldsymbol{\Sigma}}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$ | m$^{-d}$ | $d$ = 2 or 3 for vehicle localization |
| 2 | NDT Score (Sum of Likelihoods) | $\text{score}(\mathbf{T}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\,(\mathbf{q}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i^{-1}(\mathbf{q}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)\right)$ | unitless | Maximized by gradient/Hessian search |
| 3 | Pure Pursuit Curvature | $\kappa = \dfrac{2 \sin \alpha}{\ell_d}$ | m$^{-1}$ | $\alpha$ = heading error to lookahead point |
| 4 | Pure Pursuit Steering Angle | $\delta = \arctan(\kappa \cdot L)$ | rad | $L$ = vehicle wheelbase |
| 5 | Stanley Steering Law | $\delta(t) = \psi(t) + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k \cdot e_{\text{fa}}(t)}{v(t)}\right)$ | rad | $e_{\text{fa}}$ = front-axle cross-track error |
| 6 | Bicycle Kinematic Model | $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v \sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$ | m/s, rad/s | Foundation of vehicle control |
| 7 | Bicycle Dynamic Model (Lateral) | $m v (\dot{\beta} + \dot{\psi}) = 2 F_{yf} \cos\delta_f - 2 F_{yr}$ | N | $\beta$ = sideslip angle |
| 8 | EKF Prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_{k-1})$ | state vector | Linearization via Jacobian $\mathbf{F}_k = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ |
| 9 | EKF Update | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{H}_k\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^{\mathsf{T}}+\mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$ | — | Kalman gain |
| 10 | IMU Pre-integration Drift | $\sigma_\theta(t) = \sigma_{\text{gyro}} \sqrt{t}$ | rad | $\sigma_{\text{gyro}}$ = gyroscope noise density (rad/s/√Hz) |
| 11 | LiDAR Range Resolution | $\Delta r = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$ | m | $B$ = chirp bandwidth (Hz) |
| 12 | Vehicle Stopping Distance | $d_s = v\, t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2\mu g}$ | m | $t_r$ = reaction time, $\mu$ = friction coeff. |
| 13 | Trajectory Curvature Continuity | $\kappa(s) = \text{continuous} \Rightarrow \text{jerk-bounded motion}$ | m$^{-1}$ | Required for passenger comfort |
| 14 | Lookahead Time Policy | $\ell_d = v \cdot t_{\text{look}},\ \ t_{\text{look}} \approx 1.0\text{ s}$ | m | Speed-adaptive lookahead |
| 15 | Voxel Grid Downsampling | $\text{Leaves} = \text{floor}(x/L_v)\ \text{for leaf size } L_v$ | m | $L_v$ typically 0.1 m – 0.2 m |
| 16 | Centripetal Acceleration | $a_c = v^2 / r = v^2 \kappa$ | m/s$^2$ | Comfort limit $\le 1.8$ m/s$^2$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotaxis**: Industry pioneers use Autoware-based stacks for **last-mile delivery robots** and **urban robotaxi pilots**.
- **AGVs in Logistics**: Warehouses deploy Autoware-forklifts and tuggers for material handling.
- **Academic Research**: Over **2,500+ research papers** cite Autoware.AI/Universe as the experimental platform.
- **Sim-to-Real Transfer**: Autoware integrates with **CARLA**, **LGSVL**, and **AWSIM** simulators for closed-loop testing.
- **Open Standards Compliance**: Aligns with **ASAM OpenDRIVE**, **Lanelet2**, and **AUTOSAR** for HD map formats and vehicle interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Normal Distributions Transform (NDT) Score Function

The NDT algorithm is the **most important localization algorithm in Autoware**. Below is a complete, step-by-step derivation of its score function and gradient, which examiners commonly ask in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Step 1 — Voxelization of the Reference Map
Given a pre-built 3D point cloud map $\mathcal{M} = \{\mathbf{m}_j \in \mathbb{R}^3\}_{j=1}^{M}$, partition the 3D space into cubic cells (voxels) of side length $L_v$ (typically $L_v = 1.0$ m or $2.0$ m). For each non-empty voxel $C_k$, the **mean vector** and **covariance matrix** are computed:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|} \sum_{\mathbf{m}_j \in C_k} \mathbf{m}_j
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|-1} \sum_{\mathbf{m}_j \in C_k} (\mathbf{m}_j - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)(\mathbf{m}_j - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\mathsf{T}}
$$

**Logic:** The voxelized map models the *probability* of finding a surface point in space. Each voxel is treated as a local **multivariate normal distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)$ — this converts the discrete map into a continuous differentiable score function.

### Step 2 — Transform a Live Scan Point
Let $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{t}, \mathbf{R})$ be a candidate pose, where $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the translation and $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ is the rotation matrix. A live scan point $\mathbf{p}_i$ is transformed into map frame as:

$$
\mathbf{p}_i' = \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{p}_i) = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t}
$$

**Logic:** The algorithm will optimize the pose $\mathbf{T}$ so that the transformed live points best "match" the voxelized map.

### Step 3 — Gaussian Likelihood of a Point
Assume the transformed point $\mathbf{p}_i'$ falls inside voxel $C_k$. Its likelihood under the voxel Gaussian is:

$$
p(\mathbf{p}_i' \vert \mathbf{T}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \sqrt{\det \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)\right)
$$

**Logic:** This is the standard multivariate Gaussian density. The exponent term measures the Mahalanobis distance — small distance ⇒ high likelihood.

### Step 4 — Total NDT Score Function
Summing the log-likelihoods over all $N$ scan points gives the **score to be maximized**:

$$
\text{score}(\mathbf{T}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k(i)})^{\mathsf{T}} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k(i)}^{-1} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k(i)})\right)
$$

**Logic:** Taking the log converts the product of Gaussians into a sum, making gradient-based optimization tractable. Autoware uses the **negative log-likelihood** for minimization:

$$
\mathbf{T}^{\star} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{T}} \;\; -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(\mathbf{p}_i' \vert \mathbf{T})
$$

### Step 5 — Gradient for Newton's Method
Differentiating the score w.r.t. the pose parameters $\mathbf{T}$ (typically represented by 6-D vector $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (t_x, t_y, t_z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$) gives the gradient and Hessian needed by **Newton's method** (Autoware's default solver is *more-thuente line search* or *Newton's method*):

$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \text{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} -(\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k(i)})^{\mathsf{T}} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k(i)}^{-1} \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}_i'}{\partial \boldsymbol{\xi}} \cdot \exp(\cdot)
$$

$$
\mathbf{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}_i'}{\partial \boldsymbol{\xi}}^{\mathsf{T}} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k(i)}^{-1} \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}_i'}{\partial \boldsymbol{\xi}} \right] \cdot \exp(\cdot)
$$

**Logic:** The gradient tells the optimizer *which direction* to nudge the pose; the Hessian (second derivative) tells *how far* to jump — this is a 2nd-order convergence method, hence very fast.

### Step 6 — Update Rule
The pose is updated iteratively:

$$
\boldsymbol{\xi}_{n+1} = \boldsymbol{\xi}_n - \mathbf{H}^{-1} \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \text{score} \big\vert_{\boldsymbol{\xi}_n}
$$

Convergence is reached when $\Vert \Delta \boldsymbol{\xi} \Vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** NDT is faster than ICP because it operates on a *continuous score function* rather than a discrete point-to-point correspondence. This is one reason Autoware's default localizer is NDT, not ICP.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pure Pursuit Lateral Controller

Pure Pursuit is the default lateral controller in Autoware Universe. Here is the complete geometric derivation.

### Step 1 — Vehicle Geometry Setup
Consider the rear-axle reference point of a vehicle with wheelbase $L$. The vehicle is moving with speed $v$ and steering angle $\delta$. The instantaneous turning radius is $R$. From the bicycle model:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan \delta}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Goal Point
A **lookahead point** $\mathbf{g}$ is selected on the reference trajectory at a curvilinear arc-distance $\ell_d$ ahead of the rear axle. The lookahead point in the vehicle's local frame is:

$$
\mathbf{g}_{\text{local}} = (\ell_d \cos \alpha,\ \ \ell_d \sin \alpha)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the heading angle between the vehicle's longitudinal axis and the vector to $\mathbf{g}$.

### Step 3 — Geometric Curvature
The path from the rear axle to the lookahead point is an arc of a circle passing through the rear axle and the lookahead point. The radius of this circle is found from the **inscribed angle theorem**:

$$
R = \frac{\ell_d}{2 \sin \alpha}
$$

Hence the curvature (inverse of radius) is:

$$
\kappa = \frac{2 \sin \alpha}{\ell_d}
$$

### Step 4 — Steering Command
From the bicycle model, $\tan \delta = L/R = L \kappa$, therefore:

$$
\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 L \sin \alpha}{\ell_d}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Speed-Adaptive Lookahead
To improve stability across the entire speed envelope, Autoware uses a **velocity-scaled lookahead**:

$$
\ell_d = K_0 + K_v \, v
$$

Typical values: $K_0 = 4$ m, $K_v = 0.5$ s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Sanity Check:** If $L = 2.7$ m, $\alpha = 5^{\circ}$, $\ell_d = 6$ m, then:
> $\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2 \cdot 2.7 \cdot \sin 5^{\circ}}{6}\right) = \arctan(0.0784) \approx 4.49^{\circ}$
> This is a reasonable steering angle for highway driving.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Sensor Fusion

The localization stack fuses IMU, GNSS, and LiDAR odometry. The EKF equations are derived as follows.

### Step 1 — Prediction Step
Given a process model $\mathbf{x}_{k} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_{k-1}) + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}$ with $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{Q})$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1}, \mathbf{u}_{k-1})
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^{\mathsf{T}} + \mathbf{Q}_k
$$

where $\mathbf{F}_k = \left.\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1}}$ is the Jacobian.

### Step 2 — Measurement Update
Given measurement $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{v}_k$ with $\mathbf{v} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})$:

$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^{\mathsf{T}} \big(\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^{\mathsf{T}} + \mathbf{R}_k\big)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{h}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1}))
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}
$$

where $\mathbf{H}_k = \left.\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1}}$.

### Step 3 — Why EKF and not KF?
The vehicle's motion is **non-linear** (rotation, $\tan \delta$ terms, etc.). The KF requires linear dynamics. The EKF linearizes via first-order Taylor expansion around the current estimate, giving an accurate local approximation at a low computational cost.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs. Autoware Module

| Autoware Module | Core Algorithm | Output | Frequency | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Localization | NDT Scan Matching | 6-DoF Pose | 10 – 50 Hz | Fast, derivative-based | Local minima risk |
| Localization | EKF Fusion | Smoothed Pose | 100 Hz | Multi-sensor fusion | Linearization error |
| Perception | Voxel Grid Filter | Downsampled PC | 10 Hz | Reduces compute | Loss of detail |
| Perception | Euclidean Clustering | Object Clusters | 10 Hz | Simple, fast | Struggles with overlap |
| Perception | PointPillars | 3D BBoxes | 10 Hz | Deep learning accuracy | GPU required |
| Planning | Lattice Planner | Trajectory | 10 Hz | Multi-objective | Tuning-heavy |
| Planning | Behavior FSM | Maneuver | 10 Hz | Rule-based safety | Brittle at edge cases |
| Control | Pure Pursuit | Steering | 50 Hz | Simple, stable | No obstacle avoidance |
| Control | MPC | Steering + Throttle | 20 – 50 Hz | Constraint handling | Computationally heavy |
| Control | PID | Throttle / Brake | 50 Hz | Classical, robust | No model awareness |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Autoware High-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the canonical **Sense → Perceive → Plan → Act** pipeline of Autoware, with ROS 2 topics connecting each module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorL["LiDAR Sensor"] --> driverA["Velodyne Driver"]
    sensorC["Camera Sensor"] --> driverB["Camera Driver"]
    sensorG["GNSS Receiver"] --> driverC["GNSS Driver"]
    sensorI["IMU Sensor"] --> driverD["IMU Driver"]

    driverA --> topicA["/points_raw"]
    driverB --> topicB["/image_raw"]
    driverC --> topicE["/gnss_pose"]
    driverD --> topicF["/imu_raw"]

    topicA --> locNode["NDT Localization"]
    topicF --> locNode
    topicE --> locNode
    locNode --> topicG["/ndt_pose"]
    topicG --> ekfNode["EKF Sensor Fusion"]
    topicF --> ekfNode
    ekfNode --> topicH["/localization/pose_twist"]

    topicA --> pclNode["PointCloud Preprocessing"]
    pclNode --> groundNode["Ground Segmentation"]
    groundNode --> clusterNode["Euclidean Clustering"]
    clusterNode --> detectNode["3D Object Detection"]
    topicB --> detectNode
    detectNode --> topicI["/detected_objects"]

    topicH --> routeNode["Route Planner"]
    topicH --> behaviorNode["Behavior Planner"]
    routeNode --> behaviorNode
    topicI --> behaviorNode
    behaviorNode --> motionNode["Motion Planner Lattice"]
    topicH --> motionNode
    motionNode --> topicJ["/planning/trajectory"]

    topicJ --> controlNode["Pure Pursuit and PID Controller"]
    topicH --> controlNode
    controlNode --> topicK["/vehicle/control_cmd"]

    topicK --> vehicleNode["Drive By Wire Actuator Interface"]
    vehicleNode --> actuatorL["Steering Motor"]
    vehicleNode --> actuatorT["Throttle Actuator"]
    vehicleNode --> actuatorB["Brake Actuator"]

    topicH --> rviz["RViz2 Visualization"]
    topicI --> rviz
    topicJ --> rviz
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The flow goes left to right — raw sensors (left) → ROS 2 topics (middle) → processing nodes (center) → vehicle actuators (right). Topic names like `/points_raw` follow the ROS naming convention.

---

## 4.2 Autoware Localization Subsystem (Detailed)

The localization block is the most-examined subsystem. The following Mermaid diagram breaks it down into its internal building blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputGroup["Input Sources"] --> fuser

    subgraph fuser["EKF Sensor Fusion Block"]
        direction TB
        imuIn["IMU Preintegration"] --> fuserCore["EKF Predict Step"]
        gnssIn["GNSS Position"] --> fuserCore
        ndtIn["NDT Scan Match Pose"] --> fuserCore
        fuserCore --> fuserUpdate["EKF Update Step"]
    end

    fuserUpdate --> outPose["/localization/pose_twist"]

    subgraph ndtGroup["NDT Scan Matcher Block"]
        direction TB
        pclMap["Prebuilt PCD Map"] --> voxel["Voxelize into Cells"]
        voxel --> meanCov["Compute Mean and Covariance per Cell"]
        liveScan["Live LiDAR Scan"] --> transform["Transform by Current Pose Guess"]
        transform --> score["Evaluate Gaussian Score"]
        score --> newtonStep["Newton Optimization"]
        newtonStep --> converged{"Converged"}
        converged -- No --> transform
        converged -- Yes --> ndtOut["NDT Pose Output"]
    end

    ndtOut --> ndtIn
    outPose --> planner["Planning and Control Modules"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice the **feedback loop** from the EKF output back into the NDT transform — this is called the **pose prediction feedback**. The EKF provides a *predicted* pose, which becomes the *initial guess* for the next NDT scan match. This dramatically reduces NDT computation time.

---

## 4.3 Autoware Planning Pipeline (Hierarchical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mission["Mission Goal e.g. Destination Address"] --> route["Route Planner Lanelet2 / OpenDRIVE"]
    route --> behavior["Behavior Planner State Machine"]

    subgraph behaviorFSM["Behavior FSM States"]
        direction TB
        s1["Lane Following"]
        s2["Stop Sign Handling"]
        s3["Traffic Light Handling"]
        s4["Intersection Negotiation"]
        s5["Lane Change"]
        s6["Emergency Stop"]
    end

    behavior --> behaviorFSM

    behaviorFSM --> lattice["Lattice Motion Planner"]
    obstacles["Detected Objects"] --> lattice
    reference["Reference Path from HD Map"] --> lattice

    lattice --> traj["Optimized Trajectory 50 ms Horizon"]
    traj --> smoother["Trajectory Smoothing Spline"]
    smoother --> pubTraj["Publish /planning/trajectory"]
    pubTraj --> ctrl["Vehicle Control"]
```

---

## 4.4 Sensor-Fusion State Machine in Autoware

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> WaitingForSensors : Load PCD Map and Vehicle Params
    WaitingForSensors --> NDTRunning : Valid LiDAR and IMU Arrived
    NDTRunning --> EKFTracking : Initial Pose Converged
    EKFTracking --> Healthy : Covariance Below Threshold
    EKFTracking --> Recovering : Covariance Exceeded or NDT Lost
    Recovering --> NDTRunning : Reinitialize Scan Match
    Healthy --> EmergencyStop : Critical Fault Detected
    Recovering --> EmergencyStop : Multiple Consecutive Failures
    EmergencyStop --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Use Case:** This state diagram is the **Autoware localization state machine** (also documented in `autoware_universe/localization/pose_initializer`). Students should be able to draw this on an exam paper if asked.
