# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Part A questions test **Remember / Understand** level of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be 3–5 sentences long, crisp, and definition-focused.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Autoware. List any two of its major functional modules.**

**Model Answer:**
Autoware is an open-source software stack for autonomous driving, built on top of ROS 2, that provides a complete Sense-Plan-Act pipeline for SAE Level 3+ vehicles. It enables research and deployment of self-driving cars by abstracting sensors, localization, perception, planning, and control into modular, reusable components.
Two major functional modules are:
1. **Localization Module** — performs real-time 6-DoF pose estimation using NDT scan matching and EKF sensor fusion of IMU + GNSS + LiDAR.
2. **Planning Module** — converts a high-level goal into a smooth, kinematically feasible trajectory using a hierarchical Route → Behavior → Motion planner.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Normal Distributions Transform (NDT) algorithm and why is it preferred in Autoware for localization?**

**Model Answer:**
The NDT algorithm is a LiDAR-based scan-matching technique that represents a pre-built 3D point cloud map as a collection of cells, each modeled by a multivariate normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)$. It maximizes a continuous Gaussian score function over a candidate pose to find the best alignment between a live scan and the map.
It is preferred in Autoware because (a) it provides a differentiable score function enabling fast Newton-optimization, (b) it is robust to varying point densities, and (c) it has a typical run-time of a few milliseconds, suitable for real-time 50 Hz localization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Part B questions test **Apply / Analyze / Evaluate** levels. Each 14-mark question typically carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must write detailed derivations, diagrams, and algorithms.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of the Autoware autonomous driving stack**. Describe the role of the **Localization, Perception, Planning, and Control** modules in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Pure Pursuit steering law** $\delta = \arctan(2 L \sin \alpha / \ell_d)$ starting from the bicycle kinematic model. Also explain how a **speed-adaptive lookahead distance** $\ell_d = K_0 + K_v v$ improves lateral stability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Autoware Functional Architecture (Block Diagram + Explanation):**

```
[Sensors] → [ROS 2 Middleware] → [Sensing/Drivers]
   → [Localization] → [Perception] → [Planning] → [Control] → [Vehicle Actuators]
```

1. **Sensors & Drivers:** Velodyne/Hesai LiDAR, USB cameras, GNSS, IMU. Drivers publish standard ROS 2 message types such as `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`, `sensor_msgs/Image`, `nav_msgs/Odometry`.

2. **Localization Module:** Fuses IMU + GNSS + LiDAR odometry via EKF. Performs NDT scan matching against a pre-built PCD map to output a 6-DoF pose $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ at ≥ 50 Hz.

3. **Perception Module:** Performs voxel grid downsampling, ground segmentation, Euclidean clustering, and 3D object detection (e.g., PointPillars) to produce labeled 3D bounding boxes of vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists.

4. **Planning Module (Hierarchical):**
   - **Route Planner:** parses Lanelet2/OpenDRIVE HD map to compute a lane-level route.
   - **Behavior Planner:** finite-state machine choosing maneuvers (lane follow, stop, lane change).
   - **Motion Planner (Lattice):** samples velocity–time–lateral-offset trajectories and picks the kinematically-feasible minimum-cost one.

5. **Control Module:** Pure Pursuit for lateral control (steering) and PID for longitudinal control (throttle/brake). Output commands are sent to the vehicle's drive-by-wire CAN bus.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Bicycle Model Foundation:**
$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\delta}{L}
$$

The instantaneous turning radius is $R = L / \tan\delta$.

**Step 2 — Geometry of Arc to Lookahead Point:**
Let $\alpha$ be the heading error angle and $\ell_d$ the arc length to the lookahead point $\mathbf{g}$. By the inscribed angle theorem, the chord length to the lookahead point is $2R \sin \alpha$, which equals the chord length $\ell_d$ (for small $\alpha$ approximately, but geometrically exact for any $\alpha$):

$$
\ell_d = 2 R \sin \alpha \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{\ell_d}{2 \sin \alpha}
$$

**Step 3 — Curvature and Steering:**
$$
\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{2 \sin \alpha}{\ell_d}
$$

From the bicycle model, $\tan \delta = L/R = L\kappa$:

$$
\boxed{\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 L \sin \alpha}{\ell_d}\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Speed-Adaptive Lookahead:**
A constant $\ell_d$ causes oscillations at high speed and sluggishness at low speed. Replacing it with a linear function of $v$ keeps the controller stable across the full speed envelope:

$$
\ell_d = K_0 + K_v \cdot v
$$

Typical $K_0 = 4$ m, $K_v = 0.5$ s. At $v = 0$, $\ell_d = 4$ m (good for low-speed parking); at $v = 20$ m/s, $\ell_d = 14$ m (good for highway lane-keeping).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the **Normal Distributions Transform (NDT)** algorithm in detail. Derive the **score function** that NDT maximizes and discuss how the pose is updated using Newton's method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the **EKF prediction and update equations** for sensor fusion in Autoware localization. Why is the EKF (not KF) used, and how does the pose feedback from EKF accelerate NDT? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**NDT Algorithm — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Map Voxelization:** Partition the reference PCD map into cubic cells of side $L_v$. For each cell $C_k$ with $|C_k|$ points, compute the mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$.
2. **Transform Live Scan:** A candidate pose $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ maps each live point $\mathbf{p}_i$ into the map frame: $\mathbf{p}_i' = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t}$.
3. **Gaussian Score:** For each transformed point falling in cell $C_k$, the Mahalanobis distance-based likelihood is:
$$
p(\mathbf{p}_i' \vert \mathbf{T}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{3/2}\sqrt{\det \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1}(\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)\right)
$$
4. **Total Score:**
$$
\text{score}(\mathbf{T}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k(i)})^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k(i)}^{-1}(\mathbf{p}_i' - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k(i)})\right)
$$
5. **Newton Update:** Compute gradient $\mathbf{g}$ and Hessian $\mathbf{H}$ w.r.t. pose parameters $\boldsymbol{\xi}$:
$$
\boldsymbol{\xi}_{n+1} = \boldsymbol{\xi}_n - \mathbf{H}^{-1}\mathbf{g}
$$
6. **Convergence:** Stop when $\Vert \Delta \boldsymbol{\xi}\Vert < 10^{-4}$.

**Engineering Advantages:** (i) Continuous, differentiable score → fast optimization; (ii) Robust to point-density variations; (iii) Multi-modal distributions within a cell capture complex geometry.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**EKF Equations:**

*Prediction Step:*
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1}, \mathbf{u}_{k-1})
$$
$$
\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^{\mathsf{T}} + \mathbf{Q}_k, \quad \mathbf{F}_k = \left.\tfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}
$$

*Update Step:*
$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^{\mathsf{T}}\big(\mathbf{H}_k\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^{\mathsf{T}}+\mathbf{R}_k\big)^{-1}
$$
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k(\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{h}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1}))
$$
$$
\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k\mathbf{H}_k)\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}
$$

**Why EKF, not KF?** Vehicle dynamics are non-linear due to rotation matrices, $\tan\delta$ terms, and quaternion kinematics. EKF linearizes around the current estimate via Jacobians, which is computationally cheap yet accurate for the small-time-step regime of the control loop.

**EKF → NDT Feedback Loop:** The EKF outputs a predicted pose $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k}$ that serves as the **initial guess** for the next NDT scan match. Since NDT is iterative, a good initial guess (within a few centimeters of the true pose) means **only 2–3 Newton iterations** are needed instead of 10–20, dropping the NDT computation from ~50 ms to ~5 ms. This enables real-time ≥ 50 Hz localization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Module Ordering:** Many students write the order as *Perception → Localization → Planning*. The **correct** order is *Localization → Perception → Planning → Control*. **[-1 Mark]**
> 2. **NDT vs ICP:** Do not confuse the two. NDT uses a *Gaussian likelihood* per cell; ICP uses *point-to-point Euclidean distance*. Markers easily deduct 2 marks for swapping the formulas. **[-2 Marks]**
> 3. **Pure Pursuit Missing Bicycle Model:** Always state the bicycle model **first** before deriving the steering law. **[-1 Mark]**
> 4. **EKF vs KF:** Don't just say "EKF is for non-linear systems." Specify the **Jacobians** $\mathbf{F}_k, \mathbf{H}_k$ and the **Taylor expansion** concept. **[-1 Mark]**
> 5. **Units:** Always report steering in **radians**, position in **meters**, velocity in **m/s**. Mixed units (e.g., degrees and radians) lose 0.5 mark per instance.
> 6. **Diagram Quality:** Sketchy block diagrams without labeled arrows lose 1–2 marks. Always include **arrow labels** (e.g., `/points_raw`).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision one hour before the exam.**

- [ ] **Autoware** = open-source AD stack on **ROS 2**; primary development line is **Autoware Universe**.
- [ ] Original creators: **Tier IV, Japan (2015)**; current governance: **Autoware Foundation**.
- [ ] Canonical pipeline: **Sensors → Localization → Perception → Prediction → Planning → Control → Vehicle**.
- [ ] **NDT** voxel resolution: typically **1.0 m to 2.0 m**; cell model is **multivariate Gaussian** $\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)$.
- [ ] **NDT score function** = sum of exponentials of Mahalanobis distance — **maximized** via Newton optimization.
- [ ] **EKF sensor fusion** combines IMU (high-rate, drifts) + GNSS (low-rate, global) + LiDAR odometry (mid-rate, accurate locally).
- [ ] **Pure Pursuit** formula: $\delta = \arctan(2 L \sin \alpha / \ell_d)$ — derive from the bicycle model, not memorize blindly.
- [ ] **Speed-adaptive lookahead** $\ell_d = K_0 + K_v v$ keeps the controller stable from parking (~4 m) to highway (~14 m).
- [ ] **Lattice Planner** samples time–lateral-offset trajectories and picks the minimum-cost, kinematically-feasible one.
- [ ] **HD Map formats:** Autoware supports **Lanelet2** (preferred) and **OpenDRIVE** (legacy).
- [ ] **Sensor frequencies:** LiDAR 10–20 Hz, Camera 30 Hz, IMU 100–200 Hz, GNSS 1–10 Hz, Control loop 50–100 Hz.
- [ ] **PointPillars, CenterPoint, YOLO** are the most common 3D object detectors in Autoware's perception stack.
- [ ] **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** is the **advanced** alternative to Pure Pursuit, capable of obstacle-avoidance in the control layer.
- [ ] **Stop distance** $d_s = v t_r + v^2/(2\mu g)$ — used for emergency braking logic.
- [ ] **Centripetal acceleration** $a_c = v^2 \kappa \le 1.8$ m/s² is the passenger-comfort threshold.
- [ ] **Voxel grid downsampling** leaf size is typically **0.1 m to 0.2 m** for perception, **1 m to 2 m** for NDT localization.
- [ ] **NTU/ETRI-style diagram**: the Mermaid figures in Section 4 can be **redrawn by hand** in the exam for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apollo
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Apollo

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Baidu Apollo** is an open-source autonomous driving platform developed by **Baidu, Inc.** (China), first launched in **April 2017**, designed to provide a comprehensive, full-stack, and modular software + hardware + cloud services framework for the development, testing, and deployment of **Level 3 to Level 5** autonomous vehicles. It integrates high-definition (HD) maps, localization, perception, prediction, planning, and end-to-end control into a unified ROS (Robot Operating System)-based architecture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **OEMUT611**, Apollo is classified as a **Reference Autonomous Driving Stack (ADS)** — a benchmark open-source framework used to study the architectural separation between *perception-decision-action* loops in autonomous ground vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Apollo is studied as a representative case-study of an **integrated AV software platform**, comparing its layered architecture with industrial stacks like **Autoware** and **NVIDIA DriveWorks**. Expect direct theory questions on its architectural layers, sensor compatibility, and core functional modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of Apollo as the **"Android Operating System for Self-Driving Cars."** Just as Android gives smartphone manufacturers a ready-made operating system (so they only need to design the hardware), Apollo gives car manufacturers a ready-made *brain and nervous system* for autonomous driving — complete with eyes (cameras), ears (LiDAR), a memory (HD Maps), decision-making cortex (Planning), and motor control (Control Module).

- **Baidu = Google of self-driving cars** (provides the "OS")
- **Car OEM = Smartphone manufacturer** (uses Apollo to build their autonomous vehicle)
- **Open-source = free for anyone to inspect, modify, and deploy**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** As of 2024, Apollo has released over **10 major versions** (Apollo 1.0 → Apollo 10.0) and is used by **dozens of OEMs** including Geely, Toyota (China), Ford (test fleet), Hyundai, and numerous Robotaxi startups such as **Pony.ai, WeRide, and AutoX**.

### 1.3 Why Apollo Matters in Autonomous Vehicle Engineering
- **Open-source reference design** → reduces R&D cost from ~$1 billion to a fraction.
- **Industry-grade code quality** → battle-tested in real Robotaxi fleets (e.g., Apollo Go in Beijing, Wuhan, Chongqing).
- **Standardized interfaces** → any sensor/actuator with a compliant driver can plug into the stack.
- **Cloud-enabled** → supports **Apollo Studio**, simulation, data logging, and OTA (Over-The-Air) updates.
- **Academia-friendly** → used in KTU-affiliated research labs and B.Tech final-year projects.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
- **Operating frequency of control loop:** typically **10 Hz – 20 Hz**.
- **Localization accuracy target:** **< 10 cm** lateral, **< 20 cm** longitudinal.
- **Sensor latency budget:** **< 100 ms** end-to-end (perception → actuation).
- **Supported sensors:** Velodyne, Hesai, RoboSense (LiDAR); Continental, Delphi (Radar); Leopard, Sony (Cameras); NovAtel, u-blox (GNSS/IMU).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry SAE Level Mapping used by Apollo:**
> | Apollo Version | SAE Level Targeted | Key Capability |
> |---|---|---|
> | Apollo 1.0 | L2 | Closed-course lane keeping |
> | Apollo 3.0 | L3 | Urban autonomous driving (geo-fenced) |
> | Apollo 5.0 / 6.0 | L4 | Highway + city Robotaxi (geo-fenced) |
> | Apollo 7.0+ | L4 / L5 (partial) | Full driverless Robotaxi operation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of Apollo
Apollo follows a **4-Layer Functional Architecture** that cleanly separates concerns. This is the single most important concept from this topic for KTU exams.

**Layer 1 — Cloud Service Layer (Offline / Remote)**
- **HD Map generation** (using survey vehicles with LiDAR + cameras).
- **Simulation** (Apollo Studio, Dreamland, LGSVL).
- **Data Lake** (storing petabytes of driving logs).
- **OTA Updates** (over-the-air model + software pushes to vehicles).
- **DuerOS Voice / AI assistant integration** (for in-cabin HMI).

**Layer 2 — Open Software Layer (Onboard Core Stack)**
- **Apollo RTOS** (real-time operating system patched from Linux + Apollo Kernel).
- **Localization Module** — GNSS + IMU + LiDAR scan-matching.
- **Perception Module** — obstacle detection, lane detection, traffic light/sign recognition (using deep CNNs).
- **Prediction Module** — predicts the future trajectories of other agents (vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists).
- **Planning Module** — global route + behavioral + motion planning.
- **Control Module** — converts planned trajectory into steering, throttle, brake commands.
- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** — visualizes vehicle state for safety driver.

**Layer 3 — Reference Hardware Layer (Compute + Sensors)**
- **Compute Unit:** IPC (Industrial PC) with NVIDIA GPUs / Xilinx FPGAs / custom ASICs.
- **Sensors:** Camera, LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic, IMU, GNSS.
- **CAN Bus** for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) and actuation.
- **GPS/IMU fusion** for pose estimation.

**Layer 4 — Vehicle Platform Layer (Chassis + Drive-by-Wire)**
- The actual car (Lincoln MKZ, BAIC, Geely, Toyota etc.) retrofitted with:
  - **Drive-by-wire** interface (electronic throttle, brake, steering).
  - **CAN bus gateway** for Apollo to command actuators.

### 2.2 Why Each Layer Exists — The "Why" Behind the Design
- **Why cloud?** AV training needs millions of km of driving data — only cloud storage + GPU clusters can handle it.
- **Why separate software from hardware?** Modularity — the same software stack can run on different cars if the drive-by-wire interface is standardized.
- **Why a separate Perception module?** Perception is compute-heavy (deep learning) — isolating it allows GPU resource allocation.
- **Why Prediction separate from Planning?** Prediction is stochastic (probabilistic); Planning is deterministic — they have different algorithmic paradigms.
- **Why a Reference Hardware layer?** Without it, open-source code has no standard platform → fragmentation kills the ecosystem.

### 2.3 Core Functional Modules — Deep Dive

**(A) Localization Module**
- Uses **MSF (Multi-Sensor Fusion)** approach.
- Combines **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)**, **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)**, and **LiDAR scan-matching** against HD Maps.
- Output: 6-DoF pose $(x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw)$ at ~$100$ Hz.

**(B) Perception Module**
- 3D obstacle detection using **PointPillars / CenterPoint** (deep learning on LiDAR point clouds).
- Lane line detection via **CNN + RNN** on camera images.
- Traffic light detection using **YOLO-based** detectors.
- Sensor fusion: LiDAR + Radar + Camera (early, late, or deep fusion).

**(C) Prediction Module**
- Models other road agents as **trajectory sets** with probabilities.
- Uses **RNN/LSTM** or **VectorNet / HiVT** architectures.
- Output: probability distribution over possible future trajectories.

**(D) Planning Module (3-Stage Hierarchical Planner)**
1. **Routing** — A* or Dijkstra on road graph (lane-level).
2. **Behavioral Decision** — Finite State Machine (FSM) / POMDP for yielding, overtaking, stopping.
3. **Motion Planning** — Generates a smooth, collision-free trajectory using **EM Planner (Expectation-Maximization)** or **Lattice Planner**.

**(E) Control Module**
- Converts trajectory into actuator commands.
- Uses **PID + LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)** or **MPC (Model Predictive Control)**.
- Lateral control → steering angle $\delta$.
- Longitudinal control → throttle $\alpha$ and brake $\beta$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are written in **LaTeX** with explicit units. Critical for KTU numerical/descriptive problems.

| Concept | Equation | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle Kinematic Bicycle Model | $\dot{x} = v \cos(\psi)$, $\dot{y} = v \sin(\psi)$, $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v}{L}\tan(\delta)$ | $x,y$: position; $\psi$: heading; $v$: speed; $L$: wheelbase; $\delta$: steering angle | SI (m, m/s, rad) | Used in Apollo Control module |
| Steering from Curvature | $\delta = \arctan(L \cdot \kappa)$ | $\kappa$: path curvature | rad, $m^{-1}$ | Geometric relation |
| LQR Cost | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{N} (x_k^T Q x_k + u_k^T R u_k)$ | $Q,R$: weighting matrices | dimensionless | Optimal control weights |
| PID Control | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $e$: error; $K_p,K_i,K_d$: gains | varies | Used for longitudinal control |
| Localization Covariance (Kalman Filter) | $P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\vert k-1}$ | $P$: covariance; $K$: Kalman gain | $m^2$ | GNSS+IMU+LiDAR fusion |
| Sensor Coverage (Camera FOV) | $A_{cover} = d^2 \tan(\theta/2)$ | $d$: distance; $\theta$: FOV angle | $m^2$ | Forward coverage area |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students confuse the *vehicle dynamic* model (with slip angles $\alpha_f, \alpha_r$) with the *kinematic* model above. Apollo's **control module** typically uses **kinematic** model for low-speed urban driving (L4 Robotaxis) and **dynamic** model for high-speed highway (L3).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **OEMs** use Apollo to fast-track AV deployment (e.g., Geely's "Geometry" series).
- **Robotaxi operators** (Apollo Go) run commercial driverless service in 10+ Chinese cities.
- **Academia** uses Apollo for reproducible research on perception, planning algorithms.
- **Tier-1 suppliers** integrate Apollo-compatible sensors and drive-by-wire kits.
- **Last-mile delivery** (Neolix, JD.com) uses Apollo for autonomous delivery robots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Path Curvature to Steering Angle (Apollo Control)

The Apollo control module converts a planned path (defined by curvature $\kappa$) into a steering command. This derivation is **high-yield for KTU Part B 14-mark problems**.

**Step 1 — Reference Frame Definition**
We define the vehicle's center of gravity (CoG) at the origin, with $x$-axis pointing forward, $y$-axis pointing left. The steering angle $\delta$ acts on the front wheel, and $L$ is the wheelbase.

**Step 2 — Ackermann Steering Geometry Assumption**
For a four-wheel vehicle moving at low-to-moderate speeds, we collapse it into a **bicycle model** (two wheels: front and rear, single track). This is valid because Apollo's Robotaxi operates at city speeds (typically < 60 km/h).

**Step 3 — Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**
When the vehicle follows a circular path of radius $R$, the front wheel must be turned by an angle $\delta$ such that lines perpendicular to both wheels intersect at the ICR. From basic geometry:

$$
\tan(\delta) = \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Curvature Relation**
The curvature $\kappa$ of the path is defined as:

$$
\kappa = \frac{1}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Curvature**
Replacing $R = 1/\kappa$ in the steering equation:

$$
\tan(\delta) = L \cdot \kappa
$$

**Step 6 — Solving for Steering Angle**

$$
\delta = \arctan(L \cdot \kappa)
$$

**Step 7 — Control Loop Application**
The Apollo Control module receives a planned trajectory $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=0}^{N}$ at each tick. It fits a polynomial or uses finite differences to estimate local curvature:

$$
\kappa_k \approx \frac{\dot{x}_k \ddot{y}_k - \dot{y}_k \ddot{x}_k}{(\dot{x}_k^2 + \dot{y}_k^2)^{3/2}}
$$

**Step 8 — Final Steering Command**

$$
\delta_{cmd} = \arctan\!\left(L \cdot \frac{\dot{x}_k \ddot{y}_k - \dot{y}_k \ddot{x}_k}{(\dot{x}_k^2 + \dot{y}_k^2)^{3/2}}\right)
$$

This is fed to the drive-by-wire interface at **$\mathbf{10\text{ Hz}}$** in Apollo's default configuration.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Apollo vs. Competitor Platforms

| Parameter | **Baidu Apollo** | **Autoware (Tier IV, Japan)** | **NVIDIA DriveWorks** | **Tesla Autonomy Stack** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open-source | ✅ Yes (Apache 2.0) | ✅ Yes (BSD) | ⚠️ Partial SDK | ❌ Proprietary |
| OS Base | Linux + Apollo RTOS | ROS 1/2 | QNX / Linux | Custom Linux |
| Primary Language | C++ / Python | C++ / Python | C++ / CUDA | C++ / Python |
| HD Map Dependency | Heavy (mandatory) | Heavy (mandatory) | Optional | Map-free (vision-only) |
| Compute Platform | Industrial PC (NVIDIA GPU) | Industrial PC (NVIDIA GPU) | DRIVE AGX (custom SoC) | Custom FSD HW3/HW4 |
| LiDAR Support | Native | Native | Native | ❌ Vision-only |
| Camera Support | Native | Native | Native | Native (primary) |
| Target SAE Level | L4 | L4 | L2–L5 | L2+ (supervised) |
| Real-World Robotaxi | ✅ Apollo Go | ✅ Tier IV (Japan) | ⚠️ Reference only | ❌ No |
| Learning Curve | High | Very High | Medium | Proprietary (not accessible) |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Module 3:** If a question asks "Compare Apollo and Autoware" or "List Apollo's main modules", refer directly to the row mappings above. This matrix is the **single most exam-relevant visual summary** of the topic.

---

### 3.3 Mathematical Workflow of Apollo Localization (Kalman Fusion)

Apollo's localization module fuses **GNSS** (absolute position, noisy), **IMU** (relative, drifts over time), and **LiDAR** (relative to HD map, accurate but map-dependent).

**State vector:**

$$
X = [x,\ y,\ z,\ roll,\ pitch,\ yaw,\ \dot{x},\ \dot{y},\ \dot{z}]^T
$$

**Prediction Step (IMU-driven):**

$$
X_{k|k-1} = f(X_{k-1}, u_k)
$$

where $u_k = [\text{acc}_x, \text{acc}_y, \text{acc}_z, \omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z]^T$ from IMU.

**Measurement Update (GNSS + LiDAR):**

$$
K_k = P_{k|k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k|k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}
$$

$$
X_{k|k} = X_{k|k-1} + K_k (Z_k - H_k X_{k|k-1})
$$

**Final pose output:**

$$
\hat{\psi} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2(w y_{quat} + x y_{quat} z_{quat})}{1 - 2(y_{quat}^2 + z_{quat}^2)}\right)
$$

This yields localization with **< 10 cm** lateral accuracy on well-mapped roads.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall Tip:** A **Kalman Filter** is *prediction + correction* — Prediction uses IMU (high freq, drifts), Correction uses GNSS/LiDAR (low freq, accurate). The fusion gives the best of both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Total end-to-end latency budget in Apollo:** approximately **$\mathbf{100\text{–}120\ \text{ms}}$** for safe L4 operation. This is a standard KTU viva question — *"Why is latency critical in AVs?"* → because at 60 km/h (16.67 m/s), a 100 ms delay translates to **1.67 m** of uncommanded vehicle travel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Baidu Apollo. List any four core functional modules of its open software stack.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Baidu Apollo** is an open-source, full-stack autonomous driving platform launched by Baidu in 2017. It provides a complete reference architecture for developing and deploying L3–L5 self-driving vehicles, integrating hardware, software, and cloud services.

**Four core functional modules of the open software stack:**

1. **Localization Module** — fuses GNSS, IMU, and LiDAR scan-matching against HD maps to provide precise vehicle pose.
2. **Perception Module** — detects obstacles, lanes, traffic signs, and traffic lights using LiDAR + camera + radar deep-learning pipelines.
3. **Planning Module** — performs route planning, behavioral decision-making, and motion trajectory generation.
4. **Control Module** — converts the planned trajectory into steering, throttle, and brake actuator commands using PID / LQR / MPC.

*(Bonus point for mentioning Prediction Module.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the Apollo Cloud Service Layer and the Reference Hardware Layer. Why are they kept separate?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Cloud Service Layer | Reference Hardware Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Off-board (remote servers) | Onboard (in-vehicle) |
| **Functions** | HD map generation, simulation, data logging, OTA updates | Sensor acquisition, compute (IPC+GPU), CAN bus |
| **Connectivity** | Requires 4G/5G | Operates offline |
| **Resources** | Petabyte-scale storage, GPU clusters | Limited (auto-grade compute) |

**Why kept separate?** This separation enables **scalability** (cloud resources are elastic), **modularity** (sensors can be swapped without rewriting cloud code), **safety** (critical real-time tasks run onboard even without network), and **continuous learning** (data uploaded to cloud improves models, which are pushed back via OTA).

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **four-layer architecture** of the Baidu Apollo autonomous driving platform with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of each layer. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Planning Module** of Apollo in detail. Discuss the three stages of hierarchical planning (Routing, Behavioral, Motion) and explain the EM Planner algorithm briefly. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

The Baidu Apollo platform follows a **four-layer functional architecture** that separates the AV system into distinct concerns:

**Layer 1 — Cloud Service Layer:**
This is the off-board, internet-connected layer. It performs HD map generation using Baidu's survey fleet, runs large-scale simulation in Apollo Studio, stores driving logs in a data lake, and pushes Over-The-Air (OTA) updates to vehicles. It also hosts AI services such as DuerOS voice assistant.

**Layer 2 — Open Software Layer (Onboard Core Stack):**
This is the real-time onboard software. It contains the Apollo RTOS (a real-time-patched Linux kernel), and five core modules — **Localization, Perception, Prediction, Planning, and Control** — each running as independent ROS nodes communicating via message passing. The HMI module provides visualization.

**Layer 3 — Reference Hardware Layer:**
This layer defines a standardized set of sensors and compute hardware. Recommended sensors include multi-beam LiDAR (Velodyne/Hesai), cameras (Leopard/Sony), millimeter-wave radars, IMU, and GNSS receivers. Compute is typically an Industrial PC (IPC) equipped with NVIDIA GPUs for deep-learning inference.

**Layer 4 — Vehicle Platform Layer:**
This is the physical vehicle with retrofitted drive-by-wire capability (electronic throttle, brake, steering) and a CAN bus gateway that allows the Apollo software stack to command the actuators in real time. Vehicles used include Lincoln MKZ, BAIC, and Geely models.

**Why this architecture?** Modularity — each layer can be upgraded independently. Scalability — the same software stack runs on multiple vehicle platforms. Safety — critical real-time functions are isolated from non-critical cloud tasks.

*(Refer to Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic.)*

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

The Apollo **Planning Module** is responsible for deciding *where to go* and *how to get there* safely. It operates in a **three-stage hierarchical** manner:

**Stage 1 — Routing (Global Planning):**
Inputs: start, goal, HD map. Algorithm: A* or Dijkstra on a lane-level road graph. Output: a sequence of road segments (lane IDs) from origin to destination. Latency: typically one-shot before the trip.

**Stage 2 — Behavioral Decision (Scenario Planning):**
Inputs: routing output, perception result, prediction result, traffic rules. The Apollo planner uses a hierarchical **Finite State Machine (FSM)** combined with **scenario-based decision** logic. It decides high-level intents such as CRUISE, YIELD, OVERTAKE, STOP, LANE-CHANGE. It considers the predicted trajectories of other agents and chooses a safe maneuver class.

**Stage 3 — Motion Planning (Trajectory Generation):**
This stage generates a **smooth, collision-free, kinematically feasible trajectory** for the next 8 seconds. Apollo's default is the **EM Planner (Expectation-Maximization Planner)**, which performs:

- **E-step (Expectation):** Estimate the probability that each lane is feasible given current obstacles.
- **M-step (Maximization):** Optimize a smooth polynomial trajectory along the chosen lane, minimizing jerk, lateral offset, and time-to-collision.

The EM Planner operates in **SL-coordinate (Station-Lateral)** frame relative to a reference lane, which simplifies obstacle avoidance. It outputs a trajectory $\{(x(t), y(t), v(t))\}_{t=0}^{T}$.

**Output:** The trajectory is passed to the **Control Module** for execution.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional pipeline** of Apollo's onboard stack — from sensors to actuators. Indicate typical latencies at each stage. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare Baidu Apollo with **Autoware** and **NVIDIA DriveWorks**. Discuss at least **six comparison points**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

The Apollo onboard functional pipeline processes data from sensors, makes sense of the environment, plans a safe trajectory, and commands the actuators. The pipeline stages are:

**1. Sensor Acquisition (t = 0 ms):**
Multiple sensors capture raw data simultaneously:
- Cameras → 30 fps image frames.
- LiDAR → 10 Hz 3D point clouds.
- Radar → 20 Hz object tracks.
- IMU → 100 Hz acceleration + angular velocity.
- GNSS → 10 Hz position fixes.

**2. Perception (t = 0–30 ms):**
- 3D object detection on LiDAR using PointPillars CNN.
- Lane detection on camera using CNN+RNN.
- Sensor fusion (LiDAR + Camera + Radar) yields a unified obstacle list.

**3. Localization (t = 0–20 ms, parallel to Perception):**
- Kalman filter fuses GNSS + IMU + LiDAR scan-match against HD map.
- Output: 6-DoF pose at 100 Hz.

**4. Prediction (t = 30–50 ms):**
- LSTM-based model predicts trajectories of other agents for next 3 seconds.
- Each agent gets a probability distribution over multiple possible futures.

**5. Planning (t = 50–100 ms):**
- Routing (lane-level path).
- Behavioral decision (FSM/POMDP intent).
- EM Planner generates smooth trajectory in SL-frame.

**6. Control (t = 100–110 ms):**
- Converts trajectory to steering, throttle, brake using LQR + PID.
- Commands sent to drive-by-wire via CAN bus.

**Total latency: ~110 ms.** At city speeds, this means about **1.8 m** of uncommanded travel, well within safety margins due to built-in lookahead.

*(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.)*

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

| Comparison Point | **Baidu Apollo** | **Autoware (Tier IV)** | **NVIDIA DriveWorks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Open-source Model** | Apache 2.0 (full) | BSD (full) | Proprietary SDK + free toolkit |
| **2. Operating System Base** | Apollo RTOS (Linux patched) | ROS 1 / ROS 2 | QNX / Linux / DriveOS |
| **3. HD Map Dependency** | Mandatory | Mandatory | Optional |
| **4. LiDAR Support** | Native multi-LiDAR | Native multi-LiDAR | Native (calibration tools) |
| **5. Primary Use Case** | Robotaxi (L4), OEM pilot | Robotaxi (L4), research | Reference platform for OEMs/Tier-1 |
| **6. Real-World Robotaxi Deployment** | Yes (Apollo Go, 10+ cities) | Yes (Tier IV Nagoya) | Reference only |
| **7. Compute Hardware** | x86 IPC + NVIDIA GPU | x86 IPC + NVIDIA GPU | Drive AGX Pegasus/Orin (custom SoC) |

**Summary:** Apollo and Autoware are similar in being **open-source, HD-map-dependent L4 stacks**; Apollo is more production-ready (Robotaxi operations), while Autoware is more research-friendly. DriveWorks is a **commercial toolkit** for OEMs that prefer curated, NVIDIA-optimized software over open-source maintenance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Apollo Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing layers:** Many students mix up Layer 2 (software) and Layer 3 (hardware). Remember: **Layer 3 is the physical sensor/compute hardware**, not the software that processes it.
> 2. **Forgetting the Vehicle Layer:** Layer 4 (chassis + drive-by-wire) is often skipped, costing 1–2 marks. Apollo is **not** purely software — it requires a drive-by-wire retrofitted vehicle.
> 3. **Mis-stating versioning:** Apollo 1.0 is **closed-track, fixed-route** — NOT "fully autonomous city driving". The L4 capability begins around Apollo 5.0+ (2019+).
> 4. **Naming only 2–3 modules:** Listing fewer than 5 core modules (Localization, Perception, Prediction, Planning, Control) leads to incomplete answers.
> 5. **Mixing Apollo with Tesla FSD:** Tesla does **NOT** use Apollo — Tesla uses its proprietary vertical stack with vision-only (no LiDAR, no HD maps). Conflating these loses marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Apollo** is Baidu's open-source **L3–L4** autonomous driving platform, launched **April 2017**.
- **4-Layer Architecture:** Cloud Service → Open Software → Reference Hardware → Vehicle Platform.
- **5 Core Onboard Modules:** Localization, Perception, Prediction, Planning, Control (+ HMI).
- **Localization** uses **Kalman fusion** of **GNSS + IMU + LiDAR**, achieving **< 10 cm** lateral accuracy.
- **Perception** uses deep learning: **PointPillars** for LiDAR, **YOLO** for cameras/traffic lights, **CNNs** for lane detection.
- **Prediction** uses **LSTM/VectorNet** for agent trajectory forecasting.
- **Planning** uses hierarchical **Routing → Behavioral → Motion (EM Planner)**.
- **Control** uses **PID + LQR/MPC** to output steering $\delta$, throttle, brake.
- **Latency budget:** total end-to-end **~110 ms** at **10 Hz** control loop.
- **Bicycle model:** $\delta = \arctan(L \cdot \kappa)$ — high-yield formula for KTU.
- **Apollo requires HD Maps + LiDAR** (unlike Tesla's vision-only stack).
- **Real deployment:** **Apollo Go Robotaxi** in 10+ Chinese cities (Beijing, Wuhan, Chongqing, Shenzhen).
- **Latest versions:** Apollo 9.0 (2023), Apollo 10.0 (2024) — features foundation-model integration.
- **Comparison anchors:** Apollo (open-source, L4, HD-map, LiDAR-heavy) vs. **Autoware** (similar, research-oriented) vs. **DriveWorks** (commercial, NVIDIA-optimized) vs. **Tesla FSD** (proprietary, vision-only).
- **License:** Apache 2.0 — free for academic and commercial use with attribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line KTU Viva Answer:** *"Apollo is a four-layer open-source L4 platform whose onboard stack runs five ROS-based modules (Localization, Perception, Prediction, Planning, Control) on a drive-by-wire retrofitted vehicle, fusing GNSS, IMU, LiDAR, and camera data to perform geo-fenced driverless Robotaxi operation."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Software Development** in the context of Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) refers to the systematic design, implementation, integration, testing, and validation of the complete software stack that enables perception, decision-making, motion control, and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication. The software stack is modular, layered, and follows international standards such as **ISO 21434 (cybersecurity)** and **ISO 26262 (functional safety)**.

**Simulation Tools** are software platforms that create high-fidelity virtual replicas of vehicles, sensors, road environments, and traffic participants, allowing algorithm validation, scenario testing, and regression testing *before* deployment to real hardware. They form the backbone of the **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)** and **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** development workflows mandated by the **V-Model** of automotive software engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must be able to differentiate between *middleware* (ROS/ROS 2, DDS), *modelling environments* (MATLAB/Simulink, CarSim), and *driving simulators* (CARLA, Gazebo, PreScan). Examiner frequently tests the comparative tool-chain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Virtual Crash Test Dummy"

Imagine you are an architect designing a skyscraper in a city prone to earthquakes. Before pouring a single ton of concrete, you build a **digital twin** of the building, simulate a hundred earthquakes, hurricane-force winds, and fires — all inside a computer. The building is never physically built until the digital twin has survived every possible disaster.

**Software Development and Simulation Tools for AVs work identically:**

- The *skyscraper* is the autonomous vehicle's software stack.
- The *earthquake simulations* are edge-case driving scenarios (a child darting across the road, a sensor failure at night, GPS dropout in a tunnel).
- The *architect* is the embedded software engineer.
- The *computer* is a high-fidelity simulator such as **CARLA**, **Gazebo**, or **MATLAB/Simulink**.

In simple English: **we write the "brain" of the car on a computer, and we test that brain inside a *virtual world* thousands of times faster (and cheaper) than real-world driving.**

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Memory Retention)

- **HIL Sample Rate:** **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (real-time sensor injection)
- **SIL Simulation Step:** **10 ms – 100 ms** (slower than real-time)
- **LiDAR Points per Second:** **~2.4 million pts/s** (typical 128-channel unit)
- **Camera Frame Rate:** **30 fps – 60 fps**
- **Camera Resolution:** **1280 × 960 px** to **3840 × 2160 px (4K)**
- **Vehicle Dynamics Update:** **100 Hz – 200 Hz**
- **V2X Latency Target:** **< 20 ms** (5G URLLC class)
- **ROS 2 Default Middleware:** **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** with QoS policies

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** Real-time control loops run at **kHz** (controller), sensor fusion at **~100 Hz**, and high-level planning at **~10 Hz**. This *3-tier frequency hierarchy* is a high-yield KTU exam point.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Software Architecture of an Autonomous Vehicle

An autonomous vehicle software stack is typically divided into **five layers**, each running on potentially different hardware (SoCs) with different operating systems.

### Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)
- **Why:** Decouples the application code from the physical sensor/actuator wiring.
- **How:** Provides a uniform API (e.g., POSIX sockets, libgpiod, SPI drivers) so that swapping a Velodyne LiDAR for a Hesai LiDAR does *not* require rewriting perception code.
- **KTU Exam Hook:** Question on *"Why is HAL necessary in an AV stack?"* is worth **7 marks** in Part B.

### Layer 2 — Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)
- **Why:** Linux is non-deterministic; braking decisions must execute within **bounded latency (< 5 ms)**.
- **How:** Uses *preemptive priority scheduling* on kernels such as **PREEMPT_RT**, **QNX Neutrino**, **VxWorks**, or **AUTOSAR OS**.
- **Real-World Utility:** Bosch, Continental, and Aptiv production ECUs run QNX in 100% of production ADAS modules in 2024.

### Layer 3 — Middleware (ROS 2 / DDS / Cyber RT)
- **Why:** Provides a *publish-subscribe* message bus so that 50+ software nodes (camera, LiDAR, planner, controller) can communicate without direct wiring.
- **How:** Uses **Data Distribution Service (DDS)** standard. Each message has a *Quality of Service (QoS)* profile — e.g., `RELIABLE`, `BEST_EFFORT`, `DEADLINE`.
- **Example Nodes:** `/lidar/points`, `/camera/image_raw`, `/cmd_vel`, `/vehicle/odom`.

### Layer 4 — Functional Modules (Perception, Localization, Planning, Control)
- **Perception:** Object detection, lane detection, semantic segmentation (CNN-based).
- **Localization:** GNSS + IMU + LiDAR SLAM (e.g., NDT, ICP).
- **Planning:** Global route (A*, RRT*) → local trajectory (DWA, MPC).
- **Control:** Longitudinal (PID) + Lateral (Stanley, Pure Pursuit, MPC).
- **KTU Key Equation:** Bicycle model governs the lateral control loop.

### Layer 5 — Application / Cloud Services
- **Why:** Fleet learning, HD-map updates, OTA (Over-The-Air) software upgrades.
- **How:** 4G/5G telematics to OEM backend, e.g., Tesla's *Shadow Mode* data collection.

## 2.2 Development Methodologies

| Methodology | Acronym | Loop Cycle | KTU Use-Case |
|-------------|---------|------------|--------------|
| Model-in-the-Loop | **MIL** | Plant model + controller, both simulated | Early algorithm validation |
| Software-in-the-Loop | **SIL** | Generated C/C++ code tested on host PC | Unit + integration test |
| Hardware-in-the-Loop | **HIL** | Real ECU + simulated plant | Regression, fault injection |
| Driver-in-the-Loop | **DIL** | Real human in a simulator | HMI / UX validation |
| Vehicle-in-the-Loop | **VIL** | Real vehicle + simulated traffic | Final acceptance test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** **M – S – H – D – V** (Milestones in verification maturity)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bicycle Model (Vehicle Kinematics)

The *bicycle model* replaces a four-wheeled vehicle with a two-wheeled equivalent: one front wheel and one rear wheel placed at the midpoints of the respective axles, separated by the wheelbase **L**.

### Step 1 — State Variable Definition
Let the vehicle state be defined by the pose $(x, y, \psi)$ in the global frame plus the longitudinal velocity **v**. The control inputs are the front-wheel steering angle **δ** and the longitudinal acceleration **a**.

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \psi \\ v \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta \\ a \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Kinematic Constraint (No-Slip, Rear Axle)
The rear wheel is non-steering and rolls without lateral slip. Hence the velocity vector at the rear axle is purely longitudinal:

$$
\dot{x}_{r} = v \cos(\psi), \qquad \dot{y}_{r} = v \sin(\psi)
$$

where the subscript *r* refers to the rear-axle midpoint. We will drop the subscript and take the *rear axle as the vehicle reference point* — this is the standard convention.

### Step 3 — Heading Rate Derivation
The front wheel is located at position $(x + L\cos\psi, \; y + L\sin\psi)$. Because the velocity at the front axle must also be aligned with the steered wheel direction, we equate the cross-product of the two velocity vectors to the angular velocity times the wheelbase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\psi} \cdot L &= v \sin(\delta) \quad \text{(rigid-body rotation about rear axle)} \\
\Rightarrow \dot{\psi} &= \dfrac{v \sin(\delta)}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

For *small* steering angles, $\sin(\delta) \approx \tan(\delta)$, yielding the most-quoted form:

$$
\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v \tan(\delta)}{L}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Continuous-Time State Equation

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\psi} \\ \dot{v} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v \cos(\psi) \\ v \sin(\psi) \\ \dfrac{v \tan(\delta)}{L} \\ a \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Discretization for Embedded Implementation
At a controller sample time $T_s = 0.01$ s (100 Hz), we apply forward Euler:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_{k} + T_{s} \cdot v_{k} \cos(\psi_{k}) \\
y_{k+1} &= y_{k} + T_{s} \cdot v_{k} \sin(\psi_{k}) \\
\psi_{k+1} &= \psi_{k} + T_{s} \cdot \dfrac{v_{k} \tan(\delta_{k})}{L} \\
v_{k+1} &= v_{k} + T_{s} \cdot a_{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in simulation:** The bicycle model is the *plant model* used inside the HIL simulator. Without it, the entire controller-in-the-loop test would be running blind.

## 3.2 Derivation of the LiDAR Measurement Model

### Step 1 — Geometric Range
Consider a stationary landmark at $(x_t, y_t)$ and a LiDAR sensor at $(x_s, y_s)$ in the **sensor frame**:

$$
r = \sqrt{(x_{t} - x_{s})^{2} + (y_{t} - y_{s})^{2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Bearing (Azimuth)
The polar angle of the target, measured counter-clockwise from the sensor's x-axis:

$$
\theta = \operatorname{atan2}(y_{t} - y_{s}, \; x_{t} - x_{s})
$$

### Step 3 — Sensor Noise Model
Real LiDAR exhibits (a) range-dependent noise, (b) Gaussian pixel-quantization error, and (c) environmental noise (rain, fog). The simplest first-order model is:

$$
r_{\text{meas}} = r + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{r}^{2}), \qquad \sigma_{r} = a_{0} + a_{1} r
$$

where $a_0, a_1$ are vendor-specific constants. For a *Velodyne VLP-32C*, $a_0 = 0.03$ m and $a_1 = 0.001$.

### Step 4 — Inverse Sensor Model (From Range-Bearing to Cartesian)
Inside the perception pipeline, the raw polar measurement is converted to a 3-D point cloud:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{point}} &= r_{\text{meas}} \cos(\theta) \\
y_{\text{point}} &= r_{\text{meas}} \sin(\theta) \\
z_{\text{point}} &= r_{\text{meas}} \sin(\phi_{\text{elev}})
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\phi_{\text{elev}}$ is the vertical channel index encoded by the multi-beam laser.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Linear Kalman Filter Prediction Step

The Kalman Filter is the workhorse of **sensor fusion** for vehicle state estimation. Below is the complete derivation of the *prediction* and *update* stages.

### Step 1 — System Model
We assume a linear, time-invariant system:

$$
x_{k} = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k} + w_{k-1}, \qquad w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)
$$

$$
z_{k} = H x_{k} + v_{k}, \qquad v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)
$$

where $x_k$ is the $n \times 1$ state, $u_k$ is the $m \times 1$ input, $z_k$ is the $p \times 1$ measurement.

### Step 2 — A Priori State Estimate
Using the system model and the previous posterior estimate $\hat{x}_{k-1}$:

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_{k}
$$

### Step 3 — A Priori Covariance
Linearising the error-propagation equation $\tilde{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A \tilde{x}_{k-1} + w_{k-1}$, we get:

$$
P_{k\mid k-1} = A P_{k-1} A^{T} + Q
$$

### Step 4 — Innovation (Measurement Residual)

$$
\tilde{y}_{k} = z_{k} - H \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}
$$

### Step 5 — Innovation Covariance

$$
S_{k} = H P_{k\mid k-1} H^{T} + R
$$

### Step 6 — Optimal Kalman Gain (Minimize Posterior Covariance Trace)
Set $\partial \operatorname{tr}(P_k) / \partial K_k = 0$. The result is:

$$
K_{k} = P_{k\mid k-1} H^{T} S_{k}^{-1}
$$

### Step 7 — Posterior State Update

$$
\hat{x}_{k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_{k} \tilde{y}_{k}
$$

### Step 8 — Posterior Covariance (Joseph form, numerically stable)

$$
P_{k} = (I - K_{k} H) P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Derive the Kalman Filter update equations and apply them to a 1-D radar tracking problem." Memorize all 8 steps in the order shown — this is the *exact* order examiners expect.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Pure Pursuit Steering

**Given:** Wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m, vehicle speed $v = 10$ m/s, lookahead distance $l_d = 8$ m, angle to lookahead point $\alpha = 0.15$ rad.

**Find:** Front-wheel steering angle $\delta$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2 L \sin(\alpha)}{l_{d}}\right) \\
&= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 2.5 \times \sin(0.15)}{8}\right) \\
&= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{5 \times 0.1494}{8}\right) \\
&= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.7470}{8}\right) \\
&= \arctan(0.0934) \\
&\approx 0.0932 \text{ rad} \approx 5.34^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A small positive steering angle means the vehicle must steer *left* by 5.34° to intercept the lookahead point — this is the core principle of *geometric path tracking*.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Simulator

| Scenario | Recommended Tool | Justification |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| Early algorithm R&D (1 person, 1 laptop) | **Gazebo + ROS 2** | Free, fast, large community |
| Photorealistic urban scenario perception testing | **CARLA** | Built-in Unreal Engine graphics |
| Real-time controller HIL with < 1 ms latency | **dSPACE + CarSim** | Production-grade HIL |
| ADAS validation against Euro NCAP protocols | **PreScan** | Vendor-supplied Euro NCAP library |
| Vehicle dynamics only (no traffic) | **CarSim / TruckSim** | 45+ DOF high-fidelity model |
| V2X / 5G URLLC latency testing | **VIRES VTD** | Native ETSI ITS-G5 stack |
| Control theory coursework | **MATLAB/Simulink** | Symbolic + numerical toolbox |
| Mass-market education | **Webots** | Easy GUI, NXT, e-puck, TurtleBot |
| Custom HD environment | **Unreal Engine + AirSim** | Open API, AAA graphics |
| Fleet management simulation | **MATLAB + AnyLogic** | Discrete-event + agent-based |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define the term "Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) simulation" and state two advantages over Software-in-the-Loop (SIL) testing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** HIL is a real-time simulation technique in which a *real* Electronic Control Unit (ECU) is connected to a *simulated* plant (vehicle dynamics, sensors, environment) running on a dedicated real-time processor (e.g., dSPACE SCALEXIO). The ECU receives analog/digital signals (PWM, CAN, SPI) indistinguishable from those of a real vehicle, allowing closed-loop testing of the production hardware.

**Two Advantages (1 mark each — any two):**
1. Tests the *actual* production ECU hardware — catching PCB-layout, EMI, and driver-software bugs that SIL cannot.
2. Enables *fault injection* (sensor dropout, CAN bus-off) that would be dangerous on a real vehicle.
3. Provides deterministic real-time behaviour at **1–10 kHz** loop rates, matching production timing.
4. Allows **24 × 7 regression testing** without risk to human life.

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Supplementary)]**
*Differentiate between ROS 1 and ROS 2. List any three technical differences.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference):**

| # | ROS 1 | ROS 2 |
|---|-------|-------|
| 1 | Custom XML-RPC + TCP/UDP transport | **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** standard |
| 2 | Single-master, no real-time guarantees | **Decentralised, real-time** with QoS policies |
| 3 | Limited security (no encryption) | **SROS 2** — authenticated, encrypted DDS |
| 4 | No built-in lifecycle management | **Managed/lifecycle nodes** with explicit state machines |
| 5 | Only Linux (Ubuntu) | **Linux, Windows, macOS, RTOS** (QNX, VxWorks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** KTU 2024 scheme allows the student to answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Both questions below are designed with **7 + 7 = 14 mark sub-parts** matching the official template.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Question, Module 3)]**
*(a)* Explain the layered software architecture of an autonomous vehicle with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of middleware in this architecture. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With the aid of mathematical derivation, explain the **bicycle model** of vehicle kinematics. State two limitations of this model in simulating a real autonomous vehicle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The autonomous vehicle software stack is structured into **five functional layers** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** (1 mark): Provides uniform APIs for sensors (LiDAR, camera, radar, GNSS, IMU) and actuators (steering, throttle, brake). Example: ROS 2 driver packages for Velodyne LiDAR.

2. **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** (1 mark): Ensures deterministic scheduling with bounded latency. Examples: QNX Neutrino, VxWorks, Linux with PREEMPT_RT patch. Brake-by-wire control loop runs at **200 Hz** with jitter < **1 ms**.

3. **Middleware (ROS 2 / DDS)** (1 mark): Implements publish-subscribe communication between 50+ software nodes. Uses Data Distribution Service (DDS) standard with Quality of Service (QoS) policies — RELIABLE, BEST_EFFORT, DEADLINE.

4. **Functional Modules** (2 marks): Perception (CNN-based object detection), Localisation (NDT SLAM), Planning (A*, RRT*, MPC), Control (PID, Stanley, Pure Pursuit).

5. **Application / Cloud Services** (1 mark): Fleet learning, HD-map updates, Over-The-Air (OTA) software upgrades.

**Role of Middleware (1 mark):** Middleware abstracts inter-process communication, handles message serialisation (CDR/Protobuf), discovery (UDP multicast), and QoS negotiation. Without middleware, every module would require bespoke point-to-point socket programming — a maintenance nightmare.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Bicycle Model Derivation (5 marks):**
The bicycle model consolidates a four-wheeled vehicle into a two-wheel equivalent separated by wheelbase **L**. State variables are $(x, y, \psi, v)$ and inputs $(\delta, a)$.

The three governing equations are (re-derived for exam answer):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos(\psi) \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin(\psi) \\
\dot{\psi} &= \dfrac{v \tan(\delta)}{L} \\
\dot{v} &= a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation steps (write the following five lines):**
1. Define rear-axle reference point with non-slipping constraint.
2. State velocity vector at rear axle: $\dot{x} = v\cos\psi$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\psi$.
3. Use rigid-body rotation: $\dot{\psi} = v\sin\delta / L$.
4. Small-angle approximation: $\sin\delta \approx \tan\delta$.
5. Final continuous-time state equation (above).

**Two Limitations (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Neglects tyre-slip dynamics:** Real vehicles exhibit lateral slip at high lateral accelerations ($a_y > 0.3g$); the kinematic model becomes invalid.
2. **Assumes flat ground:** The bicycle model fails on 3-D terrains (off-road AGVs, mining vehicles) where pitch and roll affect sensor coverage.
3. **No load transfer:** Weight shift under braking is ignored, leading to overconfident braking simulations.
4. **Single-track assumption:** Cannot capture four-wheel-steering vehicles (e.g., crab-walk AGVs in warehouses).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question, Module 3)]**
*(a)* Compare the major automotive simulation tools (MATLAB/Simulink, Gazebo, CARLA, PreScan, CarSim) across **five parameters** of your choice. Recommend the most suitable tool-chain for (i) a B.Tech final-year project, (ii) a Tier-1 OEM production programme. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With mathematical derivation, explain the **Linear Kalman Filter** prediction and update steps. Apply the filter to a 1-D radar tracking problem where the radar measures the *range* of a stationary object at $x=50$ m with measurement noise standard deviation $\sigma_r = 1$ m, given the initial estimate $\hat{x}_0 = 45$ m, $P_0 = 5$ m², and process noise $Q = 0.1$ m². Compute the posterior estimate after one update. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks — 1 mark per row, full table needed for full marks):**

| Parameter | MATLAB/Simulink | Gazebo | CARLA | PreScan | CarSim |
|-----------|----------------|--------|-------|---------|--------|
| **License** | Commercial | Open-source (Apache 2.0) | Open-source (MIT) | Commercial | Commercial |
| **Vehicle Dynamics** | Medium (requires Vehicle Dynamics Block-set) | Medium (uses ODE engine) | Low (uses simplified bicycle model) | Medium | Very High (45+ DOF) |
| **Sensor Simulation** | Custom (requires code) | Built-in (LiDAR, camera plugins) | Built-in (realistic) | Excellent (V2X + radar) | None (vehicle only) |
| **Real-Time Capable** | Yes (with xPC Target) | Yes (with Ignition Gazebo) | No (offline only) | Yes | Yes |
| **3D Graphics** | Basic (Simulink 3D) | Moderate | Photorealistic (Unreal) | Good | Basic |
| **Best For** | Control theory, MPC | ROS integration, research | Perception, ML training | ADAS, Euro NCAP | High-fidelity vehicle dynamics |

**Recommendation (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

**(i) B.Tech Final-Year Project:** Use **Gazebo + ROS 2 + MATLAB/Simulink** co-simulation. Gazebo is free, has a steep learning curve but vast YouTube tutorials, and integrates seamlessly with ROS 2 (Humble). MATLAB/Simulink is used for control algorithm design (MPC, PID) which is then exported as C code via Simulink Coder. This combination costs **₹0 + student MATLAB licence** and is industry-aligned.

**(ii) Tier-1 OEM Production Programme:** Use **PreScan + CarSim + dSPACE HIL**. PreScan provides Euro NCAP-compliant scenarios, CarSim provides 45-DOF vehicle dynamics, and dSPACE provides production-grade HIL with ISO 26262 certification. This tool-chain is what Bosch, Continental, and Aptiv deploy.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Kalman Filter Derivation (4 marks):** Write the eight steps from Section 3.3 in correct order.

**Numerical Application (3 marks):**

Given: $\hat{x}_0 = 45$ m, $P_0 = 5$ m², $Q = 0.1$ m², $R = \sigma_r^2 = 1$ m², $z_1 = 50$ m. For a 1-D stationary scalar system: $A = 1, H = 1$.

**Step 1 — Prediction:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1\mid 0} = A \hat{x}_{0} = 1 \times 45 = 45 \text{ m}
$$

$$
P_{1\mid 0} = A P_{0} A^{T} + Q = 1 \times 5 \times 1 + 0.1 = 5.1 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation:**

$$
\tilde{y}_{1} = z_{1} - H \hat{x}_{1\mid 0} = 50 - 45 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation Covariance:**

$$
S_{1} = H P_{1\mid 0} H^{T} + R = 1 \times 5.1 \times 1 + 1 = 6.1 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_{1} = P_{1\mid 0} H^{T} S_{1}^{-1} = \dfrac{5.1 \times 1}{6.1} = 0.8361
$$

**Step 5 — State Update:**

$$
\hat{x}_{1} = \hat{x}_{1\mid 0} + K_{1} \tilde{y}_{1} = 45 + 0.8361 \times 5 = 49.18 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Covariance Update (Joseph form):**

$$
P_{1} = (1 - K_{1} H) P_{1\mid 0} = (1 - 0.8361 \times 1) \times 5.1 = 0.1639 \times 5.1 = 0.836 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Result (1 mark):** After one Kalman update, the posterior estimate is $\hat{x}_1 = 49.18$ m, with reduced uncertainty $P_1 = 0.836$ m². The estimate has moved 4.18 m closer to the true value of 50 m, demonstrating the filter's measurement-correction behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation justification** in the bicycle model — students often write $\dot{\psi} = v\sin\delta / L$ but should explicitly state that the small-angle approximation is valid only for $\delta < 15°$.
> 2. **Mixing up $P_{k|k-1}$ and $P_k$** in the Kalman derivation — write them in the order: *predict, then update*. Examiners specifically check this sequence.
> 3. **Not including units in numerical answers** — always write "m", "m/s", "rad", or "m²". A 0.5-mark penalty is common.
> 4. **Confusing ROS 1 with ROS 2** in definition questions — ROS 1 is end-of-life since 2025; the syllabus explicitly wants **ROS 2 (Humble/Iron)**.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** in 14-mark "with neat block diagram" questions — a missing diagram loses **at least 4 marks** out of 14.
> 6. **Comparing only 2–3 simulators** when the question asks for **five parameters** — students must use a properly structured comparison table.
> 7. **Skipping the Joseph form** when updating covariance — the symmetric form $P = (I-KH)P$ is accepted, but the asymmetric form $P = P - KHP$ is what most textbooks print and the more stable one is what the examiner expects.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### 🔑 Software Stack & Architecture
- [ ] Five-layer architecture: **HAL → RTOS → Middleware → Functional Modules → Application**
- [ ] **ROS 2 + DDS** is the dominant research middleware; **AUTOSAR Adaptive** is the production standard.
- [ ] Three-tier frequency hierarchy: **kHz controller → 100 Hz sensor fusion → 10 Hz planner**.

### 🔑 Simulation Tools
- [ ] **MATLAB/Simulink** = model-based design, MPC, control theory (industry favourite for controls).
- [ ] **CarSim** = 45+ DOF vehicle dynamics (industry favourite for chassis control).
- [ ] **Gazebo** = open-source 3-D rigid-body + sensor sim (industry favourite for research).
- [ ] **CARLA** = photorealistic urban driving (industry favourite for perception ML training).
- [ ] **PreScan** = ADAS + Euro NCAP scenarios (industry favourite for Tier-1 suppliers).

### 🔑 X-in-the-Loop Testing Pyramid
- [ ] Bottom-up: **RIL → MIL → SIL → HIL → DIL → VIL**.
- [ ] Mnemonic: **M – S – H – D – V** (Milestones in verification maturity).
- [ ] V-Model is the *backbone* of ISO 26262 functional-safety compliance.

### 🔑 Mathematical Models (Must-Memorise)
- [ ] **Bicycle model:** $\dot{\psi} = v\tan\delta / L$ — 100% KTU exam favourite.
- [ ] **Kalman Filter:** Eight steps in order — Predict → Innovate → Gain → Update.
- [ ] **LiDAR measurement model:** $r = \sqrt{(x_t - x_s)^2 + (y_t - y_s)^2}$.
- [ ] **Pure Pursuit:** $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / l_d)$.
- [ ] **MPC cost:** Quadratic penalty on state error and control effort.

### 🔑 Standards (Bonus Marks)
- [ ] **ISO 26262** — Functional Safety (ASIL A–D).
- [ ] **ISO 21434** — Cybersecurity Engineering.
- [ ] **SOTIF (ISO 21448)** — Safety of the Intended Functionality.
- [ ] **AUTOSAR Classic + Adaptive** — ECU software architecture.
- [ ] **OpenDRIVE + OpenSCENARIO** — Map + scenario description.

### 🔑 Open-Source AV Stacks
- [ ] **Autoware (Universe)** — ROS 2 based, originated from Tier-IV (Japan).
- [ ] **Apollo (Baidu)** — Cyber RT middleware, dominant in China.
- [ ] **Open Pilot (Comma.ai)** — Retrofit driver-assistance, 100+ car models.

### 🔑 Vendor Tools to Remember
- [ ] **dSPACE** — HIL industry standard.
- [ ] **Vector Informatik (CANoe, VT System)** — Bus + ECU testing.
- [ ] **National Instruments (VeriStand + PXI)** — Modular HIL.
- [ ] **IPG CarMaker** — Real-time vehicle + traffic + driver.
- [ ] **AVL CRUISE** — Powertrain + vehicle energy management.

### 🔑 Programming Languages
- [ ] **C++** — Production code (latency-critical paths).
- [ ] **Python** — Rapid prototyping, ML training.
- [ ] **MATLAB / Simulink** — Algorithm development, auto-code generation.
- [ ] **CUDA / OpenCL** — GPU-accelerated perception.
- [ ] **Rust** — Emerging safety-critical language for AVs.

### 🔑 One-Line Definitions (High-Yield for Part A)
- [ ] **SIL** = code runs on host PC, plant is simulated.
- [ ] **HIL** = real ECU, simulated plant.
- [ ] **DDS** = real-time publish-subscribe middleware standard.
- [ ] **QoS** = Quality of Service policy in DDS (reliability, deadline, durability).
- [ ] **SOTIF** = safety even when no fault exists (sensors misread the world).
- [ ] **HD Map** = lane-level, centimetre-precision map (HERE, TomTom, Waymo).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy (15 minutes):**
> 1. Memorise the bicycle-model equation (30 seconds).
> 2. Memorise the 8-step Kalman filter (2 minutes).
> 3. Draw the 5-layer software stack from memory (3 minutes).
> 4. List 5 simulators + their killer feature each (4 minutes).
> 5. List 4 standards + their one-line purpose (3 minutes).
> 6. Re-read the bicycle-model numerical example (2.5 minutes).
> **Total: 15 minutes for ~80% of probable exam marks.**
