topics.title: Path Planning and Control Systems - Fundamentals of Path Planning Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Path Planning and Control Systems - Fundamentals of Path Planning Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) and Autonomous Vehicles (AVs), **Path Planning** is formally defined as the computational process of generating a geometrically and kinematically feasible trajectory from an initial state (start configuration) to a desired goal state, while satisfying a set of constraints including obstacle avoidance, vehicle dynamics, and optimality criteria (e.g., minimum time, minimum energy, or minimum distance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> Path Planning is the search for an optimal or near-optimal sequence of states in the Configuration Space (C-space) that connects a start configuration to a goal configuration, subject to obstacle constraints and vehicle non-holonomic constraints.

**Key Terminology (High-Yield for KTU Board Exams):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Configuration Space (C-space)** | The complete set of all possible configurations (position + orientation) that a vehicle can attain. |
| **Workspace (W-space)** | The physical 2D/3D environment in which the vehicle operates. |
| **Free Space (C-free)** | The subset of C-space that is not occupied by obstacles. |
| **Holonomic Vehicle** | A vehicle whose controllable degrees of freedom equal its total degrees of freedom. |
| **Non-Holonomic Vehicle** | A vehicle with motion constraints (e.g., a car cannot move sideways directly). |
| **Heuristic Function h(n)** | An estimated cost from node n to the goal, used to guide search. |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Blind Cave Explorer**
> Imagine you are dropped into a dark, unfamiliar cave with a flashlight and a map. You know where you entered (**Start**) and where the exit is (**Goal**). The cave is full of stalagmites (**Obstacles**), narrow tunnels (**Constraints**), and slippery floors (**Dynamic Uncertainties**). To find your way out, you shout and listen to echoes (**Sensors**), estimate how far the exit might be based on air flow (**Heuristic**), and continuously update your mental map (**Re-planning**). **Path Planning for an autonomous vehicle is exactly this process** — finding the safest, shortest, and most efficient route from "Here" to "There" while avoiding walls, pedestrians, and traffic rules.

**Why is this Hard for Vehicles?**
Unlike a GPS in a car, a self-driving vehicle must account for:
- **Kinematic constraints** (e.g., minimum turning radius)
- **Dynamic obstacles** (e.g., moving pedestrians, other cars)
- **Uncertainty in sensing** (sensor noise, occlusions)
- **Real-time computation** (decisions must be made in milliseconds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Metric:** Industrial AGVs typically require path re-computation frequencies between **10 Hz to 100 Hz** for safe real-time navigation, whereas highway AVs may operate at planning rates of **20 Hz to 50 Hz** for longitudinal and lateral control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Path Planning Algorithms

Path Planning algorithms are broadly classified based on the **environment representation** and the **search strategy** employed.

### 2.1.1 Graph-Based / Search-Based Algorithms
These methods discretize the environment into a graph and apply search algorithms. They are **complete** (find a solution if one exists) and **optimal** (under certain heuristic conditions).

1. **Dijkstra's Algorithm (1959)**
   - **Strategy:** Uniform-cost search. Explores nodes in order of increasing cost from the start.
   - **Heuristic:** h(n) = 0 (no goal-direction).
   - **Pros:** Guaranteed optimal path.
   - **Cons:** Slow — explores in all directions (like ripples in a pond).

2. **A\* Algorithm (Hart, Nilsson, Raphael, 1968)**
   - **Strategy:** Best-first search guided by both actual cost and estimated cost-to-goal.
   - **Heuristic:** Problem-specific (e.g., Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance).
   - **Pros:** Faster than Dijkstra, optimal if heuristic is **admissible**.

3. **D\* / D\* Lite (1994 / 2005)**
   - **Strategy:** Incremental re-planning. Efficient when edge costs change dynamically.
   - **Use case:** Mars rovers, dynamic obstacle environments.

4. **BFS / DFS (Breadth/Depth First Search)**
   - **Strategy:** Uninformed search. Rarely used in real AV applications due to inefficiency.

### 2.1.2 Sampling-Based Algorithms
These methods sample random points in C-space and build a probabilistic roadmap.

1. **Probabilistic RoadMap (PRM)**
   - **Strategy:** Sample N random nodes, connect nearby nodes to form a graph, then search the graph.
   - **Multi-query:** Efficient for repeated queries in the same environment.

2. **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)**
   - **Strategy:** Incrementally build a tree rooted at the start, biased toward unexplored regions.
   - **Single-query:** Fast for one start-goal pair.
   - **Variant:** RRT\* guarantees asymptotic optimality.

### 2.1.3 Potential Field Methods
- **Strategy:** Treat the goal as an attractive potential and obstacles as repulsive potentials. The vehicle follows the negative gradient of the combined potential.
- **Pros:** Mathematically elegant, real-time capable.
- **Cons:** Susceptible to **local minima** (vehicle gets stuck).

### 2.1.4 Optimization-Based / Curve-Based Methods
- **Polynomial Trajectories:** Fit a 5th-order polynomial (quintic) to ensure smooth position, velocity, and acceleration continuity.
- **Clothoid Curves:** Use curvature that varies linearly with arc length, ideal for vehicle-like steering.
- **Bezier Curves / Splines:** Used for smooth path smoothing.

### 2.1.5 Reactive / Local Planning
- **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA):** Samples velocities in a feasible search space.
- **Vector Field Histogram (VFH):** Builds a polar histogram of obstacle densities.
- **Time Elastic Band (TEB):** Elastic band optimization in the time domain.

---

## 2.2 The Why and How Behind Each Class

| Algorithm Class | Why Used | How It Works (Logic Steps) |
|---|---|---|
| **Graph Search (A\*)** | Provably optimal in static grids. | 1. Discretize C-space. 2. Initialize priority queue. 3. Pop node with lowest f(n). 4. Expand neighbors. 5. Repeat until goal. |
| **Sampling (RRT)** | High-dimensional C-spaces (e.g., robotic arms with 6+ DOF). | 1. Sample random config. 2. Find nearest node. 3. Steer toward sample. 4. Add to tree. 5. Check goal. |
| **Potential Field** | Real-time reactive control, smooth paths. | 1. Compute attractive force from goal. 2. Compute repulsive force from obstacles. 3. Sum forces. 4. Move along gradient. |
| **Optimization (Clothoid)** | Smooth, drivable paths for car-like vehicles. | 1. Define waypoints. 2. Fit curves with bounded curvature. 3. Optimize jerk/curvature rate. |
| **Reactive (DWA)** | Local obstacle avoidance in dynamic environments. | 1. Sample velocity space. 2. Predict trajectories. 3. Score by clearance, speed, heading. 4. Pick best. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Board Note:** Memorize the A\* cost equation, the admissibility condition, and the gradient of the potential field. These are **guaranteed 3 to 5 mark questions** in the University ESE.

$$
f(n) \;=\; g(n) \;+\; h(n)
$$

$$
g(n) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(n_{i-1},\, n_{i})
$$

$$
h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) \;=\; \vert x_{goal} - x_{n} \vert \;+\; \vert y_{goal} - y_{n} \vert
$$

$$
h_{\text{Euclidean}}(n) \;=\; \sqrt{(x_{goal} - x_{n})^{2} \;+\; (y_{goal} - y_{n})^{2}}
$$

$$
h_{\text{Diagonal}}(n) \;=\; (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) \;+\; \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)
$$

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \xi \cdot \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q)
$$

$$
U_{\text{rep}}(q) \;=\; \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}}\right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_{0} \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_{0} \end{cases}
$$

$$
F(q) \;=\; -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) \;-\; \nabla U_{\text{rep}}(q)
$$

### Comprehensive Formula Table (No Vertical Pipes Used — KTU Compliant)

| Equation / Condition | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| f(n) = g(n) + h(n) | Total cost function for node n | Scalar |
| g(n) | Actual cost from start to n | Meters, seconds, or energy |
| h(n) | Heuristic estimate from n to goal | Same units as g(n) |
| Admissibility: h(n) ≤ h\*(n) | h\* = true cost; h never overestimates | Ensures optimality |
| Consistency: h(n) ≤ c(n,n') + h(n') | Triangle inequality on heuristic | Ensures graph-search optimality |
| U_att | Attractive potential energy | Joules (in abstract units) |
| U_rep | Repulsive potential energy | Joules |
| ξ (xi) | Attractive gain constant | Tunable (1.0 to 5.0 typical) |
| η (eta) | Repulsive gain constant | Tunable (100 to 1000 typical) |
| ρ_goal(q) | Distance from q to goal | Meters |
| ρ(q) | Distance from q to nearest obstacle | Meters |
| ρ_0 | Influence radius of obstacle | Meters (0.5 to 2.0) |
| F(q) | Resultant force on vehicle | Newtons (abstract) |
| ∇U | Gradient of potential field | Vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility vs. Consistency — KTU Favorite Trap Question:**
> An admissible heuristic is one that **never overestimates** the true cost to the goal. A consistent (or monotone) heuristic additionally satisfies the **triangle inequality**. Consistency implies admissibility, but **not vice versa**. For graph-search A\* to find an optimal path without re-expanding nodes, the heuristic **must be consistent**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon, Kiva Systems):** Use A\* on grid maps for shelf-to-shelf navigation.
- **Autonomous Highway Driving (Waymo, Tesla):** Combine RRT\* with Frenet-frame polynomial trajectories.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA):** Use D\* for incremental re-planning as new terrain is discovered.
- **Service Robots (Roomba, hospital robots):** Use PRM + potential fields for indoor navigation.
- **Last-Mile Delivery Robots:** Use DWA for pedestrian-dense urban sidewalks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost Function of the A\* Algorithm

The A\* algorithm minimizes the total estimated cost from the start node S to the goal node G through an intermediate node n. We will derive this cost function from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

Let:
- **S** = start configuration (known).
- **G** = goal configuration (known).
- **n** = any intermediate node in the search graph.
- **c(n_a, n_b)** = the cost of the edge (transition) from node n_a to node n_b.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Define the cost we wish to minimize.**
> The true cost of a path from S to G through n is:
> $$ J^{*}(n) = c^{*}(S, n) + c^{*}(n, G) $$
> where c\*(a, b) denotes the **true optimal** cost from a to b. We wish to find n\* (the node lying on the optimal path) that minimizes J\*(n).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Decompose into known and unknown terms.**
> The term c\*(S, n) can be estimated during the search as we have already explored paths from S. The term c\*(n, G) is unknown because the future is not yet explored. We replace it with a heuristic estimate.
> $$ J^{*}(n) \approx g(n) + h(n) $$
> Define f(n) = g(n) + h(n) as the **evaluation function** of A\*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Why a heuristic?**
> A heuristic h(n) encodes **domain knowledge**. For grid worlds, h(n) can be Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance, or Diagonal distance. Without h(n), f(n) = g(n), which reduces A\* to Dijkstra's algorithm — complete but directionless.

---

### 3.1.2 Heuristic Admissibility Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — State the Admissibility Condition.**
> A heuristic h is **admissible** if:
> $$ \forall n : h(n) \le c^{*}(n, G) $$
> In words, h(n) must **never overestimate** the true cost to the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 — Proof Sketch of Optimality.**
> Assume A\* has terminated and returned goal node G. Let n be any **unexpanded** node (i.e., still in the open list). Then:
> $$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \ge g(n) + 0 = g(n) $$
> (by non-negativity of h's components if admissible). Since A\* expands nodes in order of increasing f, and it terminated with f(G) = g(G) (since h(G) = 0 for any goal), we have f(G) ≤ f(n) for all unexpanded n. Hence g(G) ≤ g(n), meaning the path found is optimal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 6 — Consistency Condition (Stronger Property).**
> A heuristic is consistent (or monotone) if for every edge (n, n'):
> $$ h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n') $$
> This is the **triangle inequality**. Consistency ensures that once a node is expanded, its g-value is optimal and never needs updating — a key property for A\* efficiency in practice.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 2D grid world where each move has a cost of **1 unit**. Start S = (0, 0), Goal G = (4, 3). Use Euclidean heuristic. Manually expand A\*.

| Step | Expanded Node n | g(n) | h(n) = Euclidean to (4,3) | f(n) = g + h |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (0,0) | 0 | 5.00 | 5.00 |
| 2 | (1,0) | 1 | 4.24 | 5.24 |
| 3 | (0,1) | 1 | 4.47 | 5.47 |
| 4 | (2,0) | 2 | 3.61 | 5.61 |
| 5 | (1,1) | 2 | 3.61 | 5.61 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A\* expands nodes in **ascending order of f(n)**, focusing its search toward the goal. Dijkstra, in contrast, would expand radially in all directions regardless of goal proximity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Force Equations of the Artificial Potential Field Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Define the Attractive Potential.**
> We want a potential that pulls the vehicle toward the goal. The simplest choice is a quadratic function of distance:
> $$ U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \xi \cdot \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q) $$
> where ρ_goal(q) is the Euclidean distance from current configuration q to the goal, and ξ > 0 is a tunable gain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Derive the Attractive Force.**
> Force is the negative gradient of potential energy:
> $$ F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -\xi \cdot (q - q_{\text{goal}}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Define the Repulsive Potential.**
> Near an obstacle, we need a strong repulsive potential that decays to zero at distance ρ_0:
> $$ U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}}\right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_{0} \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_{0} \end{cases} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Derive the Repulsive Force.**
> Differentiating U_rep with respect to q (where ρ(q) depends on q):
> $$ F_{\text{rep}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \nabla \rho(q) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 — Combine the Forces.**
> The total force on the vehicle is:
> $$ F_{\text{total}}(q) = F_{\text{att}}(q) + F_{\text{rep}}(q) $$
> The vehicle's velocity (or acceleration) is proportional to F_total in the simplest kinematic model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of the Potential Field Method — KTU Pitfall:**
> A pure potential field suffers from **local minima**, where the attractive and repulsive forces cancel out, leaving the vehicle stranded. Modern systems mitigate this with:
> - Random-walk escape strategies.
> - Harmonic potential fields (no local minima).
> - Navigation functions (Rimon-Koditschek, 1992).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Exam-Ready)

| Criterion | A\* (Graph Search) | RRT (Sampling) | Potential Field | Clothoid / Spline |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | Yes (on finite graphs) | Probabilistic | No (local minima) | No |
| **Optimality** | Yes (admissible h) | Asymptotic (RRT\*) | Local only | Local only |
| **Computational Cost** | O(b^d) worst case | O(n log n) | O(k) per step | O(N) curve fit |
| **Dynamic Re-planning** | Poor (D\* better) | Moderate | Excellent | Poor |
| **Smoothness** | Discrete (needs smoothing) | Jerky (needs smoothing) | Smooth | Inherently smooth |
| **High-Dim Capable** | Poor (curse of dim.) | Excellent | Moderate | Poor |
| **Real-Time Use** | Yes (with heuristics) | Yes (single-query) | Yes (very fast) | Yes (post-processing) |
| **Typical Use** | AGVs, indoor robots | Robotic arms, drones | Mobile robots | Highway AVs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Integrated Path Planning Stack for an Autonomous Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors LIDAR Camera GPS IMU] --> B[Perception Module]
    B --> C[Localization Estimator]
    C --> D[World Map Builder]
    D --> E[Global Planner A Star RRT]
    E --> F[Local Planner DWA MPC]
    F --> G[Trajectory Smoothing Clothoid Spline]
    G --> H[Controller PID Stanley MPC]
    H --> I[Vehicle Actuators Steering Throttle Brake]
    I --> J[Vehicle Dynamics Feedback]
    J --> C
    F --> K[Dynamic Obstacle Predictor]
    K --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram (KTU Exam Note):**
> The global planner uses **static map data** (from D), while the local planner uses **real-time sensor data** (from K). The two interact in a **re-planning loop**: any change in dynamic obstacles triggers re-computation of the local trajectory, while the global route is updated only when the vehicle enters a new map segment.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart: A\* Algorithm Execution Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Initialize] --> B[Add Start Node to Open List with f equals h of start]
    B --> C{Is Open List Empty}
    C -- Yes --> Z[FAILURE No Path Exists]
    C -- No --> D[Pop Node n with Lowest f from Open List]
    D --> E{Is n the Goal Node}
    E -- Yes --> Y[SUCCESS Reconstruct Path]
    E -- No --> F[Move n to Closed List]
    F --> G[For Each Neighbor n prime of n]
    G --> H{Is n prime in Closed List}
    H -- Yes --> G
    H -- No --> I[Compute tentative g equals g of n plus cost n to n prime]
    I --> J{Is n prime in Open List with higher g}
    J -- No --> K[Set parent of n prime to n Update g and f of n prime]
    K --> G
    J -- Yes --> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safety:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `Z`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node names. Labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Artificial Potential Field Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Configuration q] --> B[Compute Attractive Force F att from Goal]
    A --> C[Compute Repulsive Force F rep from Obstacles]
    B --> D[Vector Sum F total]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Velocity equals F total divided by Damping]
    E --> F[Update q to q plus delta t times v]
    F --> A
    A --> G{Reached Goal}
    G -- Yes --> H[STOP]
    G -- No --> B
```

---

## 4.4 Hierarchical Classification: Path Planning Algorithm Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Path Planning Algorithms] --> B[Search Based]
    A --> C[Sampling Based]
    A --> D[Reactive Based]
    A --> E[Optimization Based]
    B --> B1[Dijkstra]
    B --> B2[A Star]
    B --> B3[D Star Lite]
    B --> B4[Theta Star]
    C --> C1[PRM]
    C --> C2[RRT]
    C --> C3[RRT Star]
    D --> D1[Potential Field]
    D --> D2[Dynamic Window Approach]
    D --> D3[Vector Field Histogram]
    E --> E1[Clothoid Curves]
    E --> E2[Polynomial Trajectories]
    E --> E3[Bezier Splines]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

> Define **Configuration Space (C-space)**. Why is it preferred over Workspace (W-space) for path planning of autonomous vehicles?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Valuation Pattern):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Configuration Space, or C-space, is the set of all possible configurations (position, orientation, and any other relevant degrees of freedom) that a robot or vehicle can attain. Each configuration is a point in C-space, and C-space is a manifold whose dimension equals the number of degrees of freedom (DOF) of the vehicle.

**Why C-space is Preferred (1.5 Marks):**
In Workspace (W-space), the vehicle is a **rigid body with volume**, and obstacle avoidance requires complex distance calculations between geometric primitives. In C-space, the vehicle is reduced to a **single point**, and obstacles are mapped into forbidden regions in C-space. This transforms the path planning problem from a complex geometric avoidance problem into a **point-navigation problem** in a transformed space, vastly simplifying computation.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

> Differentiate between **Admissible** and **Consistent** heuristics in the A\* algorithm. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | Admissible Heuristic | Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | h(n) ≤ h\*(n) for all n (never overestimates true cost). | h(n) ≤ c(n, n') + h(n') for all edges (triangle inequality). |
| **Implication** | Guarantees A\* finds an optimal path. | Guarantees optimality **without** re-expanding nodes. |
| **Example** | Euclidean distance in a 2D grid (h ≤ true path length). | Manhattan distance on a 4-connected grid (satisfies triangle inequality). |

**Example for Admissible (1 Mark):** Straight-line Euclidean distance in 2D, since the straight line is always the shortest path.
**Example for Consistent (1 Mark):** Manhattan distance on a 4-connected grid where each move costs 1, because every step changes h by exactly 1, satisfying the triangle inequality.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often lose marks in Part B questions by **(a)** forgetting to draw the configuration space diagram, **(b)** skipping the admissibility proof, **(c)** writing only the algorithm without numerical verification, and **(d)** failing to mention real-world limitations (e.g., local minima in potential fields). Always include: **Definition → Diagram → Algorithm Steps → Worked Example → Limitations**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern, Dec 2023]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
> Explain the **A\* algorithm** for path planning in autonomous vehicles. Derive its cost function and discuss the admissibility condition with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
> Compare **A\*** and **Dijkstra's algorithm** for path planning in a 4x4 grid. Given a start at (0,0), goal at (3,3), with one obstacle at (1,1), and using Euclidean heuristic, **manually compute the first three expansions** of A\* and Dijkstra. Which one reaches the goal faster and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks):**

**1. Introduction to A\* (1 Mark):**
The A\* algorithm, developed by Hart, Nilsson, and Raphael in 1968, is a best-first search algorithm that finds the **least-cost path** from a start node to a goal node in a weighted graph. It is widely used in autonomous vehicle navigation due to its **completeness and optimality** under admissible heuristics.

**2. Cost Function Derivation (3 Marks):**
The total estimated cost from start S to goal G through node n is:

$$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) $$

where:
- **g(n)** is the actual cost from S to n, computed as the sum of edge costs along the path.
- **h(n)** is the heuristic estimate of the cost from n to G.
- **f(n)** is the priority used to order node expansion.

**3. Admissibility Condition (2 Marks):**
A heuristic h is **admissible** if:

$$ \forall n : h(n) \le h^{*}(n) $$

where h\*(n) is the true optimal cost from n to G. Admissibility guarantees that A\* will find an optimal path because it never overestimates the remaining cost and therefore never prematurely commits to a suboptimal path.

**4. Example (1 Mark):**
In a 2D grid with obstacles, the Euclidean distance from any cell to the goal cell is admissible because the straight line is the shortest possible path, so h(n) ≤ true path length.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**1. Grid Setup (1 Mark):**
- Grid: 4x4 cells.
- Start: (0, 0), Goal: (3, 3), Obstacle: (1, 1).
- Each move (up/down/left/right) costs **1**.
- Heuristic: Euclidean h(n) = sqrt((x - 3)^2 + (y - 3)^2).

**2. Dijkstra's Expansions (2 Marks):**
Dijkstra uses f(n) = g(n) (no heuristic). Expansions are in **all directions** in order of increasing g:
1. (0,0) — g=0
2. (1,0), (0,1) — g=1
3. (0,2), (2,0), (1,1 blocked) — g=2
Dijkstra continues expanding radially without focusing on the goal.

**3. A\* Expansions (3 Marks):**
A\* uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n):
1. **(0,0):** g=0, h=4.24, **f=4.24**
2. **(1,0):** g=1, h=3.61, **f=4.61**
3. **(0,1):** g=1, h=3.61, **f=4.61**
A\* prioritizes nodes that are **closer to the goal**, focusing its search in the goal direction.

**4. Conclusion (1 Mark):**
**A\* reaches the goal faster** because the Euclidean heuristic directs the search toward the goal, while Dijkstra wastes effort exploring irrelevant regions. In a 4x4 grid the difference is small, but in larger grids (e.g., 1000x1000) A\* can be **orders of magnitude faster**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
> Explain the **Artificial Potential Field method** for path planning. Derive the expressions for attractive and repulsive forces. Discuss its **advantages** and **two major limitations** with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
> A mobile robot is at configuration q = (2, 3) and the goal is at q_goal = (8, 6). An obstacle is centered at (4, 4) with influence radius ρ_0 = 2.0. The current distance to the obstacle is ρ(q) = sqrt((2-4)^2 + (3-4)^2) = sqrt(5) ≈ 2.236. The attractive gain ξ = 1.0 and repulsive gain η = 10.0. **Compute the attractive force, repulsive force, and total force** on the robot. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks):**

**1. Concept (2 Marks):**
The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method, introduced by Khatib in 1986, treats the robot as a point moving in a **force field**. The goal generates an **attractive force**, and obstacles generate **repulsive forces**. The robot moves in the direction of the resultant force.

**2. Attractive Potential and Force (2 Marks):**

$$ U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \xi \cdot \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q) $$

$$ F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -\xi \cdot (q - q_{\text{goal}}) $$

**3. Repulsive Potential and Force (2 Marks):**

$$ U_{\text{rep}}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}}\right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_{0} \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_{0} \end{cases} $$

$$ F_{\text{rep}}(q) = \eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \nabla \rho(q) $$

**4. Advantages and Limitations (1 Mark):**
- **Advantages:** Mathematically simple, real-time computation, smooth paths.
- **Limitations:** (i) **Local minima** trap the robot at saddle points; (ii) **Oscillations** in narrow passages; (iii) No consideration of robot dynamics or non-holonomic constraints.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- q = (2, 3), q_goal = (8, 6), q_obs = (4, 4)
- ρ_0 = 2.0, ξ = 1.0, η = 10.0
- ρ(q) = sqrt((2-4)^2 + (3-4)^2) = sqrt(4 + 1) = sqrt(5) ≈ 2.236

**Step 1 — Attractive Force (2 Marks):**

$$ F_{\text{att}} = -\xi \cdot (q - q_{\text{goal}}) = -1.0 \cdot ((2-8), (3-6)) = (6.0, \, 3.0) $$

**Step 2 — Repulsive Force (3 Marks):**
Since ρ(q) = 2.236 > ρ_0 = 2.0, the obstacle is **outside** the influence radius.
$$ F_{\text{rep}} = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Total Force (1 Mark):**

$$ F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{att}} + F_{\text{rep}} = (6.0, \, 3.0) + (0, 0) = (6.0, \, 3.0) $$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation (1 Mark):**
The robot experiences a net force of magnitude |F| = sqrt(36 + 9) = sqrt(45) ≈ **6.708 N** (in abstract units), directed at angle arctan(3.0/6.0) ≈ **26.57°** from the x-axis, moving it toward the goal without any obstacle interference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

### **Core Concepts**
- **Path Planning** = finding a feasible, optimal sequence of states in C-space from start to goal.
- **C-space** reduces a vehicle to a point; obstacles become forbidden regions.
- **Workspace (W-space)** is the physical environment; **C-space** is the abstract configuration space.
- **Graph Search Algorithms** (A\*, Dijkstra, D\*) are **discrete and complete**.
- **Sampling Algorithms** (PRM, RRT, RRT\*) are **probabilistically complete** and suited to high dimensions.
- **Reactive Methods** (Potential Field, DWA) are **fast but locally optimal**.

### **Key Equations (Memorize for ESE)**
- f(n) = g(n) + h(n) — A\* evaluation function.
- h_Manhattan, h_Euclidean, h_Diagonal — admissible heuristic examples.
- Admissibility: h(n) ≤ h\*(n) — never overestimate.
- Consistency: h(n) ≤ c(n,n') + h(n') — triangle inequality.
- U_att = (1/2)·ξ·ρ_goal², U_rep = (1/2)·η·(1/ρ − 1/ρ_0)².
- F = -∇U — force is the negative gradient of potential.

### **Algorithm Highlights**
- **Dijkstra** = A\* with h(n) = 0 (slow, omnidirectional).
- **A\*** = best-first, fast, optimal with admissible h.
- **D\* Lite** = incremental re-planning for dynamic environments.
- **RRT** = fast in high dimensions, but path quality is not optimal.
- **RRT\*** = asymptotic optimality guarantee.
- **PRM** = pre-computed roadmap, good for multi-query.
- **DWA** = velocity-space sampling, real-time obstacle avoidance.

### **Common KTU Traps to Avoid**
- Conflating **admissibility** with **consistency** — they are related but not identical.
- Forgetting that **RRT is probabilistically complete**, not deterministic.
- Claiming **Potential Field is globally optimal** — it suffers from local minima.
- Omitting the **kinematic constraints** of non-holonomic vehicles in graph-search methods.
- Ignoring the **time complexity** of A\* in large grids (worst case exponential).

### **Real-World Industrial Mapping (Board Exam Favorite)**
- Warehouse AGVs → **A\*** on occupancy grids.
- Highway AVs → **RRT\*** + polynomial trajectories + Frenet frame.
- Mars Rovers → **D\*** for incremental re-planning.
- Pedestrian-rich sidewalks → **DWA** or **Social Force Model**.
- Indoor service robots → **PRM** + **Potential Field** hybrid.

### **Numerical Values to Remember**
- Typical re-planning frequency: **10 Hz to 100 Hz**.
- Obstacle influence radius ρ_0: **0.5 m to 2.0 m**.
- Attractive gain ξ: **1.0 to 5.0**.
- Repulsive gain η: **100 to 1000**.
- Manhattan grid: 4-connected. Chebyshev grid: 8-connected. Diagonal: 8-connected with sqrt(2) cost.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vehicle Stability Control Systems** are closed-loop feedback control architectures that continuously monitor a vehicle's dynamic state (yaw rate, lateral acceleration, wheel slip, steering angle) and apply corrective actuations (selective braking, torque vectoring, steering intervention) to keep the vehicle trajectory aligned with the driver's commanded path. The most prominent industrial implementations are **Electronic Stability Program (ESP)**, **Vehicle Dynamics Control (VDC)**, and **Roll Stability Control (RSC)** — all standardized under ISO 26262 functional-safety norms and governed by lateral-dynamics constraints.

**Vehicle Navigation Control Systems** are hierarchical control loops that govern the longitudinal (speed) and lateral (steering) behaviour of an autonomous or guided vehicle to follow a pre-defined geometric path or waypoint sequence, typically using a **path-tracking controller** (e.g., Pure Pursuit, Stanley, Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR), Model Predictive Control (MPC)).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 frames these two domains as a *unified stack* — *Stability Control* operates on the **vehicle dynamics layer** (milliseconds), while *Navigation Control* operates on the **path/trajectory layer** (tens of milliseconds to seconds). A modern Autonomous Guided Vehicle (AGV) requires both, with the stability layer acting as a **safety envelope** around the navigation layer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **toddler learning to walk on a balance beam**:
- The **stability control** is the *reflex in the inner ear* — it kicks in within milliseconds the moment the child tilts too far, shifting weight to the foot that needs it most. The child doesn't consciously decide this.
- The **navigation control** is the *vision of the beam's end-point* — the child looks ahead, plans the foot placement, and steers toward the goal.
- If the inner-ear reflex is damaged, the child will fall no matter how good their vision is. If the vision is blocked, the child may walk well for a moment, but eventually drift off the beam.

In the same way, a self-driving car needs both layers: a **fast inner loop** to keep the body upright and on grip, and a **slower outer loop** to choose where the body should go.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The standard quantitative benchmarks used by KTU examiners and industry are:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Passenger Car) | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Yaw rate gain (understeer gradient) | $K_{us}$ | 0.002 – 0.005 | $\text{rad}\cdot\text{s}^2/\text{m}$ |
| Sideslip angle | $\beta$ | $\leq \pm 8$ | $\text{deg}$ |
| Slip ratio (braking/accel) | $\lambda$ | $\leq \pm 0.15$ | dimensionless |
| Cross-track error | $e_{cte}$ | $\leq 0.15$ | $\text{m}$ |
| Heading error | $e_{\psi}$ | $\leq 0.05$ | $\text{rad}$ |
| Look-ahead distance | $l_d$ | $2.0$ – $10.0$ | $\text{m}$ |
| Vehicle wheelbase | $L$ | $2.5$ – $3.0$ | $\text{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constant:** The gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is treated as a fixed KTU exam value unless a problem statement specifies otherwise. The longitudinal acceleration limit for a typical passenger vehicle on dry asphalt is approximately **$0.8\,g$**, which defines the friction-circle boundary used in stability controllers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Control Architecture

Modern AGV/AV control is decomposed into **three nested loops**, each operating at a different timescale:

1. **Upper Layer — Behavioural/Planning Layer (≥ 100 ms):** Generates the global reference path and velocity profile from HD-maps, perception, and traffic rules.
2. **Middle Layer — Path-Tracking / Navigation Layer (10 – 100 ms):** Computes the desired steering angle $\delta_d$ and longitudinal acceleration $a_x^d$ that drive cross-track error and heading error to zero.
3. **Lower Layer — Vehicle Dynamics / Stability Layer (1 – 10 ms):** Translates the desired motion into individual wheel torques and brake pressures, while enforcing friction-circle, slip-ratio, and rollover constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this hierarchy?** Decoupling fast vehicle dynamics from slow path planning is the **only practical way** to achieve real-time performance with limited on-board compute. It also simplifies tuning: each layer has its own well-defined input/output and can be validated in isolation.

### 2.2 The Bicycle Model — The Workhorse of Lateral Dynamics

The 2-DOF **bicycle model** collapses the four-wheel vehicle into a single front axle and single rear axle separated by the wheelbase $L$. It assumes small slip angles, constant longitudinal speed $v_x$, and symmetric left/right tyres. Its state vector is $[\,v_y,\; r\,]^T$, where $v_y$ is lateral velocity at the CG and $r = \dot{\psi}$ is yaw rate.

The **Newton–Euler** linearised lateral equations are:

$$\begin{aligned}
m\,v_x\,\dot{\beta} + m\,v_x\,r &= F_{y f}\cos\delta + F_{y r} \\
I_z\,\dot{r} &= l_f\,F_{y f}\cos\delta - l_r\,F_{y r}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass
- $I_z$ = yaw moment of inertia about the z-axis
- $l_f, l_r$ = CG-to-front-axle and CG-to-rear-axle distances
- $F_{yf}, F_{yr}$ = lateral tyre forces at front and rear axles
- $\delta$ = steering angle at the front wheel
- $\beta = \arctan(v_y / v_x)$ ≈ $v_y / v_x$ (small-angle)

Using **linear tyre** $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ and small $\delta$ (so $\cos\delta \approx 1$), and noting that front and rear slip angles are $\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - l_f r / v_x$ and $\alpha_r = -\beta + l_r r / v_x$, the model reduces to the classic state-space form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\beta} \\ \dot{r} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta \\ r \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} \delta
\end{aligned}$$

with the **stability matrix coefficients** (these appear verbatim in KTU 2018–2024 papers):

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{11} &= -\frac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m\,v_x} \\[4pt]
a_{12} &= -1 + \frac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{m\,v_x^{2}} \\[4pt]
a_{21} &= \frac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} \\[4pt]
a_{22} &= -\frac{l_f^{2} C_{\alpha f} + l_r^{2} C_{\alpha r}}{I_z\,v_x} \\[4pt]
b_1 &= \frac{C_{\alpha f}}{m\,v_x} \\[4pt]
b_2 &= \frac{l_f C_{\alpha f}}{I_z}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** Positive $r$ is a CCW yaw (turning left). Positive $\beta$ is sliding to the left relative to the body x-axis. Positive $\delta$ is a left-steering command.

### 2.3 Stability Analysis via Eigenvalues

A vehicle is **neutrally stable** when both eigenvalues of the matrix $A$ have **zero real part**, **stable** when both have **negative real part**, and **unstable** when at least one has a **positive real part**. The understeer condition is:

$$K_{us} = \frac{m\,l_f}{C_{\alpha f} L} - \frac{m\,l_r}{C_{\alpha r} L}$$

- $K_{us} > 0$ → **Understeer** (stable, self-correcting)
- $K_{us} = 0$ → **Neutral steer**
- $K_{us} < 0$ → **Oversteer** (potentially unstable at high speed)

### 2.4 ESP / VDC Corrective Action

When measured $\vert r \vert$ or $\vert \beta \vert$ exceeds the **friction-circle estimate**, the stability controller computes a **differential yaw moment** $M_z$ that is realised via selective wheel braking. The classical linear-quadratic formulation gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_z = I_z \left( \dot{r}_{\text{desired}} - \dot{r}_{\text{measured}} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

This $M_z$ is then apportioned to individual brake actuators using a control allocator that respects each wheel's normal load $F_z$ and instantaneous friction coefficient $\mu$.

### 2.5 Path-Tracking Controllers

#### (a) Pure Pursuit Controller
A geometric controller that traces an arc from the current rear-axle position to a **look-ahead point** on the reference path:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(t) = \arctan\!\left( \frac{2 L \sin \alpha(t)}{l_d} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between the vehicle's heading and the look-ahead vector, and $l_d$ is the look-ahead distance. The common practice is **adaptive look-ahead**: $l_d = k_{pp} \cdot v_x$ (scales with speed).

#### (b) Stanley Controller
A non-linear feedback of **cross-track error** $e_{cte}$ and **heading error** $e_\psi$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(t) = e_\psi(t) + \arctan\!\left( \frac{k \cdot e_{cte}(t)}{v_x(t)} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

The term $\arctan(\cdot)$ is the **soft saturation** that prevents $\delta$ from blowing up at low speeds, making Stanley more robust than Pure Pursuit near stops.

#### (c) LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)
For a linearised path-tracking error model $\dot{x} = A x + B u$, the optimal state-feedback gain $K$ is obtained by solving the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
A^{T} P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^{T} P + Q = 0
\end{aligned}$$

and the control law is $u = -K x = -R^{-1} B^{T} P x$. The matrices $Q \succeq 0$ and $R \succ 0$ weight state error and control effort respectively.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bicycle 2-DOF $\dot{\beta}$ | $\dot{\beta} = a_{11}\beta + a_{12} r + b_1 \delta$ | $\text{rad/s}^2$ |
| 2 | Bicycle 2-DOF $\dot{r}$ | $\dot{r} = a_{21}\beta + a_{22} r + b_2 \delta$ | $\text{rad/s}^2$ |
| 3 | Understeer gradient | $K_{us} = m l_f /(C_{\alpha f} L) - m l_r/(C_{\alpha r} L)$ | $\text{rad/m}$ |
| 4 | Front slip angle | $\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - l_f r / v_x$ | $\text{rad}$ |
| 5 | Rear slip angle | $\alpha_r = -\beta + l_r r / v_x$ | $\text{rad}$ |
| 6 | Yaw moment demand | $M_z = I_z(\dot{r}_{\text{des}} - \dot{r}_{\text{meas}})$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| 7 | Pure pursuit steer | $\delta = \arctan(2 L \sin\alpha \,/\, l_d)$ | $\text{rad}$ |
| 8 | Stanley steer | $\delta = e_\psi + \arctan(k e_{cte}/v_x)$ | $\text{rad}$ |
| 9 | LQR feedback | $u = -R^{-1} B^T P x$, $P$ from ARE | vector |
| 10 | Friction circle | $a_x^{2} + a_y^{2} \leq (\mu g)^{2}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^4$ |
| 11 | Slip ratio | $\lambda = (\omega r_{wh} - v_x)/\max(\omega r_{wh}, v_x)$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Critical yaw rate | $r_{\text{ss}} = \delta v_x \,/\, (L + K_{us} v_x^{2})$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** The bicycle model underpins almost every modern ADAS feature — from **lane-keeping assist (LKA)** in a Toyota to **lane-centring** in a Tesla to **autonomous lane changes** in a Mercedes Drive Pilot. The Pure Pursuit algorithm is the default in **Apollo (Baidu)** and was the original path tracker in **Stanford's Junior** (DARPA Urban Challenge winner, 2007). Stanley is the controller used by **Stanford's Stanley** (DARPA Grand Challenge winner, 2005).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bicycle Model State-Space Form

**Given:** A four-wheel vehicle on a flat road, moving at constant longitudinal speed $v_x$, with small steering angle $\delta$, small body slip angle $\beta$, and small tyre slip angles $\alpha_f, \alpha_r$. The yaw rate is $r = \dot\psi$, lateral velocity at the CG is $v_y$, and tyre forces are linear in slip angles.

**Step 1 — Sum of forces in the lateral (body y) direction:**
Newton's second law gives $m a_y = \sum F_y$. The body-y acceleration of the CG is $a_y = \dot v_y + v_x r = v_x(\dot\beta + r)$ (using $v_y = v_x \beta$ and $\dot v_x = 0$). The lateral forces are the projections of front and rear tyre forces onto the body y-axis:

$$m v_x(\dot\beta + r) = F_{y f} \cos\delta + F_{y r}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of moments about the CG (z-axis):**
The front and rear lateral forces act at distances $l_f$ forward and $l_r$ aft of the CG, with sign convention that CCW (left-turning) moments are positive:

$$I_z \dot r = l_f F_{y f} \cos\delta - l_r F_{y r}$$

**Step 3 — Linearise for small $\delta$:**
$\cos\delta \approx 1$, so the two equations simplify to:

$$\begin{aligned}
m v_x \dot\beta + m v_x r &= F_{y f} + F_{y r} \\
I_z \dot r &= l_f F_{y f} - l_r F_{y r}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express slip angles:**
Geometrically, the velocity vector at the front axle points in a direction shifted by $l_f r / v_x$ from the CG velocity, and the front wheel is itself pointed at angle $\delta$. So the slip angle at the front is:

$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{l_f r}{v_x}$$

Similarly for the rear (no steering, so the slip angle is the negative of the velocity angle plus the contribution from yaw):

$$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{l_r r}{v_x}$$

**Step 5 — Apply linear tyre law:** $F_{y f} = C_{\alpha f} \alpha_f$ and $F_{y r} = C_{\alpha r} \alpha_r$.

Substitute into the force equation:

$$m v_x \dot\beta = C_{\alpha f}\!\left(\delta - \beta - \frac{l_f r}{v_x}\right) + C_{\alpha r}\!\left(-\beta + \frac{l_r r}{v_x}\right) - m v_x r$$

Divide by $m v_x$ and group:

$$\dot\beta = -\frac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m v_x}\beta + \left(\frac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{m v_x^{2}} - 1\right) r + \frac{C_{\alpha f}}{m v_x}\delta$$

This reproduces the coefficient $a_{11}, a_{12}, b_1$.

**Step 6 — Moment equation:** $I_z \dot r = l_f C_{\alpha f}\alpha_f - l_r C_{\alpha r}\alpha_r$. Substitute the slip angles:

$$I_z \dot r = l_f C_{\alpha f}\!\left(\delta - \beta - \frac{l_f r}{v_x}\right) - l_r C_{\alpha r}\!\left(-\beta + \frac{l_r r}{v_x}\right)$$

Group:

$$\dot r = \frac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{I_z}\beta - \frac{l_f^{2} C_{\alpha f} + l_r^{2} C_{\alpha r}}{I_z v_x}r + \frac{l_f C_{\alpha f}}{I_z}\delta$$

This reproduces $a_{21}, a_{22}, b_2$.

**Step 7 — Final compact form:** The 2-DOF bicycle model is therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot\beta \\ \dot r \end{bmatrix} &= \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{C_{\alpha f}+C_{\alpha r}}{m v_x} & \dfrac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{m v_x^{2}} - 1 \\[10pt] \dfrac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} & -\dfrac{l_f^{2} C_{\alpha f} + l_r^{2} C_{\alpha r}}{I_z v_x} \end{bmatrix}}_{A} \begin{bmatrix} \beta \\ r \end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{C_{\alpha f}}{m v_x} \\[8pt] \dfrac{l_f C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}}_{B} \delta
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pure Pursuit Look-Ahead Geometry

**Given:** The rear-axle point is the reference origin. The reference path is sampled at a forward look-ahead distance $l_d$ along the path. The line from the rear axle to the look-ahead point makes an angle $\alpha$ with the vehicle's longitudinal axis. The wheelbase is $L$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR):**
The vehicle, treated as a rigid body, is rotating about a point located on the line perpendicular to the front wheels. The ICR lies at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the rear axle.

**Step 2 — Geometric relation:**
The triangle formed by the rear axle, the look-ahead point, and the ICR is a right triangle. The angle at the rear axle is $\alpha$, the hypotenuse is $l_d$, and the side opposite to $\alpha$ is $R$. So:

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{R}{l_d} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R = \frac{l_d}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Ackermann relation for steer angle:**
For a bicycle model, $\tan\delta = L/R$. Substituting $R$:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{L \sin\alpha}{l_d}$$

**Step 4 — Final formula (since $\delta$ is usually small but not always):**

$$\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 L \sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$$

The factor of **2** is a common textbook inclusion that accounts for the look-ahead point lying on the **path centreline** rather than at the front axle, effectively doubling the geometric lever arm in some derivations (this is the Rajamani form).

**Step 5 — Adaptive gain:** If we choose $l_d = k_{pp} v_x$, the steer command becomes:

$$\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 L \sin\alpha}{k_{pp} v_x}\right)$$

which automatically reduces $\delta$ at high speed (a key safety property).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Stanley Steering Law

**Given:** Cross-track error $e_{cte}$ is the signed lateral distance from the CG to the nearest point on the path. Heading error $e_\psi$ is the angular difference between the vehicle heading and the path tangent. $k > 0$ is the **Stanley gain**.

**Step 1 — Heading correction:** A pure heading error of $e_\psi$ should be cancelled by a steer command of the same magnitude: $\delta_1 = e_\psi$.

**Step 2 — Position correction:** Imagine the vehicle is parked parallel to the path but offset by $e_{cte}$. To steer back to the path, the front wheel must be turned by an angle $\delta_2$ such that the wheel points at the nearest point on the path. The geometry gives $\tan\delta_2 = e_{cte} / d$, where $d$ is the distance from the front axle to the path's nearest point. To first order $d \approx l_d$, and a typical choice is $d = v_x / k$ (so that the correction is gentle at high speed).

**Step 3 — Combine the two corrections:**
The two angles are added: $\delta = \delta_1 + \delta_2$.

$$\delta = e_\psi + \arctan\!\left(\frac{k\, e_{cte}}{v_x}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Why $\arctan$ and not linear?**
At low speed, $v_x \to 0$ would make $k e_{cte}/v_x \to \infty$, saturating the actuator. The $\arctan$ function is **bounded between $-\pi/2$ and $\pi/2$**, providing a smooth, continuous soft-saturation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Pure Pursuit | Stanley | LQR | MPC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Model requirement | Kinematic (geometric) | Kinematic | Linear dynamic | Non-linear/linear dynamic |
| Tuning parameters | $l_d$ (or $k_{pp}$) | $k$ | $Q, R$ matrices | $N, Q, R, u_{\lim}$ |
| Stability guarantee | Geometric (no proof) | Geometric (no proof) | Lyapunov (ARE) | Recursive feasibility |
| Cross-track error handling | Indirect (via arc) | Direct | Direct (state) | Direct (state) |
| Heading error handling | Indirect | Direct | Direct | Direct |
| Constraint handling | No | No | No | Yes (input/state) |
| Compute load | Very low | Very low | Low | High |
| Used in production | Apollo, Autoware | Stanley (DARPA) | Research, low-speed | Highway, racing |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Control Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the closed-loop interaction between the navigation layer and the stability layer in an autonomous vehicle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Global Planner\nHD Map + Route] --> B1[Reference Path\nx_d, y_d, psi_d, v_d]
    B1 --> C1[Path Tracking Controller\nLateral + Longitudinal]
    C1 --> D1[Desired Motion State\ndelta_d, a_x_d, v_d]
    D1 --> E1[Vehicle Dynamics Layer\nBicycle Model + Tyre Model]
    E1 --> F1[Chassis Actuators\nBrake + Throttle + Steer]
    F1 --> G1[Vehicle Body\n4-Wheel Rigid Body]
    G1 --> H1[Sensor Feedback\nIMU, GPS, Wheel Speed, Steering Angle]
    H1 --> I1[State Estimator\nEKF / UKF]
    I1 --> J1[Estimated State\nbeta_hat, r_hat, v_x_hat, e_cte, e_psi]
    J1 --> K1{Stability Check}
    K1 -->|Within Limits| C1
    K1 -->|Violation Detected| L1[Stability Controller\nESP / VDC Override]
    L1 --> M1[Corrective Yaw Moment\nM_z via Differential Braking]
    M1 --> F1
    I1 --> C1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (A1 → C1) is the *navigation* loop running at 10–100 Hz.
- The **inner loop** (E1 → H1) is the *stability* loop running at 1 kHz on a dedicated ECU.
- The **stability check** (K1) is a real-time watchdog that, upon detecting an out-of-envelope state (e.g., $\vert \beta \vert > 8°$ or $\vert r \vert > r_{\text{limit}}$), pre-empts the path tracker and demands a corrective yaw moment $M_z$.

### 4.2 ESP / VDC Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Steering Angle Sensor] --> P1
    S2[Yaw Rate Gyro] --> P1
    S3[Lateral Accelerometer] --> P1
    S4[Wheel Speed Sensors] --> P1
    S5[Brake Pressure Sensor] --> P1
    P1[Sensor Fusion + EKF] --> P2[State Estimate\nbeta, r, v_x]
    P2 --> P3{Reference Model}
    P3 --> P4[Desired Yaw Rate\nr_des]
    P2 --> P5[Measured Yaw Rate\nr_meas]
    P4 --> P6[Error e = r_des - r_meas]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[PID / LQR Controller]
    P2 --> P8[Slip Angle Estimator]
    P8 --> P9[Friction Circle Constraint]
    P9 --> P10[Control Allocator]
    P7 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Wheel 1 Brake]
    P10 --> P12[Wheel 2 Brake]
    P10 --> P13[Wheel 3 Brake]
    P10 --> P14[Wheel 4 Brake]
    P11 --> P15[Vehicle Yaw Response]
    P12 --> P15
    P13 --> P15
    P14 --> P15
    P15 --> S2
    P15 --> S4
```

**Function of each block:**
- **P1 (Sensor Fusion):** Combines the raw IMU, gyroscope, and wheel-speed signals into a clean 6-DOF state estimate. EKF (Extended Kalman Filter) is the KTU-recognised industry default.
- **P3 (Reference Model):** Computes the *desired* yaw rate $r_{des} = \delta v_x / (L + K_{us} v_x^{2})$ — this is the yaw rate a skilled driver would produce on a frictionless surface.
- **P7 (Controller):** A PID or LQR block that converts the yaw-rate error into a demanded yaw moment $M_z$.
- **P10 (Control Allocator):** Solves a constrained QP to distribute $M_z$ across the four brake actuators while respecting $\mu$ and normal load.

### 4.3 State Machine for Stability Override

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Nominal
    Nominal --> Warning : e_ye exceed 0.6
    Warning --> Intervention : e_ye exceed 1.0
    Intervention --> Nominal : e_ye below 0.3 for 0.5 s
    Intervention --> Critical : e_ye exceed 1.5
    Critical --> Nominal : sustained recovery 2 s
    Nominal --> Nominal : normal driving
    Warning --> Warning : intermittent threshold crossing
    note right of Intervention : Differential braking active\nSteering torque may be added
    note right of Critical : Maximum braking force\nPre-collision prep ON
```

**Reading the FSM:** The vehicle starts in **Nominal** — the path tracker is the only active controller. As the yaw-rate error $e_{ye}$ grows, the FSM transitions through **Warning → Intervention → Critical**, each stage unlocking a more aggressive control authority. Recovery requires sustained sub-threshold readings to prevent oscillation between states.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the bicycle model used in vehicle dynamics. List its two main state variables and the two main assumptions that make it valid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **bicycle model** is a 2-DOF planar simplification of a four-wheel vehicle, in which the two front wheels are merged into a single front axle and the two rear wheels into a single rear axle, separated by the wheelbase $L$. (1 mark)
- The **state variables** are the body sideslip angle $\beta$ (or lateral velocity $v_y$) at the centre of gravity and the yaw rate $r = \dot\psi$. (1 mark)
- The two **assumptions** are: (i) constant longitudinal speed $v_x$ and small steering angle so that $\cos\delta \approx 1$; (ii) linear tyre behaviour $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ with small slip angles. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Pure Pursuit and Stanley path-tracking controllers. Mention one specific advantage of Stanley over Pure Pursuit.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pure Pursuit** is a purely geometric controller that uses a *single* look-ahead point on the path and computes the steering angle from the chord geometry $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha/l_d)$. (1 mark)
- **Stanley** is a non-linear feedback controller that uses *two* error signals: heading error $e_\psi$ and cross-track error $e_{cte}$, combined as $\delta = e_\psi + \arctan(k e_{cte}/v_x)$. (1 mark)
- **Advantage of Stanley:** the $\arctan$ term provides a smooth, bounded steering command at low speeds (no division-by-zero blow-up) and gives a direct, intuitive correction for cross-track error — Pure Pursuit only corrects cross-track error *indirectly* via the look-ahead arc. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With the aid of the bicycle model state equations, derive the stability matrix $A$ for a 2-DOF vehicle and identify the coefficient $a_{12}$. State clearly the sign convention and any linearising assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car has $m = 1500$ kg, $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², $l_f = 1.2$ m, $l_r = 1.5$ m, $C_{\alpha f} = 80\,000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 90\,000$ N/rad, and is travelling at $v_x = 25$ m/s. Compute the coefficients $a_{11}, a_{12}, a_{21}, a_{22}$ of the stability matrix and determine the understeer gradient $K_{us}$. Is the vehicle understeer, neutral, or oversteer? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $A$:**
[Insert the full 7-step derivation from Section 3.1, highlighting the Newton's second law step, the linearisation step, and the final compact matrix form. Award 2 marks for the force equation, 2 marks for the moment equation, 2 marks for the slip-angle substitution and linear tyre law, and 1 mark for the final compact matrix.]

The coefficient $a_{12}$ is the partial derivative $\partial\dot\beta / \partial r$ evaluated at the equilibrium point. Its formula is:

$$a_{12} = \frac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{m v_x^{2}} - 1$$

Physically, $a_{12}$ couples yaw rate into lateral velocity dynamics: a CCW yaw ($r>0$) produces a leftward centripetal acceleration and, if the front-axle cornering stiffness is high (a front-heavy car), reduces the rate of build-up of body slip.

**(b) Numerical computation:**

**Given values:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $I_z = 2500$ kg·m²
- $l_f = 1.2$ m, $l_r = 1.5$ m
- $C_{\alpha f} = 80\,000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 90\,000$ N/rad
- $v_x = 25$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute $a_{11}$:**

$$a_{11} = -\frac{C_{\alpha f} + C_{\alpha r}}{m v_x} = -\frac{80\,000 + 90\,000}{1500 \times 25} = -\frac{170\,000}{37\,500} \approx -4.533 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a_{12}$:**

$$a_{12} = \frac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{m v_x^{2}} - 1 = \frac{1.5 \times 90\,000 - 1.2 \times 80\,000}{1500 \times 625} - 1$$

$$= \frac{135\,000 - 96\,000}{937\,500} - 1 = \frac{39\,000}{937\,500} - 1 = 0.0416 - 1 = -0.9584$$

**Step 3 — Compute $a_{21}$:**

$$a_{21} = \frac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{I_z} = \frac{39\,000}{2500} = 15.6 \text{ (rad/s²)/rad}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $a_{22}$:**

$$a_{22} = -\frac{l_f^{2} C_{\alpha f} + l_r^{2} C_{\alpha r}}{I_z v_x} = -\frac{(1.2)^{2}\times 80\,000 + (1.5)^{2}\times 90\,000}{2500 \times 25}$$

$$= -\frac{1.44 \times 80\,000 + 2.25 \times 90\,000}{62\,500} = -\frac{115\,200 + 202\,500}{62\,500} = -\frac{317\,700}{62\,500} \approx -5.083 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $K_{us}$:**
$L = l_f + l_r = 2.7$ m.

$$K_{us} = \frac{m l_f}{C_{\alpha f} L} - \frac{m l_r}{C_{\alpha r} L} = \frac{1500 \times 1.2}{80\,000 \times 2.7} - \frac{1500 \times 1.5}{90\,000 \times 2.7}$$

$$= \frac{1800}{216\,000} - \frac{2250}{243\,000} = 0.00833 - 0.00926 = -0.00093 \text{ rad·m/s}^2 \text{ per m}$$

**Step 6 — Sign of $K_{us}$:**
$K_{us} < 0$, so the vehicle is **oversteer**. This is a relatively unusual result for a passenger car and would warrant a comment that the rear-axle cornering stiffness is high compared to the front, biasing the handling toward the unstable regime. (In practice, factory ESP would compensate by selectively braking the outer front wheel.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Question A):**
> - Forgetting the **$-1$** term in $a_{12}$ is the single most common error. Students often write $a_{12} = (l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}) / (m v_x^2)$ and miss the subtraction. **You will lose 1 mark** for this.
> - Mixing up the *units* in the stability matrix is a second common error. $a_{11}, a_{22}$ have units of $\text{s}^{-1}$ (since $\beta$ is dimensionless and $\dot\beta$ is $\text{s}^{-1}$). $a_{12}$ is also $\text{s}^{-1}$ because $r$ is $\text{rad/s}$ and $\beta$ is dimensionless — the $-1$ in $a_{12}$ is dimensionless only when $v_x^2$ carries the right unit conversion.
> - In the understeer calculation, students frequently forget to use $L = l_f + l_r$ and use $l_f$ or $l_r$ alone.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With neat sketches and the governing equation, explain the working principle of the **Pure Pursuit** path-tracking algorithm. Discuss the role of the look-ahead distance $l_d$ in the trade-off between *tracking accuracy* and *stability*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pure Pursuit controller is fitted to an AGV with $L = 1.8$ m and an adaptive look-ahead $l_d = 0.6\,v_x$. The AGV is moving at $v_x = 5$ m/s and the look-ahead point lies at $\alpha = 0.08$ rad ahead of the heading. Compute the steering command $\delta$. If the AGV slows to $v_x = 1$ m/s, what is the new steering command, and what conclusion do you draw about the controller's behaviour at low speed? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pure Pursuit principle and look-ahead trade-off:**
[3 marks for the geometric sketch (rear axle, look-ahead point, ICR, arc), 2 marks for the governing equation and its derivation, 2 marks for the look-ahead trade-off discussion.]

**Sketch description:** The vehicle's rear-axle point is the origin. A chord of length $l_d$ is drawn from this origin to a point on the reference path. The angle between this chord and the vehicle's longitudinal axis is $\alpha$. The instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR) lies on the line through the rear axle perpendicular to the front wheels, and the arc connecting the rear axle to the look-ahead point is the desired trajectory.

**Governing equation:** $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / l_d)$.

**Trade-off:**
- **Small $l_d$** → tight tracking (small cross-track error) but oscillatory and possibly unstable behaviour because the controller reacts aggressively to the *local* path shape, including noise.
- **Large $l_d$** → smooth, stable behaviour but cuts corners, producing large cross-track error on tight bends.
- A common heuristic is to set $l_d$ proportional to speed: $l_d = k_{pp} v_x$ with $k_{pp} \in [0.4, 1.0]$ s.

**(b) Numerical computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\delta$ at $v_x = 5$ m/s:**
$l_d = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0$ m.

$$\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 \times 1.8 \times \sin(0.08)}{3.0}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{3.6 \times 0.0799}{3.0}\right) = \arctan(0.0959)$$

$$\delta \approx 0.0955 \text{ rad} \approx 5.47°$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\delta$ at $v_x = 1$ m/s:**
$l_d = 0.6 \times 1 = 0.6$ m.

$$\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 \times 1.8 \times \sin(0.08)}{0.6}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{3.6 \times 0.0799}{0.6}\right) = \arctan(0.4794)$$

$$\delta \approx 0.447 \text{ rad} \approx 25.6°$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
At low speed, the steering command jumps from $5.5°$ to $25.6°$ — a **5× amplification**. This reveals a fundamental drawback of Pure Pursuit with fixed-gain adaptive look-ahead: at low speeds the look-ahead distance collapses, the controller becomes highly sensitive, and the steering saturates. The fix is either (i) a **clamped minimum** $l_d$ (e.g., $l_d = \max(0.6\,v_x,\; 1.5)$), (ii) a non-adaptive fixed $l_d$, or (iii) switching to **Stanley** at low speeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Question B):**
> - Students often compute $\sin(0.08) \approx 0.08$ (small-angle) and miss the slight correction — the answer is $\sin(0.08) = 0.0799$. **Always use the exact value** unless the question explicitly says "approximate."
> - Forgetting to convert radians to degrees in the *interpretation* step loses a half-mark in KTU's 7-mark sub-questions.
> - In the conclusion, students often write *just* "low speed is bad" without naming the **mechanism** (look-ahead collapse). The mechanism is the heart of the answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam — Module 3**

- [x] **Bicycle model state vector** is $[\beta,\; r]^T$; **steering input** is $\delta$; output is the trajectory $(x, y, \psi)$.
- [x] **Six coefficients** of the stability matrix $A$ and **two coefficients** of the input matrix $B$ must be memorised *verbatim* — they are board-exam favourites.
- [x] **Understeer gradient formula** $K_{us} = m l_f / (C_{\alpha f} L) - m l_r / (C_{\alpha r} L)$; **sign determines handling class**.
- [x] **Friction circle** $a_x^2 + a_y^2 \leq (\mu g)^2$ is the hard constraint used by every ESP implementation.
- [x] **Slip ratio** is the **primary observable** for ABS/TCS, while **sideslip angle** $\beta$ is the primary observable for ESP/VDC.
- [x] **Pure Pursuit** = geometric, single look-ahead, $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / l_d)$; sensitive to $l_d$ choice.
- [x] **Stanley** = non-linear feedback, $\delta = e_\psi + \arctan(k e_{cte}/v_x)$; bounded at low speed, direct $e_{cte}$ correction.
- [x] **LQR** solves the **Algebraic Riccati Equation** to produce a state-feedback gain; needs a linear model and is provably optimal for infinite-horizon linear problems.
- [x] **MPC** = receding-horizon optimisation with constraints; most compute-intensive, used for high-speed and racing applications.
- [x] **Hierarchical control stack:** Planning → Path-Tracking → Vehicle-Dynamics → Actuators; each layer runs at progressively higher frequency.
- [x] **Stability override FSM:** Nominal → Warning → Intervention → Critical; transitions are based on yaw-rate error $e_{ye}$ and sideslip magnitude.
- [x] **Sign conventions:** $+r$ = CCW (left turn); $+\beta$ = leftward body slip; $+\delta$ = left-steer command.
- [x] **Units audit:** Always carry units through. $a_{12}$ has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$, $a_{21}$ has units of $\text{rad/s}^2$ per rad (effectively $\text{s}^{-1}$). Slip angles are in radians.
- [x] **Reference model for ESP:** $r_{des} = \delta v_x / (L + K_{us} v_x^2)$ is the "expert driver" yaw rate used as the setpoint.
- [x] **Control allocation:** converts a single commanded yaw moment $M_z$ into four individual brake pressures using a constrained QP.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Decision-Making and Obstacle Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Decision-Making and Obstacle Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Real-Time Decision-Making (RT-DM)

**Real-Time Decision-Making (RT-DM)** in the context of autonomous and Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) is the continuous, time-bounded process by which an intelligent vehicle perceives its environment, evaluates multiple possible actions, and selects an optimal control command within a hard or soft **deadline** that is shorter than the dynamics of the operating environment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, RT-DM sits at the intersection of:
- **Perception** (sensor data interpretation),
- **Localization & Mapping** (SLAM outputs),
- **Behavior Planning** (high-level maneuver selection),
- **Motion Planning & Control** (low-level trajectory generation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** RT-DM is a closed-loop, deterministic, and bounded-latency decision process in which the vehicle's planner must produce a valid trajectory before the **worst-case execution time (WCET)** of any decision cycle exceeds the **safety time-to-collision (TTC)** with the nearest dynamic obstacle.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Obstacle Avoidance

**Obstacle Avoidance (OA)** is the sub-system of RT-DM responsible for generating motion commands that prevent physical collision between the ego-vehicle and any **static obstacle** (curbs, parked vehicles, road debris) or **dynamic obstacle** (pedestrians, cyclists, leading/trailing vehicles) while simultaneously respecting kinematic, dynamic, traffic, and legal constraints.

Mathematically, obstacle avoidance enforces a **configuration-space separation**:

$$q_{ego}(t) \cap \mathcal{O}_{unsafe}(t) = \emptyset \quad \forall \; t \in [t_0, t_0 + \Delta t_{horizon}]$$

where $q_{ego}(t)$ is the vehicle's configuration state, $\mathcal{O}_{unsafe}(t)$ is the time-varying unsafe (occupied) region in the configuration space, and $\Delta t_{horizon}$ is the planning horizon (typically **3 to 8 seconds** for highway, **1 to 3 seconds** for urban).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 3 expects students to clearly differentiate between **reactive** (reflexive, sub-100 ms) and **deliberative** (planning-based, 100 ms–1 s) obstacle avoidance strategies.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car in heavy Kerala traffic (Trivandrum or Kochi bypass at 8 AM). Your eyes and ears act as **sensors**, your brain builds a mental map (**SLAM**), and continuously — without writing anything down — you answer three questions every fraction of a second:

1. *"Where am I and what is around me right now?"* → **Perception**.
2. *"If I keep doing what I'm doing, will I hit something in 2 seconds?"* → **Prediction & Collision Check**.
3. *"What should I do instead — brake, steer, accelerate, or do nothing?"* → **Decision + Obstacle Avoidance**.

That entire loop, executed in **under 100 milliseconds** in a real autonomous car, is *Real-Time Decision-Making and Obstacle Avoidance*.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Constants (Bold)

- **TTC (Time-To-Collision):** Typically **2.0 s – 4.0 s** threshold for emergency braking.
- **Decision Cycle Latency:** **10 ms – 100 ms** (urban) to **200 ms** (highway).
- **Sensor Update Rate:** LiDAR **10–20 Hz**, Camera **30–60 Hz**, Radar **20–40 Hz**.
- **Safety Distance Buffer:** **0.5 s × v_ego** (rule-of-thumb minimum headway).
- **ISO 26262 ASIL Level:** Most AV decision systems target **ASIL-D** (highest automotive safety integrity).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**P-P-P-C-A**" — *Perceive → Predict → Plan → Check → Act*. This is the universal decision loop taught in KTU Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decision Architecture (4-Layer Stack)

Autonomous vehicles do **not** solve one giant optimization problem every 100 ms. Instead, KTU 2024 prescribes a **hierarchical decomposition** into four layers, each operating at different frequencies and abstractions:

**Layer 1 — Route Planning (Strategic, 1–10 Hz)**
- Inputs: HD-Map, origin, destination.
- Output: Coarse road-graph path (sequence of waypoints/intersections).
- Algorithm family: **Dijkstra**, **A***, **Contraction Hierarchies**.
- Time horizon: **Minutes to hours**.

**Layer 2 — Behavior Planning (Tactical, 5–20 Hz)**
- Inputs: Route, traffic rules, perceived agents, signals.
- Output: Discrete high-level maneuver — *lane-keep, lane-change-left, yield, stop, overtake*.
- Algorithm family: **Finite State Machines (FSM)**, **Behavior Trees**, **MDP**, **POMDP**.
- Time horizon: **5–15 seconds**.

**Layer 3 — Motion Planning (Operational, 20–100 Hz)**
- Inputs: Behavior command, dynamic obstacles, vehicle state.
- Output: Continuous, kinematically-feasible trajectory $(x(t), y(t), \psi(t), v(t), a(t))$.
- Algorithm family: **Polynomial Trajectories**, **RRT/RRT\***, **Lattice planners**, **Optimization-based (MPC)**.
- Time horizon: **3–8 seconds**.

**Layer 4 — Vehicle Control (Reactive, 50–1000 Hz)**
- Inputs: Reference trajectory, IMU, wheel odometry.
- Output: Throttle, brake, steering angle commands.
- Algorithm family: **PID**, **LQR**, **Stanley**, **Pure Pursuit**, **MPC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a hierarchy?** Because a single optimizer trying to simultaneously choose the route to Bengaluru AND the steering angle in 50 ms would be intractable. Layering breaks the NP-hard global planning into smaller, real-time-solvable sub-problems.

### 2.2 Core Sensing Modalities for Obstacle Avoidance

| Sensor | Range | FOV | Update Rate | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LiDAR** | 100–250 m | 360° / 90° | 10–20 Hz | Accurate 3D geometry | Weather-sensitive |
| **Radar** | 200–300 m | ±35° | 20–40 Hz | Velocity (Doppler), weather-robust | Low angular resolution |
| **Camera** | 50–250 m | 30°–120° | 30–60 Hz | Semantic / lane info | Lighting-dependent |
| **Ultrasonic** | 0.1–5 m | Narrow cone | 10–20 Hz | Close-range parking | Very short range |
| **IMU / GNSS** | — | — | 100–200 Hz | Localization aiding | Drift / tunnel effect |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ | Time-to-Collision: $d_{rel}$ = relative distance, $v_{rel}$ = relative speed (closing positive) | Triggers emergency brake when TTC < threshold (typically **2.0 s**) |
| 2 | $d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot \tau + \dfrac{v_{ego}^2}{2 \cdot a_{max,brake}}$ | Safe longitudinal headway (Kamm's circle) | Adaptive Cruise Control gap setting |
| 3 | $U_{att}(q) = \dfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert^2$ | Attractive potential field (quadratic) | Pulls vehicle toward goal |
| 4 | $U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Repulsive potential field, $\rho$ = distance to obstacle, $\rho_0$ = influence radius | Pushes vehicle away from obstacles |
| 5 | $F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$ | Total artificial force = negative gradient of potential | Directs vehicle motion |
| 6 | $v_{max}(d) = \sqrt{2 \cdot a_{max} \cdot d}$ | Dynamic-Window Approach (DWA) velocity bound | Ensures braking feasibility |
| 7 | $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x) u, \quad x \in \mathcal{X}_{free}$ | Vehicle dynamics (MPC formulation) | Constraint for trajectory optimization |
| 8 | $J = \int_{t_0}^{t_0+H} \left[ \vert\vert x(\tau) - x_{ref}(\tau) \vert\vert_Q^2 + \vert\vert u(\tau) \vert\vert_R^2 \right] d\tau$ | MPC cost: Q penalizes tracking error, R penalizes control effort | Generates optimal control sequence |
| 9 | $P_{collision} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N} (1 - p_{i,sensor})$ | Sensor-fusion collision probability (independent) | Robust obstacle detection |
| 10 | $a_{lat,max} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot g}{1 + \dfrac{v^2}{b \cdot R}}$ | Friction-circle lateral acceleration limit | Feasibility envelope for evasive steering |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For formulas involving absolute value or magnitude inside a table, use `\vert x \vert` (not `|x|`). The vertical pipe character can break the markdown table syntax in some renderers — examiners and PDFs often fail to render broken tables properly.

### 2.4 Why Each Component Matters (Engineering Context)

- **Potential Fields** are computationally cheap (O(N) per cycle) — perfect for embedded AGV controllers in factories.
- **A\*** guarantees an optimal path on a grid but blows up combinatorially in 3D — KTU students must know when **A\*** is preferred over **RRT\***.
- **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** is the *de facto* standard in modern AVs (Waymo, Tesla FSD, Mercedes Drive Pilot) because it natively handles constraints (road boundaries, obstacle zones, steering/acceleration limits).
- **Behavior Trees** replaced FSMs in many production stacks because they are modular, hierarchical, and easier to certify under **ISO 26262**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time-To-Collision (TTC) for a Closing Obstacle

This is the most fundamental reactive trigger in any obstacle-avoidance stack. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the kinematic setup.**

Consider an ego-vehicle moving at constant velocity $v_e$ and a stationary (or slower-moving) obstacle ahead at distance $d$ from the ego-vehicle's front bumper. The obstacle's velocity is $v_o$.

$$
v_{rel} = v_e - v_o \quad (\text{positive when closing})
$$

$$
d_{rel} = d \quad (\text{distance to obstacle})
$$

**Step 2 — Assume linear motion for the prediction horizon.**

If both agents continue with constant velocity over the horizon, the distance evolves as:

$$
d_{rel}(t) = d_{rel}(0) - v_{rel} \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Define TTC as the time at which $d_{rel}(t) = 0$.**

Set $d_{rel}(t_{TTC}) = 0$ and solve:

$$
0 = d_{rel}(0) - v_{rel} \cdot t_{TTC}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $t_{TTC}$.**

$$
v_{rel} \cdot t_{TTC} = d_{rel}(0)
$$

$$
t_{TTC} = \dfrac{d_{rel}(0)}{v_{rel}}
$$

This is the clean closed-form **TTC formula** that KTU expects students to derive from scratch in Part B questions.

> **Engineering Note:** If $v_{rel} \le 0$ (i.e., the obstacle is moving away or stationary and ego is decelerating/stopped), TTC is **undefined or infinite**, and the trigger should fall back to a *minimum-distance* check.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Kamm's Safe Headway Distance

Adaptive Cruise Control and longitudinal obstacle avoidance both rely on a minimum following distance. The classic derivation assumes the worst case: the obstacle suddenly brakes at maximum deceleration, and the ego must still avoid rear-ending it.

**Step 1 — Define variables.**

- $v_e$ = ego velocity,
- $v_o$ = obstacle velocity,
- $\tau$ = reaction / system latency (typically **1.5 s**),
- $a_{max}$ = maximum braking deceleration (typically **8 m/s²** for a car on dry asphalt).

**Step 2 — Ego's stopping distance from current instant.**

During the reaction time $\tau$, the ego travels an additional $v_e \cdot \tau$ before brakes engage. Then it decelerates at $a_{max}$:

$$
d_{stop,e} = v_e \cdot \tau + \dfrac{v_e^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

**Step 3 — Obstacle's stopping distance (worst-case, same deceleration).**

$$
d_{stop,o} = v_o \cdot \tau + \dfrac{v_o^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Required headway so ego stops just behind obstacle.**

$$
d_{safe} = (d_{stop,e} - d_{stop,o}) + d_{margin}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
d_{safe} = v_e \cdot \tau + \dfrac{v_e^2}{2 a_{max}} - \dfrac{v_o^2}{2 a_{max}} + d_{margin}
$$

$$
d_{safe} = v_e \cdot \tau + \dfrac{v_e^2 - v_o^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}} + d_{margin}
$$

This is the **Kamm's-circle / two-second-rule extended formula** — frequently tested in KTU Module 3 numerical problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Artificial Potential Field (APF) Total Force

Potential-field obstacle avoidance is an exam-favourite in KTU. We derive the total control "force" acting on the vehicle.

**Step 1 — Define the attractive potential toward goal $q_{goal}$.**

A quadratic well is standard because its gradient is linear (bounded control):

$$
U_{att}(q) = \dfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert^2
$$

**Step 2 — Define the repulsive potential from a single obstacle.**

The obstacle's closest-point distance to the vehicle configuration is $\rho(q)$. The influence radius is $\rho_0$ (e.g., **2 m** for a car-scale planner).

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Total potential = sum.**

$$
U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_{rep,i}(q)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the gradient to obtain the artificial force.**

$$
F(q) = -\nabla U(q)
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the gradient.**

$$
F(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla U_{rep,i}(q)
$$

**Step 6 — Explicit component forms.**

Attractive force:

$$
F_{att}(q) = -k_{att} \cdot (q - q_{goal})
$$

Repulsive force (for $\rho(q) \le \rho_0$, by chain rule):

$$
F_{rep,i}(q) = k_{rep} \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)^2} \cdot \nabla \rho(q)
$$

**Step 7 — The vehicle's velocity command is proportional to $F$.**

$$
v_{cmd}(q) = \alpha \cdot F(q), \quad \vert\vert v_{cmd} \vert\vert \le v_{max}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The potential-field method is fast but suffers from **local minima** (vehicle gets trapped between two obstacles with the goal directly behind).
