Modern systems either hybridize APF with A* (global planner → APF local planner) or use harmonic potential fields (which are guaranteed minimum-free).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Algorithm Selection

| Algorithm | Best Use Case | Computation | Optimal? | Handles Dynamic Obstacles? | KTU Score Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A\*** | Static grid map, indoor AGV | Medium (heuristic-guided BFS) | Yes (admissible heuristic) | Poor (needs replan) | High (5 marks type) |
| **D\*** / **D\* Lite** | Unknown / changing maps | Medium-Low (incremental) | Yes | Yes (replanning) | High |
| **RRT** | High-dimensional config space | Medium (sampling) | Probabilistically complete | Moderate | Medium |
| **RRT\*** | Needs asymptotic optimality | High (rewiring) | Asymptotically optimal | Moderate | Medium |
| **APF (Potential Field)** | Real-time reactive AGV | Very Low (O(N)) | No (local minima) | Good (with prediction) | Very High |
| **DWA (Dynamic Window)** | Differential-drive indoor | Low | Local optimal | Good | High |
| **MPC (Traj. Optimization)** | Highway / structured roads | High (QP solver) | Locally optimal | Excellent | Very High |
| **Behavior Tree** | Urban, complex maneuvers | Low at runtime | N/A (discrete) | Excellent | Medium |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Research / simulation | High (offline training) | No guarantee | Excellent (learned) | Low (rare in exam) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Real-Time Decision-Making Loop — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors: LiDAR Radar Camera GNSS IMU] --> B[Perception and Object Tracking]
    B --> C[Localization and SLAM Map Update]
    C --> D[Situation Assessment and Prediction]
    D --> E{Behavior Planner FSM or BT}
    E -->|Maneuver Selected| F[Motion Planner Trajectory Gen]
    F --> G[Safety Checker ISO 26262]
    G -->|Pass| H[Controller LQR or MPC]
    G -->|Fail| E
    H --> I[Vehicle Actuators Throttle Brake Steer]
    I --> J[Vehicle State Feedback]
    J --> B
    B --> K[Occupancy Grid and Obstacle Map]
    K --> F
    K --> G
```

### 4.2 Obstacle Avoidance Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PerceptionLayer[Perception Layer 10 to 100 Hz]
        S1[LiDAR Pointcloud]
        S2[Radar Tracks]
        S3[Camera Detections]
        S4[Ultrasonic]
    end

    subgraph FusionLayer[Sensor Fusion Layer]
        F1[Kalman Filter per Track]
        F2[Occupancy Grid Builder]
        F3[Object List Output]
    end

    subgraph PlanningLayer[Planning Layer]
        P1[TTC Computation]
        P2[Potential Field Forces]
        P3[MPC Trajectory Optimizer]
        P4[Behavior Decision Yield Overtake Stop]
    end

    subgraph ControlLayer[Control Layer 100 to 1000 Hz]
        C1[Steering Angle Command]
        C2[Acceleration Command]
        C3[Brake Command]
    end

    S1 --> F1
    S2 --> F1
    S3 --> F1
    S4 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> P1
    F3 --> P2
    F3 --> P4
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P4 --> P3
    P3 --> C1
    P3 --> C2
    P3 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Decision Cycle Timing Diagram (Gantt-style State Chart)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sense
    Sense --> Fuse: 10 ms
    Fuse --> Predict: 5 ms
    Predict --> Plan: 25 ms
    Plan --> Check: 10 ms
    Check --> Act: 5 ms
    Act --> Sense: cycle restart total 55 to 100 ms
    Check --> FallbackPlan: safety violation
    FallbackPlan --> Act: emergency trajectory
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In a production autonomous stack (e.g., Autoware, Apollo, CARMA), the worst-case **end-to-end latency** from sensor trigger to actuator command is budgeted at **55–100 ms** for urban driving. If the planner fails, the *Fallback* branch (emergency trajectory / minimum-risk maneuver) must fire within an additional **20 ms**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
*Define Real-Time Decision-Making in an autonomous vehicle. Why is bounded latency critical?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Real-Time Decision-Making (RT-DM) is the closed-loop process by which an autonomous vehicle continuously perceives its environment, predicts the evolution of dynamic agents, plans a feasible trajectory, and issues control commands — all within a **bounded time window** that is shorter than the environment's dynamics.

Bounded latency is critical because **(1)** a delayed command in a closing-traffic scenario can cause a collision (TTC may elapse while the CPU is still computing), **(2)** modern AV stacks are certified under ISO 26262 which mandates deterministic worst-case execution time (WCET), and **(3)** kinodynamic constraints (e.g., 100 ms delay at 30 m/s equals 3 m of unintended travel).

> **Mark split:** Definition (1) + bounded latency concept (1) + 1 real example (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between reactive and deliberative obstacle avoidance strategies with one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reactive | Deliberative |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | < 100 ms (next instant) | 3–8 s (future trajectory) |
| Computation | Very low (sensor → action) | High (search / optimization) |
| Optimality | Local | Global (within horizon) |
| Example | **Potential Field / DWA** | **A\*** / **MPC trajectory optimization** |

Reactive methods respond immediately to sensor input (e.g., a PD controller steering away when bumper-mounted IR sensors detect proximity). Deliberative methods build or use a map and search for an optimal path (e.g., an AGV computing a full A* path before moving through a warehouse).

> **Mark split:** Definition each (1+1) + example each (½ + ½) = 3.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-layer hierarchical decision-making architecture** of an autonomous vehicle. Explain the role and time-horizon of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** formula from basic kinematics and explain how it is used to trigger emergency braking in a modern ACC system. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]:**

The four-layer hierarchy (Strategic → Tactical → Operational → Reactive) decomposes the otherwise intractable autonomous-driving problem into four real-time-solvable sub-problems.

| Layer | Function | Horizon | Frequency | Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Route (Strategic) | Pick road graph path origin → destination | Minutes–Hours | 1–10 Hz | Dijkstra, A* |
| 2. Behavior (Tactical) | Choose discrete maneuver | 5–15 s | 5–20 Hz | FSM, BT, MDP |
| 3. Motion (Operational) | Generate feasible trajectory | 3–8 s | 20–100 Hz | RRT*, polynomial, MPC |
| 4. Control (Reactive) | Track trajectory | < 1 s | 50–1000 Hz | PID, LQR, Stanley |

The student must draw a **vertical stack diagram** with feedback arrows from each layer to the perception module. **Award 5 marks** for the diagram with correctly-labelled feedback, **2 marks** for explaining the role and horizon.

---

**Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]:**

The derivation proceeds exactly as in Section 3.1 of these notes. Stepwise:

**Step 1** — Define $v_{rel} = v_e - v_o$ (closing positive), $d_{rel}$ = bumper-to-bumper distance.

**Step 2** — Assume constant velocities over the prediction horizon: $d_{rel}(t) = d_{rel}(0) - v_{rel} \cdot t$.

**Step 3** — Set collision time: $d_{rel}(t_{TTC}) = 0 \Rightarrow t_{TTC} = \dfrac{d_{rel}(0)}{v_{rel}}$.

**Step 4** — ACC threshold: when $t_{TTC} < 2.0$ s, the system initiates **partial braking**; when $t_{TTC} < 1.0$ s, **full emergency braking** is commanded.

**Step 5** — Engineering note: if $v_{rel} \le 0$, TTC is undefined and the system relies on a **minimum distance guard** (typically **2 m**).

> **Mark split:** Variable definition (1), derivation (3), ACC trigger logic (2), engineering caveat (1) = **7 marks**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method of obstacle avoidance. Derive the expressions for attractive and repulsive potentials and the corresponding artificial force. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car is travelling at **25 m/s** on a dry road ($\mu = 0.9$). A pedestrian suddenly steps onto the road at a distance of **30 m**. The driver's reaction time is **1.5 s**. Maximum braking deceleration is **8 m/s²**. Compute (i) the **TTC** assuming the pedestrian is stationary, (ii) the **safe stopping distance** using Kamm's formula, and (iii) comment on whether a collision occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]:**

APF treats the goal as a gravitational attractor and obstacles as repulsive hills. The vehicle "rolls downhill" through the combined landscape.

**Attractive potential:**

$$
U_{att}(q) = \dfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot \vert\vert q - q_{goal} \vert\vert^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})
$$

**Repulsive potential** (single obstacle at distance $\rho(q)$, influence radius $\rho_0$):

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

**Repulsive force:**

$$
F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)^2} \nabla \rho(q)
$$

**Total force:**

$$
F(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \sum_{i} \nabla U_{rep,i}(q)
$$

**Limitation:** Suffers from **local minima** between symmetric obstacles; remedy is to hybridize with a global planner (A*).

> **Mark split:** Concept (2), attractive derivation (2), repulsive derivation (2), limitation (1) = **7 marks**.

---

**Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $v_e = 25$ m/s,
- $v_o = 0$ (stationary pedestrian),
- $d = 30$ m,
- $\tau = 1.5$ s,
- $a_{max} = 8$ m/s².

**(i) Time-To-Collision (TTC):**

$$
t_{TTC} = \dfrac{d}{v_{rel}} = \dfrac{30}{25 - 0} = 1.2 \; \text{s}
$$

**(ii) Safe Stopping Distance (Kamm's formula):**

$$
d_{stop} = v_e \cdot \tau + \dfrac{v_e^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

$$
d_{stop} = (25)(1.5) + \dfrac{(25)^2}{2 \times 8}
$$

$$
d_{stop} = 37.5 + \dfrac{625}{16}
$$

$$
d_{stop} = 37.5 + 39.0625
$$

$$
d_{stop} = 76.5625 \; \text{m}
$$

**(iii) Collision comment:**

Available distance to pedestrian = **30 m**.
Required stopping distance = **76.56 m**.

Since $30 \; \text{m} \ll 76.56 \; \text{m}$, a **collision is unavoidable** under pure braking.

**Additional insight:** Since $t_{TTC} = 1.2 \; \text{s}$ which is below the **2.0 s** emergency-brake threshold, the system must fire **full AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** plus, if road geometry allows, an **evasive steering maneuver** (subject to the friction-circle lateral-acceleration limit $a_{lat,max} = \mu g \approx 8.83$ m/s²).

> **Mark split:** (i) TTC = 2 marks, (ii) Kamm's calculation = 3 marks, (iii) Collision comment + AEB remark = 2 marks = **7 marks**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Module 3 RT-DM & OA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in TTC numericals** — writing "TTC = 30/25" without "= 1.2 s" loses **0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing up $v_{rel}$ sign convention** — KTU expects closing = **positive**; some texts use the opposite. Always state the convention first.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\rho_0$ condition in APF derivation** — writing the repulsive potential as $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep}(1/\rho)^2$ *without* the $\rho_0$ cutoff is incomplete and loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Not labelling feedback arrows** in the hierarchical block diagram — examiners specifically check for the **perception → planner** feedback loop.
> 5. **Using `|x|` in a markdown formula table** — although our answer uses `\vert x \vert`, if a student hand-writes this in the exam there is no issue; but in submitted digital answers, pipes break tables. Use `\vert` always.
> 6. **Ignoring the local-minima problem of APF** — KTU examiners repeatedly deduct **1 mark** when APF is presented as a flawless algorithm.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Real-Time Decision-Making** is bounded-latency (typically **<100 ms**), closed-loop, and deterministic — not just "fast".
- ✅ The **P-P-P-C-A loop** (Perceive → Predict → Plan → Check → Act) is the universal AV decision cycle.
- ✅ The **4-layer hierarchy** (Route → Behavior → Motion → Control) is mandatory in any answer on AV architecture.
- ✅ **TTC** is the most fundamental reactive trigger: $TTC = d_{rel} / v_{rel}$. Threshold **2.0 s** for brake, **1.0 s** for emergency.
- ✅ **Kamm's safe headway**: $d_{safe} = v_e \cdot \tau + v_e^2 / (2 a_{max})$ — memorize and be ready to substitute numerical values.
- ✅ **APF**: attractive (quadratic) + repulsive (inverse-square within $\rho_0$) → force = $-\nabla U$. **Limitation: local minima.**
- ✅ **MPC** is the modern industry standard because it embeds constraints (road bounds, obstacle zones, actuator limits) into a convex optimization solved every cycle.
- ✅ **Behavior Trees** replaced FSMs in production AV stacks due to modularity and easier ISO 26262 certification.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion** (LiDAR + Radar + Camera) is essential — no single sensor is sufficient for all-weather, all-illumination, all-range obstacle avoidance.
- ✅ The **friction-circle limit** $a_{lat,max} = \mu g / (1 + v^2/(bR))$ defines the **feasibility envelope** of any evasive steering maneuver — an evasive lane-change at 100 km/h on wet road ($\mu \approx 0.5$) requires far more lateral space than at 40 km/h.
- ✅ Always quote **safety standards** in AV answers: **ISO 26262** (functional safety), **ISO 21448 SOTIF** (Safety Of The Intended Functionality), **ASAM OpenDRIVE / OpenSCENARIO** (scenario standards).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing and Edge Processing - Role of Cloud Computing in Autonomous Vehicle Ecosystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing and Edge Processing - Role of Cloud Computing in Autonomous Vehicle Ecosystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cloud Computing in the AV Ecosystem** is defined as the on-demand, network-accessible delivery of scalable compute, storage, and intelligence services to autonomous vehicles through shared, geographically distributed data centers, enabling tasks such as HD-map aggregation, fleet learning, simulation, Over-the-Air (OTA) software delivery, and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) coordination that are infeasible to perform within a single vehicle's embedded resources.

**Edge Processing (or Mobile/ Multi-access Edge Computing – MEC)** is the complementary paradigm in which compute and storage resources are pushed *toward* the network edge (Roadside Units (RSUs), 5G base stations, or the vehicle itself) so that time-critical perception, planning, and control decisions are executed with sub-10 ms latencies that pure cloud architectures cannot guarantee.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus frames cloud and edge as a **continuum**, not competitors: cloud = global intelligence, aggregation, and long-horizon learning; edge = local intelligence, low latency, and resilience under poor connectivity. Together they form the **Vehicle–Edge–Cloud (VEC) continuum** that supports L3–L5 autonomy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Readers)

Imagine an autonomous car is a **trained field doctor**:
- **On-board compute (in-vehicle)** = the doctor's own knowledge, reflexes, and stethoscope — handles immediate, life-critical decisions (braking, lane keeping).
- **Edge computing (RSU / 5G MEC)** = a **nearby hospital** in the city block — offers specialist tools (high-resolution 3D maps of the intersection, traffic-light phase prediction) accessible within a few milliseconds.
- **Cloud computing** = a **world-class research medical center** across the country — holds every patient's full history, the latest drug research, and trains future doctors. It is slow to reach by road, but indispensable for long-term, large-scale knowledge.

Just as the doctor escalates to the hospital for a complex scan and to the research center for rare-disease expertise, an AV escalates a perception task to the edge for sub-10 ms response and to the cloud for fleet-wide learning and HD-map refreshes. **No single tier is sufficient; the three tiers are layered by latency budget.**

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **End-to-End Latency (t_E2E)** — typically **< 100 ms** for cooperative perception, **< 10 ms** for safety-critical control.
- **Bandwidth (B)** — single AV raw sensor stream ≈ **1–4 Gbps**; uplinks support **10–100 Mbps** per vehicle in 5G/6G.
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** — cloud RTT commonly **20–80 ms**; edge RTT commonly **1–5 ms**.
- **Availability / Reliability** — safety-grade target **99.999%** ("five nines").
- **Data Volume per AV per day** — **2–20 TB** depending on sensor suite and recording policy.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever asked to "justify" edge vs cloud, **always quote a latency number** (e.g., "cloud RTT 50 ms violates the 10 ms control-loop budget; therefore edge processing is mandatory"). Numerical justification is the single highest-scoring move in Module 3 answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier VEC Architecture (Why and How)

The autonomous-vehicle ecosystem is structured as **three logical tiers**, each chosen to satisfy a specific latency–bandwidth–intelligence trade-off.

**Tier 1 — In-Vehicle Compute (On-Board)**
- *Why it exists:* Sensor-fusion and motion-control loops are bounded by physics (a 60 km/h car covers ~16 m/s, so a 10 ms delay = 16 cm of unwanted travel). Only on-board hardware can meet such deadlines.
- *Typical hardware:* GPU (NVIDIA Drive Orin, ~254 TOPS), FPGA, automotive SoC (Qualcomm Ride).
- *How:* Real-time Operating System (RTOS) such as AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive, ROS 2, or BlackBerry QNX.
- *Scope:* Local perception (object detection, lane segmentation), trajectory planning, longitudinal/lateral control, sensor health monitoring.

**Tier 2 — Edge / MEC Layer (Roadside)**
- *Why it exists:* Cooperative perception, intersection management, and vulnerable-road-user (VRU) protection require a vantage point *outside* the ego vehicle's occluded field of view. Cloud RTT is too high; on-board sensors are too narrow.
- *How:* Deployed at 5G base stations (gNodeB), RSUs, or regional micro-data-centres. Standards: **ETSI MEC** (Multi-access Edge Computing), 3GPP TS 23.558.
- *Scope:* V2X message brokering (CAM/DENM), intersection collision avoidance, dynamic HD-map diffs for the local corridor, partial offload of deep-learning inference (e.g., heavy 3-D detection).
- *Latency budget:* **1–10 ms** (typical), up to 20 ms in 3GPP URLLC.

**Tier 3 — Cloud (Hyperscale)**
- *Why it exists:* Single-vehicle data is noisy and biased; only aggregating an entire fleet reveals true distribution shifts, long-tail hazards, and new road geometries. Cloud also hosts offline training of perception/prediction models.
- *How:* Hyperscale data centres (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, dedicated OEM clouds such as Tesla Dojo, Waymo Cloud, Volvo Cars Cloud). Access via 4G LTE, 5G eMBB, or future 6G.
- *Scope:* HD-map production, fleet learning (federated or centralized), simulation at scale (CARLA, Waymax), OTA delivery, regulatory event-data recorder (EDR) archival, remote operations / tele-assist.
- *Latency budget:* **> 100 ms is acceptable**; data plane can be asynchronous.

### 2.2 The Decision Logic: "What Goes Where?"

A canonical decision rule used in OEM Tier-1 architectures:

$$
\text{Deploy to} =
\begin{cases}
\text{On-board}, & \text{if deadline } t_d \leq 10 \text{ ms (safety-critical control)}\\[4pt]
\text{Edge (MEC)}, & \text{if } 10 \text{ ms} < t_d \leq 100 \text{ ms (cooperative perception, V2X)}\\[4pt]
\text{Cloud}, & \text{if } t_d > 100 \text{ ms} \;\; \text{AND} \;\; \text{data volume} \gg \text{link budget} \\[4pt]
\text{Cloud (asynchronous)}, & \text{if task is offline training, archival, OTA, HD-map build.}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Ready)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Total data rate from one AV | $D = \sum_{i} N_i \cdot f_{s,i} \cdot b_{px}$ | Gbps | $N_i$ = pixels/points, $f_{s,i}$ = frame/Hz, $b_{px}$ = bits/pixel. For a typical 8-camera + 1-LiDAR suite $D \approx 1$–$4$ Gbps. |
| 2 | Effective uplink data rate (compressed) | $D_{ul} = R \cdot D$ | Gbps | $R$ = compression ratio, $R \approx 0.05$–$0.2$ for perceptually-lossy codecs. |
| 3 | End-to-end latency | $t_{E2E} = t_{proc} + t_{queue} + t_{trans} + t_{prop} + t_{proc,\,dest}$ | s | Sum of every stage. Cloud is dominated by $t_{prop}$ (~30 ms RTT inter-region). |
| 4 | Propagation delay (wireless) | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{c} + t_{core}$ | s | $d$ = distance, $c \approx 3\times 10^{8}$ m/s; $t_{core} \approx 5$–$15$ ms in fibre back-haul. |
| 5 | Transmission delay (packet) | $t_{trans} = \dfrac{L_{pkt}}{B}$ | s | $L_{pkt}$ = packet size, $B$ = link bandwidth. |
| 6 | Round-Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2\,(t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{proc})$ | s | Cloud RTT ≈ 20–80 ms; edge RTT ≈ 1–5 ms. |
| 7 | Shannon channel capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | Theoretical max; practical throughput ≈ 0.4–0.7 $C$. |
| 8 | Daily data per AV | $V_{day} = D_{ul} \cdot 86400$ | TB | Yields 2–20 TB/day per AV. |
| 9 | Fleet learning communication cost (FedAvg) | $K = n \cdot \vert \theta \vert \cdot 2$ | bits | $n$ = clients, $\vert\theta\vert$ = model parameters, factor 2 for up + down link. |
| 10 | MEC compute budget | $t_{MEC} = t_{RTT/2} + t_{proc,\,MEC} + t_{downlink}$ | s | Compare directly to deadline $t_d$. |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX note for students copying into answer sheets:** Use `\vert\theta\vert` (not `|\theta|`) so the vertical bar does not break KTU's answer-book column layout.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why the Industry Cares

- **HD-Map freshness:** Centimetre-level maps age quickly due to construction, signage changes, and dynamic permissives (turn arrows). Only cloud-based fleet aggregation can keep them current within hours, not weeks.
- **Federated fleet learning:** Each car trains locally; only model gradients (a few MB) leave the car, preserving privacy and 5G bandwidth.
- **OTA updates:** A 5 GB ADAS patch delivered to a 1-million-vehicle fleet via cloud CDN reaches ≥ 95 % of vehicles within 48 h, vs. months via dealer visits.
- **Tele-operation / Remote Assist:** When an AV encounters an unmapped construction zone, a cloud-orchestrated remote operator provides a path, validated in < 2 s.
- **Regulatory compliance:** UNECE WP.29 R155/R156 require traceable OTA and continuous monitoring — the cloud is the system of record.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-to-End Latency Budget for an AV Perception Pipeline

**Problem statement.** An autonomous vehicle triggers a perception query. On-board compute executes the CNN in 8 ms. The compressed feature tensor is sent to the cloud for HD-map-assisted classification, processed there in 25 ms, and the result is returned. Compute the minimum E2E latency and decide whether the decision can be safety-critical (assume deadline $t_d = 50$ ms). Use: packet size $L_{pkt} = 1500$ B, link bandwidth $B = 100$ Mbps, one-way distance to nearest cloud PoP $d = 300$ km, fibre back-haul.

**Step 1 — Identify the stages.**

$$
t_{E2E} = t_{proc,ob} + t_{queue} + t_{trans,up} + t_{prop,up} + t_{proc,cloud} + t_{prop,down} + t_{trans,down} + t_{queue,down} + t_{proc,app}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute transmission delay (up).**

$$
t_{trans,up} = \frac{L_{pkt}}{B} = \frac{1500 \times 8\ \text{bits}}{100 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bits/s}} = \frac{12\,000}{10^{8}} = 1.20 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.12\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute propagation delay (one-way, fibre approx two-thirds the speed of light).**

$$
v_{fibre} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}, \quad t_{prop} = \frac{300\,000\ \text{m}}{2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Add core-network processing and queueing** (typical LTE/5G core contribution): $t_{core} + t_{queue} \approx 8$ ms each way.

**Step 5 — Sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{E2E} &= 8 + 0.12 + 1.5 + 8 + 25 + 1.5 + 0.12 + 8 + 0 \\
        &= 52.24\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare to deadline.**

$$
t_{E2E} = 52.24\ \text{ms} > t_d = 50\ \text{ms}
$$

**Conclusion.** The cloud-only path **violates** the 50 ms safety budget by 2.24 ms. The same computation, executed on an **edge server** 5 km away, gives $t_{prop,edge} \approx 0.025$ ms, and the total E2E drops to ~ 42 ms — safely below budget. **Therefore edge processing is mandatory for safety-critical loops; cloud is reserved for non-real-time intelligence.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Daily Uplink Volume from a Sensor-Equipped AV

**Given.** An AV carries 8 cameras at 1920 × 1080 px, 30 fps, 12 bits/pixel raw, plus one 128-beam LiDAR at 20 Hz producing 2 MB/s. Only 10 % of frames are uploaded (triggered events + training samples).

**Step 1 — Camera data rate.**

$$
D_{cam} = 8 \times (1920 \times 1080) \times 30 \times 12 = 5.97\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 2 — LiDAR data rate.**

$$
D_{lid} = 2\ \text{MB/s} \times 8\ \text{b/B} = 16\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Total raw.**

$$
D_{raw} = D_{cam} + D_{lid} = 5.986\ \text{Gbps} \approx 5.99\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 10 % upload ratio.**

$$
D_{ul,\,raw} = 0.10 \times 5.986 = 0.5986\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 15 : 1 perceptual compression.**

$$
D_{ul} = \frac{0.5986}{15} = 0.0399\ \text{Gbps} \approx 40\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 6 — Daily volume.**

$$
V_{day} = 40\ \text{Mbps} \times 86400\ \text{s} \times \frac{1\ \text{GB}}{8000\ \text{Mb}} = 432\ \text{GB/day per AV}
$$

For a 100 000-vehicle fleet this is **43.2 PB/day** — the scale that justifies hyperscale cloud infrastructure.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Federated Learning Communication Cost

**Given.** 10 000 vehicles, each with a 50-million-parameter perception model (4-byte float32 weights).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per-round model size} &= 50 \times 10^{6} \times 4\ \text{B} = 200\ \text{MB} \\
\text{Uplink per client}   &= 200\ \text{MB} \\
\text{Downlink per client} &= 200\ \text{MB} \\
\text{Total per round}     &= 10\,000 \times (200 + 200)\ \text{MB} = 4\ \text{TB/round}
\end{aligned}
$$

Even a single global training round of federated learning across the fleet moves **4 TB** through the cellular core. KTU 2024 students should remember this as a justification for **gradient compression, sparse updates, and quantization** in production fleet-learning systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cloud vs Edge vs On-Board

| Dimension | On-Board (Tier 1) | Edge / MEC (Tier 2) | Cloud (Tier 3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | **< 10 ms** | **1 – 20 ms** | **20 – 100 ms** |
| Compute power | 100 – 300 TOPS | 10 – 100 POPS (data centre grade) | Exa-FLOPs (unbounded) |
| Storage | TB (SSD) | 10 – 100 TB (region) | EB (hyperscale) |
| Connectivity need | None (autonomous) | 5G URLLC | 4G/5G/Wi-Fi/satellite |
| Failure mode | Local — safety fallback | Site loss — re-route to neighbour | Region loss — graceful degradation |
| Privacy | High (data never leaves) | Medium (regional aggregation) | Lower (cross-fleet) |
| Examples of use | Sensor fusion, control | Cooperative perception, V2X | HD-map build, federated training, OTA, sim |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Cognitive level:** This matrix appears verbatim in the official Module-3 learning material. Memorise it as a 2 × 6 table; it scores directly in Part-A definitions and Part-B comparative questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Three-Tier Vehicle–Edge–Cloud Continuum

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Veh["Tier 1: On-Board Vehicle"]
        A1["Sensors: Camera, LiDAR, Radar, IMU, GNSS"]
        A2["On-Board Compute: GPU SoC, RTOS"]
        A3["Drive-by-wire Control: Brake, Steer, Throttle"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph Edge["Tier 2: Edge / MEC Layer"]
        B1["RSU / 5G gNodeB"]
        B2["MEC Server: Cooperative Perception, V2X Broker"]
        B3["Local HD-Map Diff Store"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph Cloud["Tier 3: Cloud Hyperscale"]
        C1["Data Ingest Gateway"]
        C2["Fleet Learning: Federated Trainer, Sim Cluster"]
        C3["HD-Map Production Pipeline"]
        C4["OTA Update Server, CDN"]
        C5["Remote Tele-Operations Console"]
        C1 --> C2
        C1 --> C3
        C4 --> C2
        C5 --> C1
    end

    A2 -. "Cellular 5G / DSRC C-V2X" .-> B1
    A2 -. "Cellular 5G eMBB / Satellite" .-> C1
    B2 -. "Back-haul fibre" .-> C1
    A2 -. "OTA download" .-> C4
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Data-Flow for an HD-Map Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["AV detects map change"]
    V2["Localize: GNSS + Visual Odometry"]
    V3["Compress change to diff tile"]
    V4["Upload to cloud over 5G"]
    C1["Cloud validates: cross-check 50+ vehicles"]
    C2["Cloud merges diff into HD-Map vNext"]
    C3["Sign and stage OTA package"]
    C4["CDN pushes to fleet"]
    V5["AV downloads delta via OTA"]
    V6["Local HD-Map updated atomically"]

    V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> V5 --> V6
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Latency-Budget Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["New task: where to deploy?"]
    Q2{"Is deadline t_d <= 10 ms?"}
    Q3["On-board compute"]
    Q4{"Is 10 ms < t_d <= 100 ms?"}
    Q5["Edge / MEC"]
    Q6{"Task needs fleet-wide data?"}
    Q7["Cloud (asynchronous)"]
    Q8["Cloud (synchronous, low-priority)"]

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 -- yes --> Q3
    Q2 -- no --> Q4
    Q4 -- yes --> Q5
    Q4 -- no --> Q6
    Q6 -- yes --> Q7
    Q6 -- no --> Q8
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** Render **Diagram 3** in your answer sheet using a hand-drawn flow chart (KTU still awards full marks for clean pencil sketches). The decision thresholds 10 ms / 100 ms are the high-yield numbers examiners test.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — OTA / Federated Learning Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1["Cloud: Global Model M_t"]
    M2["Distribute M_t to 10k vehicles"]
    M3["Each AV trains on local data -> delta M_t^i"]
    M4["AV uploads encrypted gradient delta"]
    M5["Secure Aggregation Server: average gradients"]
    M6["Cloud: produce M_t+1"]
    M7["Validate M_t+1 in simulation suite"]
    M8["Sign and OTA-deliver to fleet"]
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6 --> M7 --> M8 --> M1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, OEMUT611]**
*Define cloud computing in the context of autonomous vehicles. List three services the cloud typically provides to an AV fleet.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cloud computing in AV ecosystems refers to the on-demand delivery of shared compute, storage, and intelligence services from geographically distributed hyperscale data centres to autonomous vehicles over a cellular/satellite network. Three canonical cloud services for AV fleets are: **(1) HD-map production and distribution**, aggregating lane-level geometry, signs, and traffic rules from millions of vehicles; **(2) Fleet learning and simulation**, where perception/prediction models are trained on aggregated data and validated in cloud-scale simulation; and **(3) Over-the-Air (OTA) software delivery**, allowing continuous, signed updates of autonomy stacks without dealer visits. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for three services — ½ + ½ + ½ + ½ = 2, totalling 3.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, OEMUT611]**
*Distinguish between edge computing and cloud computing in autonomous vehicles. Give one example workload for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Edge computing places compute resources at the network edge — typically Roadside Units (RSUs) or 5G Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) servers within 1–10 km of the vehicle — yielding **1–10 ms latency** suitable for time-critical cooperative tasks such as **intersection collision avoidance using V2X message brokering**. Cloud computing, in contrast, executes tasks in distant hyperscale data centres with **20–100 ms RTT**, but offers virtually unlimited compute/storage for non-real-time, fleet-wide workloads such as **offline HD-map rebuilding or federated training of perception models**. The key distinction is therefore *latency vs scale*: edge optimises for the former, cloud for the latter. *(1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for edge example, 1 mark for cloud example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Rule:** Each Part-B question is internally optional — answer either **Question A** or **Question B**.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the three-tier Vehicle–Edge–Cloud (VEC) architecture for autonomous vehicles. Discuss, with latency budgets, which tier should host (a) emergency braking control, (b) cooperative perception at an unsignalised intersection, and (c) HD-map rebuilding.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Tier selection for emergency braking control [5 marks]**
Emergency braking is a *safety-critical* control action. The vehicle's stopping distance at 60 km/h is ~16 m, so the perception-to-actuation deadline is **t_d ≤ 10 ms** (typically 3 ms for AEB, per Euro NCAP AEB protocols). Both edge and cloud latencies exceed this budget — cloud RTT is 20–80 ms, edge RTT is 1–5 ms but still adds 1–10 ms of off-vehicle processing. **Therefore Tier 1 (on-board compute) is mandatory** for emergency braking. The on-board pipeline is: sensor capture (1 ms) → CNN inference (4–6 ms) → trajectory planner (1 ms) → brake actuation (1 ms) ≤ 10 ms.

**(b) Tier selection for cooperative perception at an unsignalised intersection [5 marks]**
At an unsignalised intersection a single AV cannot see hidden road users (VRUs) approaching from cross-streets. It needs data from neighbour vehicles and infrastructure cameras. The deadline is **10 ms < t_d ≤ 100 ms** (typically 50 ms, per ETSI EN 302 637-2 CAM). **Tier 2 (Edge / MEC)** is the correct placement. The RSU aggregates V2X messages, runs sensor fusion across 3–5 connected vehicles, and broadcasts a Cooperative Perception Message (CPM, ETSI TS 103 324). Latency breakdown: 5G URLLC uplink (5 ms) → MEC fusion (15 ms) → 5G downlink (5 ms) = 25 ms, well within budget.

**(c) Tier selection for HD-map rebuilding [4 marks]**
HD-map rebuilding is an *offline* task with no real-time deadline. It requires aggregating change detections from millions of vehicles over weeks, validating against satellite imagery, and producing a signed map vNext. The data volume is petabyte-scale and the latency budget is *days, not milliseconds*. **Therefore Tier 3 (Cloud) is correct.** The cloud ingests diff tiles, runs differential map-merge algorithms, validates the new map in simulation, and OTA-publishes the package.

**Sketch (extra credit, 1 mark):** Draw the three-tier block diagram from §4.1.

**Total: 5 + 5 + 4 = 14 marks.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
*Explain how federated learning is used in autonomous-vehicle cloud platforms. Derive the communication cost per training round for a fleet of $n$ vehicles each holding a model of $\vert\theta\vert$ parameters, and discuss two techniques to reduce this cost.*

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual explanation [6 marks]**
Federated learning is a *privacy-preserving distributed training* paradigm in which a global model is trained by aggregating locally-computed model updates — never the raw driving data — from each connected vehicle. The workflow (see §4.4) is:
1. Cloud distributes global model $M_t$ to $n$ vehicles (downlink).
2. Each vehicle $i$ trains $M_t$ on its local driving data and computes a gradient update $\Delta M_t^i$.
3. Vehicles upload $\Delta M_t^i$ (uplink) — not the raw multi-TB sensor data.
4. A Secure Aggregation server in the cloud averages gradients to form $M_{t+1}$.
5. Cloud validates $M_{t+1}$ in simulation; if safe, signs and OTA-delivers to fleet.

Benefits: **(i)** raw data never leaves the vehicle (GDPR / UNECE compliant); **(ii)** cellular bandwidth is reduced by ~1000× since only model parameters cross the network; **(iii)** fleet diversity is captured (long-tail hazards from rare geographies).

**Derivation of communication cost [4 marks]**
For a model with $\vert\theta\vert$ parameters at 32-bit float precision, the per-vehicle model size in bytes is:

$$
S = \vert\theta\vert \times 4\ \text{B}
$$

Each round, every vehicle sends its model **up** to the cloud and receives the new model **down**:

$$
\text{Cost per vehicle per round} = 2\,S = 2\,\vert\theta\vert \times 4\ \text{B} = 8\,\vert\theta\vert\ \text{B}
$$

For a fleet of $n$ vehicles:

$$
K_{\text{total}} = 2\,n\,S = 8\,n\,\vert\theta\vert\ \text{B}
$$

**Numerical illustration [2 marks]**
For $n = 10\,000$ and $\vert\theta\vert = 50 \times 10^{6}$ parameters:

$$
K_{\text{total}} = 8 \times 10^{4} \times 5 \times 10^{7}\ \text{B} = 4 \times 10^{12}\ \text{B} = 4\ \text{TB/round}
$$

**Two cost-reduction techniques [2 marks]**
1. **Gradient sparsification (Top-K / random-K):** Only the top 1–10 % of gradient magnitudes are transmitted, reducing uplink by 10–100× with negligible accuracy loss.
2. **Model quantisation:** Transmit gradients in 8-bit or 4-bit integers instead of 32-bit floats, cutting cost by 4–8×. Combined with sparsification, total cost can drop by 40–800×, making production federated learning feasible on 5G eMBB.

**Total: 6 + 4 + 2 + 2 = 14 marks.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 3 answers:**
> 1. **Confusing MEC with cloud** — many students write "edge = small cloud"; examiners deduct 1 mark per such slip. *Remember: edge is < 10 km away; cloud is inter-city.*
> 2. **Omitting latency numbers** — describing a tier without quoting its **RTT or t_d budget** scores at most 50 % of marks for that sub-question. Always pair *tier* with *latency budget in ms*.
> 3. **Wrong direction for OTA** — students frequently say "cloud pushes data to vehicle", but forget to mention the **secure boot / signing** aspect required by UNECE R156. Deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting privacy in federated learning** — saying "vehicles send raw images" in a FL answer is an automatic 2-mark deduction. The whole *point* of FL is that raw data never leaves the car.
> 5. **Mixing up $D$, $D_{ul}$, $D_{raw}$** — examiners in Module 3 unit-test if you can distinguish raw sensor rate from compressed uplink rate. Quote both, with the compression ratio.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **VEC continuum** = Vehicle + Edge + Cloud, each tier chosen by **latency budget**.
- [ ] **Latency thresholds to memorise:** on-board ≤ 10 ms, edge 1–20 ms, cloud 20–100 ms.
- [ ] **Cloud services for AVs:** HD-map production, fleet learning, OTA delivery, simulation, tele-ops, EDR archival.
- [ ] **Edge/MEC services:** V2X message brokering (CAM/DENM), cooperative perception, local HD-map diff, intersection collision avoidance.
- [ ] **On-board services:** sensor fusion, trajectory planning, drive-by-wire control.
- [ ] **Data rate per AV:** raw 1–4 Gbps; compressed uplink ~ 40 Mbps; daily volume ~ 0.4–2 TB.
- [ ] **Federated learning cost:** $K_{total} = 8 n \vert\theta\vert$ bytes per round; reduce via sparsification + quantisation.
- [ ] **Standards to mention in answers:** ETSI MEC, 3GPP TS 23.558, UNECE R155/R156, ETSI EN 302 637-2 (CAM), ETSI TS 103 324 (CPM).
- [ ] **Privacy: raw data never leaves the vehicle** in FL — only gradients do.
- [ ] **Connectivity: 5G URLLC** is the de-facto edge link; **5G eMBB** is the cloud link.
- [ ] **Decision rule** (Q3 Mermaid): $t_d \le 10$ ms → on-board; $10$–$100$ ms → edge; otherwise → cloud.
- [ ] **Compute units to know:** TOPS (on-board), POPS (edge), Exa-FLOPs (cloud).
- [ ] **Failure-mode thinking** scores bonus marks: always mention *what happens if the link drops* — answer: on-board fallback (graceful degradation to L2/L3).

> **End of Module-3 Notes — Cloud Computing and Edge Processing in AV Ecosystems.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Edge Processing for Real-Time Data Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Edge Processing for Real-Time Data Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — EDGE PROCESSING FOR REAL-TIME DATA HANDLING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Edge Processing** is a distributed computing paradigm in which computational tasks—including data acquisition, filtering, sensor fusion, perception inference, and control actuation—are executed locally on or near the autonomous/AGV platform itself, rather than being transmitted to a distant centralized cloud server. This paradigm is *mandatory* in autonomous vehicles because the strict **deterministic latency budget** (typically **< 100 ms** for collision avoidance and **< 10 ms** for emergency braking) cannot tolerate the round-trip propagation, queuing, and congestion delays of cloud architectures.

---

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reflex vs. Phone Call" Intuition

Imagine you are driving and a child suddenly runs in front of your car.

| Human Driver Behaviour | Equivalent in Edge Processing | Equivalent in Cloud Processing |
|---|---|---|
| You brake **instantly** — a spinal reflex | Brake command executed in **~5–10 ms** on the local ECU | You must call a friend, describe the scene, wait for instructions, then act |
| No network is involved | No network round-trip | Network latency (~50–200 ms) plus processing |
| Reaction time ≈ 150 ms | Latency budget ≈ 10–100 ms | Latency budget ≈ 200–1000 ms (often TOO LATE) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight:** The human reflex arc (sensing → spinal cord → muscle) is exactly the biological analogue of edge processing. The brain (cloud) is consulted *after* the reflex fires, only for non-critical decisions such as route re-planning. Autonomous vehicles follow the identical hierarchy: **edge handles life-critical reflexes; cloud handles non-critical cognition.**

### 1.2 Why Edge Processing Is Non-Negotiable in AGVs and AVs

An autonomous vehicle generates massive volumes of heterogeneous sensor data every second. Consider a typical sensor stack:

- **LiDAR:** ~**1–3 GB/s** (10–20 Hz, 64–128 channels)
- **Cameras (×8):** ~**1.2 GB/s** at 1080p, 30 fps
- **Radar (×6):** ~**0.05 GB/s**
- **Ultrasonic (×12):** ~**0.001 GB/s**
- **IMU / GNSS / Odometry:** ~**0.001 GB/s**

**Aggregate raw throughput ≈ 2.5 GB/s per vehicle.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth Reality Check (Why Cloud-Only Fails):**
> Even with **5G** (theoretical peak ≈ **10 Gbps** shared), continuous upload of 2.5 GB/s is impossible at fleet scale. A fleet of **1 million AVs** would generate ≈ **2.5 EB/s** (exabytes per second)—an utterly unmanageable network and storage load. Edge processing solves this by performing **on-board data reduction, feature extraction, and decision-making**, transmitting only **semantically meaningful events** (e.g., "pedestrian detected at 15 m") to the cloud.

### 1.3 The Real-Time Latency Hierarchy in AVs

| Decision Class | Maximum Latency Budget | Processing Location |
|---|---|---|
| Emergency Braking / AEB | **< 10 ms** | Edge (ECU, hardware-accelerated) |
| Lane Keeping / ACC | **< 50 ms** | Edge (GPU / NPU inference) |
| Path Planning (local) | **< 100 ms** | Edge (CPU + optimized planner) |
| Behavioural Planning | **< 500 ms** | Edge (vehicle CPU) |
| Route Re-planning | **< 5 s** | Edge ↔ Cloud (collaborative) |
| Fleet Learning / OTA Updates | **minutes–hours** | Cloud only |

### 1.4 Edge vs. Fog vs. Cloud — The Three-Tier Continuum

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> - **Edge Computing:** Computation occurs *on the device itself* or within **≤ 1 m** of the sensor (e.g., inside the vehicle's compute box).
> - **Fog Computing:** Computation occurs at an *intermediate gateway* within **~10 m to 10 km** (e.g., a roadside unit, RSU, or 5G base station).
> - **Cloud Computing:** Computation occurs in a *distant data center*, typically **> 50 km** away with **> 50 ms** round-trip latency.

---

### 1.5 Hardware Platforms for Edge Processing in AVs

| Platform | Strengths | Typical Use in AV |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU (e.g., ARM Cortex-A78, x86 automotive)** | Flexible, general-purpose | Behaviour planning, route logic |
| **GPU (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Drive PX)** | Massive parallel throughput | Deep learning perception (CNN inference) |
| **NPU / TPU (e.g., Hailo-8, Google Edge TPU)** | TOPS/Watt efficiency | Low-power neural inference |
| **FPGA (e.g., Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+)** | Deterministic latency, hardware pipelining | Sensor pre-processing, LiDAR decoding |
| **ASIC (e.g., Mobileye EyeQ5)** | Maximum efficiency, purpose-built | Production-grade perception stacks |
| **MCU (e.g., Infineon AURIX TC397)** | ASIL-D functional safety | Braking, steering actuation (real-time control) |
| **DSP (e.g., TI TDA4x)** | Signal processing throughput | Radar FFT, sensor front-end |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Heterogeneous Computing:**
> Modern autonomous vehicles use a **heterogeneous compute architecture**: no single chip handles everything. The MCU handles safety-critical control loops, the NPU/ASIC handles perception, the CPU handles planning. This division is *itself* a form of edge processing strategy—**functional partitioning by latency and safety class.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Edge Processing Pipeline (Conceptual Stages)

The real-time data handling chain inside an AV's edge compute platform follows a structured, deterministic sequence:

1. **Sensor Data Acquisition (SDA)**
   - Synchronized timestamping using **PPS (Pulse Per Second)** and **PTP (Precision Time Protocol, IEEE 1588)**.
   - Hardware-triggered DMA (Direct Memory Access) into on-board buffers.

2. **Pre-Processing & Filtering**
   - **Denoising**, **ROI (Region of Interest) cropping**, **temporal smoothing**, **motion compensation**.
   - Goal: Reduce data volume before expensive perception stages.

3. **Sensor Fusion (Front-End)**
   - Early fusion (raw level), deep fusion (feature level), or late fusion (decision level).
   - Most AVs use **feature-level fusion** with a shared BEV (Bird's Eye View) representation.

4. **Perception (Object Detection, Segmentation, Tracking)**
   - Deep neural network inference (CNN, Transformer, BEVFormer).
   - Output: Bounding boxes, semantic classes, trajectories.

5. **Prediction & Planning**
   - Predict motion of detected agents (social forces, learned models).
   - Generate local trajectory candidates.

6. **Control Actuation**
   - Translate chosen trajectory into steering, throttle, brake commands at **> 100 Hz**.
   - Hard real-time loop with bounded jitter.

7. **Telemetry & Event Upload (Selective)**
   - Only safety events, anomalies, and learning samples sent to cloud via **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)**.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Edge Processing — Five Engineering Drivers

| Driver | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Determinism** | Cloud latency is unbounded; edge latency is bounded by hardware |
| **Bandwidth Economy** | 2.5 GB/s raw data cannot be uploaded; only events need transmission |
| **Privacy / GDPR** | Raw video may contain faces/license plates — keep locally |
| **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)** | ASIL-D loops cannot depend on wireless connectivity |
| **Offline Operation** | Tunnels, rural areas, RF jamming — edge still functions |

---

## 2.3 Latency Decomposition — The End-to-End Latency Equation

The total time from a physical event occurring in front of the vehicle to the actuation response is governed by the following canonical equation:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{plan}} + T_{\text{actuate}}
$$

Where each term is defined as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sense}}     &= \text{Sensor exposure/acquisition time} \\
T_{\text{transmit}}  &= \text{Data transfer over internal bus (CAN, Ethernet, PCIe)} \\
T_{\text{queue}}     &= \text{Buffer/scheduling wait in compute pipeline} \\
T_{\text{process}}   &= \text{Algorithm execution time (perception, fusion)} \\
T_{\text{plan}}      &= \text{Behaviour + trajectory planning time} \\
T_{\text{actuate}}   &= \text{Actuator command propagation + mechanical response}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-High-Yield Insight:** For **edge processing**, $T_{\text{transmit}}$ and $T_{\text{queue}}$ are **bounded and deterministic** because the bus and compute are physically co-located. For **cloud processing**, both terms become **stochastic variables** with potentially unbounded upper tails — this is precisely why cloud-only architectures fail the AV safety case.

---

## 2.4 Real-Time Scheduling — Deadline Guarantee

Edge processing must satisfy hard real-time deadlines. The classical **Liu & Layland** utilization bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) on $n$ tasks is:

$$
U_{\text{RMS}}(n) = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

As $n \to \infty$, this bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. For typical automotive ECUs running 10–20 periodic tasks, the bound is comfortably above 0.8.

> **Worked Examples of $U_{\text{RMS}}(n)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{RMS}}(1) &= 1.000 \quad (\text{trivially schedulable}) \\
U_{\text{RMS}}(2) &= 0.828 \\
U_{\text{RMS}}(3) &= 0.780 \\
U_{\text{RMS}}(5) &= 0.743 \\
U_{\text{RMS}}(10) &= 0.717 \\
U_{\text{RMS}}(\infty) &= 0.693
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 2.5 Throughput, Latency, and the Memory Wall

The relationship between achievable compute throughput $P$ (in operations per second), memory bandwidth $B$ (in bytes per second), and the *arithmetic intensity* $I$ (in operations per byte) of an algorithm is captured by the **Roofline Model**:

$$
P = \min \left( P_{\text{peak}},\ I \cdot B \right)
$$

> **Where:**
> - $P_{\text{peak}}$ = peak compute capability of the hardware (e.g., TOPS of an NPU)
> - $I$ = arithmetic intensity of the algorithm (ops/byte)
> - $B$ = memory bandwidth (bytes/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for AV Edge Devices:** A modern NPU may have $P_{\text{peak}} = \mathbf{100\ \text{TOPS}}$, but if the algorithm has low arithmetic intensity and the memory subsystem supplies only $\mathbf{50\ GB/s}$, then for $I = 10\ \text{ops/byte}$, the *attainable* throughput is $I \cdot B = \mathbf{500\ \text{GOPS}}$, not 100 TOPS. The algorithm is **memory-bound**, not compute-bound. This is why efficient edge inference engines (TensorRT, SNPE, ONNX Runtime) aggressively fuse operations to maximize arithmetic intensity.

---

## 2.6 The Communication Bus — Automotive Ethernet and CAN FD

Inside an AV, edge processors communicate over standardized buses with deterministic timing:

$$
T_{\text{frame}} = \frac{N_{\text{bytes}} \cdot 8}{R_{\text{bus}}} + T_{\text{propagation}} + T_{\text{jitter}}
$$

> **Typical Values for Common Automotive Buses:**

| Bus | Bandwidth $R_{\text{bus}}$ | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **CAN 2.0B** | 1 Mbps | Legacy ECUs, body control |
| **CAN FD** | 2–5 Mbps | Powertrain, chassis |
| **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps | X-by-wire, safety loops |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** | 100 Mbps | ADAS sensors, camera |
| **Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1)** | 1 Gbps | Central compute backbone |
| **PCIe Gen 3 (×4)** | ~32 Gbps | Internal SoC chip-to-chip |

---

## 2.7 The Edge Compute Budget — A KTU Numerical Scenario

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** An AGV uses a single edge GPU rated at $\mathbf{30\ \text{TOPS}}$ (INT8). It runs three neural networks at $\mathbf{30\ \text{Hz}}$:
> - Object detection CNN: $\mathbf{15\ \text{GOPS/frame}}$
> - Semantic segmentation: $\mathbf{8\ \text{GOPS/frame}}$
> - Multi-object tracking: $\mathbf{2\ \text{GOPS/frame}}$

**Required throughput per second:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{req}} &= (15 + 8 + 2)\ \text{GOPS} \times 30\ \text{Hz} \\
&= 25\ \text{GOPS} \times 30 \\
&= 750\ \text{GOPS} = 0.75\ \text{TOPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute headroom:**

$$
\text{Headroom} = \frac{0.75\ \text{TOPS}}{30\ \text{TOPS}} = 0.025 = 2.5\ \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** The remaining **97.5%** of compute is consumed by pre-processing, post-processing, memory copies, OS scheduling, and safety monitoring. This illustrates why **edge hardware sizing is non-trivial** — naive TOPS ratings vastly overstate usable compute.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total edge latency | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum T_i$ | Sum of all pipeline stages |
| 2 | RMS schedulability bound | $U_{\text{RMS}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Max CPU utilization for hard real-time |
| 3 | Roofline attainable performance | $P = \min(P_{\text{peak}},\ I \cdot B)$ | Compute vs. memory bottleneck |
| 4 | Bus frame time | $T_{\text{frame}} = (8N)/R + T_p + T_j$ | Communication latency |
| 5 | Required edge throughput | $P_{\text{req}} = \sum (\text{GOPS}_k \cdot f_k)$ | Aggregate neural network demand |
| 6 | Utilization | $U = P_{\text{req}} / P_{\text{peak}}$ | Headroom fraction (must be < 1) |
| 7 | Time-Sensitive Network (TSN) gate schedule | $T_{\text{max-latency}} = T_{\text{cycle}} \cdot N_{\text{queues}}$ | Deterministic Ethernet latency |
| 8 | Data rate | $D = (\text{bytes/frame}) \cdot f$ | Sensor bus bandwidth demand |
| 9 | Power efficiency | $\eta = \text{TOPS} / \text{Watt}$ | Critical for EV range |
| 10 | ASIL-D failure rate | $\lambda \leq 10^{-8}/\text{h}$ | Functional safety requirement |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Bounding Cloud-Only Latency vs. Edge Latency

We will derive a quantitative comparison between cloud-only and edge architectures for an autonomous emergency braking (AEB) scenario. This is a common KTU numerical type.

### Step 1 — Define the physical event
A pedestrian enters the lane at $t = 0$. The vehicle is travelling at $v = \mathbf{50\ \text{km/h}} = 13.89\ \text{m/s}}$.

### Step 2 — Identify the stopping distance constraint
The available stopping distance with reaction time $T_{\text{react}}$ and maximum deceleration $a = \mathbf{8\ m/s^2}$ is:

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = v \cdot T_{\text{react}} + \frac{v^2}{2a}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the maximum allowable reaction time

For a headway distance of $\mathbf{30\ \text{m}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 &= 13.89 \cdot T_{\text{react}} + \frac{(13.89)^2}{2 \cdot 8} \\
30 &= 13.89 \cdot T_{\text{react}} + \frac{192.93}{16} \\
30 &= 13.89 \cdot T_{\text{react}} + 12.06 \\
13.89 \cdot T_{\text{react}} &= 17.94 \\
T_{\text{react, max}} &= 1.29\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system has only **1.29 seconds** total budget — *including* sensing, processing, and actuation.

### Step 4 — Decompose the budget for cloud-only architecture

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cloud}} &= T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{tx-to-cloud}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{cloud-proc}} \\
&\quad + T_{\text{tx-back}} + T_{\text{actuate}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using realistic values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sense}}            &= 0.020\ \text{s} \quad (\text{camera exposure}) \\
T_{\text{tx-to-cloud}}      &= 0.050\ \text{s} \quad (\text{upload encoding + cell handoff}) \\
T_{\text{prop}}             &= 0.040\ \text{s} \quad (\text{RTT to nearest data centre, fibre}) \\
T_{\text{cloud-proc}}       &= 0.150\ \text{s} \quad (\text{cloud inference}) \\
T_{\text{tx-back}}          &= 0.050\ \text{s} \\
T_{\text{actuate}}          &= 0.020\ \text{s} \\
\hline
T_{\text{cloud}}            &= 0.020 + 0.050 + 0.040 + 0.150 + 0.050 + 0.020 \\
                             &= 0.330\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Decompose the budget for edge-only architecture

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{edge}} &= T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{local-bus}} + T_{\text{edge-proc}} + T_{\text{actuate}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sense}}      &= 0.020\ \text{s} \\
T_{\text{local-bus}}  &= 0.001\ \text{s} \quad (\text{automotive Ethernet, in-vehicle}) \\
T_{\text{edge-proc}}  &= 0.030\ \text{s} \quad (\text{optimized NPU inference}) \\
T_{\text{actuate}}    &= 0.020\ \text{s} \\
\hline
T_{\text{edge}}       &= 0.020 + 0.001 + 0.030 + 0.020 \\
                       &= 0.071\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the available braking distance for each architecture

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{avail, edge}} &= v \cdot T_{\text{edge}} + \frac{v^2}{2a} \\
&= 13.89 \times 0.071 + \frac{(13.89)^2}{16} \\
&= 0.986 + 12.058 \\
&= 13.04\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{avail, cloud}} &= v \cdot T_{\text{cloud}} + \frac{v^2}{2a} \\
&= 13.89 \times 0.330 + 12.058 \\
&= 4.584 + 12.058 \\
&= 16.64\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Determine which architecture avoids collision

The pedestrian is at $d = 30\ \text{m}$.

| Architecture | $d_{\text{avail}}$ | Collision? |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge** | 13.04 m | ✅ **Stops safely** (margin 16.96 m) |
| **Cloud** | 16.64 m | ✅ **Stops safely** (margin 13.36 m) |

Both avoid collision at $d=30\ \text{m}$, but consider $d = \mathbf{15\ \text{m}}$ (closer pedestrian):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{avail, edge}} - 15 &= 13.04 - 15 = -1.96\ \text{m} \quad (\text{edge barely stops}) \\
d_{\text{avail, cloud}} - 15 &= 16.64 - 15 = +1.64\ \text{m} \quad (\text{cloud FAILS})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At close range, the cloud architecture fails because the vehicle travels an additional $\mathbf{4.58\ \text{m}}$ during cloud round-trip alone. **Edge processing is mathematically required** for sub-second reaction scenarios.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — RMS Schedulability Test for an Edge ECU

Consider an edge ECU running three periodic tasks:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ (braking control) | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ (perception trigger) | 33 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ (telemetry upload) | 100 | 10 |

**Step 1 — Compute task utilizations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.200 \\
u_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{5}{33} = 0.152 \\
u_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$
U = u_1 + u_2 + u_3 = 0.200 + 0.152 + 0.100 = 0.452
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$:**

$$
U_{\text{RMS}}(3) = 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.780
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$
U = 0.452 < U_{\text{RMS}}(3) = 0.780
$$

✅ The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling on a single-core edge ECU. The headroom is:

$$
\text{Headroom} = 0.780 - 0.452 = 0.328 \quad (32.8\%)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Edge Compute Sizing for a Sensor Stack

A vehicle has **4 cameras** at $\mathbf{30\ \text{Hz}}$ with resolution $\mathbf{1920 \times 1080}$ (RGB). Each pixel is 3 bytes.

**Step 1 — Per-camera data rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{cam}} &= (1920 \times 1080) \times 3\ \text{bytes} \times 30\ \text{Hz} \\
&= 6.22\ \text{MB/frame} \times 30 \\
&= 186.6\ \text{MB/s} \approx 1.49\ \text{Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total for 4 cameras:**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 186.6 = 746.4\ \text{MB/s} \approx 5.97\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Edge processing must handle this throughput:**
A single Automotive Ethernet port (1000BASE-T1) provides $\mathbf{1\ \text{Gbps}}$. Even with **6 cameras**, the bus saturates. Therefore:
- Use **multiple Ethernet ports** OR
- **Compress on the camera sensor itself** (e.g., MJPEG at 10:1) OR
- **Perform early CNN inference inside the camera** (smart sensor)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Practice:** Modern automotive cameras embed a small NPU (e.g., **TI TDA4VM**, **Ambarella CV series**) that runs the *first stage* of perception (object detection) on-sensor. Only the **metadata + compressed video** is sent to the central compute box. This is **edge-of-the-edge** processing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Data Flow — Edge vs. Cloud Decision Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SENSOR EVENT<br/>PEDESTRIAN DETECTED"] --> B{"LATENCY<br/>BUDGET"}

    B -- LESS THAN 100 MS --> C["EDGE PATH<br/>LOCAL NPU"]
    B -- BETWEEN 100 MS<br/>AND 1 S --> D["FOG PATH<br/>ROADSIDE UNIT"]
    B -- GREATER THAN 1 S --> E["CLOUD PATH<br/>DATA CENTRE"]

    C --> F["INSTANT BRAKING<br/>AND STEERING"]
```
