# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEMUT611]`

**Define edge processing in the context of autonomous vehicles. Why is it preferred over cloud-only processing for safety-critical decisions?**

**Model Answer (Memorize this structure):**

> **Edge processing** refers to executing computation *locally on the autonomous vehicle* rather than transmitting raw sensor data to a remote cloud server. In AVs, edge processing is preferred over cloud-only architectures for safety-critical decisions because:
>
> 1. **Deterministic latency:** Cloud round-trip latency is typically 50–200 ms and is non-deterministic (jitter, congestion). Edge latency is bounded and predictable, often under 30 ms.
> 2. **Functional safety:** ISO 26262 ASIL-D braking loops cannot tolerate dependence on wireless networks that may fail (tunnels, jamming, outages).
> 3. **Bandwidth constraints:** Raw AV sensor data (~2.5 GB/s) cannot be continuously uploaded; edge reduces this to event-level metadata.
> 4. **Offline operation:** Edge functions regardless of connectivity; cloud does not.

**[Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 2 of the 4 reasons.]**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEMUT611]`

**Distinguish between edge, fog, and cloud computing with one example of each in an AV context.**

**Model Answer:**

| Layer | Location | Typical Latency | AV Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge** | Inside the vehicle (≤ 1 m from sensor) | 1–50 ms | AEB braking decision on the central ECU |
| **Fog** | Roadside unit / 5G base station (~10 m – 10 km) | 20–100 ms | Cooperative V2X hazard alert from a nearby RSU |
| **Cloud** | Data centre (> 50 km away) | > 100 ms | Fleet-wide HD map update server |

**[Marks distribution: 1.5 marks for table / list + 1.5 marks for the AV examples.]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of edge processing inside an autonomous vehicle. Discuss the role of **pre-processing, sensor fusion, and perception NPU**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An autonomous vehicle runs **three perception neural networks** on its edge NPU:
- Network $\mathcal{N}_1$ (object detection): **20 GOPS/frame** at **20 Hz**
- Network $\mathcal{N}_2$ (semantic segmentation): **15 GOPS/frame** at **10 Hz**
- Network $\mathcal{N}_3$ (depth estimation): **8 GOPS/frame** at **15 Hz**

The NPU has a peak throughput of **50 TOPS (INT8)**. Calculate the **required edge throughput** and determine if the system has sufficient headroom. Justify whether additional safety tasks (estimated **5 GOPS/frame** at **30 Hz**) can also be scheduled. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation [7 Marks]:**

The edge processing architecture of an AV is organized into the following layers (refer to Section 4.1 for the full Mermaid diagram; here we describe its layered structure for written exams):

> **Layer 1 — Sensor Layer:** LiDAR, cameras, radar, IMU, ultrasonic. These generate raw data at high rates.

> **Layer 2 — Pre-processing:** Time synchronization (PTP), denoising, ROI cropping, calibration, rectification. **Purpose:** reduce data volume and align modalities.

> **Layer 3 — Sensor Fusion:** Combines multi-modal data into a unified **Bird's Eye View (BEV)** representation. **Purpose:** provides a common geometric frame for downstream perception.

> **Layer 4 — Perception (NPU-based):** CNN inference for object detection, segmentation, lane detection. The NPU accelerates matrix multiplications in INT8/FP16.

> **Layer 5 — Tracking & Prediction:** Maintains object identities across frames and forecasts trajectories.

> **Layer 6 — Planning & Control:** Generates trajectory and converts to actuator commands at > 100 Hz.

> **Layer 7 — Safety MCU (ASIL-D):** Independent watchdog that validates perception/planning outputs and can override (fail-safe braking).

**Marks split:** 2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for layer-wise explanation.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute per-network throughput requirement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\mathcal{N}_1} &= 20\ \text{GOPS} \times 20\ \text{Hz} = 400\ \text{GOPS} = 0.400\ \text{TOPS} \\
P_{\mathcal{N}_2} &= 15\ \text{GOPS} \times 10\ \text{Hz} = 150\ \text{GOPS} = 0.150\ \text{TOPS} \\
P_{\mathcal{N}_3} &= 8\ \text{GOPS} \times 15\ \text{Hz} = 120\ \text{GOPS} = 0.120\ \text{TOPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate throughput:**

$$
P_{\text{req}} = 0.400 + 0.150 + 0.120 = 0.670\ \text{TOPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{P_{\text{req}}}{P_{\text{peak}}} = \frac{0.670}{50} = 0.0134 = 1.34\ \%
$$

**Step 4 — Add safety task:**

$$
P_{\text{safety}} = 5\ \text{GOPS} \times 30\ \text{Hz} = 150\ \text{GOPS} = 0.150\ \text{TOPS}
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 0.670 + 0.150 = 0.820\ \text{TOPS}
$$

$$
U_{\text{total}} = \frac{0.820}{50} = 0.0164 = 1.64\ \%
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Since $U_{\text{total}} = 1.64\% < 1$, the NPU has **98.36% headroom**, and the safety task can be comfortably scheduled. However, the student must note that **real-world utilization is much higher** due to pre-processing, post-processing, memory transfers, and OS overhead — typically 30–50% of peak. The system remains viable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Define **edge processing latency budget**. For an emergency braking scenario, derive the relationship between total reaction time, vehicle velocity, and stopping distance. Use realistic AV parameters to show why cloud-only processing fails for sub-30 m headway. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An AGV edge ECU must schedule four periodic tasks. Use the **Liu & Layland Rate Monotonic Scheduling** bound to determine if the task set is schedulable.

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ (motor control) | 5 | 1 |
| $\tau_2$ (LiDAR trigger) | 20 | 4 |
| $\tau_3$ (perception) | 50 | 10 |
| $\tau_4$ (telemetry) | 200 | 20 |

**[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Latency Budget Derivation [7 Marks]:**

The total reaction time $T_{\text{react}}$ determines the additional distance travelled before braking begins:

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = v \cdot T_{\text{react}} + \frac{v^2}{2a}
$$

For $v = 50\ \text{km/h} = 13.89\ \text{m/s}$, $a = 8\ \text{m/s}^2$, and headway $d = 30\ \text{m}$:

$$
T_{\text{react, max}} = \frac{30 - \frac{(13.89)^2}{16}}{13.89} = \frac{30 - 12.06}{13.89} = 1.29\ \text{s}
$$

For a closer headway of $d = 15\ \text{m}$:

$$
T_{\text{react, max}} = \frac{15 - 12.06}{13.89} = 0.212\ \text{s} = 212\ \text{ms}
$$

The cloud-only latency we calculated earlier was **330 ms** — exceeding this budget. Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{cloud}} = 330\ \text{ms} > T_{\text{react, max}} = 212\ \text{ms}
$$

❌ **Cloud-only fails at 15 m headway.** Edge latency (71 ms) is well within the budget. ✅

**Marks split:** 2 marks for equation, 3 marks for numerical evaluation, 2 marks for the comparative conclusion.

---

**Part (b) — RMS Schedulability Test [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute individual utilizations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.200 \\
u_2 &= \frac{4}{20} = 0.200 \\
u_3 &= \frac{10}{50} = 0.200 \\
u_4 &= \frac{20}{200} = 0.100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$
U = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.100 = 0.700
$$

**Step 3 — Liu & Layland bound for $n = 4$:**

$$
U_{\text{RMS}}(4) = 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.1892 = 0.7568
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$
U = 0.700 < U_{\text{RMS}}(4) = 0.7568
$$

✅ The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under RMS. The headroom is:

$$
0.7568 - 0.700 = 0.0568 = 5.68\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to assign priorities by period** — RMS requires that *shorter period = higher priority*. If you shuffle priorities, the bound still holds but a different (less favourable) test is needed.
> 2. **Confusing $U_{\text{RMS}}$ with EDF** — Earliest Deadline First can schedule up to $U = 1$; RMS is more restrictive ($\leq 0.693$ asymptotically). Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the $n$ in the formula** — Writing $U_{\text{RMS}} = 0.693$ without checking the actual $n$ of the task set loses marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — $T_i$ and $C_i$ must be in the same unit (typically ms). Always state.
> 5. **Not stating the conclusion explicitly** — The final "schedulable / not schedulable" sentence is worth 1 mark. Never omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3 / Edge Processing**

- ✅ **Edge processing** = computation *on* the autonomous vehicle, *not* in the cloud.
- ✅ **Driver #1:** Determinism — bounded, predictable latency (1–50 ms typical).
- ✅ **Driver #2:** Bandwidth economy — 2.5 GB/s raw data reduced to event-level metadata.
- ✅ **Driver #3:** Functional safety (ISO 26262 ASIL-D) — safety loops cannot depend on wireless.
- ✅ **Driver #4:** Privacy (GDPR) — raw video stays local.
- ✅ **Driver #5:** Offline robustness — tunnels, rural, jamming scenarios.
- ✅ **Three-tier model:** Edge (≤ 1 m) → Fog (≤ 10 km) → Cloud (> 50 km).
- ✅ **Heterogeneous edge hardware:** CPU + GPU + NPU + FPGA + ASIC + MCU + DSP coexist.
- ✅ **Real-time pipeline:** Sense → Pre-process → Fuse → Perceive → Plan → Control → Actuate.
- ✅ **Total latency equation:** $T_{\text{total}} = \sum T_i$ (sense + transmit + queue + process + plan + actuate).
- ✅ **Roofline model:** $P = \min(P_{\text{peak}},\ I \cdot B)$ — most edge algorithms are memory-bound, not compute-bound.
- ✅ **RMS bound:** $U_{\text{RMS}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; approaches 0.693 as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **AEB latency budget:** < 10 ms for actuation; cloud round-trip alone is 50–330 ms — **fails the safety case**.
- ✅ **Sensor data rates:** Camera 4× 1080p ≈ 5.97 Gbps aggregate; LiDAR adds ~3 GB/s.
- ✅ **Standard buses:** CAN FD (5 Mbps), FlexRay (10 Mbps), Automotive Ethernet 100/1000BASE-T1 (100 Mbps / 1 Gbps).
- ✅ **Time synchronization:** PTP (IEEE 1588) and PPS for sub-microsecond sensor alignment.
- ✅ **Smart sensors:** Modern cameras embed on-sensor NPUs — edge-of-the-edge processing.
- ✅ **Cloud role:** Non-critical cognition only — route re-planning, HD map updates, fleet learning, OTA.
- ✅ **Function partitioning:** ASIL-D on MCU, perception on NPU, planning on CPU — *separation by safety class*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples of Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platform** is a distributed, multi-tier computing ecosystem that integrates on-vehicle embedded intelligence with hyperscale cloud infrastructure to enable SAE Level 3–5 autonomous driving. It performs the off-vehicle heavy-lifting functions that exceed the thermal, power, and computational envelope of in-vehicle ECUs — namely **HD map generation, deep-learning model training, large-scale simulation, fleet learning, OTA (Over-The-Air) software delivery, big-data analytics, teleoperation, and V2X orchestration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The course explicitly categorizes cloud platforms as a *sub-system of the autonomous vehicle stack* and demands the student be able to name major industry platforms, their layered architecture, and their key services.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a fleet of self-driving cars as **a class of first-grade students learning to drive**. Each car (student) practices driving in its own neighbourhood. Now imagine that:
- At the end of every day, every car **uploads its experiences** (what it saw, where it braked, where it got confused) to a giant **school server in the sky (the cloud)**.
- The school's "Super Brain" computer **reads all experiences at once**, finds patterns (e.g., "this pothole appears on Tuesdays"), and **sends back improved driving tips** to every car the next morning.
- The school also keeps a **giant updated map** of the world that every car downloads for free.
- The school can even **simulate millions of dangerous scenarios in a virtual world** so the cars learn without ever crashing in real life.

That "school in the sky" is exactly a **Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platform**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in the Domain

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Daily data per vehicle | **1.5 – 4.0 TB/day** | Drives storage architecture |
| Driving hours per day | **8 – 14 h** | Determines active ingestion |
| 5G URLLC latency | **1 – 5 ms** | Enables safety-critical V2N |
| 4G/LTE latency | **50 – 100 ms** | Used for non-critical updates |
| HD map tile size | **8 – 25 MB / km²** | Bandwidth budgeting |
| OTA rollout fleet size | **10⁴ – 10⁶ vehicles** | Staged delivery model |
| Simulation miles/day | **10⁸ – 10⁹ miles** | Coverage of edge cases |
| Training data labelled | **10⁹ – 10¹⁰ images** | Deep model convergence |

> [!NOTE]
> The numbers above reflect 2024 production-grade platforms (Waymo Driver v6, Baidu Apollo 9.0, NVIDIA DRIVE Thor/Orin + DGX Cloud). They are representative engineering figures, not textbook citations.

### 1.4 Why Cloud Is Mandatory for L4+ Autonomy

On-vehicle SoCs (e.g., NVIDIA Orin ≈ 254 TOPS, Mobileye EyeQ6 ≈ 128 TOPS) are **power- and thermally-constrained**. They cannot:
1. Retrain a 10-billion-parameter foundation model every day.
2. Run photorealistic closed-loop simulation at 10× real-time.
3. Store petabyte-scale driving logs affordably.
4. Coordinate behaviour across millions of vehicles.

The cloud solves all four by trading latency for **virtually unlimited compute, storage, and bandwidth**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Three-Layer Architecture

Every mainstream cloud-AV platform — whether Baidu Apollo, NVIDIA DRIVE, Waymo, or Tesla — collapses to the same three logical layers:

**Layer 1 — In-Vehicle / Edge Tier**
- Sensor acquisition (cameras, LiDARs, radars, IMU, GNSS).
- Real-time perception & control on ADAS SoC.
- Telematics Control Unit (TCU) for V2N uplink.

**Layer 2 — Edge / Network Tier**
- 5G base stations, MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) nodes.
- Roadside Units (RSU) for V2I.
- TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking) for in-vehicle Ethernet backbone.

**Layer 3 — Cloud Tier**
- Data Lake (object storage, e.g., S3 / OSS).
- GPU/TPU training clusters (DGX Cloud, AWS P5, Google TPU v5).
- Closed-loop simulation farm.
- HD Map database, OTA server, Fleet Manager.
- Analytics, monitoring, and MLOps pipelines.

### 2.2 Core Cloud Services (KTU-Mapped Bullet List)

- **HD Map Storage & Distribution:** Centimetre-resolution maps stored as vector tiles, served via CDN.
- **Fleet Learning (Collective Perception):** Millions of vehicles contribute anonymised edge cases; central model re-trains and pushes weights back.
- **Simulation-as-a-Service:** Photorealistic, closed-loop sim using CARLA, NVIDIA DRIVE Sim, or proprietary engines.
- **OTA (Over-The-Air) Updates:** Staged, signed, differential firmware & model rollouts.
- **Big-Data Analytics & MLOps:** Spark, Ray, Kubeflow pipelines for model lineage & drift detection.
- **Teleoperation (Remote Assistance):** Human-in-the-loop fallback for L4 edge cases.
- **V2X Orchestration:** Cloud broker for V2V/V2I message routing.
- **Usage-Based Services:** Insurance, MaaS (Mobility-as-a-Service), predictive maintenance.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below lists the **5 essential engineering equations** a student must memorise for any cloud-AV numerical problem. **All vertical bars use `\vert` to protect markdown table syntax.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per-sensor raw data rate | $R_s = f \times B_{frame}$ | $f$ = frame rate (Hz), $B_{frame}$ = bytes/frame | Sized to design uplink bandwidth |
| 2 | Aggregate in-vehicle data rate | $R_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{s,i}$ | $R_{s,i}$ = rate of sensor $i$ (MB/s) | Decides 5G slice capacity |
| 3 | Daily data volume per vehicle | $V_{day} = R_{agg} \times T_{drive}$ | $T_{drive}$ = active driving hours (s) | Storage & cost estimation |
| 4 | HD map tile bandwidth | $B_{map} = \dfrac{M_{tile}}{T_{update}}$ | $M_{tile}$ = MB/tile, $T_{update}$ = s | Map diff download budgeting |
| 5 | End-to-end round-trip latency | $L_{e2e} = L_{per} + L_{net} + L_{dec} + L_{ctrl}$ | Each $L$ in ms | SLA design for V2N functions |

**Unit conversions worth memorising:**
$1\text{ TB} = 10^{6}\text{ MB}$, $1\text{ PB} = 10^{3}\text{ TB}$, $1\text{ EB} = 10^{3}\text{ PB}$.

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Urban Robotaxi (Waymo One, Cruise, Baidu Apollo Go):** Cloud is mandatory for *continuous fleet learning* — each new construction zone in Phoenix is uploaded, validated, and broadcast globally in <24 h.
- **Highway L2+ (Tesla FSD, GM Super Cruise):** Cloud enables *shadow-mode training* where the in-car AI predicts what it would do, compares with the human, and only confident improvements are auto-pushed.
- **Mining & AGV (Caterpillar Cat® MineStar, Komatsu FrontRunner):** Cloud coordinates *hundreds of off-road haul trucks*; LTE/5G private networks carry LiDAR + GNSS to a central dispatcher.
- **Logistics (TuSimple, Plus, Embark):** Long-haul trucks use cloud *platooning coordination* and *fuel-optimisation* AI that needs a continental view.
- **Smart-City Integration (Singapore, Helsinki, Dubai):** Cloud is the *digital twin* that connects AVs to traffic lights, parking, and emergency vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing the Cloud Storage of an AV Fleet

A robotaxi operator runs a fleet of **N = 1 000 vehicles**. Each vehicle carries:
- 8 cameras at 30 Hz, 2 MB/frame,
- 1 front LiDAR at 10 Hz, 75 MB/frame,
- 5 radars at 20 Hz, 0.05 MB/frame.

Average active driving = **10 h/day**. Estimate the **daily data volume per vehicle** and the **fleet storage requirement per month**.

**Step 1 — Compute each sensor's raw rate.**

$$
R_{cam} = f \times B_{frame} = 30 \text{ Hz} \times 2 \text{ MB} = 60 \text{ MB/s per camera}
$$

$$
R_{LiDAR} = 10 \text{ Hz} \times 75 \text{ MB} = 750 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
R_{radar} = 20 \text{ Hz} \times 0.05 \text{ MB} = 1 \text{ MB/s per radar}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute aggregate rate per vehicle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{agg} &= 8 \times R_{cam} + 1 \times R_{LiDAR} + 5 \times R_{radar} \\
&= 8(60) + 1(750) + 5(1) \\
&= 480 + 750 + 5 \\
&= 1\,235 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute driving time in seconds.**

$$
T_{drive} = 10 \text{ h} \times 3\,600 \text{ s/h} = 36\,000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute daily volume per vehicle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{day,1} &= R_{agg} \times T_{drive} \\
&= 1\,235 \text{ MB/s} \times 36\,000 \text{ s} \\
&= 44\,460\,000 \text{ MB} \\
&= 44.46 \text{ TB/day per vehicle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Scale to the fleet and to a 30-day month.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{day, fleet} &= N \times V_{day,1} = 1\,000 \times 44.46 = 44\,460 \text{ TB/day} \\
V_{month, fleet} &= 30 \times 44\,460 = 1\,333\,800 \text{ TB} \\
&\approx 1.33 \text{ EB/month (Exabyte)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even after 10× video compression, the fleet still produces **~130 PB/month**. This is why every hyperscaler (AWS, Azure, Google, Alibaba, Baidu) treats AV data as a *first-class storage class* and offers tiered S3-like tiers (hot, warm, cold, glacier) at sub-$0.001/GB/month.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — End-to-End Latency Budget for a Cloud-Assisted Lane Change

A vehicle uses cloud for strategic *lane-change decision* but on-board for *longitudinal control*. The allocated latencies are:

- Perception: 50 ms,
- V2N round-trip (uplink + 5G + downlink): 25 ms,
- Cloud decision: 30 ms,
- Actuation: 5 ms.

**Step 1 — Sum the per-stage latencies.**

$$
L_{e2e} = L_{per} + L_{net} + L_{dec} + L_{ctrl}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
L_{e2e} = 50 + 25 + 30 + 5 = 110 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with safety budget.** For a 120 km/h highway lane change, ISO 21448 (SOTIF) budgets ~300 ms. Our 110 ms is **well within budget**, so the architecture is safe.

> [!NOTE]
> A *pure on-board* L4 system typically keeps $L_{e2e} \leq 100$ ms. By *off-loading only the decision* to the cloud, we keep safety-critical loops local — a pattern used by Mercedes Drive Pilot and BMW Personal Pilot L3.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major Cloud-AV Platforms

| Feature | Baidu Apollo Cloud | NVIDIA DRIVE Cloud | Waymo (Google) Cloud | Tesla Fleet Learning | AWS Reference AV | Azure AV (MS + BMW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Baidu, China (2017) | NVIDIA, USA (2015) | Alphabet/Google, USA (2009) | Tesla, USA (2014) | Amazon, USA (2018) | Microsoft + OEMs, USA/EU |
| **HD Map** | Apollo Map (China focus) | DRIVE Map (partners) | Waymo HD Map (proprietary) | Tesla Vision Map (vision-only) | Customer-built (HERE/Mobilis) | HERE + Mobileye |
| **Simulation** | Dreamview + CARLA | DRIVE Sim (Omniverse) | CarCraft, SimulationCity | World Simulator (custom) | Robomaker + Sim | Azure HPC + dSPACE |
| **OTA** | Apollo Studio | DRIVE Update | Waymo OTA | Tesla OTA (legendary) | AWS IoT Jobs | Azure IoT Hub |
| **Training HW** | Baidu昆仑 / A100 | DGX H100 / DGX Cloud | Google TPU v5 pod | Dojo (custom D1) | EC2 P5 (H100) | Azure ND H100 v5 |
| **V2X** | 5G + C-V2X | DRIVE IX partner | V2X pilot (Phoenix) | Cellular (no DSRC) | AWS IoT Core | C-V2X via Airbiquity |
| **Open Source** | YES (Apache 2.0) | Partial (SDKs) | NO | NO | YES (ref. arch.) | Partial |
| **L4 Deployment** | Apollo Go (Beijing, Wuhan) | Reference (Geely, Mercedes) | Waymo One (SF/Phoenix/LA) | FSD (Supervised, L2+) | Customer (Zoox, Motional) | BMW iX, VW ID |
| **Daily Data / Fleet** | ~10 PB | ~5 PB (partners) | ~20 PB (proprietary) | ~100 PB+ (largest) | Variable | Variable |
| **Telemetry Rate** | ~150 MB/s | ~200 MB/s | ~250 MB/s | ~300 MB/s | Configurable | Configurable |
| **Key Differentiator** | Largest open AV stack | GPU + Omniverse sim | Mature L4 service | Vertical integration + Dojo | Vendor-neutral | Enterprise integration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tesla is statistically distinct** because it is the *only* major platform that has **no HD map dependency** and **no LiDAR** — it relies purely on vision + neural-net fleet learning. This is the cornerstone of Elon Musk's "vision-only" argument and is a frequently-asked KTU question.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Validation of the Analytical Matrix

1. **Identify metric** → e.g., *HD Map dependency*.
2. **Cross-check** each platform's published white paper / open-source repo.
3. **Mark TRUE** only if the platform explicitly states HD map is **production-required**.
4. **Score qualitatively** as ✔ (full), ◐ (partial), ✘ (none).
5. **Consolidate** to a single row of the comparison table.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Universal Cloud-AV Reference Architecture

### 4.3 Mermaid Service-Map — Baidu Apollo Cloud (Open Source Reference)


### 4.4 Architectural Reading Guide

- The **solid arrows** depict *primary data flow* (telemetry, map, OTA).
- The **return arrows** (`A2 → S6`, `CDN → F1`) depict *closed-loop feedback*.
- Layer 1 must guarantee **ASIL-D** functional safety; Layer 3 needs only **ASIL-QM** (quality-managed) because it is not safety-critical.
- The 5G URLLC slice (Layer 2) is the **contractual SLA boundary** between vehicle and cloud.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define a Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platform. List any four key services it provides to a connected fleet of L4 vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platform* is a hyperscale, distributed computing system that augments in-vehicle ADAS processors with off-board services such as HD map distribution, large-scale deep-learning model training, photorealistic closed-loop simulation, Over-The-Air (OTA) software delivery, fleet learning, big-data analytics, V2X orchestration, and remote teleoperation. *(Definition — 1 mark)*
Any four key services: HD map storage and distribution, fleet learning, simulation-as-a-service, OTA updates. *(2 marks for naming four)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
*Explain the role of HD map storage and distribution in a cloud-AV platform. Why is it classified as a cloud function and not an in-vehicle function?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
HD maps are centimetre-resolution vector databases containing lane geometry, traffic signs, curbs, and 3-D landmarks. They are **produced, validated, version-controlled, and broadcast** by the cloud. The in-vehicle SoC consumes only the relevant tile via CDN. *(2 marks)* They are *cloud-classified* because the raw survey data is petabyte-scale, requires periodic re-surveying (typically quarterly), and is shared across millions of vehicles — economically and thermally infeasible to host on-board. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> • Students frequently **omit units** in numerical answers → lose 1–2 marks.
> • Students frequently **forget the safety-critical boundary** (Layer 1 = ASIL-D) → lose CO-mapping marks.
> • Students frequently **write Tesla and Waymo as identical** → examiner deducts 2 marks for lack of architectural clarity.
> • Always draw the **block diagram with closed-loop arrows**; without it, the 7-mark structural question is capped at 4 marks.
> • For *comparison* questions, **use a table** — KTU examiners reward tabular comparison with at least 1 extra mark.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Services Focus

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three-layer reference architecture** of a cloud-based autonomous driving platform. Clearly label the data flow and functional role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Define the three layers.* The architecture comprises the **In-Vehicle Tier**, the **Edge Network Tier**, and the **Cloud Platform Tier**. *(1 mark)*

*Step 2 — In-Vehicle Tier.* Contains sensors (cameras, LiDAR, radar, GNSS, IMU), ADAS SoC, and the Telematics Control Unit (TCU). Performs *real-time* perception and longitudinal control at <100 ms latency. Must comply with **ISO 26262 ASIL-D**. *(2 marks)*

*Step 3 — Edge Network Tier.* Includes 5G gNB, MEC servers, and RSU. Acts as the *low-latency* relay between vehicle and cloud. Supports URLLC (1–5 ms) and C-V2X protocols (3GPP Release 16/17). *(2 marks)*

*Step 4 — Cloud Platform Tier.* Hosts the data lake, GPU training cluster, HD map database, simulation farm, OTA server, and MLOps pipelines. Performs *strategic, non-real-time* functions. *(1 mark)*

*Step 5 — Draw the block diagram* with closed-loop arrows (use the diagram in Section 4.1 as reference). *(1 mark)*

**(b)** Explain in detail the working of the following four cloud services for autonomous driving: **(i) Fleet Learning, (ii) HD Map Distribution, (iii) OTA Updates, (iv) Simulation-as-a-Service**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(i) Fleet Learning (2 marks):* Each vehicle runs a "shadow mode" where the AI predicts what it *would* do, compared with the human driver's actual action. Disagreements (edge cases) are uploaded to the data lake. A central trainer re-trains the model on aggregated edge cases, then ships new weights via OTA. Pattern used by **Tesla, Waymo, Baidu Apollo**.

*(ii) HD Map Distribution (2 marks):* Maps are partitioned into geographic tiles (~8–25 MB each). Vehicles download only the tiles covering their planned route, served via CDN edge caches. Map diffs (delta updates) are pushed within hours of new road construction. Pattern used by **Waymo HD Map, Mobileye REM, Baidu Apollo Map**.

*(iii) OTA Updates (1.5 marks):* Model and firmware packages are cryptographically signed, versioned, and rolled out in **canary fleets** (1% → 10% → 50% → 100%) to enable rollback if KPIs regress. Example: **Tesla Holiday Update** reaching 1 M+ vehicles in <48 h.

*(iv) Simulation-as-a-Service (1.5 marks):* Photorealistic engines (NVIDIA DRIVE Sim, CARLA, Waymo's SimulationCity) replay edge cases in closed-loop, varying weather, lighting, and traffic. Achieves ~10⁸ simulated miles per day — orders of magnitude beyond real-world fleet exposure.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Industry Platforms Comparison Focus

**(a)** With a case study, describe the **architecture and key services of the Baidu Apollo Cloud Platform**. Mention at least three specific open-source components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Context.* Baidu Apollo is the world's largest open-source AV project, released under Apache 2.0 in 2017, currently at v9.0. Powers the Apollo Go robotaxi service in 10+ Chinese cities. *(1 mark)*

*Step 2 — Architecture.* Comprises five sub-platforms: **Open Software Stack, Cloud Service Platform, Open Vehicle Hardware Platform, Reference Vehicle Platform, and Open Data Platform**. *(1 mark)*

*Step 3 — Open-source components (3 required):* **Cyber RT** (real-time middleware, ~1 ms scheduling); **Apollo Dreamview** (Web-based visualizer); **Apollo Map** (HD map format & editor); **Apollo Studio** (OTA + DevOps). *(3 marks)*

*Step 4 — Key cloud services.* HD map distribution, fleet learning, simulation in Dreamview, OTA via Apollo Studio, and the Apollo Open Platform (open datasets like ApolloScape with 100K+ labelled frames). *(2 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the cloud platforms of **Tesla Fleet Learning** and **Waymo (Google) Cloud Infrastructure** across any four parameters. State one architectural difference and one similarity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Comparison Table (4 marks):*

| Parameter | Tesla Fleet Learning | Waymo Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| Sensors used | Vision only (no LiDAR) | Vision + LiDAR + Radar |
| HD Map | Vision-built map, no survey HD map | Hand-surveyed HD map |
| Training HW | Custom **Dojo D1** (in-house) | Google **TPU v5 pod** |
| OTA cadence | Weekly (Holiday updates) | Monthly (geofenced) |
| Open Source | NO | NO |
| L4 service | FSD (L2+ supervised) | Waymo One (L4) |
| Data volume | ~100 PB/day (largest) | ~20 PB/day |
| Latency tolerance | Mid (~50 ms) | Low (~20 ms) |

*Architectural similarity (1.5 marks):* Both use **closed-loop fleet learning** — vehicle → cloud → model → vehicle.

*Architectural difference (1.5 marks):* Tesla is **vision-only + end-to-end neural net**, while Waymo is **multi-sensor + modular rule-augmented pipeline with hand-surveyed HD map**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platform** is *not optional* for L4+ — it is the **strategic brain** complementing the in-vehicle **tactical brain**.
- The **three universal layers** are *In-Vehicle*, *Edge/Network*, and *Cloud Platform* — memorise this for every architecture question.
- **Five formulae to remember:** per-sensor data rate, aggregate rate, daily volume, map tile bandwidth, and end-to-end latency.
- **1 vehicle ≈ 44 TB/day raw**; **1 000-vehicle fleet ≈ 1.3 EB/month**.
- **Major platforms to memorise:** Baidu Apollo (open-source, China), NVIDIA DRIVE (GPU + sim), Waymo (mature L4, USA), Tesla (vision-only, vertical integration), AWS Reference AV, Azure AV.
- **Tesla's two unique traits:** *no HD map* and *no LiDAR*.
- **Baidu Apollo's unique trait:** *largest open-source AV stack* under Apache 2.0.
- **OTA rollouts** must be *staged* (canary → ramp → fleet) and *cryptographically signed*.
- **Fleet learning** requires three components: *shadow-mode*, *aggregation*, and *re-distribution*.
- **Cloud is ASIL-QM**, not safety-critical; the safety envelope always lives in the **In-Vehicle Tier (ASIL-D)**.
- **5G URLLC** is the latency enabler — know the 1–5 ms range and how it differs from 4G (50–100 ms).
- Always include **units** in numerical answers; always include a **block diagram with closed-loop arrows** in 7-mark architecture questions; always **use a comparison table** in 7-mark platform-comparison questions.
- The 5G/V2N slice is the **SLA boundary** — a high-yield phrase for KTU 14-mark answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Note:** The Module-3 internal-choice 14-mark question is *always* either **architecture-centric** or **platform-comparison-centric**. Prepare both Question-A and Question-B patterns above and you will be safe for any variant the examiner sets.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Integration and Calibration - Integration of ADAS and AGV Components into Vehicle Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Integration and Calibration - Integration of ADAS and AGV Components into Vehicle Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Integration and Calibration** in the context of ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) and AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) refers to the engineering discipline of combining heterogeneous hardware subsystems (sensors, ECUs, actuators, and communication networks) and software stacks (perception, planning, control, and HMI) into a single, cohesive, functionally correct cyber-physical system that satisfies the OEM's vehicle-level performance, safety, and reliability specifications.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** terminology, the process is defined as the systematic fusion of:

- **Perception Layer** — Radar, LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic, IMU, GNSS
- **Computation Layer** — Domain ECUs, zonal controllers, central HPC (High-Performance Computer)
- **Actuation Layer** — Steering-by-wire, Brake-by-wire, Throttle-by-wire, Drive-by-wire
- **Communication Layer** — CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1)
- **Middleware & Software Layer** — AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive, ROS 2, Hypervisors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> *"System Integration and Calibration involves harmonizing discrete ADAS/AGV subsystems into a unified vehicle platform through sensor fusion, network arbitration, time synchronization, and end-of-line (EOL) calibration — ensuring the integrated system meets ISO 26262 (ASIL), ISO 21448 (SOTIF), and AIS standards."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body as an Integrated Vehicle:**
> Imagine integrating all ADAS/AGV components into a vehicle is like assembling a human body for a marathon.
>
> - The **Eyes, Ears, and Skin** are sensors (Camera, Radar, Ultrasonic).
> - The **Spinal Cord and Brain** are ECUs and the central HPC.
> - The **Muscles and Tendons** are actuators (steering, braking motors).
> - The **Nervous System** is the vehicle communication bus (CAN/Ethernet).
> - **Calibration** is the *physical therapy* that aligns reflexes, balance, and timing.
>
> If the eyes see an obstacle (perception) but the message takes 500 ms to reach the muscles via a slow nerve (latency on CAN), the runner trips. **System integration ensures every "nerve" speaks the same language at the right speed**, and **calibration ensures every "joint" reacts with the correct angle and force**.

---

## 1.3 Core Engineering Metrics Used in Integration

The following constants and standards govern every integrated ADAS/AGV deployment:

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **ASIL-D** | ISO 26262 | Highest automotive safety integrity level for steering/braking |
| **SOTIF** | ISO 21448 | Safety of the Intended Functionality for perception systems |
| **Time Sync Drift** | **< 1 µs** | PTP (IEEE 1588) synchronization across Ethernet nodes |
| **CAN Bus Bit Rate** | **500 kbps** (Classic) / **2–5 Mbps** (CAN-FD) | Powertrain & chassis communication |
| **Automotive Ethernet** | **100 / 1000 Mbps** | High-bandwidth sensor data (LiDAR, 4K cameras) |
| **Sensor Refresh Rate** | **20–50 Hz** | Radar/Camera frame rate for real-time fusion |
| **End-of-Line Tolerance** | **± 0.1°** (camera yaw) | Geometric calibration accuracy |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for KTU

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 carries direct weight in evaluating whether a student can **map individual component specifications to a working vehicle system**. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to:

1. Identify the correct communication protocol for a given sensor.
2. Explain the sensor fusion architecture (front-end vs. back-end fusion).
3. Justify calibration tolerances mathematically.
4. Describe the failure modes introduced by poor integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — Sensor Fusion:**
> *Sensor Fusion is the mathematical combination of sensory data from disparate sources such that the resulting information has less uncertainty than would be possible when these sources were used individually.* (Source: ISO/TR 21730 — adopted in KTU reference materials.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of ADAS/AGV Integration

The integration of ADAS and AGV components follows a **layered architecture** known as the **V-Model of Automotive Systems Engineering**, where each layer must pass a defined validation gate before progressing downward.

### Pillar 1 — Hardware Integration (Electrical & Mechanical)

- **Mounting Geometry:** Sensors must be rigidly mounted with vibration isolation to preserve extrinsic calibration.
- **Power Supply:** ISO 7637-2 compliant transient protection; load dump resilience up to **+35 V**.
- **Connector Standard:** USCAR-2, Mini-50, or Mat-enet for high-speed Ethernet links.

### Pillar 2 — Communication Network Integration

The vehicle network must support multiple data rates simultaneously. The hierarchy is:

$$\text{Powertrain/Chassis} \rightarrow \text{CAN-FD} \rightarrow \text{FlexRay (legacy)} \rightarrow \text{Automotive Ethernet}$$

A typical modern ADAS architecture uses **domain-centralized zonal E/E (Electrical/Electronic)** topology where:

$$\text{Perception Sensors} \rightarrow \text{Zonal Gateway} \rightarrow \text{Vehicle HPC} \rightarrow \text{Actuation ECUs}$$

### Pillar 3 — Software & Middleware Integration

- **AUTOSAR Classic** for real-time ECU control (µC-level, OSEK OS).
- **AUTOSAR Adaptive** for high-performance computing (POSIX OS, service-oriented).
- **ROS 2** for prototyping and AGV deployments.
- **Hypervisor** (e.g., QNX, INTEGRITY) for mixed-criticality isolation.

### Pillar 4 — Calibration & Synchronization

Calibration has two distinct meanings in this context:

1. **Intrinsic Calibration** — Characterizes the sensor itself (focal length, lens distortion).
2. **Extrinsic Calibration** — Determines the sensor's pose (position + orientation) relative to the vehicle's reference frame.

---

## 2.2 Sensor Fusion Architectures (High-Yield)

### (a) Front-End (Raw / Low-Level) Fusion

Raw sensor data is fused at the signal level **before** object detection. Highest accuracy but highest bandwidth.

$$\mathbf{z}_{fused}(t) = \mathcal{F}\left(\mathbf{z}_{radar}(t), \, \mathbf{z}_{lidar}(t), \, \mathbf{z}_{cam}(t)\right)$$

### (b) Back-End (Object / High-Level) Fusion

Each sensor produces object lists independently, then a tracker fuses them.

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}(k|k) = \hat{\mathbf{x}}(k|k-1) + K(k)\left[\mathbf{z}_{obj}(k) - H\hat{\mathbf{x}}(k|k-1)\right]$$

Where:
- $\hat{\mathbf{x}}(k|k)$ — fused state estimate at time $k$
- $K(k)$ — Kalman gain
- $H$ — measurement matrix
- $\mathbf{z}_{obj}(k)$ — vector of measured object positions

### (c) Centralized vs. Distributed Fusion

| Aspect | Centralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| Computation | Central HPC | Per-sensor ECU |
| Latency | Low (~10 ms) | Higher (~30–50 ms) |
| Bandwidth | Very High | Low |
| Use Case | L3+ ADAS, Robotaxis | Entry-level ADAS, AGV |

---

## 2.3 Extrinsic Calibration Mathematics (High-Yield)

The pose of a sensor with respect to the vehicle's rear-axle coordinate frame is described by a **rigid-body transformation**:

$$\mathbf{T}_{sensor}^{vehicle} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{3 \times 3} & \mathbf{t}_{3 \times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{R}$ — Rotation matrix ($\in SO(3)$, determinant = +1)
- $\mathbf{t}$ — Translation vector (in metres)

For a **camera-lidar pair**, the calibration minimizes the re-projection error:

$$\min_{\mathbf{R},\mathbf{t}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \pi\left(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{P}_{lidar,i} + \mathbf{t}\right) - \mathbf{p}_{cam,i} \right\|^2$$

Where $\pi(\cdot)$ is the camera projection function and $\mathbf{P}_{lidar,i}, \mathbf{p}_{cam,i}$ are corresponding 3D-to-2D points on a calibration target.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every high-yield equation for Module 4 — System Integration. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Rigid-body transformation (extrinsic cal.) | $SO(3)$ + $\mathbb{R}^3$ |
| 2 | $\mathbf{x}_{k} = \mathbf{F}_{k}\mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}_{k}\mathbf{u}_{k} + \mathbf{w}_{k}$ | Linear KF prediction | State vector $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{P}_{k\|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_{k}\mathbf{P}_{k-1\|k-1}\mathbf{F}_{k}^{T} + \mathbf{Q}_{k}$ | KF covariance prediction | Covariance $\mathbf{P}$ |
| 4 | $K_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\|k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^T(\mathbf{H}_k\mathbf{P}_{k\|k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\|k-1} + K_k(\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\|k-1})$ | KF state update | State vector |
| 6 | $f_{sync} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta t_i$ | Time-sync offset (PTP) | $\vert \Delta t \vert \le 1\,\mu s$ |
| 7 | $\tau_{latency} = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{comm} + \tau_{actuate}$ | Total AEB latency | $< 150$ ms |
| 8 | $\mathbf{y}_{cam} = \mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}\right]\mathbf{X}_{world}$ | Pin-hole camera projection | Pixel coordinates |
| 9 | $\text{ASIL} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Exposure}, \text{Controllability})$ | ISO 26262 risk classification | A, B, C, D |
| 10 | $\text{PFA} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of Failure on AGV mission | $\lambda$ = failure rate |

---

## 2.5 Functional Safety Mapping (ISO 26262 ASIL Decomposition)

When integrating ADAS functions, ASIL requirements can be decomposed across redundant ECUs:

$$\text{ASIL } D(x) \rightarrow \text{ASIL } C(x) + \text{ASIL } A(x)$$

A steering-integrated ADAS function (e.g., LKA) typically requires **ASIL-D** on the actuation ECU, while perception may be **ASIL-B** with **SOTIF** arguments.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility & Industrial Relevance

- **OEM Production Lines:** End-of-Line (EOL) calibration stations align every sensor with **±0.1°** yaw accuracy using laser trackers or checkerboard targets.
- **AGV Warehouses:** Mobile robots integrate 2D LiDAR + IMU + wheel odometry with continuous online calibration (SLAM).
- **Software-Defined Vehicles (SDV):** Over-the-Air (OTA) integration updates demand that calibration metadata be version-controlled alongside the perception stack.
- **Cost Driver:** Poor integration is the **#1 cause of ADAS field recalls** (NHTSA data, 2022–2024) — typically 30–40% of warranty cost in L2+ vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kalman Filter Update for Sensor Fusion

We now derive the **Kalman Filter (KF) measurement update** step used when fusing radar and camera object reports — a KTU-favourite derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the State Vector

For a tracked object (e.g., a pedestrian ahead of the vehicle), the state vector contains position and velocity in the vehicle frame:

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ v_x \\ v_y \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Linear Motion Model (Prediction Step)

Assuming constant velocity over one time step $\Delta t$, the state transition matrix is:

$$\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The predicted state is therefore:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_{k}$$

For simplicity, we assume no control input ($\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_{k} = 0$), so:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1}$$

### Step 3 — Covariance Prediction

The a-priori covariance matrix is propagated using the process noise covariance $\mathbf{Q}$:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{P}_{k-1|k-1}\mathbf{F}^T + \mathbf{Q}_{k}$$

### Step 4 — Define the Measurement Model

A radar reports range $r$, bearing $\theta$, and range-rate $\dot{r}$:

$$\mathbf{z}_{radar} = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \theta \\ \dot{r} \end{bmatrix} = h(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{v}$$

Where $h(\mathbf{x})$ is the non-linear measurement function. For a linearized EKF, the measurement matrix $\mathbf{H}$ is the Jacobian of $h$.

### Step 5 — Compute the Kalman Gain

$$K_k = \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^T\left(\mathbf{H}_k\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k\right)^{-1}$$

Where $\mathbf{R}_k$ is the measurement noise covariance (sensor-dependent).

### Step 6 — State and Covariance Update

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + K_k\left(\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1}\right)$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k|k} = \left(\mathbf{I} - K_k\mathbf{H}_k\right)\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1}$$

### Step 7 — Fusion of Two Sensor Reports

When both radar and camera report at time $k$, sequential updates are performed:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k|k}^{radar} = \left(\mathbf{I} - K_k^{radar}\mathbf{H}^{radar}\right)\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1}$$

Then the camera update uses the new covariance:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k|k}^{fused} = \left(\mathbf{I} - K_k^{cam}\mathbf{H}^{cam}\right)\mathbf{P}_{k|k}^{radar}$$

The resulting $\mathbf{P}_{k|k}^{fused}$ is **smaller** than either individual posterior — confirming the fusion claim from Section 1.4.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Latency Budget for AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)

A KTU 2024 favourite: **derive the maximum allowable system latency for AEB.**

### Step 1 — Define Total Reaction Distance

At a closing speed $v$ (m/s), the stopping distance is:

$$d_{stop} = v \cdot \tau_{total} + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{brake}}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{total}$ — total system latency (s)
- $a_{brake}$ — maximum braking deceleration (m/s²), typically **0.8 g ≈ 7.84 m/s²**

### Step 2 — Set Up the Latency Equation

The total latency decomposes as:

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{compute} + \tau_{comm} + \tau_{actuate}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation (Example: $v = 30$ m/s ≈ 108 km/h, target distance $d = 50$ m)

For the vehicle to stop **just before** the obstacle:

$$50 = 30 \cdot \tau_{total} + \frac{30^2}{2 \cdot 7.84}$$

Solving the deceleration term:

$$\frac{900}{15.68} \approx 57.4 \text{ m}$$

Since $57.4 > 50$, **braking alone cannot prevent collision**. The driver assistance or perception must trigger earlier (at $d > 57.4$ m). Therefore:

$$d_{trigger} = v \cdot \tau_{total} + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{brake}}$$

Substituting $d_{trigger} = 60$ m (with 10 m safety margin):

$$60 = 30 \cdot \tau_{total} + 57.4$$

$$\tau_{total} = \frac{60 - 57.4}{30} = 0.087 \text{ s} = 87 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4 — Latency Allocation Across Subsystems

| Subsystem | Allocation | Typical Realization |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing ($\tau_{sense}$) | **20 ms** | 50 Hz radar + 30 Hz camera |
| Compute ($\tau_{compute}$) | **30 ms** | Perception + planning on HPC |
| Comm ($\tau_{comm}$) | **10 ms** | Automotive Ethernet, PTP sync |
| Actuate ($\tau_{actuate}$) | **27 ms** | Brake-by-wire ISO 13849 |
| **Total** | **87 ms** | Within UN R152 AEB limit |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Communication Protocols

| Protocol | Bit Rate | Latency | Use Case in ADAS/AGV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CAN 2.0** | 500 kbps | ~1 ms | Body control, simple sensors |
| **CAN-FD** | 2–5 Mbps | ~0.5 ms | Powertrain, chassis, ADAS sensors |
| **LIN** | 20 kbps | ~10 ms | Low-cost actuators (mirrors) |
| **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps | < 1 ms (TDMA) | X-by-wire (legacy) |
| **Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1** | **100 Mbps** | < 0.1 ms | ADAS sensors, zonal gateways |
| **Automotive Ethernet 1000BASE-T1** | **1 Gbps** | < 0.1 ms | LiDAR, 4K surround view, HPC |
| **PCIe 4.0 (internal)** | 16 GT/s | < 1 µs | Sensor-to-SoC on-board |

---

## 3.4 Intrinsic Camera Calibration — Pinhole Model

A pinhole camera maps a 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w$ to image coordinates $\mathbf{y}$:

$$\mathbf{y}_{cam} = \mathbf{K}\left[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}\right]\mathbf{X}_{world}$$

Where the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ is:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $f_x, f_y$ — focal lengths (pixels)
- $c_x, c_y$ — principal point (pixels)
- $s$ — skew (≈ 0 for modern sensors)

Calibration determines $\mathbf{K}$ by minimizing the **re-projection error** over $N \ge 10$ checkerboard images:

$$\min_{\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \left\| \mathbf{p}_{ij} - \hat{\mathbf{p}}(\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i, \mathbf{X}_j) \right\|^2$$

Where $\mathbf{p}_{ij}$ is the observed corner and $\hat{\mathbf{p}}$ is the predicted projection.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — ADAS/AGV Integration

The following diagram maps every major integration block, its communication protocol, and the data flow direction. It is rendered as a left-to-right block architecture rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
    HPC[Vehicle HPC\nADAS Domain SoC\nAUTOSAR Adaptive] --> FUSION
```

```mermaid
    CAM->>ETH: Object list @ t=20 ms
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define sensor fusion and explain the difference between front-end and back-end fusion with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sensor fusion is the mathematical combination of sensory data from heterogeneous sources to produce information with lower uncertainty than any individual source.

- **Front-End Fusion (Low-Level):** Raw data from sensors is fused **before** object detection. Example: Fusing raw LiDAR point cloud with raw camera pixels in a bird's-eye-view representation for semantic segmentation. Bandwidth-heavy but most accurate.
- **Back-End Fusion (High-Level / Object-Level):** Each sensor independently detects objects, producing object lists (tracks). These lists are then fused by a central tracker. Example: Radar and camera each output a "pedestrian at (x,y)" list; an EKF tracker fuses them.

*(Mark split: Definition = 1 mark, Front-end + example = 1 mark, Back-end + example = 1 mark.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List any three in-vehicle communication protocols used in ADAS integration and state one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **CAN-FD** (2 Mbps) — Used for radar and chassis ECUs in ADAS sensor data exchange.
2. **Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1** (100 Mbps) — Used for high-bandwidth LiDAR and surround-view camera data.
3. **FlexRay** (10 Mbps, TDMA) — Used in legacy steer-by-wire systems requiring deterministic timing.

*(Mark split: 1 mark per protocol + application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. **Internal choice** means students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the layered architecture of an integrated ADAS system. Identify the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle travelling at $v = 25$ m/s must stop before an obstacle at $d = 45$ m using AEB. The maximum braking deceleration is $a = 7.84$ m/s². The sensing latency is 20 ms, compute latency is 30 ms, communication latency is 10 ms, and actuation latency is 25 ms. Determine whether the system can prevent the collision. If not, by what distance will it overshoot? **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Layered ADAS Architecture (7 marks):**

| Layer | Function | Typical Components |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Perception** | Detect and classify objects | Camera, Radar, LiDAR, Ultrasonic |
| **2. Localization** | Estimate ego-position | GNSS, IMU, HD-Map matching |
| **3. Fusion & Tracking** | Combine sensor outputs | EKF/UKF tracker, occupancy grid |
| **4. Planning** | Decide trajectory | Behavior planner, motion planner |
| **5. Control** | Execute trajectory | VCU, longitudinal + lateral controllers |
| **6. Actuation** | Apply physical force | Steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire, throttle |
| **7. HMI** | Inform driver | Cluster display, audio alerts |

*(Award 1 mark per layer with brief description; 7 layers fit 7 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical AEB Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute total latency:**

$$\tau_{total} = 20 + 30 + 10 + 25 = 85 \text{ ms} = 0.085 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute stopping distance:**

$$d_{stop} = v \cdot \tau_{total} + \frac{v^2}{2a}$$

$$d_{stop} = 25 \times 0.085 + \frac{25^2}{2 \times 7.84}$$

$$d_{stop} = 2.125 + \frac{625}{15.68}$$

$$d_{stop} = 2.125 + 39.86 = 41.99 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with obstacle distance:**

Since $d_{stop} = 41.99 \text{ m} < 45 \text{ m}$, the system **CAN prevent the collision**, with a safety margin of:

$$\Delta d = 45 - 41.99 = 3.01 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The integrated ADAS system successfully prevents the collision with a 3.01 m residual margin under the given latencies.

---

### **Question B — [Alternative Choice for Question A]**
**(a)** Explain the extrinsic calibration procedure for a camera mounted on an AGV. List the required equipment and the key mathematical formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AGV uses a 2D LiDAR with angular resolution $\Delta \theta = 0.5°$ and update rate of 20 Hz. The vehicle moves at 1.5 m/s. Compute the angular and translational uncertainty introduced between two consecutive scans, and comment on how this affects SLAM performance. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Extrinsic Camera Calibration on AGV (7 marks):**

1. **Equipment Required (2 marks):**
   - Calibrated checkerboard target (e.g., 9×6 grid, 25 mm squares)
   - Mounted tripod / wall fixture
   - Camera with known intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$
   - Calibration software (MATLAB Camera Calibrator, OpenCV `calibrateCamera`)

2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - Capture $N \ge 15$ images of the target from varied poses.
   - Detect corner features $\mathbf{p}_{ij}$ in each image.
   - Solve for extrinsic $\{\mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i\}$ per image and intrinsic $\mathbf{K}$ jointly by minimizing re-projection error.

3. **Mathematical Formulation (2 marks):**

$$\min_{\mathbf{K},\mathbf{R}_i,\mathbf{t}_i} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \left\| \mathbf{p}_{ij} - \hat{\mathbf{p}}(\mathbf{K},\mathbf{R}_i,\mathbf{t}_i,\mathbf{X}_j) \right\|^2$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{p}} = \pi(\mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}_i \mid \mathbf{t}_i]\mathbf{X}_j)$ is the predicted projection.

---

**(b) LiDAR Scan Uncertainty on AGV (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Angular Uncertainty per scan (1 mark):**

$$\delta \theta = \pm \frac{\Delta \theta}{2} = \pm 0.25° = \pm 4.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Time between scans (1 mark):**

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Translational displacement per scan (1 mark):**

$$\delta x = v \cdot \Delta t = 1.5 \times 0.05 = 0.075 \text{ m} = 7.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Transverse point displacement due to angular uncertainty at range $R$ (2 marks):**

$$\delta y = R \cdot \delta \theta$$

For $R = 10$ m:

$$\delta y = 10 \times 4.36 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0436 \text{ m} \approx 4.4 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on SLAM performance (2 marks):**

The combined translational drift of 7.5 cm per scan, coupled with angular uncertainty of ~4.4 cm at 10 m range, introduces a **scan-matching noise floor** that must be handled by the SLAM front-end (e.g., ICP variant with sufficient iterations). Velocity-induced motion distortion must be corrected by interpolating scans using wheel odometry / IMU before matching — this is exactly where **system integration of LiDAR + IMU + wheel encoder** becomes critical for AGV accuracy.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Module 4 — System Integration:**
> 1. **Confusing intrinsic vs. extrinsic calibration** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you state "calibrate the sensor" without specifying which type.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the latency budget — Always state milliseconds or seconds explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring SOTIF (ISO 21448)** — Many students only cite ISO 26262; modern KTU papers expect a one-line mention of SOTIF for perception systems.
> 4. **Wrong bus protocol mapping** — Do **NOT** claim LiDAR streams over CAN; it requires Automotive Ethernet. Examiners are strict here.
> 5. **Skipping the prediction step** in Kalman filter derivations — Show both $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k}$ explicitly.
> 6. **No mention of redundancy / ASIL decomposition** — For 14-mark questions on integration, you must discuss **fault tolerance** to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4, Integration Topic:**

- ☐ System integration = combining **hardware, network, software, calibration** layers.
- ☐ **ASIL-D** applies to steering and braking actuation; **ASIL-B** typical for perception.
- ☐ **SOTIF (ISO 21448)** addresses perception edge cases; complements ISO 26262.
- ☐ **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)** is the modern backbone for ADAS sensors.
- ☐ **CAN-FD** handles radar and chassis; **LIN** for low-cost actuators only.
- ☐ **Intrinsic calibration** = sensor-internal (focal length, distortion); **Extrinsic** = sensor pose relative to vehicle.
- ☐ **Sensor fusion** = front-end (raw) vs. back-end (object); EKF is the workhorse algorithm.
- ☐ **Time synchronization** via IEEE 1588 PTP with drift **< 1 µs** is mandatory for fusion.
- ☐ AEB total latency budget = **~100–150 ms** including sensing, compute, comm, actuation.
- ☐ Calibration tolerances: yaw **±0.1°**, pitch **±0.2°**, translation **±5 mm** for camera.
- ☐ **ASIL decomposition** allows a D-class safety goal to be split across redundant ECUs.
- ☐ AGV LiDAR + IMU + wheel-odometry fusion is essential to mitigate scan-to-scan motion distortion.
- ☐ End-of-Line (EOL) calibration uses **laser tracker** or checkerboard with $>15$ poses.
- ☐ OTA updates in SDVs require **version-controlled calibration metadata**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT611 - AUTOMATED GUIDED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the systematic process of comparing a sensor's or actuator's output against a *traceable reference standard* of known accuracy, and adjusting or documenting the deviation (error) such that the device operates within specified tolerances over its intended operating range.

In the context of **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** and **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**, calibration establishes a deterministic mathematical relationship between the **physical measurand** (e.g., distance, angle, force, voltage) and the **device's output signal**, thereby ensuring the vehicle's perception, localization, and control systems meet **ISO 26262** functional safety and **SAE J3016** performance expectations.

Mathematically, calibration seeks to determine the function $\mathcal{C}$ such that:

$$
y = \mathcal{C}(u, \boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

where:
- $u$ is the raw device output (volts, counts, radians)
- $y$ is the calibrated, physically meaningful output
- $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is the vector of calibration parameters (e.g., scale, offset, axis misalignment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Traceability** refers to the unbroken chain of comparisons linking a measurement back to an internationally accepted standard such as those maintained by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** or the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ruler and Scale" Intuition

Imagine you buy a tailor's measuring tape from a street vendor. The tape claims to read in **centimeters**, but the actual marks are slightly stretched — every "10 cm" mark is really 10.3 cm away from zero. If you measure a piece of cloth, you will cut it too long. The vendor must therefore **compare** the tape against a *master ruler* (the reference standard), note the deviation, and either physically correct the tape or hand you a *correction chart*.

**Calibration in an AGV is exactly this process**, except the "tape" is a **LiDAR**, **camera**, **IMU**, or **steering encoder**, and the "master ruler" is a high-precision reference instrument (e.g., a Faro laser tracker for spatial calibration, or a precision turntable for IMU bias determination).

> [!TIP]
> Think of calibration as giving the vehicle a **pair of correctly prescribed spectacles** — without it, every perception decision, lane change, and braking action is made through a "fog" of systematic error.

## 1.3 Why Calibration is Non-Negotiable in AGVs/AVs

| Subsystem | Typical Sensor/Actuator | Consequence of Poor Calibration |
|---|---|---|
| Localization | GNSS, IMU, Wheel Encoder | **Position drift** of several meters over 1 km |
| Perception | LiDAR, Camera, Radar | **Misclassification** of obstacles, false lane detections |
| Steering | Servo motor + encoder | **Lane departure**, unstable path tracking |
| Propulsion | BLDC motor, throttle | Inconsistent acceleration, range errors |
| Braking | Electro-hydraulic actuator | Increased **stopping distance**, safety risk |

> [!WARNING]
> Even a **1°** error in LiDAR mounting angle can produce a lateral position error of approximately **17.5 cm** at a distance of 10 m. At highway speeds, such errors are catastrophic.

## 1.4 Standard Calibration Performance Metrics

- **Accuracy**: Closeness of measurement to the *true value* (qualitative).
- **Precision / Repeatability**: Closeness of *repeated* measurements to each other.
- **Resolution**: Smallest detectable change in input.
- **Linearity**: Maximum deviation of the calibration curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of full scale (**%FS**).
- **Hysteresis**: Difference in output when approached from increasing vs. decreasing input.
- **Drift**: Slow change of output with time at constant input.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Calibration Techniques

Calibration techniques form a four-tier hierarchy used universally in vehicular mechatronics:

1. **Factory Calibration (Offline)** — performed under controlled laboratory conditions before deployment.
2. **Online / In-Situ Calibration** — performed during vehicle operation using self-consistency constraints.
3. **Dynamic vs. Static Calibration** — based on whether the input varies during the process.
4. **Absolute vs. Relative Calibration** — based on whether an *external reference* is used.

### 2.1.1 Sensor Calibration Types (Detailed)

**(a) Single-Point (Zero) Calibration**
- Forces the output to read *zero* (or a known offset) when the measurand is zero.
- Suitable only for sensors with known linear response and known gain.
- Equation: $y = a \cdot u + b$, with $b$ determined by the zero point.

**(b) Two-Point (Zero + Span) Calibration**
- Uses two reference inputs $x_1$ and $x_2$ to solve for both slope $a$ and offset $b$:

$$
a = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{u_2 - u_1}, \qquad b = y_1 - a u_1
$$

- Industry standard for analog pressure transducers, strain gauges, and accelerometers.

**(c) Multi-Point Calibration**
- Acquires $N \geq 3$ reference points and fits a higher-order polynomial (typically cubic) using **least-squares regression**.
- Used when non-linearity exceeds 0.1% FS.

**(d) Look-Up Table (LUT) Calibration**
- Stores empirical correction values at grid points; intermediate values obtained by **linear interpolation**.
- Common in ECUs for thermistor-based temperature sensors and throttle-position sensors.

### 2.1.2 Actuator Calibration

Actuator calibration verifies the *commanded-vs-actual* relationship. The key parameters are:

- **Static gain** $K$ — ratio of output to input at steady state.
- **Dead-zone** $\Delta$ — minimum input required to produce any motion.
- **Saturation** $U_{sat}$ — maximum output deliverable.
- **Backlash** $B$ — clearance in gear trains causing bidirectional error.
- **Time constants** $\tau_1, \tau_2$ — first/second-order dynamic response.

> [!NOTE]
> For an automotive **steering-by-wire** actuator, calibration must characterize the **PWM-to-steer-angle** transfer function, including backlash in the steering column and compliance in the rack.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y = a u + b$ | Linear sensor model | Two-point calibration |
| 2 | $y = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3$ | Cubic polynomial model | Non-linear sensor fit |
| 3 | $a_j = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$ | Normal equation for LS fit | Multi-point calibration |
| 4 | $\text{NL} = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{fit} - y_{ref} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Non-linearity (%FS) | Sensor spec |
| 5 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Coefficient of determination | Goodness of fit |
| 6 | $\sigma_{repeat} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n-1}}$ | Repeatability (1$\sigma$) | Precision metric |
| 7 | $H = \dfrac{\vert y_{up} - y_{down} \vert_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Hysteresis (%FS) | Bidirectional error |
| 8 | $\mathbf{T}_{sen}^{veh} = \mathbf{T}_{ext} \mathbf{T}_{sen}^{int}}$ | Rigid-body extrinsic transform | LiDAR-to-vehicle mount |
| 9 | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - f(u_i, \boldsymbol{\theta}))^2$ | LS objective function | Parameter estimation |
| 10 | $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P} \mathbf{H}^\top (\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{H}^\top + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Kalman filter gain | Online bias estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every formula above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value and **must not** be confused with the markdown table pipe symbol.

## 2.3 Calibration of Common AGV/AV Sensors

### 2.3.1 LiDAR Extrinsic Calibration
Determines the **6-DOF rigid transform** $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ between the LiDAR frame $\{L\}$ and the vehicle frame $\{V\}$:

$$
\mathbf{p}_V = \mathbf{R}_{L}^{V}\, \mathbf{p}_L + \mathbf{t}_{L}^{V}
$$

Common method: place a checkerboard / planar target at $N$ known poses and minimize point-to-plane residuals.

### 2.3.2 Camera Intrinsic & Extrinsic Calibration
- **Intrinsic** parameters: focal length $f_x, f_y$, principal point $c_x, c_y$, distortion coefficients $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2, k_3$ (Brown–Conrady model).
- **Extrinsic** parameters: rotation $\mathbf{R}_C^V$, translation $\mathbf{t}_C^V$.
- Standard tool: **Zhang's method** using planar checkerboard (≥ 10 views at varied orientations).

### 2.3.3 IMU Calibration
- **Bias** $\mathbf{b}_a$, $\mathbf{b}_g$ — determined by static averaging over 30–60 s.
- **Scale factor** $s_a$, $s_g$ — determined by comparing against gravity and a known rotation rate.
- **Misalignment matrix** $\mathbf{M}_a$ — determined by 6-position tumble test (g, −g on each axis).

### 2.3.4 Wheel Odometry Calibration
Calibrates the *effective wheel radius* $r_{eff}$ and *track width* $L$ by commanding the vehicle to drive a known closed-loop trajectory (e.g., a square) and minimizing the terminal pose error.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Calibration Target | Resulting Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| Highway pilot AV | Camera-LiDAR fusion | Lane-level accuracy at 130 km/h |
| Warehouse AGV | Wheel encoder + floor-marker | ± 5 mm docking precision |
| Mobile robot manipulator | Arm joint encoders | Sub-mm pick-and-place |
| Agricultural autonomous tractor | GNSS + IMU | < 2 cm row-guidance error |
| Autonomous mining truck | LiDAR + radar | Collision avoidance at 50 t payload |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Two-Point (Zero + Span) Linear Calibration

We assume a linear sensor model:

$$
y = a u + b
$$

where $y$ is the *true* physical quantity and $u$ is the raw sensor reading (e.g., voltage).

**Step 1 — Apply first reference input.**
Apply a known reference $y = y_1$ and record the raw output $u = u_1$:

$$
y_1 = a u_1 + b \quad (1)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply second reference input.**
Apply a second known reference $y = y_2$ and record $u = u_2$:

$$
y_2 = a u_2 + b \quad (2)
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract (1) from (2) to isolate $a$.**

$$
y_2 - y_1 = a (u_2 - u_1)
$$

Solving for $a$:

$$
a = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{u_2 - u_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $a$ back into (1) to find $b$.**

$$
b = y_1 - a u_1 = y_1 - \frac{(y_2 - y_1)\, u_1}{u_2 - u_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain the calibrated estimator.**

$$
\boxed{\, y = \left(\frac{y_2 - y_1}{u_2 - u_1}\right) u + \left( y_1 - \frac{(y_2 - y_1)\, u_1}{u_2 - u_1} \right) \,}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification using the two reference points.**
- For $u = u_1$: $y = a u_1 + b = a u_1 + (y_1 - a u_1) = y_1$ ✓
- For $u = u_2$: $y = a u_2 + b = a u_2 + (y_1 - a u_1) = a(u_2 - u_1) + y_1 = y_2$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> This is the derivation most frequently tested in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions on instrumentation.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Least-Squares Multi-Point Calibration

We now consider $N$ calibration points $(u_i, y_i)$ with $N \geq 3$, and seek the best-fit polynomial of degree $M$:

$$
\hat{y} = \sum_{j=0}^{M} a_j u^j
$$

**Step 1 — Define the residual at each point.**

$$
r_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{M} a_j u_i^j
$$

**Step 2 — Define the scalar cost function (sum of squared residuals).**

$$
J(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i^{\,2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{M} a_j u_i^j \right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $J$ with respect to each $a_k$ and set to zero** (first-order optimality).

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial a_k} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i^k \left( y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{M} a_j u_i^j \right) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the normal-equation form.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i^k y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=0}^{M} a_j u_i^{k+j}
$$

**Step 5 — Write in matrix form.** Let $X_{ij} = u_i^j$ (the $N \times (M+1)$ Vandermonde-like matrix) and $\mathbf{a} = [a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_M]^\top$, $\mathbf{y} = [y_1, \ldots, y_N]^\top$.

$$
\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \, \mathbf{a}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the linear system.**

$$
\boxed{\, \mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \,}
$$

**Step 7 — Validity check using coefficient of determination.**

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}
$$

A value of $R^2 \geq 0.999$ is generally accepted for vehicular-grade sensor calibration.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Extrinsic LiDAR-to-Vehicle Calibration via Point-to-Plane Minimization

We aim to find $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ such that each LiDAR-measured point $\mathbf{p}_L^{(i)}$ is mapped onto a known planar target with normal $\mathbf{n}$ passing through $\mathbf{d}$:

$$
\mathbf{n}^\top \bigl( \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_L^{(i)} + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{d} \bigr) = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Form the residual for the $i$-th point.**

$$
r_i = \mathbf{n}^\top \bigl( \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_L^{(i)} + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{d} \bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Sum squared residuals for $N$ points.**

$$
J(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \mathbf{n}^\top \bigl( \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_L^{(i)} + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{d} \bigr) \right]^2
$$

**Step 3 — Linearize using small-angle approximation** (for fine calibration refinement). Let $\mathbf{R} \approx \mathbf{I} + [\boldsymbol{\delta\theta}]_\times$ and substitute:

$$
r_i \approx \mathbf{n}^\top \bigl( \mathbf{p}_L^{(i)} + [\boldsymbol{\delta\theta}]_\times \mathbf{p}_L^{(i)} + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{d} \bigr)
$$

Using $[\boldsymbol{\delta\theta}]_\times \mathbf{p} = -[\mathbf{p}]_\times \boldsymbol{\delta\theta}$:

$$
r_i \approx \mathbf{n}^\top (\mathbf{p}_L^{(i)} - \mathbf{d}) + \mathbf{n}^\top \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{n}^\top [\mathbf{p}_L^{(i)}]_\times \boldsymbol{\delta\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Build the linear system** $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{t}^\top, \boldsymbol{\delta\theta}^\top]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^6$ and:

$$
\mathbf{A}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{n}^\top & -\mathbf{n}^\top [\mathbf{p}_L^{(i)}]_\times \end{bmatrix}, \qquad b_i = -\mathbf{n}^\top (\mathbf{p}_L^{(i)} - \mathbf{d})
$$

**Step 5 — Solve via least squares.**

$$
\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{A}^\top \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^\top \mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 6 — Iterate** until $\vert \boldsymbol{\delta\theta} \vert$ falls below a chosen threshold (typically $10^{-4}$ rad).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Calibration Methods

| Method | Inputs Required | Computational Cost | Best Use Case | Achievable Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Point | 1 reference | Negligible | Zero-offset trim | ± 1–2 %FS |
| Two-Point | 2 references | Negligible | Linear sensors | ± 0.5 %FS |
| Multi-Point LS | $N \geq 5$ refs | $O(N M^2)$ | Non-linear sensors | ± 0.1 %FS |
| LUT + Interpolation | $N \geq 20$ refs | $O(\log N)$ | ECU thermal maps | ± 0.05 %FS |
| LiDAR Extrinsic | Multiple target poses | $O(N k)$ iterations | AV perception | ± 0.1° / ± 1 cm |
| IMU 6-Position | 6 static orientations | Medium | MEMS IMU | ± 0.005 °/s |
| Online EKF | Continuous data | $O(k^3)$ per step | Drift compensation | Application dependent |

> [!TIP]
> Always choose the **simplest method** that meets the accuracy requirement — extra complexity increases cost and reduces reliability.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Calibration Objective] --> B[Select Reference Standard]
    B --> C[Setup Test Environment]
    C --> D[Acquire Reference and Device Readings]
    D --> E[Compute Calibration Parameters]
    E --> F{Parameters Valid?}
    F -- No --> G[Adjust Setup or Method]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Document Calibration Certificate]
    H --> I[Apply Correction in Real Time]
    I --> J[Schedule Recalibration]
```

## 4.2 Sensor Calibration Architecture for an AGV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SensorSuite[Sensor Suite]
        S1[LiDAR]
        S2[CAMERA]
        S3[IMU]
        S4[WHEEL ENCODER]
        S5[GNSS]
    end

    subgraph CalUnit[Calibration Processor]
        CP1[Intrinsic Cal]
        CP2[Extrinsic Cal]
        CP3[Bias Cal]
        CP4[Online Estimator]
    end

    subgraph RefStd[Reference Standards]
        R1[Laser Tracker]
        R2[Checkerboard]
        R3[Tumble Table]
        R4[Surveyed Markers]
    end

    S1 --> CP2
    S2 --> CP1
    S2 --> CP2
    S3 --> CP3
    S4 --> CP4
    S5 --> CP4

    R1 --> CP2
    R2 --> CP1
    R2 --> CP2
    R3 --> CP3
    R4 --> CP4

    CP1 --> OUT[Calibrated Sensor Stream]
    CP2 --> OUT
    CP3 --> OUT
    CP4 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Actuator Calibration Loop (Steering-by-Wire Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CMD[Commanded Steer Angle] --> ECU[Vehcile ECU]
    ECU --> PWM[PWM Signal Generator]
    PWM --> MTR[Steering Servo Motor]
    MTR --> GB[Gearbox with Backlash B]
    GB --> RW[Road Wheel]
    RW --> FDBK[Steering Angle Encoder]
    FDBK --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> COMP[Comparator: Actual vs Reference]
    REF[Reference Steering Target] --> COMP
    COMP --> CAL[Calibration Algorithm]
    CAL --> ECU
    CAL --> CERT[Calibration Report]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic — When to Recalibrate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Periodic Check Triggered] --> Q1{Age > 6 Months?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> RECAL[Perform Recalibration]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Vehicle in Accident?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> RECAL
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Drift Exceeds Threshold?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> RECAL
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Environmental Change?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> RECAL
    Q4 -- No --> CONT[Continue Operation]
    RECAL --> CONT
```

## 4.5 Online vs Offline Calibration Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OFF[Offline Calibration]
        O1[High-Precision Reference]
        O2[Controlled Environment]
        O3[Single Calibration Snapshot]
        O1 --> O2 --> O3
    end

    subgraph ON[Online Calibration]
        N1[Self-Consistency Constraints]
        N2[Continuous Update via Filter]
        N3[Real-Time Adaptation]
        N1 --> N2 --> N3
    end

    OFF -- Reference Values --> ON
    ON -- Validates --> OFF
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define sensor calibration. List any two performance metrics used to evaluate a calibrated sensor.**

**Model Answer:**
Sensor calibration is the process of comparing a sensor's output with a traceable reference standard and adjusting it to reduce systematic errors.

Two performance metrics:
1. **Accuracy** — closeness of the measured value to the true value.
2. **Repeatability (Precision)** — closeness of successive measurements under identical conditions.

*(Alternative: Linearity, Hysteresis, Drift, Resolution.)* — **3 marks**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between static and dynamic calibration of a sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Calibration | Dynamic Calibration |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Constant or slowly varying | Time-varying |
| Purpose | Determine gain, offset, linearity | Determine bandwidth, time constants, phase lag |
| Instruments used | Dead-weight tester, gauge blocks | Shakers, function generators |
| Example | Pressure transducer calibration | Accelerometer frequency response |

— **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)
